*301. (cid:174)ीमती गनीबेन नागाजी ठाकोर:
(cid:179)या आयुष म(cid:253)ं ी यह बताने क(cid:236) कृपा करग(cid:164) े िक:
(क) सरकार (cid:300)ारा िवगत तीन वष(cid:334) के दौरान गजु रात सिहत रा(cid:186)यवार/संघ रा(cid:186)य(cid:177)े(cid:253)वार कुल
िकतने आयुव(cid:165)िदक िचिक(cid:194)सा महािव(cid:299)ालय (cid:214)थािपत िकए गए ह(cid:167);
(ख) (cid:179)या सरकार का भिव(cid:213)य म(cid:164) और अिधक आयुव(cid:165)िदक िचिक(cid:194)सा महािव(cid:299)ालय (cid:214)थािपत करने
का िवचार है; और
(ग) यिद हां, तो त(cid:194)संबंधी (cid:202)यौरा (cid:179)या है?
आयुष म(cid:253)ं ालय के रा(cid:186) य म(cid:253)ं ी; तथा (cid:214)वा(cid:214)(cid:195)य और प(cid:229)रवार क(cid:208)याण म(cid:253)ं ालय म(cid:164) रा(cid:186)य म(cid:253)ं ी ((cid:174)ी

(क) से (ग): िववरण सभा पटल पर रखा गया है।
िववरण
(क): िपछले तीन वष(cid:334) के दौरान सरकार (cid:300)ारा (cid:214)थािपत आयुव(cid:165)िदक मेिडकल कॉलेज(cid:332) क(cid:236),

राजक(cid:236)य आयवु (cid:165)िदक कॉलेज (cid:214)थािपत नह(cid:233) िकया गया है।

21.03.2025 24
(a) whether Assam tea workers are prone to post-TB fungal infection and
if so, the details thereof; and
(b) the steps taken/proposed to be taken by the Government to help the
workers in this regard?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS
AND FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
(a) & (b) A Statement is laid on the Table of the House.
STATEMENT
(a) and (b): There is no specific information available with the Government
with regard to fungal infection in lungs post TB-treatment in tea garden workers.
However, Government of India has implemented National TB Elimination
Programme (NTEP) under the aegis of the National Health Mission (NHM)
across all the States/UTs including Assam. The key steps taken to address TB
are as under:
 Targeted interventions in high TB burden areas through State and District
Specific Strategic plans.
 Provision of free drugs and diagnostics to TB patients.
 Intensified TB case-finding among vulnerable populations.
 Decentralization of TB screening and treatment services to the Ayushman
Arogya Mandir.
21.03.2025 25
 Private sector engagement with incentives for notification & management
of TB cases.
 Scaling up of molecular diagnostic laboratories to sub-district levels.
 Ni-kshay Poshan Yojana of Rs 1,000 per month per patient as nutritional
support for the entire duration of treatment.
 Intensified Information, Education & Communication (IEC) interventions to
reduce stigma, enhance community awareness and improve health
seeking behaviour.
 Provision of TB Preventive Treatment to contacts of TB patients and
vulnerable population.
 Tracking of notified TB cases through Ni-kshay portal.
 Provision of additional nutritional support to TB patients and household
contacts under Ni-kshay Mitra initiative.
In addition, Assam National Health Mission (NHM) has executed a
Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with Tea Garden Hospitals under Public
Private Partnership (PPP) to provide health care services for all the employees
of tea gardens and its adjoining areas.
Further, the State Government of Assam has taken the following steps for
tea garden workers:
 Free diagnosis and treatment of TB through tea garden health facililities.
 Transformation of 354 tea garden health facilities into Ayushman Arogya
Mandirs under the National Health Mission (NHM), Assam.
21.03.2025 26
 Provide comprehensive primary healthcare to tea garden population
through dedicated Mobile Medical Units (MMUs).
 Awareness Programme on Health through Directorate of Tea Tribes and
Adivasi Welfare.
SHRI BAIJAYANT PANDA: Sir, I thank the hon. minister for the very detailed
response about the steps being taken by the Union Health Ministry on the
scourge of TB.
Sir, for a very long time, the problem with TB has been that a very large
number of cases remained undetected. It has been reported that the TB Mukt
Abhiyan has targeted this, and it has been identifying a lot of cases which can
be treated. The figures reported that in this TB Mukt Abhiyan, five lakh screening
camps have been held; six crore individuals have been screened; and two lakh
new cases have been found. Identifying the cases is a very good first step.
I would like to ask the hon. Minister what steps are being planned. What
is the roadmap ahead for addressing the treatment of these individuals so that
we can really become TB Mukt in Bharat?
SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL: Speaker Sir, we started with the National TB
Elimination Programme and we are moving fast in the direction of creating a TB-
free India by 2025. I would like to begin by quoting some of the achievements of
India in respect of TB elimination, which are being lauded in the WHO Global TB
Report.
Today, in India with the consistent efforts of the Government, the
21.03.2025 27
incidence rate has gone down by 17.7 per cent, TB mortalities have reduced by
21.4 per cent, TB treatment coverage has expanded by 32 per cent between the
year 2015 and 2023 and even the MDR TB cases have gone down by 20 per
cent.
In so far as the detection of the missed cases are concerned, we have
brought down the missing cases from 15 lakhs to 2.5 lakhs and it is the constant
effort of the Government that we continue to find these missing cases for which
active screening is required.
We lately launched the 100 days intensified campaign in 347 high
burdened prioritized districts wherein we went on especially to the vulnerable
groups of population. We carried out active screening, and as soon as the cases
are diagnosed, we try to put them on treatment and also link them to the
nutritional support.
I would like to quote some of the achievements of the 100 days campaign
and how we have progressed in this campaign. This campaign will end after two
days on the 24th March.

SHRI BAIJAYANT PANDA: Sir, this detection of undetected cases, as I said, is
a very big first step, and the hon. Minister has pointed out the steps that are
being planned.
My question is regarding the NTEP – the National Tuberculosis
Elimination Programme.
In relation to this, under the Nikshay Poshan Yojana, which the hon.
Minister has referred to, an amount of Rs.1000 is given. It is a very important
aspect because the people suffering from TB are often unable to work and this
financial assistance is very important.
I would like to ask the hon Minister about the number of patients that are
being assisted by this financial assistance, and whether this is being done
through DBT or through some other method.
SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL: Sir, nutrition is a very big risk factor for TB
patients. We have to give drugs but at the same time, we also have to provide
the right amount of nutrition to the TB patients for which the Government of India
under the NTEP started giving a financial incentive of Rs.500 which has lately
been enhanced to Rs.1000.
I am very happy to state that we have disbursed so far Rs.3,635.29 crore
amount through DBT to 1.24 crore TB patients so far. I would also like to mention
21.03.2025 29
here that in respect of nutrition, there are patients who are severely
malnourished and whose body mass index is less than 18.5. For them, we have
introduced an energy-dense nutrition supplement which is provided in the first
two months of the treatment.
The third important thing is, now we have extended the nutrition food
basket, which was up-till-now given to only TB patients. to the family members,
the close contact of the TB patients also.

DR. SHASHI THAROOR: Thank you very much, hon. Speaker.
Sir, I am afraid that this is a genuine problem. It has even affected our
country’s reputation abroad because spurious drugs have been sold. There were
also reports that the army is receiving goods of substandard quality from many
of our pharmaceutical manufacturers. … (Interruptions)
The real issue is lack of credible central standards to actually inspect
these firms. In my own State of Kerala, 90 per cent of the drugs sold are from
other States. But we have no common central standard to evaluate the quality
of drugs that are coming.
Sir, you would not believe that under the Act of 1940, you have to get the
details about the constitution of the manufacturing and marketing companies in
order to file a formal complaint in court. There is no central database and no
central pool for obtaining these documents. We have to send letters to the Drug
Controller of the State or the Union Territory in which the manufacturer is
located. Then, if there are no answers, an Investigative Officer has to be sent to
Uttarakhand or Himachal or somewhere else to physically pick-up the
21.03.2025 44
documents. In nearly 90 per cent of the times, you do not get the documents.
What can the Central Government do about ensuring central standards
and creating a common database to which State Governments can have access
and thereby creating a central regulatory environment that protects the country’s
reputation from spurious drugs? Thank you very much.

21.03.2025 46
NATIONAL TB ELIMINATION PROGRAMME
*305. SHRIMATI DAGGUBATI PURANDESWARI:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to sta
te:
(a) whether any significant progress has been made under the National TB
Elimination Programme (NTEP) in terms of reducing TB incidence and
mortality rates in the country and if so, the details thereof;
(b) whether the Government has any plans to sustain the decline in TB cases
and if so, the details thereof;
(c) whether specific interventions are being implemented to enhance active TB
case-finding among vulnerable populations in the country and if so, the
details thereof;
(d) the manner in which the integration of various health initiatives such as the
Ayushman Arogya Mandir is expected to improve TB screening and
treatment services; and
(e) the measures taken/proposed to be taken by the Government to ensure that
nutritional support through the Nikshay Poshan Yojana reaches all eligible
TB patients effectively?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS
AND FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
(a) to (e) A Statement is laid on the Table of the House.
21.03.2025 47
STATEMENT
(a) to (e) Government of India has implemented National TB Elimination
Programme (NTEP) under the aegis of the National Health Mission (NHM)
As per the World Health Organisation’s Global TB Report, 2024, the
incidence rate of TB in India has shown a 17.7% decline from 237 per lakh
population in 2015 to 195 per lakh population in 2023, which is more than double
the global reduction, while deaths due to TB has reduced by 21.4% from 28 per
lakh population in 2015 to 22 per lakh population in 2023.
The steps taken by the Government in all State/UTs, to accelerate and
sustain the decline in TB cases are as under.
 Targeted interventions in high TB burden areas through State and
District Specific Strategic plans.
 Provision of free drugs and diagnostics to TB patients.
 Intensified TB case-finding among vulnerable populations.
 Decentralization of TB screening and treatment services to the
Ayushman Arogya Mandir.
 Private sector engagement with incentives for TB case notification &
management of TB cases.
 Scaling up molecular diagnostic laboratories to sub-district levels.
 Ni-Kshay Poshan Yojana of Rs1,000 per month per patient as nutritional
support for the entire duration of treatment.
21.03.2025 48
 Intensified information, Education & Communication (IEC) interventions
to reduce stigma, enhance community awareness and improve health
seeking behavior.
 Convergence of efforts and resources of line ministries for TB
elimination.
 Provision of TB Preventive Treatment to contacts of TB patients and
vulnerable population.
 Tracking of notified TB cases through Ni-Kshay portal.
 Provision of additional nutritional support to TB patients and household
contacts under Ni-kshay Mitra initiative.
The Government has launched a 100-Day TB Elimination Campaign in
identified 347 priority districts across 33 State/UTs, to find missing TB cases,
reduce TB deaths and prevent new cases. As part of Jan Bhagidari, efforts have
been made for a whole of government approach in collaboration with line
ministries. In addition, the private sector, civil society organizations and local
community volunteers have been leveraged to mobilize vulnerable population.
Under NHM, comprehensive primary healthcare is provided through 12
package of services at Ayushman Arogya Mandir. These activities at the
community level has contributed in identification of vulnerable population and
enhance TB screening, diagnosis and treatment services.
21.03.2025 49
The Government introduced Ni-kshay Poshan Yojana (NPY) in April 2018
through direct benefit transfer (DBT) as nutritional support to TB patients. With
effect from 1st November 2024, NPY incentive to TB patients have been
enhanced from Rs 500 to Rs1,000 per month for the entire duration of
treatment. The Government periodically reviews this inititative to ensure that
nutritional support reaches all eligible TB patients.
SHRIMATI DAGGUBATI PURANDESWARI: Sir, in the reply given, it has been
mentioned that the incidences of TB have come down by 17.7 per cent since
2015. This is a remarkable achievement for sure, and I would like to congratulate
the hon. Union Health Minister, Nadda ji, and the hon. Minister of State, Anupriya
Patel ji, actually achieving this success.
Having said that, despite this progress, there are certain high-risk
populations such as the urban slum dwellers, the tribal population, the people
who are exposed to hazardous occupations like working in mines and also the
construction workers who face a disproportionate TB burden in our country.
Sir, the hon. Minister in his reply earlier had mentioned that they have
engaged mobile health vans and also hand-held screening machines to actually
reach out to the people. But we need to understand that the tribal population
lives in very difficult terrains which are far-flung.
So, my question is, how successfully have these mobile vans and the
hand-held TB screening machines reached the tribal areas, and what are the
measures taken to improve awareness among the tribal people because they
go by traditions and they do not believe in the modern technologies and
21.03.2025 50
interventions.
(cid:174)ीमती अनुि(cid:255)या पटेल : अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) महोदय, म(cid:167)ने अभी पूव(cid:170) के (cid:255)(cid:307) म(cid:164) बह(cid:242)त िव(cid:214)तार से ह(cid:369)ं ेड डेज
इंट(cid:164)िसफाई कै (cid:204)पेन के बारे म(cid:164) बताया था । Under this campaign, our special focus was on

SHRIMATI DAGGUBATI PURANDESWARI: Sir, we have made great strides
in TB control. However, Multi-Drug Resistant TB is still a challenge for us in our
country. So, my question is, what are the strategies that the Government is
implementing to ensure better treatment adherence. Largely, these people who
are undergoing treatment tend to discontinue it because of the long period of
treatment and also the logistic problems they face. What are the measures that
21.03.2025 51
the hon. Minister is undertaking to ensure that there is better treatment
adherence? Also, would this include digital treatment monitoring and
community-based interventions as well?
SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL: Sir, for MDR TB patients, we earlier had a
treatment regimen which was for a longer duration of nine to eleven months. But
recently, we have come up with an even more short duration regimen, which is
only for six months. So, it is shorter, it is safer, and it is efficacious. It is called
BPaLM; the hon. Union Minister has already explained it. We are maintaining a
good amount of drug stocks. We are also tracking and monitoring so that drugs
in sufficient quantity are always available. We have also enhanced the number
of the MDR TB treatment centres in the country from 126 to 826.

THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS
AND FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
(a) to (d): A Statement is laid on the Table of the House.
STATEMENT
(a) to (d): The Kilkari program is a mobile based service launched on
15thJanuary 2016 as a part of its Digital India initiative, for new and expectant
mothers aimed at encouraging them to make healthier choices for their new-
born care by delivering messages about pregnancy, childbirth and childcare
directly to the beneficiaries.
21.03.2025 53
It is an audio based service and hence overcomes the literacy challenges
of rural India. Kilkari delivers free, weekly, time appropriate audio messages
about pregnancy, child birth and child care via Interactive Voice Response (IVR)
to women registered in RCH portal. Messaging begins in the second trimester
of pregnancy and continues until the child is one-year-old. The pregnant mother
data is fetched from RCH portal to Kilkari through web service which has been
implemented between both the applications.
The program informs mothers and families about behaviours and
practices to be adopted during pregnancy and infancy. The weekly messages
help families to educate, remind and also reinforce the prioritized actions for
each week during this crucial period. This action not only saves lives of pregnant
women and children from several risks but also ensures a healthy outcome.
The Kilkari project is currently operational in 27 States/UTs namely
Andhra Pradesh, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Assam, Bihar, Chhattisgarh,
Chandigarh, Delhi, Haryana, Himachal Pradesh, Jammu & Kashmir, Jharkhand,
Madhya Pradesh, Odisha, Rajasthan, Tripura, Uttar Pradesh, West Bengal,
Uttarakhand, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Goa, Dadra & Nagar Haveli and
Daman & Diu, Arunachal Pradesh, Manipur, Mizoram and Nagaland. Currently,
Kilkari program is running in 13 languages viz. Assamese, Bengali, Bhojpuri,
Gujarati, Hindi, Kannada, Konkani, Marathi, Meitei, Mizo, Odia, Telugu and
English.
The number of successful calls achieved under Kilkari project during last
few years is given below:
21.03.2025 54
Successful calls achieved under Kilkari
S. No. Total Number of Successful
Year
Calls
1 2017-18 6,44,29,759
2 2018-19 8,34,19,787
3 2019-20 8,00,04,848
4 2020-21 7,32,10,598
5 2021-22 4,75,45,063
6 2022-23 7,71,16,051
7 2023-24 9,80,83,574
8 2024-25 (Till Feb'25) 16,22,94,406
In Maharashtra State, a total of 1,77,65,144 successful calls were
achieved in FY 2024-25 (upto February 2025) and 7,54,094 successful calls
were achieved in the district of Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar of Maharashtra.
Similarly, a total of 59,414successful calls were achieved in FY 2024-25 (upto
February 2025) in the Union Territory of Dadar & Nagar Haveli and Daman &
Diu (DNH & DD) and 35,097successful calls were achieved in the district of
Dadra & Nagar Haveli.
The calls have been helpful to mothers and families in all states due to
its uniqueness in nature. The information provided through audio messages are
helpful in terms of ante and postnatal care as well as nutrition. The messages
were especially tailored according to the gestational period of each beneficiary
which gave them a sense of acknowledgement. The messages were helpful in
giving timely information to beneficiaries about the appropriate healthcare
services to be sought. Kilkari complemented ASHAs work of mobilising
21.03.2025 55
beneficiaries to access health services and reduced the burden on them.
Continued Kilkari support to families has led to changes in behaviours and has
also improved service utilisation. It has also proved useful during the pandemic
when it was difficult to hold physical visits and awareness campaigns to
encourage healthcare seeking behaviour.

है।

EXPENDITURE INCURRED UNDER AB-PMJAY
*309. SHRI CHARANJIT SINGH CHANNI:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
21.03.2025 75
(a) whether the Government has information on expenditure incurred in various
States under Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-
PMJAY) and if so, the details thereof, State-wise;
(b) whether the Government has any plan to increase the insurance coverage
under the said scheme in future and if so, the details thereof;
(c) whether the Government has any plan to empanel all private hospitals under
the said scheme in future and if so, the details thereof; and
(d) if not, the reasons therefor?
THE MINISTER OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF
CHEMICALS AND FERTILIZERS (SHRI JAGAT PRAKASH NADDA):
(a) As on 01.03.2025, Ayushman Bharat - Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana
(AB-PMJAY) has facilitated approximately 8.99 crore hospital admissions
amounting to an expenditure of ₹1.26 lakh crore. The State/UT-wise
details of number of hospital admissions and expenditure incurred are at
Statement.
AB-PMJAY currently provides free healthcare access up to Rs. 5 lakh per
eligible beneficiary family per year for secondary and tertiary care
hospitalization in 27 different medical specialties corresponding to 1961
procedures. Regular updation such as inclusion of new procedures,
empanelment of new hospitals, inclusion of new beneficiaries and other
improvements are done in the scheme as per new requirements from time
to time.
21.03.2025 76
Under AB-PMJAY, a network of over 30,957 hospitals have been
empanelled for providing healthcare services to the scheme beneficiaries.
This includes 13,866 private hospitals.
As public health is a State subject, the responsibility of empaneling
hospitals under AB-PMJAY lies with the respective State/UT. Also,
hospital empanelment is a continuous process and is undertaken based
on the requirements and availability of healthcare providers (meeting AB-
PMJAY criteria) within a given State / UT. The Government of India
encourages States/UTs to empanel more hospitals under the scheme to
ensure access to quality healthcare to all eligible beneficiaries.

ऑग(cid:165)िनक उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332) हेत ु गणु व(cid:176)ा िनय(cid:253)ं ण (cid:255)योगशालाओ ंक(cid:236) (cid:214)थापना/स(cid:320)ु ढ़ीकरण, सू(cid:224)म पोषकत(cid:194)व(cid:332)

िसफा(cid:229)रश करता है। इस (cid:214) क(cid:236)म के तहत उपलि(cid:202)ध िन(cid:204)नानुसार है:

IMPORTANCE OF AYUSH SYSTEMS OF TREATMENT
*311. SUSHRI MAHUA MOITRA:
Will the Minister of AYUSH be pleased to state:
21.03.2025 81
(a) whether the Government has propagated the importance of Ayurveda, Yoga
and Naturopathy systems of treatment among people to lead a healthy and
long life in recent years and if so, the details thereof;
(b) the details of the total number of private day care centres allowed in CGHS
by the Government in cities like Kolkata, Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru etc.;
(c) whether the Government has recently restricted these day care centers in
CGHS in the above said cities; and
(d) if so, the details thereof along with the reasons therefor?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER
OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) The Government through its various initiatives has been able to propagate
the importance of Ayush system of medicine including Ayurveda, Yoga and
Naturopathy system, among people in the country. With the view to fulfil the
mandate of propagation of Ayush system of Healthcare, the Ministry of Ayush
implements a Central Sector Scheme for Promotion of Information Education
and Communication (IEC) in Ayush. This aims to reach out to all sections of the
population across the country. Under this Scheme, Ministry organises National/
State level Arogya Fairs, Yoga Fests/ Utsavs, Ayurveda Parvs, celebrate
important days of Ayush system, participate in Health fairs/ melas, Exhibitions
etc, provide financial assistance for organising seminars, workshops,
conferences and conduct multimedia campaigns etc. to create awareness
among citizens regarding Ayush system of health care.
21.03.2025 82
Also, the Ministry is implementing the Centrally Sponsored Scheme
namely National Ayush Mission (NAM) through State/UT Governments for
development and promotion of different Ayush system in the country and
providing financial assistance to them as per the proposal received in their State
Annual Action Plans (SAAPs). State/UT Governments may avail financial
assistance by submitting proposals through State Annual Action Plans (SAAPs)
as per NAM guidelines.
(b) Currently, the empanelment of private Ayush Day Care Centres is limited to
Delhi/NCR. As on date, a total of 54 Private Ayush Day Care Centres are
empanelled under CGHS Delhi/NCR.
(c) and (d) These Day Care Centres have not been restricted for providing
treatment to CGHS beneficiaries.

भाप उ(cid:194)पादन और आपूित(cid:170), उपचार भंडारण और िनपटान सुिवधाए,ं सो(cid:208)व(cid:164)ट (cid:229)रकवरी (cid:255)णाली,
क(cid:184)चे पानी क(cid:236) आपूित(cid:170), उ(cid:194)कृ(cid:310)ता क(cid:164) (cid:254) आिद शािमल ह(cid:167)।
उव(cid:170)रक िवभाग (cid:300)ारा (cid:255)दान क(cid:236) गई जानकारी के अनुसार, दूरदराज के (cid:177)े(cid:253)(cid:332) सिहत देश म(cid:164) उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332) क(cid:236)

(iv) कृिष एवं िकसान क(cid:208)याण िवभाग (डीएएडं एफड(cid:202)(cid:208)यू) तथा उव(cid:170)रक िवभाग (cid:300)ारा रा(cid:186)य कृिष िवभाग

जाती है।

CASES ELIGIBLE FOR LOK ADALATS
*313.SHRI G SELVAM:
Will the Minister of LAW AND JUSTICE be pleased to state:
(a) the number of Lok Adalats held in the State of Tamil Nadu during the last
five years and the total number of cases disposed of during this period;
(b) the number of pending cases eligible for resolution through Lok Adalats in
various States across the country;
(c) whether Lok Adalats have been effective in reducing the burden on regular
courts and if so, the details thereof and if not, the reasons therefor;
(d) whether the Government has evaluated the effectiveness of Lok Adalats in
reducing case pendency and if so, the key findings of such evaluation;
(e) whether the Government plans to expand the jurisdiction of Lok Adalats to
handle more categories of cases, if so, the details thereof and if not, the
reasons therefor; and
(f) whether the Government has proposed any reforms to improve the efficiency
and credibility of Lok Adalats, if so, the details thereof and if not, the reasons
therefor?
21.03.2025 87
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF LAW AND JUSTICE; AND
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF PARLIAMENTARY AFFAIRS
(SHRI ARJUN RAM MEGHWAL):
(a) to (c) Lok Adalat is organised across the country as per the provisions of the
Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987 read with National Legal Services Authority
(Lok Adalats) Regulations, 2009 for the subject matters as prescribed under the
said Act and Regulations in the Courts as defined under Section 2 (1) (aaa) of
the said Act. In Lok Adalat, the disputes/ cases pending in the court of law or at
pre-litigation stage are settled amicably. The Lok Adalat has been given
statutory status under the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987. The award
made by Lok Adalat is deemed to be a decree of a civil court and is final and
binding on all parties and no appeal lies against the Award before any court.
The State/UT-wise (including Tamil Nadu)and year-wise details of number of
Lok Adalats organized, cases received and cases disposed of by National Lok
Adalats, State Lok Adalats and Permanent Lok Adalats (Public Utility Services)
during the last five years are at Statement-I, Statement-II and Statement-III
respectively.
Lok Adalats reduce case load of courts by resolvingsubstantive number
of cases from time to time.The details of the number of cases settled by the Lok
Adalats during the last two years and current year (upto December) are as
follows:
(i) National Lok Adalat
21.03.2025 88
Year Pre-litigative cases Pendingcases Total cases
settled settled settled

2024-25 1,61,277
(d) Lok Adalats are organised under the aegis of National Legal Services
Authority (NALSA) and NALSA constantly monitor theworking of Lok
Adalats.Further, the State Legal Services Authorities (SLSAs) have been issued
guidelines/ directions by NALSA vide National Legal Services Authority (Lok
Adalat) Regulations, 2009 to organise more Lok Adalats so that pendency of
cases could be reduced. Presently, there is no proposal toevaluate
21.03.2025 89
theeffectiveness of Lok Adalats in reducing case pendency.However, in order to
monitor the performance of the legal services authorities, NALSA receives
monthly activity reports from all the SLSAs highlighting all the activities carried
out in a particular month. Thereafter, a final activity report on monthly basis is
sent by NALSA to the Government which is reviewed and compiled. Apart from
monthly activity reports, NALSA also receives Annual Reports from all the
SLSAs and prepares its own Annual Report, which is laid before both Houses of
the Parliament.Further, All India and Regional Meets are also organized by
NALSA to monitor the performance of Legal Services Authorities.
(e) Regulation 10 of the National Legal Services Authority (Lok Adalats)
Regulations, 2009provides for “Reference of cases and matters” to the Lok
Adalats. Presently, there is no proposal to amend these regulations.
(f) To improve the efficiency and accessibility of Lok Adalats, E-Lok Adalat was
conceptualized during Covid which significantly improved access to justice for
people who were otherwise unable to participate in the Lok Adalats. The first E-
Lok Adalat was held on 27.06.2020 and since then E-Lok Adalats have been
organized in 28 States / UTs, wherein 10.30 crore cases were taken up out of
which 1.15 crore cases were disposed of.

आय(cid:213)ु मान भारत - (cid:214)वा(cid:214)(cid:195)य और आरो(cid:181)य क(cid:164) (cid:254) (एबी-एचड(cid:202)(cid:208)यूसी) (िजस े वत(cid:170)मान म(cid:164) आय(cid:213)ु मान

िदनांक 25 नवंबर, 2023 के प(cid:253)(cid:332) के मा(cid:197)यम स े आय(cid:213)ु मान भारत - (cid:214)वा(cid:214)(cid:195)य और आरो(cid:181)य क(cid:164) (cid:254) (एबी-

पि(cid:306)म बंगाल सरकार न े (cid:214)वा(cid:214)(cid:195)य और प(cid:229)रवार क(cid:208)याण म(cid:253)ं ालय, भारत सरकार और रा(cid:186)य के बीच
ह(cid:214)ता(cid:177)(cid:229)रत समझौता (cid:178)ापन के खंड 10.3 और 10.10 के (cid:255)ावधान(cid:332) का अनपु ालन नह(cid:233) िकया है।

आरो(cid:181) य क(cid:164) (cid:254)" का नाम बदलकर आय(cid:213)ु मान आरो(cid:181) य क(cid:164) (cid:254) िकए जाने के िनण(cid:170)य क(cid:236) सूचना दी है। इस पर
सहमित दी गई है।

िन(cid:213)पादन को बढ़ावा देना और उपलि(cid:202)धय(cid:332) को परु (cid:214)कृत करना आिद शािमल ह(cid:167)।

के िलए (cid:255)ित वष(cid:170) सामा(cid:198)य समी(cid:177)ा िमशन (सीआरएम) आयोिजत िकए जाते ह(cid:167)। वष(cid:170) 2024 म(cid:164) 16व (cid:164)
सीआरएम न े पि(cid:306)म बंगाल के मालदा और 24 उ(cid:176)री परगना िजल(cid:332) का दौरा िकया, िजसम(cid:164) सिु वधा
क(cid:164) (cid:254)(cid:332) का दौरा, लाभािथ(cid:170)य(cid:332) स े बातचीत, (cid:214)वा(cid:214)(cid:195)य प(cid:229)रचया(cid:170) टीम के साथ चचा(cid:170) सिहत साव(cid:170)जिनक

गई ह ै या श(cid:322)ु िकए जाने का िवचार ह ै और यिद हां, तो त(cid:194)संबंधी (cid:202)यौरा (cid:179)या ह;ै

(घ) उ(cid:265) पहल/अिभयान कब तक पूरा होने क(cid:236) संभावना ह?ै
(cid:214) वा(cid:214) (cid:195) य और प(cid:229)रवार क(cid:208) याण म(cid:253)ं ी; तथा रसायन और उव(cid:170)रक म(cid:253)ं ी ((cid:174)ी जगत (cid:255)काश नड्डा):
(क) स े (घ) भारत सरकार ‘सभी के िलए (cid:214)वा(cid:214)(cid:195)य सवे ा’ के ल(cid:224)य को (cid:255)ा(cid:302) करन े के िलए (cid:255)ितब(cid:294) है।

21.03.2025 105
CAPACITY EXPANSION AT KANDLA PORT
*316. DR. SANJAY JAISWAL:
SHRI DEVUSINH CHAUHAN:
Will the Minister of PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS be pleased
to state:
(a) whether any capacity increase is being targeted at Kandla Port with the
investment of Rs. 57000 crore;
(b) if so, the details thereof and the targets set therefor;
(c) whether there is any plan to integrate Kandla Port’s enhanced capacity with
global supply chains and emerging trade corridors, such as the International
North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC); and
(d) if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS (SHRI
SARBANANDA SONOWAL):
(a) and (b) Yes. The capacity increase is being targeted at Kandla, Deendayal
Port Authority with investment of Rs.57000 Cr. (Approx.). The detail of the same
is as below:
i) Development of new Smart Port at Kandla
A New Smart Port using of around 6 km waterfront has been planned to cater
the trade requirements to handle Multipurpose and Container cargo at a capacity
addition of 135 MMTPA (Approx.) for tentative cost of Rs.27,000 Cr.
21.03.2025 106
ii) Mega Shipbuilding Marine Cluster at Kandla
In line with the Ministry’s vision, Deendayal Port with its extensive coastline and
huge land parcel is planning to develop a Mega Shipbuilding Cluster, with project
components that include Shipbuilding Yard, Marina, Fishing Harbour and
Township at Kandla for tentative cost of Rs. 30,000 Cr.
(c) and (d) Yes, India is a part of International North – South Transport Corridor
(INSTC) and India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEEC). DPA plays
a vital role in same.
ि(cid:257)(cid:179)स को टै(cid:229)रफ का खतरा
317. (cid:174)ी राम(cid:255)ीत मडं लः

21.03.2025 109
AYUSHMAN VAY VANDANA CARDS
*318. SHRI DHAVAL LAXMANBHAI PATEL:
SHRI MAHESH KASHYAP:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE to be pleased to
state:
(a) whether the Government has any plans to expand the scope of the
Ayushman Bharat - Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY) to
include additional healthcare benefits or services in the future;
(b) if so, the details thereof; and
(c) the measures put in place to ensure that the benefits of Ayushman Vay
Vandana Cards reach the most vulnerable sections of the population
particularly in rural and underserved areas of Valsad, Dang and Navsari
Parliamentary Constituencies of Gujarat?
THE MINISTER OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF
CHEMICALS AND FERTILIZERS (SHRI JAGAT PRAKASH NADDA):
(a) to (c) Ayushman Bharat - Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY)
is a flagship scheme of the Government which provides health cover of Rs. 5
lakhs per family per year for secondary and tertiary care hospitalization to
approximately 55 crore beneficiaries corresponding to 12.37 crore families
constituting economically vulnerable bottom 40% of India’s population. Recently,
the scheme has been expanded to cover 6 crore senior citizens of age 70 years
and above belonging to 4.5 crore families irrespective of their socio-economic
status through Ayushman Vay Vandana cards.
21.03.2025 110
Under AB-PMJAY, the health benefit packages have been revised five
times since launch of the scheme. Initially, the scheme included 1,359 packages,
but the Health Benefit Package (HBP) 2022 now comprises 1,961 packages
across 27 medical specialties. A total of 365 new treatment procedures have
been added and the rates for 832 packages have been increased. High-end
procedures such as bone marrow transplants and cochlear implant surgeries
have been introduced in addition to the already covered kidney transplants.
Further, States have been provided flexibility to further customize the Health
Benefit Packages to local context.
There is a comprehensive media and outreach strategy to spread
awareness about AB-PMJAY especially in rural and underserved areas. This
includes advertisement over traditional media platforms including newspapers,
community radio, street plays, digital displays, radio campaigns, mass
messaging and telecast of beneficiary testimonials via Doordarshan etc. In
addition to this, State Health Agencies have also engaged the wide network of
frontline workers i.e., ASHAs, AWWs and VLEs (Village Level Entrepreneurs)
who are pivotal in creating mass awareness at the grassroot level. In addition to
this, the Ayushman Vay Vandana card beneficiaries may give a missed call to
helpline number 1800-110-770 or call to 24x7 call centre (14555) for any
assistance/queries.
Further, the government has issued directions to the States to undertake
extensive IEC activities to raise the awareness about the expansion of AB-
PMJAY for all senior citizens of the age 70 years and above.
21.03.2025 111
As on 17.03.2025, more than 53 lakh Ayushman Vay Vandana
beneficiaries have been enrolled under the scheme, including 19,665 in Valsad
district, 612 in Dang district, and 7,146 in Navsari district of Gujarat.
RECOGNITION OF AYURVEDA PRACTITIONERS AND RESEARCHERS
*319. SHRI RAVINDRA SHUKLA ALIAS RAVI KISHAN:
SHRI NABA CHARAN MAJHI:
Will the Minister of AYUSH be pleased to state:
(a) the steps taken by the Government to expand the reach of Ayurveda and
confer recognition with award to encourage emerging Ayurveda
practitioners and researchers, State/UT-wise particularly in Jalgaon
Parliamentary constituency of Maharashtra;
(b) the corrective measures being taken to enhance the infrastructure for
Ayurveda research, education and clinical practice in rural and urban
areas of the country, State/UT-wise particularly in Jalgaon Parliamentary
constituency of Maharashtra;
(c) whether the Government plans to introduce a mentorship programme
involving the awardees to guide young Ayurveda practitioners and
researchers with a special focus on Maharashtra particularly in Jalgaon
Parliamentary constituency and if so, the details thereof;
(d) whether the Government has any special plans to promote Ayurveda
research and healthcare infrastructure in Haryana, specifically in the
21.03.2025 112
Bhiwani-Mahendergarh Parliamentary constituency and if so, the details
thereof; and
(e) the details of initiatives being taken to promote Ayurveda among the youth
and encourage research and innovation in Maharashtra especially in
Jalgaon Parliamentary constituency?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER
OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) and (b) Ayurveda is a recognized system of medicine under the Ministry of
Ayush and various initiatives are undertaken to encourage emerging Ayurveda
practitioners and researchers to promote its education, research and practice.
National Dhanwantari Ayurveda Award, instituted by the Ministry of
Ayush, recognizes individuals who have made exceptional contributions to
Ayurveda in research, practice, and propagation, and is presented annually
during Ayurveda Day celebrations on Dhanvantari Jayanti (Dhanteras). The
education and practice of Ayurveda are regulated under the provisions of the
National Commission for Indian System of Medicine Act, 2020. Currently, there
are 562 Ayurveda colleges in the country, including 132 colleges in Maharashtra.
The Government has set up an apex Central Research Council namely, the
Central Council for Research in Ayurvedic Sciences (CCRAS), which operates
30 institutes/centres across the country.
21.03.2025 113
Further, public health is a State subject, accordingly, the responsibility of
enhancing Ayurveda infrastructure in rural and urban areas, including in the
Jalgaon Parliamentary Constituency, primarily lies with the respective State/UT
Governments. However, under the Centrally Sponsored Scheme of the National
Ayush Mission (NAM), the Government provides financial assistance for setting
up Ayush educational institutions in States where the availability of such
institutions is inadequate, particularly in the Government sector. Accordingly,
State/UT Governments can avail themselves for this financial assistance by
submitting suitable proposals through their State Annual Action Plans (SAAPs),
as per the provisions outlined in the NAM guidelines.
(c) The Ministry of Ayush is implementing the AYURGYAN Scheme to enhance
and develop capacity in the Ayush healthcare sector at the country level. It seeks
to improve health practices through sustainable Ayush methods, encourage
professionals to undergo professional orientation, update knowledge of teachers
and doctors, and promote the use of information technology for disseminating
Ayush developments. The scheme also focuses on encouraging research and
development in priority areas to validate claims and enhance the acceptability
of Ayush approaches and drugs in the global market. This scheme addresses
the diverse needs of Ayush practitioners, educators, researchers, and
entrepreneurs, developing a comprehensive approach to elevate the Ayush
systems through education, research, and innovation.
21.03.2025 114
(d ) and (e) The Ministry of Ayush has a mandate for coordination and promotion
of research and development including assistance therefor in Ayurveda systems
of Health Care. The Ministry of Ayush is implementing a Central Sector Scheme
for Promotion of Information, Education and Communication (IEC) in Ayush. The
Scheme is meant for creation of awareness amongst the citizens about the
efficacy of the Ayush Systems, their cost-effectiveness and the availability of
herbs used for prevention and treatment of common ailments at their door steps
through various channels including the production of audio-visual educational
material to achieve the objective of health for all, dissemination of proven results
of Research and Development work conducted in Ayush Systems at National/
State level through fair and providing a forum where horizontal and vertical
interaction among stakeholders of Ayush Systems can take place through
conferences, seminars and fairs/melas at State and National levels and
encourage stakeholders to participate in them.
The Central Council for Research in Ayurvedic Sciences (CCRAS) an
autonomous body under Ministry of Ayush works towards the scientific
advancement of Ayurveda through research, clinical care, and public outreach.
In Maharashtra, CCRAS operates through its peripheral institutes, namely the
Central Ayurveda Research Institute (CARI), Mumbai, Regional Ayurveda
Research Institute (RARI), Nagpur and Regional Ayurveda Research Institute
(RARI), Pune. These institutes actively engage in clinical services and
21.03.2025 115
Information, Education and Communication (IEC) initiatives to enhance
awareness about Ayurveda.
To promote Ayurveda among the youth and encourage research and innovation,
CCRAS has undertaken several initiatives. The Council disseminates Ayurvedic
knowledge through electronic and print media in English, Hindi, and regional
languages. It actively participates in National/State-level Arogya Melas, health
camps, exhibitions and outreach programs such as the Scheduled Caste Sub-
Plan (SCSP) Research Program and the Tribal Health Care Research Program
(THCRP). Additionally, the CCRAS website serves as a repository of IEC
materials and is hyperlinked to other relevant platforms for wider accessibility.
All India Institute of Ayurveda (AIIA), New Delhi an autonomous body under the
Ministry of Ayush has set up an incubation centre i.e All India Institute of
Ayurveda -Incubation Centre for Ayurveda Innovation and Entrepreneurship
(AIIA-iCAINE ) to support Ayush-related Start-ups and Entrepreneurs. AIIA-
iCAINE has provided mentorship and various clinical facilities to the start-ups at
AIIA, New Delhi.
The Ayurveda Startup Grand Challenge is designed to promote innovation and
entrepreneurship in the Ayush sector. The key stakeholders involved are the
Ministry of Ayush, AIIA, Start Up India and Invest India. Grants and financial
assistance are provided to startups that present innovative solutions and
products in the Ayush sector. Startups get opportunities to connect with potential
investors, industry leaders, and other entrepreneurs through various events and
21.03.2025 116
workshops. Winners and promising startups receive recognition which can
enhance their credibility and market visibility. Fiscal incentives in the form of
cash prizes are provided to the first three toppers.
To nurture research and to motivate Ayurveda students to pursue research as a
career, inculcate research aptitude, and establish a foundation for high-quality
research in Ayurveda, CCRAS is implementing the following programs:
i. Studentship Programme for Ayurveda Research Ken (SPARK):
Financial support to Ayurveda undergraduates to promote interest and
aptitude for research.
ii. Ayush Ph.D. Fellowship: Fellowship support for research scholars.
iii. Post-Doctoral Fellowship (PDF): Support for advanced Ayurveda
research.
iv. SMART Programme: Facilitating integrative research between
Ayurveda academia and tertiary care hospitals.
v. CCRAS-AGNI (2023-24): Promoting evidence-based validation of
Ayurveda practices in five categories, including treatment,
formulations, and instruments.

TB ELIMINATION CAMPAIGN IN JAILS
3452: SHRIMATI D K ARUNA:
SHRI EATALA RAJENDER:
SHRI CHAMALA KIRAN KUMAR REDDY:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) whether his Ministry has asked all the States and the Union Territories
(UTs) to organise a 100-day intensified campaign on tuberculosis (TB)
elimination in prisons and correctional institutions as part of the Union
Government’s commitment for eliminating the disease from India and if so,
the details thereof;
(b) whether his Ministry has asked the States and UTs to organise screening
camps (Nikshay Shivir) in all prisons, covering all prison inmates in
21.03.2025 123
consultation with the State health department, State TB officers and district
TB officers and if so, the details thereof; and
(c) whether his Ministry agree that inadequate screening and lack of
awareness are among the key challenges in controlling TB inside prisons and
if so, the details thereof alongwith the changes made/implemented so far in
Madhya Pradesh and Telangana with the details of funds sanction/utilized
thereon?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS
AND FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
(d) to (c) The Government in consultation with the State Governments has
launched a 100-Day TB Elimination Campaign in identified 347 priority
districts across 33 State/UTs, to find missing TB cases, reduce TB deaths and
prevent new cases. The campaign focuses on vulnerable population in the
community which includes congregate settings like prisons. The campaign
was implemented in 9 districts of Telangana and 23 districts of Madhya
Pradesh.
Further, special awareness activities were taken up by the State Governments
and through Jan Bhagidari to improve health seeking behaviour of vulnerable
population. The details of prison inmates screened for TB, as well as funds
utilized for the campaign in Telangana and Madhya Pradesh, as informed by
the respective States are as under:
21.03.2025 124
Telangana Madhya Pradesh
No. of prison inmates 7,341 21,259
screened (in numbers)
Funds Utilized for campaign 4.53 2.31
related activities (in Rs. Cr)

SHORTAGE OF SHIPPING CONTAINERS
3454. DR. KALANIDHI VEERASWAMY:
Will the Minister of PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS be pleased
to state:
(a) the specific measures being implemented to address the rising freight costs
and shipping delays that exporters are currently facing across the country
including Tamil Nadu;
(b) the steps taken/proposed to be taken by the Government to alleviate the
shortage and non-availability of shipping containers, which has significantly
impacted trade;
(c) the manner in which the Government is addressing congestion at major ports
and the plans/mechanism put in place to improve port infrastructure and
logistics to facilitate smoother operations;
(d) whether any support is being offered to exporters who are struggling with
increased costs and delays, if so, the details thereof;
21.03.2025 133
(e) the manner in which the Government ensures that these challenges do not
adversely affect their competitiveness in global markets;
(f) whether there is a coordinated effort between various ministries and
agencies to tackle these shipping and logistics issues, if so, the details
thereof; and
(g) the manner in which the effectiveness of these efforts are being monitored?
THE MINISTER OF PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS (SHRI
SARBANANDA SONOWAL):
(a) to (g) The freight costs for Cargo operations through the non-major ports
under the Tamil Nadu Maritime Board (TNMB) are very competitive to market
standards. Further, the non-major ports under the TNMB do not face significant
challenges related to shipping container shortages or unavailability.
Various steps have been taken at major ports to streamline logistics such
as the construction of new berths, terminals and parking plazas, Mechanization/
Modernization/ Optimization of existing berths and terminals, streamlining of
processes through digitalization, expansion of hinterland connectivity through
rail and road, etc.
PALNA SCHEME
3455. SHRI ANURAG SHARMA:
SHRI TRIVENDRA SINGH RAWAT:
SHRI YOGENDER CHANDOLIA:
SHRIMATI HIMADRI SINGH:
21.03.2025 134
SHRI DULU MAHATO:
SHRI VISHWESHWAR HEGDE KAGERI:
SHRI TEJASVI SURYA:
SHRI CHINTAMANI MAHARAJ:
SHRIMATI KAMALJEET SEHRAWAT:
SUSHRI KANGNA RANAUT:
SHRI MUKESHKUMAR CHANDRAKAANT DALAL:
Will the Minister of WOMEN AND CHILD DEVELOPMENT be pleased
to state:
(a) the details of the measures being taken to ensure stricter enforcement of
Internal Complaints Committees (ICCs) in all eligible companies; and
(b) the details of the impact of Palna scheme since its launch in 2022 along with
the manner in which children have been benefited so far?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF WOMEN AND CHILD
DEVELOPMENT (SHRIMATI SAVITRI THAKUR):
(a) The ‘Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition,
and Redressal) Act’ (SH Act), 2013 provides for constitution of Internal
Committee (IC) where the number of employees is 10 or more and also
mandates for constitution of Local Committee (LC) by the District Officer notified
under the Act to deal with the cases of workplaces where the number of
employees is less than 10 or when the complaint is against the employer itself.
21.03.2025 135
The Government of India has taken numerous steps for effective
implementation of the SH Act, 2013. The Ministry of Women and Child
Development recently launched the SHe-Box portal duly encompassing various
provisions of ‘the SH Act, 2013. This portal is an initiative of the Ministry to
provide a publicly available centralised repository of information related to
Internal Committees (ICs) and Local Committees (LCs) formed across the
country, whether in government or private sector. It also provides a common
platform to file complaints and track the status of such complaints. The portal
includes a feature where complaints registered on it will be automatically
forwarded to the IC/ LC of the workplaces concerned within the Central
Ministries/ Departments, States/ UTs and in Private sector. The portal provides
for designating a nodal officer for every workplace who is required to ensure
updation of data/ information on a regular basis for real time monitoring of
complaints.
For effective implementation of the Act, Ministry endeavors to include IC details
of all workplaces including all sectors across the country whether, Central or
State Government departments, Private sector entities or Professional Bodies
including all educational/ medical colleges, hospitals etc. on the She-box portal
to facilitate the lodging of complaint of aggrieved woman. In addition to SHe-
Box portal, following measures have been taken by Government towards the
prevention of sexual harassment since the enactment of the said Act.
(i) Ministry of Women and Child Development being the Nodal Ministry for SH
Act observes the Enactment Day i.e. 9th December of the SH Act every year
21.03.2025 136
and issues letters/ advisories to all sectors (including Central Government/
State Government/ Private Bodies/ Trade organization/ Educational
Institutions/ and other organizations) for effective implementation of the Act
and also to constitute IC/ LC immediately.
(ii) The Ministry has also issued a Handbook on SH Act, 2013. The Handbook
provides information about the Act in an easy-to-use practical manner. The
soft copy of the Handbook has been uploaded on the Ministry’s website for
the purposes of mass dissemination and it has also been sent to Ministers
and officials concerned of various Ministries/ Departments, State
Governments and Union Territory Administrations.
(iii) The Ministry has prepared a training module in collaboration with Institute of
Secretariat Training and Management (ISTM) for training and Gender
Sensitization programmes of personnel. Ministry also launched a ‘Guide on
Gender-Inclusive Communication on 28th November 2023 to help removing
gender stereotypes.
(iv) The Ministry also offers regular training sessions to key stakeholders,
including officers from Central and State/UT Governments, through a hybrid
mode. These sessions are conducted with the support of its autonomous
bodies, such as the National Commission for Women (NCW) and the
National Institute of Public Cooperation and Child Development (NIPCCD).
(v) The Department of Personnel and Training, Government of India has also
issued advisories time to time to all the Central Ministries/ Departments to
complete the inquiry in a time bound manner and to include the information
21.03.2025 137
related to number of cases filed/ disposed under the SH Act in their annual
report.
(vi) MWCD has also requested Ministry of Corporate Affairs vide letter dated 26th
June, 2015 to make constitution of the Internal Committee (IC) a mandatory
disclosure under the Companies Act, 2013. The Ministry of Corporate Affairs
vide its order dated 31st July 2018 has accepted the request by amending
the Company (Account) Rules, 2014 thereby making the disclosure
regarding the implementation of SH Act mandatory within Director’s Report.
(vii) To enforce stricter enforcement of Internal Complaints Committees (ICCs) in
all eligible companies, amendment has been made in the Companies
(Accounts) Rules, 2014, vide notification dated 31/07/2018 which provides
that, every company in its Board Report, to be attached with the Financial
Statement filed annually, has to include a statement that the company has
complied with provisions relating to the constitution of Internal Complaints
Committee under the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace
(Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 [14 of 2013].
(b) The Government of India, Ministry of Women and Child Development
introduced Palna sub-scheme, a Centrally Sponsored Scheme under
Samarthya component of Umbrella Mission Shakti w.e.f. 1st April, 2022 for
participation of States/UTs to provide daycare facilities through Anganwadi-
cum-Creches (AWCCs) to children (06 months – 06 years) of mothers
irrespective of their employment status.
21.03.2025 138
Crèche Workers Under supervision of Anganwadi worker do proper care
and safety of children attending the Creche:
i. Provide proper arrangements for sleep and rest of children.
ii. Ensure personal hygiene and Creche helper keep personal hygiene of every
child and keep washrooms clean, help for toilet training.
iii. Inculcate proper toilet habits and toilet training.
iv. Facilitate regular health check-ups and referrals in liaison with
Asha/AWWs/Primary Health Centre (PHC)/AWC
v. Ensure that the food is hygienically and age appropriately cooked,
stored/preserved and fed to the children at appropriate intervals.
vi. Follow all laid out safety procedures for taking/handing over the child and
w.r.t. quality of toys and other materials given to the children.
During the 15th Finance Cycle, a total of 17,000 AWCCs have been envisioned
to be set up in various States/UTs, out of which, 11395 AWCCs have been
approved by the Ministry for operationalization in various States/UTs. As on
28.2.2025, 1761 AWCCs are operational with 28783 current
beneficiaries. Besides this, 1284 Standalone Creches are also operational
across the country with 23368 current beneficiaries.
AI ACTION SUMMIT
3456. SHRI YUSUF PATHAN
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state :-
21.03.2025 139
(a) whether it is a fact that Prime Minister of India has recently visited France for
AI Action Summit;
(b) if so, the details thereof;
(c) the details of the key principles outlined in “Statement on Inclusive and
Sustainable Artificial Intelligence” for people and the Planet; and
(d) the manner in which India and France plan to address the digital divide and
ensure accessibility in artificial intelligence development, as discussed
during the summit?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST
AND CLIMATE CHANGE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (SHRI KIRTI VARDHAN SINGH):
(a) Yes.
(b) and (c) The AI Action Summit, held in Paris on February 10-11, 2025,
witnessed participation from 30 Heads of State/Government, 57 Ministers,
12 Heads of International Organizations, and 41 CEOs and business
leaders from the field. A Statement titled "Inclusive and Sustainable
Artificial Intelligence for People and the Planet" was adopted, the key
principles of which include: Public Interest and Accessibility to reduce
digital divides and ensuring that AI systems are ethical, inclusive, and
trustworthy; Diversity and Human-Centric Approach; and Innovation and
Sustainability. The discussions also focused on ethical and inclusive
global AI governance to ensure safe, reliable, and environmentally
responsible AI development. The Public Interest AI Platform and Incubator
21.03.2025 140
was launched during the Summit to bridge gaps between public and
private efforts, reduce fragmentation, and promote accessibility. It
focuses on supporting digital public goods, capacity building, data
sharing, transparency, and collaborative model development.
(d) India and France have outlined a collaborative approach to addressing
the digital divide and ensuring accessibility in AI development including
open source AI frameworks to enhance trust and transparency in AI
systems while advocating for a human-centric approach. India and France
separately adopted the Bilateral Declaration on Artificial Intelligence which
underscores the shared commitment of both nations to develop safe,
secure, and trustworthy AI systems that align with democratic values and
the common good. The declaration highlights efforts to bridge the digital
divide by promoting capacity-building, fostering international
cooperation, and preventing AI-driven inequalities or misinformation.
ADEQUATE FACILITIES FOR DEPORTED INDIANS
3457. SHRI D M KATHIR ANAND
DR. T SUMATHY ALIAS THAMIZHACHI THANGAPANDIAN
SHRIMATI MALA ROY
SHRI T.M. SELVAGANAPATHI
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state :-
21.03.2025 141
(a) whether it is a fact that many Indian were deported by the United States of
America to India by three flights in the month of February, 2025;
(b) whether the Union Government has received the details of the persons to be
deported from USA in the current year, if so, the details thereof, State-wise;
(c) whether the Union Government has requested US officials to
provide adequate facilities for the Indian citizens to be deported
from USA, if so, the details thereof;
(d) whether the Prime Minister has raised his concerns with US President and
top US officials for the ill treatment done to Indian Nationals deported from
USA, if so, the details thereof;
(e) whether it is also true that Indian migrants were handcuffed and their legs
shackled and they were forced to do a shuffled walk which is more commonly
associated with hardened criminals;
(f) whether the Government has any plan to send our own aircraft to bring back
Indians who were designated as illegal immigrants from USA and are to be
deported in the coming days; and
(g) if so, the steps taken/proposed to be taken by the Government in this regard?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST
AND CLIMATE CHANGE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (SHRI KIRTI VARDHAN SINGH):
21.03.2025 142
(a) to (g) From January 2025 till date, a total of 388 Indian nationals have been
deported to India from the United States. Of these, 333 Indian nationals, after
verification, were deported directly from the U.S. to India on three separate
chartered flights which landed on 5th, 15th and 16th February 2025 respectively.
Separately, the U.S. through Panama deported 55 Indian nationals who arrived
in India on commercial flights.
The U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) authorities have recently
shared with us information pertaining to an additional 295 individuals who are
detained in their custody with final orders of removal from the U.S. The Ministry
of External Affairs, along with other concerned agencies, is currently verifying
the details of these 295 individuals.
The Government of India remains engaged with the U.S. side regarding the
humane treatment of deportees during such operations. The Ministry has
strongly registered its concerns with the U.S. authorities on the treatment meted
out to deportees on the flight that landed on February 5th, particularly with
respect to use of shackles, especially on women.
The U.S. Standard Operating Procedure to organize and execute deportations
effective from November 2012 calls for the use of restraints on deportees. The
U.S. authorities have conveyed that restraints are applied to ensure the safety
and security of the deportation mission. While women and minors are generally
not shackled, the flight officer in charge of a deportation flight has the final say
on the matter. The U.S. side has confirmed that no women or children were
restrained on the deportation flights that landed in India on 15th and 16th
21.03.2025 143
February 2025 respectively. This has also been confirmed and recorded by our
agencies after interviewing the deportees on their arrival in India.
The need to promote safe, orderly and legal migration while cracking down on
illegal immigration networks was discussed during the recent visit of Prime
Minister to the US on 12-13 February 2025. India’s consistent stance against
illegal immigration while seeking humane treatment of deportees was reiterated.
Both sides acknowledged the need to cooperate closely in aggressively
addressing illegal immigration and human trafficking by taking strong action
against bad actors, criminal facilitators, and illegal immigration networks.
The U.S. is deporting only those Indian nationals who have been apprehended
for violating the U.S. immigration laws. Illegal mobility and migration has many
other associated activities, also of an illegal nature. Moreover, those of our
nationals who have been enticed into illegal migration have themselves become
prey to other crimes. Those who have recently returned have testified to their
harrowing experiences.
The Government of India is focused on identifying and taking strong action
against the illegal immigration agents, the migration rackets and the entire
criminal ecosystem, on which such illegal immigration thrives.
'TB MUKT BHARAT' CAMPAIGN IN IDENTIFIED DISTRICTS
3458. SHRI PRATAP CHANDRA SARANGI:
SHRI ANOOP PRADHAN VALMIKI:
DR. VINOD KUMAR BIND:
21.03.2025 144
SHRI ANURAG SHARMA:
SHRIMATI KAMALJEET SEHRAWAT:
SHRI DULU MAHATO:
DR. RAJESH MISHRA:
SHRI NABA CHARAN MAJHI:
SHRI BIBHU PRASAD TARAI:
SHRI SURESH KUMAR KASHYAP:
SHRI RAJKUMAR CHAHAR:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) the specific roles and responsibilities likely to be played by the Ministry of
MSME in the TB Mukt Bharat Abhiyaan to ensure effective implementation
and outreach in the identified districts including Hathras and Jharkhand
districts;
(b) the manner in which the Ministry proposes to coordinate with the MSME
associations and regional field offices to maximize awareness and
participation in the said Abhiyaan;
(c) the kind of medical support and follow-up care likely to be provided to
MSME workers diagnosed with TB through the screening camps;
(d) the manner in which the said collaboration with the Ministry of Health and
Family Welfare exemplifies the holistic approach of the Government;
(e) the various outcomes expected by the Ministry out of the said
collaboration; and
21.03.2025 145
(f) the details of the schemes and the outcomes under the said abhiyaan in
Hathras district?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS
AND FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
(a) and (b) The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) and Ministry of
Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSME) have agreed on facilitating
collaborative actions to strengthen national response towards TB elimination in
all State/UTs. During the TB Mukt Bharat Abhiyan, the specific responsibilities
played by Ministry of Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSME) in campaign
districts, including Hathras of Uttar Pradesh and 4 districts of Jharkhand, to
maximize awareness and mobilization of vulnerable population are as under:
 Engagement of all offices, institutions, and attached organizations of
MSME in the campaign.
 Display of IEC materials in all offices including field officers.
 Awareness generation of all staff on TB.
 Taking Ni-kshay Shapath (pledge) in all
organisations/offices/institutions.
 Ni-kshay Shivir (screening camps) in MSME and industrial hubs.
 Dissemination of awareness messages on social media.
 Encourage registration of Ni-kshay Mitra from various organisations
and institutions of MSME
21.03.2025 146
(c) All TB patients diagnosed during the campaign, including MSME workers
are linked to free treatment and appropriate health care by the State/District
health department concurred.
(d) and (e) This collaboration supplements the government’s approach to
achieve the national goals towards TB elimination with a whole of government
approach to improve health seeking behaviour, early detection of TB,
appropriate treatment of identified cases and to prevent new cases.
(f) In Hathras, during the Abhiyan, 10,540 Ni-kshay Shivir were held and
3,67,048 vulnerable populations were screened through which 1,062 new TB
patients were identified.
क(cid:167)सर के रोगी
3459. (cid:174)ी सजं य ह(cid:229)रभाऊ जाधवः
(cid:174)ी अरिवंद गणपत सावंतः
(cid:174)ी सजं य उ(cid:176)मराव देशमुखः
(cid:179)या (cid:214)वा(cid:214)(cid:195)य और प(cid:229)रवार क(cid:208)याण म(cid:253)ं ी यह बतान े क(cid:236) कृपा करग(cid:164) े िकः
(क) (cid:179)या देश म(cid:164) (cid:255)ितवष(cid:170) क(cid:167) सर के रोिगय(cid:332) क(cid:236) स(cid:180)ं या म(cid:164) विृ (cid:294) हो रही ह ै और यिद हां, तो त(cid:194)संबंधी
(cid:202)यौरा (cid:179)या ह ै और वत(cid:170)मान म(cid:164) देश म(cid:164) क(cid:167) सर रोिगय(cid:332) क(cid:236) रा(cid:186)य-वार स(cid:180)ं या िकतनी ह;ै

FOOD TESTING INFRASTRUCTURE IN NORTH-EASTERN STATES
3460. SHRI KAMAKHYA PRASAD TASA:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to
state:
(a) whether it is a fact that the food testing infrastructure facilities are
comparatively less in the north-eastern States and if so, the details thereof
along with the action taken by the Government in this regard;
(b) the details of central assistance provided to the Assam Government to set
up food testing Infrastructure and improve manpower; and
(c) whether the Government has taken several initiatives regarding food safety
and consumer empowerment in the country and if so, the details thereof
alongwith the initiatives taken for food safety?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER
OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) and (b) Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) provides
financial and technical assistance to State Food Testing Laboratories including
provision of Mobile Food Testing Labs. For testing of various food commodities,
21.03.2025 153
FSSAI has notified 243 primary laboratories and 22 Referral Laboratories across
the country. State wise details of these laboratories is at Statement.
FSSAI has assisted the state governments by strengthening the food
testing ecosystem by implementing the Scheme “Strengthening of Food Testing
System in the Country including provision of Mobile Food Testing Labs''
(SoFTeL). A total grant of Rs 92.63 crore has been released under the Central
Sector Scheme to the of North Eastern States during 2016-2023 out of which Rs
18.57 crore has been released to the State of Assam for strengthening of food
testing infrastructure.
Further, a total grant of Rs. 96.67 Crore has also been sanctioned under
(Memoranda of Understanding) MoU from 2020-21 to 2023-24 for
strengthening of food testing infrastructure in the North Eastern States out of
which 21.96 Crore has been released to the State of Assam.
FSSAI has also introduced the Mobile Food Testing Laboratory (MFTL)
referred to as “Food Safety on Wheels” (FSW). These FSWs are equipped with
basic infrastructure for on-spot testing of adulteration in various food
commodities. Currently, FSSAI has sanctioned 70 FSWs to North Eastern
States out of which 23 FSWs are sanctioned for the State of Assam.
Further, Ministry of Food Processing Industries has informed that under
PM Kisan SAMPADA Yojana (PMKSY) the North-Eastern region receives
prioritized financial assistance for setting-up /upgradation of Food Testing
Laboratories to address its unique challenges. For Non-government entities in
21.03.2025 154
General Areas term loans provided by banks cover at least 20% of the total
project cost. However, this requirement is reduced to 10% for projects in
Difficult Areas, including the North-Eastern region. Similarly, non-government
entities are required to contribute at least 20% of the total project cost as equity
in General Areas, whereas in the North-Eastern region, the equity contribution
is reduced to 10%. Additionally, in terms of financial assistance, grants for
equipment cover up to 50% of the cost of eligible equipment for private
organizations in General Areas, but up to 70% in Difficult/ North-Eastern Areas.
The implementation schedule also provides additional flexibility for projects in
NER. While projects in General Areas must be completed and operationalized
within 24 months from the date of approval, those in the North-Eastern Region
(NER) and other Difficult Areas are given an extended timeline of 30 months.
(c) The details of initiatives taken by FSSAI regarding food safety and consumer
empowerment in the country are as below:-
 FSSAI has empowered consumers by providing a digital grievance
redressal mechanism through the ‘Food Safety Connect" mobile
application. This platform allows consumers to report food safety
violations, register complaints related to food quality and report any
unlicensed or unregistered food businesses they come across.
 The FSSAI has introduced a nation-wide campaign 'Eat Right India'
movement in 2018 with a focus on preventive and promotive healthcare
through social and behavioral change. This initiative is built on three core
21.03.2025 155
pillars - Eat Safe, Eat Healthy and Eat Sustainable.
 A campaign called 'Aaj se Thoda Kam' encourages consumers to
gradually reduce their intake of fat, salt and sugar through dietary
modifications.
 FSSAI through its YouTube channel has created a dedicated playlist “How
to check for adulteration” containing more than 70 short videos for
checking adulteration.
 FSSAI has also launched several social media campaigns, including
Monday Motivation, Recipe Ravivaar, Know Your Food Labels,
Adulteration Awareness, and the Myth Busters series. These campaigns
aim to empower consumers with the knowledge they need to make
informed choices about food safety and nutrition.
 Further, various online challenges, like - "Healthy Recipe Contest: Ghar
Ki Rasoi- Tasty Bhi, Healthy Bhi," Tadke Bina Zaika (without the use of
visible fat), Indigenous Food Challenge National Low Salt Cooking
Challenge have also been conducted in order to spread awareness
among the citizens of the country to reduce consumption of fat, salt and
sugar.
 Additionally, resources like the DART Book provide simple, at-home tests
for detecting food adulterants, while initiatives such as Food Safety on
Wheels (FSWs) utilize mobile testing vans to reach remote areas, offering
food testing and raising awareness about food safety issues.
21.03.2025 156
 ‘Magic Box’ is a food safety kit which consists of reliable "quick screening
tests" that a common person can perform at the household level, enabling
a broad assessment of potential adulteration in their food in cases of
doubt.
