STATEMENT-V
The detail of measures taken by Ministry of Health and Family Welfare to
address anemia, in addition to mentioned above:
The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare implements Reproductive, Maternal,
Newborn, Child, Adolescent Health and Nutrition (RMNCAH+N) strategy in a life
cycle approach under National Health Mission (NHM), which includes
interventions to address anemia and malnutrition as placed below:
Nutrition Rehabilitation Centers (NRCs) are set up at public health

facilities to provide in-patient medical and nutritional care to children under
5 years suffering from Severe Acute Malnourishment (SAM) with medical
complications with special focus on improving the skills of mothers and
caregivers on timely, adequate and appropriate feeding for children.
Mothers’ Absolute Affection (MAA) Programme is implemented to improve

breastfeeding coverage which includes early initiation of breastfeeding
and exclusive breastfeeding for first six months followed by counselling
on age-appropriate complementary feeding practices.
Lactation Management Centres: Lactation Management Units (LMU) are

facilities established to provide lactation support to mothers and to
facilitate expression of mother’s own milk and Comprehensive Lactation
21.03.2025 261
Management Centres (CLMC) are established to ensure availability of
safe, pasteurized Donor Human Milk for feeding of sick, preterm and low
birth weight babies admitted in Neonatal Intensive Care Units and Special
Newborn Care Units.
Under National Deworming Day (NDD)albendazole tablets are

administered in a single fixed day approach via schools and Anganwadi
centres in two rounds (February and August) to reduce the soil transmitted
helminth (STH) infestation among all children and adolescents (1-19
years).
Village Health Sanitation and Nutrition Days (VHSNDs) are observed for

provision of maternal and child health services and creating awareness
on maternal and child care including nutrition in convergence with Ministry
of Women and Child Development.
The States are provided funds by the National Health Mission based on the
proposals received in their Annual Programme Implementation Plans.
ADVOCATES ACT,1961
3487. SHRI V K SREEKANDAN:
Will the Minister of LAW AND JUSTICE be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government is considering to amend the Advocates
Act,1961,if so, the details thereof;
21.03.2025 262
(b) whether the proposed amendments fail to address the pertinent issues
faced by the legal fraternity, if so the details thereof; and
(c) whether the Government discussed with the legal fraternity to allay the
fears arising out of the proposed amendments, if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF LAW AND JUSTICE; AND
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF PARLIAMENTARY AFFAIRS
(SHRI ARJUN RAM MEGHWAL):
(a) The Government of India has proposed amendments to the Advocates Act,
1961 and following careful consideration and through research, the Draft
Advocates (Amendment) Bill, 2025 was published on the Department of Legal
th
Affairs website on13 February 2025 for public consultation, which was open
th
until 28 February 2025.
(b) and (c) The proposed amendments to the Advocates Act, 1961, aim to
modernize the legal profession, enhance its accessibility, and ensure its
continued relevance in a rapidly evolving world, aligning the profession with
global standards. A wide range of suggestions, particularly from the legal
community, has been received. In response to these concerns, the consultation
process has been concluded, and a revised bill will be presented a fresh for
public consultation, including for input from the legal fraternity. The Ministry has
been also actively engaging with the Bar Council of India throughout the
process of amending the Advocates (Amendment) Act, 1961 as its commitment
to engage legal fraternity.
21.03.2025 263
ILLEGAL IMMIGRATION OF INDIAN CITIZENS
3488 SHRI ASADUDDIN OWAISI
SHRI K RADHAKRISHNAN
SHRI VIJAYAKUMAR ALIAS VIJAY VASANTH
ADV DEAN KURIAKOSE
ADV ADOOR PRAKASH
SHRI K C VENUGOPAL
SHRI B. MANICKAM TAGORE
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state:-
(a) whether the Government is aware of reports of large numbers of Indian
citizens attempting illegal entry into various countries particularly the United
States of America;
(b) if so, the details of Indian citizens arrested for illegal immigration to the United
States during the last year indicating the number of such arrests, State-wise;
(c) the number of Indian citizens deported from various countries over the past
five years, year-wise and country-wise;
(d) whether the Ministry incurs any expenditure when Indian citizens are
deported from other countries and if so, the total expenditure incurred year-
wise over the past five years; and
21.03.2025 264
(e) whether the Government has undertaken discussions or agreements with the
United States or other countries to address the issue of illegal immigration
by Indian citizens and if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST
AND CLIMATE CHANGE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (SHRI KIRTI VARDHAN SINGH):
(a) The Government of India is not in receipt of any firm information from the US
Administration regarding the total number of illegal immigrants in the United
States holding Indian passports. Foreign nationals who have entered the U.S.
illegally, or who have overstayed their visa validity, or found to be staying in the
U.S. without any valid documentation, or have criminal convictions against them
are likely to be deported. It is the obligation of all countries to take back their
nationals, if they are found to be living illegally abroad. This is however subject
to an unambiguous verification of their nationality. This is not a policy practiced
only by India; it is a generally accepted principle in international relations.
(b) and (c) Many foreign countries, including USA, do not provide information on
illegal stay in their countries, except when they are under orders of deportation
and travel document/nationality verification is required. As such, our Missions
and Posts do not have any reliable data on the number of Indians staying or
working illegally in foreign countries. The procedure followed for deportation of
foreign nationals also varies from country to country. Some countries do not
arrest the deportees and keep them in detention/deportation centers until
deportation. Moreover, the information about arrest/detention and deportation is
21.03.2025 265
not shared with Indian Missions/Posts abroad and the deportation is done
directly by the host Government if the deportee has a valid travel document.
Indian Missions/Posts abroad are contacted by the host Governments only in
such cases where the nationality verification and issuance of Emergency
Certificate (EC) to the deportee is required. Since all foreign countries do not
share details of the Indian deportees, the exact number of Indians deported from
foreign countries is not available with this Ministry. The U.S. has been carrying
out deportation operations for several years now. The last five years
deportations data from the U.S. to India is given below:
Year No. of Deportees
2020 1889
2021 805
2022 862
2023 617
2024 1368
2025 (till date) 388
(d) and (e) Deportation is generally the responsibility of the sending country.
Government of India remains engaged with the United States in promoting safe,
orderly and legal migration while cracking down on illegal immigration networks.
VISA-ON-ARRIVAL
3489 DR. M K VISHNU PRASAD
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state :-
21.03.2025 266
(a) the details of the foreign countries which have been offered Visa-on-Arrival
by India;
(b) the names of the countries which have offered Visa-on-Arrival facility for
Indians visiting those countries abroad;
(c) the details of foreign counties which were added to the Visa-on-Arrival to
India list during the last five years; and
(d) the details of other counties which provides Visa-on-Arrival for Indians visiting
those countries during the last five years?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST
AND CLIMATE CHANGE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (SHRI KIRTI VARDHAN SINGH):
(a) Currently, India provides Visa-on-arrival facility to three countries i.e Japan
and Republic of Korea (for certain categories only) and UAE (with certain
conditions).
(b) As on date, 39 countries provide visa-on-arrival facilities to Indian passport
holders. More details are available on the website of Ministry of External
Affairs [https://www.mea.gov.in/VFFIN.htm]
(c) No country has been added to the list of countries in the last five years which
have been provided with Visa on Arrival.
(d) Formulation of policies w.r.t issuance of visas is a sovereign matter of the
respective country and the matter regarding providing visa free entry/visa-
21.03.2025 267
on-arrival facility also depends on various factors including bilateral
relations and principle of reciprocity. This data keeps on changing. Details
of countries providing visa-on-arrival facility to Indian ordinary passport
holders is published by Ministry of External Affairs on the Ministry’s website,
which is available on the following link: https://www.mea.gov.in/VFFIN.htm.
ENVIRONMENTAL CONCERNS IN MORMUGAO PORT AUTHORITY
REGION
3490. SHRI CAPTAIN VIRIATO FERNANDES:
Will the Minister of PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS be pleased
to state:
(a) whether the Government has earmarked funds under any infrastructure
financing schemes to address environmental concerns in the Mormugao
Port Authority region;
(b) if so, the details thereof;
(c) whether it is a fact that a portion of the revenue generated from coal imports
is being utilized to mitigate pollution in the surrounding areas of Mormugao,
Vasco-da-Gama, Dabolim, Cortalim, and along the coastal villages through
which the coal trains are passing;
(d) if so, the details thereof; and
(e) if not, whether the Government is likely to consider a dedicated fund for
sustainable development in port-affected areas and if so, the details thereof?
21.03.2025 268
THE MINISTER OF PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS (SHRI
SARBANANDA SONOWAL):
(a) and (b) Yes. Central Government and Mormugao Port Authority (MgPA)
has earmarked funds under infrastructure financing schemes to address
environmental concerns in the MgPA region. The details are at Statement.
(c) and (d) Yes. Certain amount of the revenue generated from coal imports is
used by the Port to mitigate pollution within the Port and its surrounding areas.
To control dust pollution, various measures are implemented throughout the
year i,e. mechanized closed conveyor system for coal handling, DFDS dust
suppression, in-motion silo loading, wind screens, ground wetting, tarpaulin-
covered trucks, recycled water drainage, wheel washing, cement-paved roads,
green belts, and real-time air quality monitoring for dust control etc.
(e) Does not arise.

SALE OF SPURIOUS BLOOD
3491. SHRI TANUJ PUNIA
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) Whether it is a fact that a racket is operating to sell spurious blood on a large
scale in the country;
(b) if so, the details thereof;
(c) the action taken/proposed to be taken by the Government so far against the
people who involved in the illegal trade of spurious blood in the country; and
(d) the action proposed to be taken by the Government so that the trade of
spurious blood does not occur again?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS
AND FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
(a) to (d) No such information has been reported to this Ministry.
It is to be mentioned that blood is deemed to be a drug as per the definition in
Drugs and Cosmetics Act and all the provisions related to manufacturing and
sale of spurious drugs also apply to blood.
The government has made the registration of blood centres mandatory
according to the provisions of the Drugs and Cosmetics Act and the Rule there
under. Furthermore, the government has notified the Drugs and Cosmetics
(Second Amendment) Rules, 2020, which pertains to the operation of blood
21.03.2025 270
banks/centers, blood processing, and related matters. The implementation and
monitoring of the aforementioned Act and Rules fall within the jurisdiction of the
States/UTs.
ODR AND VIRTUAL COURTS IN MAHARASHTRA
3492. SHRI VISHALDADA PRAKASHBAPU PATIL:
Will the Minister of LAW AND JUSTICE be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government has plans to expand the scope of Virtual Courts
beyond handling traffic challans, if so, the details thereof and if not, the
reasons therefor;
(b) the steps taken by the Government to encourage High Courts to establish
Virtual Benches, particularly in Maharashtra; and
(c) whether Online Dispute Resolution (ODR) mechanisms are being
implemented across States/UTs, particularly in Maharashtra, if so, the
details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF LAW AND JUSTICE; AND
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF PARLIAMENTARY AFFAIRS
(SHRI ARJUN RAM MEGHWAL):
(a) to (c) A novel concept of Virtual Courts has been introduced under the
eCourts Project. It is one of the tools used for implementing Online Dispute
Resolution (ODR). The aim is to reduce the footfalls in the courts, by eliminating
physical presence of violators or advocates in courts’ premises. As of now,
21.03.2025 271
Virtual courts are being used for traffic challan matters. Under the Phase III of
the eCourts project, Rs. 413.08 Cr have been allocated for the expansion of
Virtual Courts across India. As per information provided by the eCommittee,
Supreme Court of India, modalities are being worked out to expand the usage
of Virtual Courts to various other summary cases, specially those involving the
imposition of fines for minor regulatory violations whose statistics indicate that a
plea of guilt is entered on the first date.
The proliferation of virtual hearings conducted by the High Courts is
detailed at Statement. To use the facility to operate more virtual benches is to
be considered by the High Courts, including the High Court of Bombay.
Under the eCourts Project phase III, an amount of Rs. 23.72 crore has
been allocated for Online Dispute Resolution (ODR). Under the Phase III, an
ODR centre for each High Court and a dedicated software platform to handle
court related ODR has been planned. A separate software team and a
centralised cloud-based hardware is planned to be set aside for running these
ODR platforms over a period of time, which will facilitate in tackling the pendency
of cases. In view of the importance of enabling dispute resolution through the
use of Information and Communication Technology, Section 30 of the Mediation
Act, 2023, contains exclusive provisions for Online Mediation.

ILLEGAL EXPORT OF UNLICENSED OPIOIDS AND REGULATORY
OVERSIGHT
3494. SHRI AZAD KIRTI JHA:
21.03.2025 278
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government is aware of reports alleging that Indian
Pharmaceutical Companies, including Aveo Pharmaceuticals, have been
illegally manufacturing and exporting unlicensed opioid-based drugs to West
African countries, contributing to a major public health crisis and if so, the
details thereof;
(b) the steps taken/proposed to be taken by the Government to investigate these
allegations and hold the responsible entities accountable;
(c) whether the loopholes in the existing regulations on pharmaceutical exports
have enabled the continued export of such unlicensed and harmful drugs, if
so, the details thereof;
(d) the measures taken/proposed to be taken by the Central Drugs Standard
Control Organisation (CDSCO) to ensure stricter enforcement and
monitoring of pharmaceutical exports, particularly in the case of opioid-
based medications; and
(e) whether the Government has conducted any inspections or audits of
pharmaceutical manufacturers engaged in the export of opioid-based drugs
and if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS
AND FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
21.03.2025 279
(a) to (e) In order to assess the regulatory compliance of drug manufacturing
premises in the country, the Central Drugs Standard Control Organization
(CDSCO), in collaboration with state regulators, initiated risk-based inspections
of drug manufacturing and testing firms in December 2022. As of now, 905 units
have been inspected, resulting in 694 actions being taken. These actions include
Stop Production Orders (SPO), Stop Testing Orders (STO), license
suspensions/cancellations, warning letters, and showcause notices, depending
on the severity of non-compliance.
Based on a media report relating to M/s Aveo Pharmaceuticals, a joint
team from CDSCO and the State drugs regulatory authority conducted a
comprehensive audit of the firm between 21st and 22nd February 2025. The
findings from the audit led to the issuance of a stop activity order, halting all
operations at the company's premises.
With regard to export of opioid drugs, CDSCO has a system of issuing
export NoC for new drugs and drugs not approved in India based on submission
of customer details, quantity and country specific purchase order alongwith
regulatory approval of the product in importing country.
NATIONAL CRECHE SCHEME
3495. SHRIMATI KANIMOZHI KARUNANIDHI:
Will the Minister of WOMEN AND CHILD DEVELOPMENT be pleased to
state:
21.03.2025 280
(a) the number and details of the functional creche facilities available under the
National Creche Scheme in the country from 2017 to till date indicating their
capacity, and the number of children benefiting from them, State and UT-
wise;
(b) the details of the steps taken by the Government to improve and expand the
creche infrastructure, particularly in rural and underserved areas, to ensure
better accessibility for working parents;
(c) whether the Government is planning to establish Government-supported
creches in industrial clusters to increase female labour force participation, if
so, the details thereof; and
(d) whether the Government has conducted any assessment of the impact of
the amendment to The Maternity Benefit Act (MBA) in 2017, which made it
mandatory for companies with more than 50 employees to offer creche
facility in the premises, with costs to be borne by the employer and if so, the
findings of such assessment?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF WOMEN AND CHILD
DEVELOPMENT (SHRIMATI SAVITRI THAKUR):
(a) to (d) The erstwhile ‘National Crèche Scheme (NCS)’, for Children of
Working Mothers was being implemented by the Ministry of Women and Child
Development as a Centrally Sponsored Scheme from 01.01.2017 to 31.03.2022
21.03.2025 281
for providing Crèche services. As part of the scheme, Stand-alone Crèches were
being operated through NGOs as implementing agencies.
As per report of Development Monitoring and Evaluation Office (DMEO), NITI
Aayog, it was seen that a majority of states have emphasised that they prefer to
run Anganwadi-cum-crèches (AWCCs) instead of separate stand-alone crèches
as it increases their financial, HR and administrative burden attributed to the
implementation of an additional scheme. Accordingly, Department of
Expenditure, Ministry of Finance during the Expenditure Finance Committee
(EFC) for Mission Shakti recommended that Creches may be merged with the
nearest Anganwadi Centres, and run as AWCCs.
Accordingly, the Ministry introduced Palna sub-scheme under Mission Shakti for
all States/UTs w.e.f. 01 April 2022, for providing day care facilities and protection
to children.
Palna is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme ensuring the participation of State/UT
government to ensure better day-to-day monitoring and proper implementation
of scheme, and is implemented with a funding ratio of 60:40 between Centre and
State Governments and UTs with legislature except North East and Special
Category States where ratio is 90:10. For UTs without legislature, 100% funding
is provided by the central government.
Anganwadi centres are the world's largest childcare institutions dedicated to
providing essential care and support to children ensuring delivery of care
facilities till the last mile. In a first of its kind approach, Ministry has extended the
services of childcare through Anganwadi cum Crèche. This will ensure whole
21.03.2025 282
day childcare support ensuring their well-being in a safe and secure
environment. Anganwadi cum Crèche initiative aims to increase the women work
force participation in the economy. The objective of Palna component is to
provide quality crèche facility in safe and secure environment for children (from
ages 6 months – 6 years), nutritional support, health and cognitive development
of children, growth monitoring, immunization, education etc. Crèche facilities
under Palna are to be provided to all mothers, including those working in
unorganized sectors, and irrespective of their employment status.
Proposals for establishment and operation of Crèches are received from the
respective State Governments/UT Administrations, who are also responsible for
contributing their corresponding share for the implementation of the Scheme.
During the 15th Finance Cycle, a total of 17,000 AWCCs have been envisioned
to be set up in various States/UTs, out of which, 11395 AWCCs have been
approved by the Ministry for operationalization in various States/UTs. Number
of functional creche facilities under Palna scheme along with the number of
beneficiaries is at Statement.
State Governments are appropriate Government under Maternity Benefit Act
except some activities like mines and circus. Section 11A of the Act provides for
Creche facility in every establishment having fifty or more employees, either
separately or along with common facilities. No such assessment of the impact
of the Act has been done centrally.

RECRUITMENT IN AIIMS
3496. ADV PRIYA SAROJ:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) the details of department wise regular posts, total number of new created
posts and category-wise (UR, SEBC, SC, ST) recruitment made for Senior
Residents (SRs), Junior Residents (JRs) and Research Associates (RAs)
during the last 20 years in AIIMS, New Delhi on the basis of tenure;
(b) the number of SRs/JRs/Research Associates of General Category who are
working on contract basis or otherwise against the posts meant for
SEBC/SC/ST category-wise and department-wise along with their tenure
indicating month, year and with and without break; and
21.03.2025 285
(c) the percentage of such employees as mentioned in comparison to the total
sanctioned strength in AIIMS, New Delhi, category-wise and department-
wise?

PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):

posts and creation of new posts and admission / recruitment against vacant
posts is a continuous process. The category-wise current status of Junior
Residents (JR) and Senior Residents (SR) in AIIMS, New Delhi is as under:

DEVELOPMENT OF BUCKINGHAM CANAL
3497. SHRI MADDILA GURUMOORTHY:
Will the Minister of PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS be pleased to
state:
(a) whether the Government is aware of the urgent need for the development of
the Buckingham Canal as part of the inland waterways network, considering
21.03.2025 287
its historical significance in trade and transport along the eastern coast of
India;
(b) whether any steps have been taken to declare the Buckingham canal a
National Waterway and if so, the details of the plans, timelines and budget
allocations therefor;
(c) the manner in which the revitalization of the Buckingham Canal will enhance
cost-effective and environmental friendly cargo transport between coastal
cities and ports and the measures being taken to achieve this; and
(d) whether the Government has evaluated the socio-economic benefits of
developing the Buckingham canal, especially regarding tourism and
livelihood opportunities for local communities, particularly those in fishing and
transport?
THE MINISTER OF PORTS, SHIPPING AND WATERWAYS (SHRI
SARBANANDA SONOWAL):
(a) and (b) Buckingham Canal consisting of 316 km long North Buckingham Canal
(Pedaganjam to Central Station Chennai) and 110 km long South Buckingham
Canal (Central Station Chennai to Marakanam) is constituent of National
Waterway-4 (NW-4). Detailed Project Report (DPR)was prepared in 2008 and
updation of DPR has been taken up by Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI).
Further, IWAI has identified potential tourism/cargo movement in Buckingham
Canal and have requested Government of Tamil Nadu for undertaking cargo/river
cruise tourism projects under a framework of co-operation as portions of
21.03.2025 288
Buckingham canal are encroached. A total provision of Rs 19.17 crore has been
made by Government of Indiafor NW-4 for the FY 2022-2026.
(c) and (d) The revitalisation of Buckingham Canal entails cost efficient
logistics for fisheries/local communities/ industries and reduction in congestion
from road. The DPR prepared by IWAI in 2008 encompasses the socio-economic
benefits of revival of this canal system.

सरकार ने फा(cid:214) फे टय(cid:179)ु त एवं पोटाशय(cid:179)ु त (पीए(cid:193) डके ) उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332) के िलए िदनांक 01.04.2010
से पोषकत(cid:194) व आधा(cid:229)रत सि(cid:202)सडी नीित काया(cid:170)ि(cid:198)वत क(cid:236) है। इस नीित के अंतग(cid:170)त, अिधसूिचत पीए(cid:193) डके
उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332) पर उनक(cid:236) पोषकत(cid:194) व मा(cid:253)ा के अनुसार वािष(cid:170)क/ अध(cid:170)-वािष(cid:170)क आधार पर तय क(cid:236) गई सि(cid:202)सडी
क(cid:236) एक िनयत रािश (cid:255)दान क(cid:236) जाती है। पीए(cid:193)डके (cid:177)े(cid:253) िविनयंि(cid:253)त है और उव(cid:170)रक कं पिनय(cid:332) को
तक(cid:170) संगत (cid:214)तर(cid:332) पर एमआरपी िनधा(cid:170)(cid:229)रत करने क(cid:236) अनुमित है। उव(cid:170)रक कं पिनयां बाजार के उतार-
चढ़ाव के अनसु ार उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332) का उ(cid:194) पादन/आयात करती ह(cid:167)। सरकार (cid:255)मुख उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332) और क(cid:184)चे माल के
अंतरा(cid:170)(cid:213)(cid:367)ीय म(cid:208)ू य(cid:332) क(cid:236) िनगरानी करती है और िकसान(cid:332) को पीएडं के उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332) क(cid:236) वहनीय क(cid:236)मत(cid:332) पर
आपूित(cid:170) सिु नि(cid:306)त करने हेतु पीए(cid:193)डके उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332) के िलए वािष(cid:170)क/अध(cid:170)-वािष(cid:170)क आधार पर एनबीएस दर(cid:164)
िनधा(cid:170)(cid:229)रत करते समय उतार-चढ़ाव, यिद कोई हो, को सि(cid:204)मिलत िकया जाता है।
हाल ही म(cid:164) भू-राजनीितक संकट के कारण, िजससे उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332) क(cid:236) आपूित(cid:170) म(cid:164) बड़ा (cid:211)यवधान पैदा
ह(cid:242)आ, खरीफ 2024 म(cid:164) िकसान(cid:332) को पीएडं के उव(cid:170)रक(cid:332) क(cid:236) सहज उपल(cid:202)धता सुिनि(cid:306)त करने के िलए
सि(cid:248)य कदम उठाए गए। जलु ाई 2024 म(cid:164), सरकार ने िदनांक 01.04.2024 से 31.12.2024 तक
क(cid:236) अविध के िलए एनबीएस दर(cid:332) के अित(cid:229)र(cid:265) 3500 (cid:255)ित मीि(cid:367)क टन क(cid:236) दर स े डीएपी पर एकबारगी
िवशेष पैके ज के मा(cid:197)यम स े िनयिमत आयात(cid:332) को सुिनि(cid:306)त करने के िलए त(cid:194)काल उपाय िकए, िजसे
िकसान(cid:332) को वहनीय क(cid:236)मत पर डीएपी क(cid:236) िनरंतर उपल(cid:202)धता सिु नि(cid:306)त करने के िलए अब िदनांक
31.03.2025 तक बढ़ा िदया गया है।

MAHILA SHAKTI KENDRAS
3501. SHRI VISHNU DATT SHARMA:
Will the Minister of WOMEN AND CHILD DEVELOPMENT be pleased to stat:
(a) whether the Government is implementing the Mahila Shakti Kendras
(MSKs) to empower rural women, especially in the State of Madhya
Pradesh and if so, the details thereof;
(b) whether the Government has established MSKs in the districts of Katni,
Panna and the town of Khajuraho in the Khajuraho Parliamentary
Constituency;
21.03.2025 312
(c) if so, the details thereof along with the measures being taken to ensure the
success of the said scheme; and
(d) if not, the reasons therefor?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF WOMEN AND CHILD
DEVELOPMENT (SHRIMATI SAVITRI THAKUR):
(a) to (d) The Mahila Shakti Kendra (MSK) Scheme was approved in November,
2017 as a centrally sponsored scheme to empower rural women through
community participation. A third party evaluation study of MSK scheme was
conducted by NITI Aayog in 2020. Taking into account the findings of the
evaluation and after consultation with States/ UTs and stakeholders, the scheme
was discontinued with effect from 01.04.2022.
However, Ministry of Women and Child Development is implementing the
Umbrella Scheme named as ‘Mission Shakti’ with effect from 01.04.2022 across
the country including the State of Madhya Pradesh which aim at strengthening
interventions for women safety, security and empowerment. The ‘Mission Shakti’
has two verticals ‘Sambal’ for safety and security of women and ‘Samarthya’ for
empowerment of women.
“Hub for Empowerment of Women (HEW)”, a component under Samarthya,
sub-scheme of ‘Mission Shakti’ is functioning as a Project Management Unit
(PMU) for monitoring and supervisory unit of Mission Shakti Scheme.
The HEW centres also engage in widespread outreach and awareness through
organising awareness activities, camps, and campaigns to disseminate
21.03.2025 313
information related to women centric schemes, programmes, policies and
legislations.
The HEW are operational in the State of Madhya Pradesh at State level as well
as District level including the districts of Katni, Panna and the districts falling
within the Khajuraho Parliamentary Constituency.
HIV SELF-TESTING
3502: SHRI ANURAG SINGH THAKUR:
SHRI KHAGEN MURMU:
SHRIMATI APARAJITA SARANGI:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) the manner in which the Government plans to integrate HIV Self-Testing
(HIVST) into the existing National AIDS and STD Control Programme
(NACP) Phase-V;
(b) the steps taken/proposed to be taken by the Government to ensure that the
Self-Testing kits are affordable and accessible especially for marginalized
and vulnerable populations;
(c) the role of public-private partnerships in expanding the reach of HIV self-
testing; and
(d) whether there are any existing collaborations with private healthcare
providers and NGOs and if so, the details thereof?
21.03.2025 314
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS
AND FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
(a) to (d) Under the National AIDS and STD Control Programme (NACP), a
health care provider performs HIV screening coupled with pre-test and post-test
counselling. The screened reactive cases undergo confirmatory testing for HIV
with pre-test and post-test counselling at Integrated Counselling and Testing
Centres (ICTCs) through a stringent three test algorithm to ensure that test
results are accurate. Pre – test and post – test counselling is an integral part of
HIV testing under the NACP due to the stigma and discrimination associated
with HIV. Till date, the Drug Controller General of India has not issued certificate/
license to any HIV self – testing kit for use in the country. There is no provision
to integrate HIV self-testing into the existing National AIDS and STD Control
Programme (NACP) Phase-V.
आगं नवाड़ी काय(cid:170)कता(cid:170) और सहाियका को (cid:198)यूनतम मजदूरी
3503. (cid:174)ी अमरा रामः

(क) आंगनवाड़ी काय(cid:170)कता(cid:170)ओ ंऔर आंगनवाड़ी सहाियकाओ ंके पद(cid:332) पर काय(cid:170)रत किम(cid:170)य(cid:332) क(cid:236) सं(cid:180)या

जा रहा है;

मिहला और बाल िवकास म(cid:253)ं ालय म(cid:164) रा(cid:186)य म(cid:253)ं ी ((cid:174)ीमती सािव(cid:253)ी ठाकु र):
(क) अनमु ोिदत पद तथा उ(cid:265) पद(cid:332) पर काय(cid:170)रत आंगनवाड़ी काय(cid:170)कि(cid:253)(cid:170)य(cid:332) (एड(cid:202)(cid:208)यूड(cid:202)(cid:208)यू) और
आंगनवाड़ी सहाियकाओ ं(एड(cid:202)(cid:208)यूएच) क(cid:236) स(cid:180)ं या का रा(cid:186)य/संघ रा(cid:186)य (cid:177)े(cid:253)वार (cid:202)यौरा िववरण -I म (cid:164)
िदया गया है। आंगनवाड़ी काय(cid:170)कि(cid:253)(cid:170)यां और सहाियकाए ं (cid:214)थानीय समुदाय क(cid:236) “मानद काय(cid:170)कि(cid:253)(cid:170)यां” ह(cid:167),
जो समदु ाय क(cid:236) सहायता के िलए बाल देखभाल और िवकास के (cid:177)े(cid:253) म(cid:164) अपनी सेवाए ंदेने हेत ु (cid:214)वे(cid:184)छा
से आगे आती ह(cid:167) िजसके िलए उ(cid:198)ह(cid:164) मानदये का भगु तान िकया जाता है। भारत सरकार ने िदनांक 1
अ(cid:179)टूबर, 2018 से म(cid:180)ु य आंगनवाड़ी के (cid:198)(cid:254)(cid:332) पर आंगनवाड़ी काय(cid:170)कि(cid:253)(cid:170)य(cid:332) का मानदये 3,000 (cid:321)पये
से बढ़ाकर 4,500 (cid:321)पये (cid:255)ित माह; लघु आंगनवाड़ी के (cid:198)(cid:254)(cid:332) पर 2,250 (cid:321)पये से बढ़ाकर 3,500 (cid:321)पये
(cid:255)ित माह कर िदया है; आंगनवाड़ी सहाियकाओ ंका मानदेय 1,500 (cid:321)पये से बढ़ाकर 2,250 (cid:321)पये
(cid:255)ित माह कर िदया ह;ै तथा आंगनवाड़ी सहाियकाओ ं के िलए 250 (cid:321)पये तथा आंगनवाड़ी
काय(cid:170)कि(cid:253)(cid:170)य(cid:332) के िलए 500 (cid:321)पये (cid:255)ित माह का काय(cid:170)-िन(cid:213) पादन आधा(cid:229)रत (cid:255)ो(cid:194)साहन श(cid:322)ु िकया है।
इसके अलावा, रा(cid:186)य/संघ रा(cid:186)य (cid:177)े(cid:253) अपने संसाधन(cid:332) से इन काय(cid:170)कि(cid:253)(cid:170)य(cid:332) को अित(cid:229)र(cid:265) मौि(cid:254)क
(cid:255)ो(cid:194)साहन/मानदये भी दे रहे ह(cid:167) जो रा(cid:186)य दर रा(cid:186)य अलग-अलग है। (cid:202)यौरा िववरण -II म(cid:164) िदया गया
है।
आंगनवाड़ी काय(cid:170)कि(cid:253)(cid:170)य(cid:332) (एड(cid:202)(cid:208)यूड(cid:202)(cid:208)यू) और आंगनवाड़ी सहाियकाओ ं(एड(cid:202)(cid:208)यूएच) को (cid:255)ो(cid:194)सािहत

21.03.2025 316
i. पदो(cid:198)नित : िमशन स(cid:177)म आंगनवाड़ी और पोषण 2.0 के तहत आंगनवाड़ी काय(cid:170)कि(cid:253)(cid:170)य(cid:332) के िलए
पदो(cid:198)नित के अवसर बढ़ाए गए ह(cid:167)। आंगनवाड़ी काय(cid:170)कि(cid:253)(cid:170)य(cid:332) के 50% पद 5 वष(cid:170) का अनुभव रखन े
वाली आंगनवाड़ी सहाियकाओ ं(cid:300)ारा भर े जाने ह(cid:167) तथा पय(cid:170)वे(cid:177)क(cid:332) के 50% पद 5 वष(cid:170) का अनुभव

को पूरा करती ह(cid:332)।

WOMEN AND CHILD COUNSELLING CENTRES
3504. SHRI MAGUNTA SREENIVASULU REDDY:
SHRI PUTTA MAHESH KUMAR:
Will the Minister of WOMEN AND CHILD DEVELOPMENT be pleased
to state:
21.03.2025 322
(a) whether the Government has undertaken any schemes/Programmes/
initiatives for the construction and development of Women and Child Counselling
Centres across the country;
(b) if so, the details of such programmes/schemes/initiatives and the funds
allocated/utilised for the same during the last five years, State-wise and
district-wise in Andhra Pradesh;
(c) the number of women and child counselling centres proposed, approved and
presently functional across the country, State-wise and districts-wise
including Prakasam and Eluru district in said State;
(d) the number of women and child specialist psychologists, therapists and
psychiatrists' positions available along with those presently employed and
vacancies in child counselling centres in the country, State-wise and district-
wise in the said State; and
(e) whether the Government has put forward any guidelines/manual regarding
the functioning/maintenance of child counselling centres, if so, the details
thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF WOMEN AND CHILD
DEVELOPMENT (SHRIMATI SAVITRI THAKUR):
(a) to (c) The One Stop Centre (OSC) is a component of the Sambal vertical
under Mission Shakti provides integrated support and assistance under one
roof to women affected by violence and those in distress, both in private and
public spaces. It also provides services like medical aid, legal aid and advice,
temporary shelter, police assistance and psycho-social counselling to needy
21.03.2025 323
women. As the scheme is demand driven, the States and UTs are encouraged
to set-up at least one OSC in each district and additional OSC(s) in those
districts which have higher rate of crime against women or have bigger
geographical area and in aspirational districts. As on date, 894 OSCs are
approved and out of which, 802 OSCs are operational across the country either
in own building or pre-existing government building or rented accommodation.
The State wise approved and operational OSCs is at Statement-I. The details
of district-wise approved and operational OSCs in Andhra Pradesh including
Prakasam and Eluru district is at Statement-II. Details of State-wise funds
released for the One Stop Centres (OSCs) during the last five years are at
Statement-III.
Further, the Ministry is implementing a Centrally Sponsored Scheme namely,
'Mission Vatsalya' through States/Union Territories to deliver various services
for Children in Need of Care and Protection and Children in Conflict with Law. It
includes both Institutional Care services and Non-Institutional Care
services. Institutional Care is provided through the Child Care Institutions, as a
rehabilitative measure. The programmes and activities in Homes, inter-alia,
include age-appropriate education, access to vocational training, recreation,
health care, counselling etc. Under the Non-Institutional Care component,
support is provided to the children through Adoption, Foster Care, After Care
and Sponsorship. There is provision of counsellor in the District Child Protection
Unit and in the Child Care Institution also.
21.03.2025 324
The Ministry of Women and Child Development in collaboration with National
Institute of Mental Health and Neurosciences (NIMHANS), Bengaluru
established Support, Advocacy and Mental Health Interventions for Children in
Vulnerable Circumstances and Distress (SAMVAD), a national initiative and
integrated resource for child protection, mental health, and psychosocial care,
that provides training and capacity building and other forms of guidance and
technical support, to child protection functionaries.
(d) and (e) As per the Mission Shakti guidelines, the Central Government
provides 100% financial assistance to States/ UTs for appointment/hiring 13
manpower resources including 1 Psycho-social Counsellor at each OSC. The
overall implementation of the OSC scheme lies with the State Governments/UT
Administrations. The Ministry has issued ‘Mission Shakti’ and ‘Mission Vatsalya’
guideline for implementation of women and child related schemes during 15th
Finance Commission period 2021-22 to 2025-26.

AIIMS IN MADURAI
3505 THIRU DAYANIDHI MARAN:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to
state:
(a) the current status of the All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS)
project in Madurai along with the expected timeline for its completion;
(b) the details of total budget allocated/utilized for the Madurai AIIMS Project;
(c) whether there are any delays in fund disbursement that are affecting the
progress of the project and if so, the details thereof;
(d) the main reasons for the delay in the Madurai AIIMS project and the manner
in which these are being addressed;
(e) whether there are contingency plans to avoid further delays in construction
and operationalisation of the said project and if so, the details thereof; and
(f) whether the Union Government has provided regular updates to the State
Government and the public about the progress of the Madurai AIIMS project
and if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER
OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) to (c) Establishment of All India Institute of Medical Sciences(AIIMS) Madurai
has been approved under Pradhan Mantri Swasthya Suraksha Yojana (PMSSY)
21.03.2025 329
at a project cost of Rs.2021.51 crore. Currently, 26% of Phase-I work has been
completed. As per the Loan Agreement signed with Japan International
Cooperation Agency (JICA) target date of completion of the project is October,
2026. As on date, Rs. 239.27 crore has been released for construction of AIIMS
Madurai. There is no delay in disbursement of funds for the project activities.
(d) to (f) The foundation stone of AIIMS Madurai was laid in 2019 , however the
land for the project was handed over by the State Government in 2020. It was
decided to obtain funding for the project through Japan International
Cooperation Agency(JICA) and the Loan Agreement was signed between
Government of Japan and Government of India in March, 2021. The Project cost
was also revised on account of inclusion of 150 bedded Infectious Disease Block
and some other additions. The progress of the project is being regularly
monitored at the level of the Institute and the Ministry. In addition, a Steering
Committee has been constituted under the Chairpersonship of Principal
Secretary (Health), Government of Tamil Nadu to monitor and facilitate smooth
implementation of the project .

(ख) से (घ) आयुष औषिध गणु व(cid:176)ा एवं उ(cid:194)पादन संवध(cid:170)न योजना (एओजीयूएसवाई) के मा(cid:197)यम से

काया(cid:170)ि(cid:198)वत क(cid:236) गई काय(cid:170) योजना इस (cid:255)कार है:-

कुल 2324 213673
EMPOWERMENT OF WOMEN AND CHILDREN
3507. SHRI ESWARASAMY K:
Will the Minister of WOMEN AND CHILD DEVELOPMENT be pleased to
state:
(a) the details of schemes for empowerment of women and development of
children by Non-Governmental Organisations (NGOs) in Tamil Nadu;
(b) whether Government has put in place any mechanism to monitor the work
being done by NGOs;
(c) if so, the details thereof;
(d) the details of the beneficiaries of various schemes during the last two years
and the current year, State-wise; and
(e) whether these schemes have helped in improving the standard of life of
beneficiaries, if so, the details of the method of assessment and the results
thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF WOMEN AND CHILD
DEVELOPMENT (SHRIMATI SAVITRI THAKUR):
21.03.2025 340
(a) to (c) The Ministry of Women and Child Development releases funds directly
to States/UTs under its various schemes and missions. It does not release any
funds to any Non-Governmental Organisations (NGOs).
(d) The State-wise details of beneficiaries under the schemes of the Ministry
during the last two years and the current year are at Statement.
(e) Yes Sir.
Mission Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 seeks to address the challenge
of malnutrition through improved nutrition content and delivery. It is a Centrally
Sponsored Scheme, where the responsibility of implementation lies with the
States/UTs. The mission adopts a strategic approach to reduce malnutrition
and improve overall health and immunity. This includes activities such as
community engagement, outreach, behaviour change initiatives, and advocacy.
It emphasizes maternal nutrition, infant and young child feeding norms, and the
treatment of Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) and Moderate Acute Malnutrition
(MAM), with additional focus on wellness through AYUSH practices.
Supplementary Nutrition is provided to target groups following revised nutrition
norms that prioritize diet diversity, ensuring a balanced intake of calories,
proteins, healthy fats, and essential micronutrients. Fortified rice is supplied to
Anganwadi Centres to address micronutrient deficiencies and combat anaemia.
Additionally, millet-based meals are encouraged at least once a week for Hot
Cooked Meals and Take Home Rations (THR). Under the Wheat Based
Nutrition Programme (WBNP) and Scheme for Adolescent Girls (SAG),
subsidized food grains such as wheat, fortified rice, and coarse grains are
21.03.2025 341
distributed to States/UTs through the Department of Food and Public
Distribution.
Mission Vatsalya scheme is implemented through the State/ UT Governments
as per standards of care and protection to secure the best interest of child which
is provided under The Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act,
2015 (JJ Act, 2015) (as amended in 2021).
Mission Shakti –
(i) One Stop Centre (OSC) scheme is being implemented by the Government
across the country including State of Tamil Nadu to provide integrated support
and assistance under one roof to women affected by violence and those in
distress both in private and public spaces. It also provides an integrated range
of services including medical aid, legal aid and advice, temporary shelter, police
assistance, psycho-social counselling to needy women.
(ii) Women Helpline provides 24X7 emergency and non-emergency response
through telephonic short-code 181 to women, by linking them with appropriate
authorities such as Police, One Stop Centres, Hospitals, Legal Services
Authorities etc. Additionally, it provides information about women welfare
schemes and programs to all women.
(iii) Beti Bachao Beti Padhao (BBBP) scheme launched on 22nd January 2015,
is a mindset change program, aims at valuing the girl child and preventing
gender- biased sex-selective practices. BBBP is a centrally sponsored scheme
21.03.2025 342
under Sambal vertical of Mission Shakti with 100% funding by the Central
Government across all the districts of the country. The scheme aims to work
towards the protection and education of girl child through multi-sectoral
interventions and encouraging greater spend on activities that have on ground
impact, e.g., promotion of sports among girls, awareness about PC-PNDT Act
etc.
(iv) Nari Adalat is an initiative aimed at empowering women by ensuring justice
and to offer services such as alternate dispute resolution, grievance redressal,
counselling, evidence-based decision-making, pressure group tactics,
negotiation, mediation, and reconciliation.
(v) Ministry of Women and Child Development is implementing Pradhan Mantri
Matru Vandana Yojana (PMMVY) across the country, including rural areas of
the country w.e.f. 01.01.2017. The PMMVY is a Centrally Sponsored Maternity
Benefit Scheme under which cash incentives of ₹5,000/- is provided directly to
the Bank/Post Office account of the beneficiary in Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT)
mode for first child. The eligible beneficiaries receive the remaining cash
incentive, as per approved norms towards maternity benefit under Janani
Suraksha Yojana after institutional delivery, so that on an average a woman gets
₹6,000/-. Cash incentive of ₹6,000/- is also provided under PMMVY to eligible
beneficiaries for second child subject to the second child being a girl.
(vi) Sakhi Niwas Scheme aims to promote availability of safe and conveniently
located accommodation for working women in urban, semi-urban and also in
21.03.2025 343
rural areas where employment opportunity for women exists. Under the Scheme,
financial assistance is provided for running of Sakhi Niwas in rented premises
for working women and for those women who are under training for job.
Provision of Day Care Centre for children of the residents of the Sakhi Niwas is
an important aspect of the scheme.
(vii) ‘Shakti Sadan Scheme’ is an Integrated Relief and Rehabilitation Home for
women in distressful situations including trafficked women. It aims at creating a
safe and enabling environment for the women in such difficult situations, to
enable them to overcome the adverse circumstances.
(viii) Ministry of Women and Child Development has introduced Palna sub-
scheme under Mission Shakti for all States/UTs w.e.f. 01 April 2022, for
providing day care facilities and protection to children. Palna is a Centrally
sponsored scheme ensuring the participation of State/UT government for day-
to-day monitoring and proper implementation of scheme.
Anganwadi centres are the world's largest childcare institutions dedicated to
providing essential care and support to children ensuring delivery of care
facilities till the last mile. In a first of its kind approach, Ministry has extended the
services of childcare through Anganwadi cum Crèche (AWCC). This will ensure
whole day childcare support ensuring their well-being in a safe and secure
environment. Anganwadi cum Crèche initiative aims to increase the women work
force participation in the economy. The objective of Palna component is to
provide quality creche facility in safe and secure environment for children (from
21.03.2025 344
ages 6 months – 6 years), nutritional support, health and cognitive development
of children, growth monitoring, immunization, education etc

SPREAD OF GBS
3508. SHRIMATI ANITA SUBHADARSHINI:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:-
(a) whether there is an increase in the number of patients affected by Guillain-
Barre Syndrome (GBS) in the country in recent months;
(b) if so, the details thereof, State-wise;
(c) whether the Government has constituted any expert committee to find out
the cause of spread of GBS and if so, the details thereof;
(d) whether the Government has formulated any roadmap to check the spread
of GBS in future; and
(e) if so, the details thereof?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER
OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
21.03.2025 366
(a) and (b) As on 12th March, 2025, a total of 229 cases (suspected and
confirmed) of Guillain-Barre-Syndrome (GBS) have been reported in the country
only from Maharashtra State. The incidence of GBS shows that the cases have
been in declining trend in recent months.
(c) to (e) Central technical team was deployed on 24/01/2025 comprising experts
from the National Centre for Disease Control (NCDC), National Institute of
Mental Health and Neuro Sciences (NIMHANS) and National Institute of Virology
(NIV), Pune on site to study the pathogens and outbreak. Majority of cases have
been reported from specific clusters in Pune, with additional cases in Nanded.
The study was focused on identifying the exact source of the outbreak, with a
thorough examination of water supply systems, sources of water, and other
relevant factors. The investigation indicated that the most likely precursor of
GBS in this population group is prior infections caused by Campylobacter.
To check and monitor the future spread of the disease surveillance
mechanism has been established under the Integrated Disease Surveillance
Program (IDSP)/Integrated Health Information Platform (IHIP) portal, which
captures suspected cases. Steps taken by the Government to prevent the
spread of GBS is given in Statement.
STATEMENT
Steps taken by the Government to prevent the spread of GBS
Strengthening sanitation and hygiene measures.

Efforts are made to ensure an uninterrupted supply of treated and

21.03.2025 367
clean water.
Water sources are examined for leaks and damages, and

corrective measures are taken.
Nodal officer have been appointed in private hospitals and

chlorination have been done in wells.
Tankers of clean water have been arranged.

Have conducted massive Information, Education, and

Communication (IEC) campaigns to raise awareness at the
community level,
Designated isolation wards set-up in government hospitals and

special packages under PM-JAY scheme have been included for
treatment of GBS.
Have expanded testing for suspected cases and collaborated with

local authorities to contain the spread.
CHILD CARE INSTITUTIONS
3509. SHRI HARIBHAI PATEL :
SHRI RAJU BISTA :
Will the Minister of WOMEN AND CHILD DEVELOPMENT be pleased
to state:
21.03.2025 368
(a) whether the Government has recently increased support for Child Care
Institutions (CCIs) under the Mission Vatsalya scheme and if so, the
details of the support provided during FY 2023-24, State-wise;
(b) whether there have been any enhancements in non-institutional care
services for children in need of care and protection and if so, the number
of children supported under these services;
(c) whether the Government has established any new District Child
Protection Units or Child Welfare Committees as part of Mission Vatsalya
and if so, the details of these establishments State-wise;
(d) whether there are any specific initiatives aimed at improving educational
and vocational training opportunities for children in CCIs, if so, the details
thereof;
(e) whether the Government anticipates that these efforts will lead to
improved outcomes for children in care and if so, the details thereof along
with the manner in which it will be measured; and
(f) the details of the support provided under Mission Vatsalya in the district
of Darjeeling and Kalimpong since its inception?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF WOMEN AND CHILD
DEVELOPMENT (SHRIMATI SAVITRI THAKUR):
(a) Ministry of Women and Child Development is implementing a Centrally
Sponsored Scheme namely ‘Mission Vatsalya’, through the State/ UT
Governments on predefined cost sharing basis between the Central and the
21.03.2025 369
State Governments to deliver services for children in difficult circumstances
which includes institutional care and non-institutional care services. The Child
Care Institutions (CCIs) established under the Mission Vatsalya scheme
support, inter-alia, age-appropriate education, access to vocational training,
recreation, health care, counselling etc. State-wise funds released under
Mission Vatsalya Scheme during the year 2023-24 and number of CCIs
supported under the scheme during the year 2023-24 is at Statement-I.
(b) Non-Institutional Care support is provided by way of sponsorship, foster
care and after care to children in need of care and protection. The number of
children approved under Mission Vatsalya Scheme during the financial years
2022-23, 2023-24 and 2024-25 is as under :
Financial Year Number of approved
children
2022-23 62,675
2023-24 1,21,861
2024-25 1,70,895
(c) Section 106 of Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015
provides that every State Government shall constitute a Child Protection Unit
for every District, consisting of such officers and other employees as may be
appointed by that Government, to take up matters relating to children with a
view to ensure the implementation of this Act, including the establishment and
maintenance of institutions under this Act, notification of competent authorities
in relation to the children and their rehabilitation and co-ordination with various
21.03.2025 370
official and non-official agencies concerned and to discharge such other
functions as may be prescribed.
Further, Section 27 (i) of Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children)
Act, 2015 provides that the State Government shall by notification in the Official
Gazette constitute for every district, one or more Child Welfare Committees for
exercising the powers and to discharge the duties conferred on such
Committees in relation to children in need of care and protection under this Act
and ensure that induction training and sensitisation of all members of the
committee is provided within two months from the date of notification.
Details of State-wise District Child Protection Units and Child Welfare
Committees supported under Mission Vatsalya during FY 2023-24 are at
Statement-II.
(d) The Child Care Institutions (CCIs) established under the Mission Vatsalya
scheme support, inter-alia, age-appropriate education, access to vocational
training, recreation, health care, counselling etc.
(e) The improved outcome can be envisaged based on the statutory and
service delivery structures created under Mission Vatsalya Scheme, utilization
of funds, beneficiaries supported under the scheme, presence of structures at
State and District level namely, State Child Protection Society (SCPS), State
Adoption Resource Agency (SARA), Child Welfare Committee (CWC),
Juvenile Justice Board (JJB), District Child Protection Unit (DCPU) and Child
Care Institution (CCI).

HEALTHCARE INFRASTRUCTURE IN KONASEEMA ANDHRA
PRADESH
3510. SHRI G M HARISH BALAYOGI:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
21.03.2025 374
(a) the number and details of healthcare centres/hospitals operational in
Konaseema district in the State of Andhra Pradesh including Government,
private and specialty hospitals, especially their capacity and facilities
available;
(b) the number and details of medical personnel currently available across all
healthcare centres/hospitals in Konaseema district, categorized health
centre-wise, especially doctors (specialists and general), nurses,
paramedics and other staff;
(c) the details regarding the current vacant positions across all healthcare
centers/hospitals in Konaseema district including reasons for vacancies and
steps being taken to fill them, health centre-wise and position-wise;
(d) the doctor-patient and nurse-patient ratio in Konaseema along with the steps
undertaken to improve these ratios;
(e) the details regarding the availability of essential medical equipment,
diagnostic facilities and critical care units in Government hospitals in
Konaseema district;
(f) whether the Government has any plan to strengthen healthcare
infrastructure in Konaseema district, if so, the details thereof; and
(g) the details regarding the availability and implementation of telemedicine and
digital healthcare services in Konaseema district?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER
OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
21.03.2025 375
(a) to (c) Health Dynamics of India (HDI) (Infrastructure and Human Resources),
2022-23 is an annual publication, based on healthcare administrative data
reported by States/UTs. Details of Primary Health Centres (PHCs), Community
Health Centres (CHCs), Sub-District/Divisional Hospitals (SDHs), District
Hospitals (DHs) and Medical Colleges (MC) functioning in rural and urban areas
of Andhra Pradesh including Konaseema district along with status of health
manpower may be seen at the following link of HDI 2022-23:
https://mohfw.gov.in/sites/default/files/Health%20Dynamics%20of%20India%2
0%28Infrastructure%20%26%20Human%20Resources%29%202022-
23_RE%20%281%29.pdf
Further, as informed by State Government, a total of 153 Private Hospitals are
functioning in the State of Andhra Pradesh.
Details of health manpower sanctioned, in-position and vacant in Konaseema
district is as follows:
S.No. Name of Post Sanctioned Existing Vacant
1. Community health Officer (CHOs) 23 18 5
2. Staff Nurse 138 126 12
3. Public Health Nurse (NT) 29 20 9
4. Pharmacist 46 42 4
5. Lab Technician 47 45 2
21.03.2025 376
(d) As informed by State, the Doctor-Patient and Nurse-Patient ratio in
Konaseema district are 1:21279 and 1:13173 respectively. The National Health
Mission (NHM) provides support for improvement in health infrastructure,
availability of adequate human resources in health facilities, to improve
availability and accessibility to quality healthcare especially for the under-served
and marginalized groups in rural areas. The Ministry of Health and Family
Welfare provides technical and financial support to the States/UTs to strengthen
the public healthcare system, based on the proposals received in the form of
Programme Implementation Plans (PIPs) under NHM. The Government of India
provides approval for the proposals in the form of Record of Proceedings (RoPs)
as per norms and available resources.
The Government of India has taken number of initiatives in the form of incentives
and honorarium to the medical professionals for encouraging better service
delivery in rural and remote areas across the country, which include:
(i) Hard Area Allowance to specialist doctors for serving in rural and remote
areas so that they find it attractive to serve in public health facilities in such
areas.
(ii) States are also allowed to offer negotiable salary to attract specialist
including flexibility in strategies such as “You Quote We Pay”.
(iii) Non-monetary incentives such as preferential admission in postgraduate
courses for staff serving in difficult areas and improving accommodation
arrangement in rural areas have also been introduced under NHM.
(iv) Multi-skilling of doctors is supported under NHM to overcome the shortage
21.03.2025 377
of specialists such as Comprehensive Emergency Obstetric and Newborn
Care (CEmONC) and Life Saving Anaesthesia Skills (LSAS).
(e) and (f) This Ministry supports ‘Free Diagnostics Service Initiative’ programme
under NHM with the aim to provide accessible and affordable pathological and
radiological diagnostics services closer to the community, which in turn reduces
the Out of Pocket Expenditure (OOPE). Diagnostics services are provided free
of cost at all levels of public health facilities across the country including
Konaseema district of Andhra Pradesh [14 tests at SHCs, 63 at PHCs, 97 at
CHCs, 111 test at SDH and 134 tests at District Hospitals].
XV-Finance Commission has recommended grants aggregating to Rs. 70,051
Crores over the period of five years (2021-2026) through local government to
strengthen the healthcare system in various States including the Andhra
Pradesh. A total amount of Rs 2601.32 crore has been allocated under FC-XV
health grant for the State of Andhra Pradesh for FY 2021-22 to FY 2025-26.
Under the XV-FC Grants, 7 BPHUs (Block Public Health Units) have been
approved for Konaseema district for infrastructure strengthening.
(g) Digital interventions have been introduced for healthcare delivery, with
eSanjeevani-national telemedicine service playing a key role in enhancing
remote healthcare access through video consultations. E-Sanjeevani is
implemented in two variants: eSanjeevani OPD, which enables direct patient-to-
doctor video consultations and eSanjeevani AB-AAM (Ayushman Arogya
Mandirs), which facilitates doctor-to-doctor teleconsultations, allowing primary
21.03.2025 378
healthcare providers to seek expert opinions from specialists in higher-level
hospitals.
As on 06.03.2025, in Konaseema district of Andhra Pradesh, 13,16,692 tele-
consultations have been conducted in eSanjeevani AAM through 42 Spoke cum
Hub operational as Hub and 455 Spokes served by a team of 514 Medical
practitioners and health workers.
PROBLEM DUE TO SNAKE BITES
3511. SHRI RAMASAHAYAM RAGHURAM REDDY:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:-
(a) whether the Government is aware that snake bites are a big problem in the
country and if so, the details thereof;
(b) whether the Government is aware that Telangana has a particularly high
number of snake bite cases and if so, the details thereof;
(c) the steps taken by the Government to redress the problem in this regard;
(d) whether any funds have been allocated for the same and if so, the details
thereof; and
(e) whether there has been any awareness campaign on poisonous snakes
and if so, the details thereof alongwith the funding provided in this regard?
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER
OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
21.03.2025 379
(a) to (c) The Government is aware of the snake bites problem. The States and
UTs of India are reporting the snakebite cases regularly on Integrated Disease
Surveillance Program (IDSP)/Integrated Health Information Platform (IHIP)
portal. Telangana reported 2479 snakebite incidents cases in 2024 on the
IDSP/IHIP.
The Government of India has taken several steps to address the issue of
snakebite. The intiatives taken by the Government is at Statement-I.
(d) and (e) The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare provides technical and
financial support to the States/UTs under the National Health Mission (NHM),
based on the proposals received in Program Implementation Plans (PIPs) from
the States/UTs. The details of the PIP State/UT wise may be seen at the
following link:
https://nhm.gov.in/index1.php?lang=1andlevel=1andsublinkid=1377andlid=744
Various initiatives taken by the Government on awareness generation on
snakebites is at Statement-II.
STATEMENT-I
The intiatives taken by the Government
 The States/UTs are reporting snakebite cases and deaths on Integrated
Disease Surveillance Program (IDSP)-Integrated Health Information
Portal (IHIP) on real time basis.
 The Guidelines for prevention and control of Snakebite have been
developed under the National Action Plan for Prevention and Control of
21.03.2025 380
Snakebite Envenoming (NAPSE) which may be accessed at
https://ncdc.mohfw.gov.in/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/NATIONAL-
ACTION-PLAN-FOR-PREVENTION-AND-CONTROL-OF-
SNAKEBITE-ENVENOMING-NAPSE.pdf)
 Snakebite related treatment is part of Health System Strengthening
(HSS) component of NHM. Support is provided to all States/UT for
ensuring availability of emergency medicines, equipment’s, transport
mechanism related to snakebite and other emergency conditions. To
ensure the affordability and accessibility of polyvalent Anti Snake Venom
(ASV) at each level, ASV has been included under the National and
State Essential Drug List. The States/UTs gets funds for ASV through
National Free Drug Initiative under National Health Mission (NHM).
 To strengthen effective implementation of snakebite prevention and
control activities in the country, funds are being provided to states for
the following:

Training of health professionals on Snake bite management,
emergency care, control etc. at State and District level;

Meetings for advocacy, inter-sectoral coordination etc.,

Surveillance and monitoring

Public Awareness through IEC
 Have provided capacity building to health care providers to equip
21.03.2025 381
doctors in all public health facilities across the country with the
necessary skills for the emergency management of snakebite cases.
STATEMENT-II
Various initiatives taken by the Government on awareness generation
on snakebites

A Booklet on Snakebite Prevention and Control: Available in both
Hindi and English, this booklet provides comprehensive guidelines on
snakebite prevention, control, and initial first aid measures for
snakebite victims.

Posters on Do’s and Don’ts for Snakebites: Designed for use in the
general community, these posters are available in Hindi and English,
offering practical advice to minimize risks and ensure proper response
to snakebites.

A Video on Snakebite Awareness: Produced in 10 regional
languages, this video aims to educate diverse populations effectively
about snakebite prevention, first aid, and the importance of seeking
timely medical attention.

ANALYSIS OF DIAGNOSTIC TECHNIQUES ACT
3513: DR. T SUMATHY ALIAS THAMIZHACHI THANGAPANDIAN:
SHRI SELVARAJ V:
SHRI SUBBARAYAN K:
Will the Minister of HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government is aware that 4.6 lakhs girls were missed at birth each
year between 2013-2017 in the country and if so, the details thereof;
(b) whether the Government is planning to undertake any analysis on how girls go
missing at birth despite the existence of the Pre-Conception and Pre-Natal
Diagnostic Techniques (PCPNDT) Act, 1994;
(c) if so, the details thereof and if not, the reasons thereof;
(d) whether the attention of the Government has been drawn towards a statement
made by the President of Indian Medical Association (IMA) against PC-PNDT
Act made to prevent female foeticide saying that the prohibitions under this act
need to be dispensed with owing to its massive failure over 30 years; and
(e) if so, the details thereof and the response of the Government thereto?
21.03.2025 384
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY
WELFARE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF CHEMICALS
AND FERTILIZERS (SHRIMATI ANUPRIYA PATEL):
(a) to (e) The Government of India enacts the Pre-conception and Pre-Natal
Diagnostic Techniques (Prohibition of Sex Selection) Act, 1994 for awareness
generation and advocacy measures to build a positive environment for the girl
child through gender-sensitive policies, provisions and legislation. As per the
latest Sample Registration Survey (SRS) report 2020, the Sex Ratio at Birth
(SRB) (female per 1000 male) stands at 907.
The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare undertakes regular review meetings,
awareness generation activities, sensitization and capacity building workshops
as well as issues regular advisories to strengthen the implementation of the PC
and PNDT Act across the country.
As per information received from Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA), 'Police' and
'Public Order' are State subjects under the Seventh Schedule to the Constitution
of India. The responsibilities to maintain law and order, protection of life and
property of the citizens including women and children rest with the respective
State Governments. The State Governments are competent to deal with such
offences under the extant provisions of laws.
However, Ministry of Home Affairs has been issuing advisories from time to time
with a view to support and supplement the efforts of States/UTs to effectively
21.03.2025 385
deal with crimes against women and children, which are available at
www.mha.gov.in. These advisories inter-alia consist instructions for effective
implementation of law with special emphasis on crime against women and
children.
VISIT OF EUROPEAN COMMISSION PRESIDENT
3514. SHRI SUDHEER GUPTA
SHRI DHAIRYASHEEL SAMBHAJIRAO MANE
SHRI RAVINDRA VASANTRAO CHAVAN
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state :-
(a) the details of major agreements signed during the visit of European
Commission President Ursula von der Leyen to India recently;
(b) whether India and European Union has signed or proposes to sign any Free
Trade Agreement;
(c) if so, the details thereof and the salient features of the said trade agreement
between the two partners;
(d) whether India and European Union has also signed an agreement on security
and defence partnership, artificial intelligence and the inter operation ability
of digital public infrastructure and if so, the details thereof; and
(e) the manner in which the said free trade agreement with European Union is
likely to be helpful in realising the India's dream of achieving the status of
developed nation or Viksit Bharat by 2047?
21.03.2025 386
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST
AND CLIMATE CHANGE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (SHRI KIRTI VARDHAN SINGH):
(a) and (d) At the invitation of Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi, President
of the European Commission, H.E. Ms. Ursula von der Leyen, accompanied by
the European Union College of Commissioners, paid an official visit to India on
27-28 February 2025. This was the first ever visit of the EU College of
Commissioners together to India. During the visit, Prime Minister held delegation
level talks with President Ursula von der Leyen. The second ministerial meeting
of the India – EU Trade and Technology Council (TTC) and bilateral ministerial
meetings between the European Commissioners and their Indian counterparts
were also held during the visit.
During the meetings, all areas of bilateral co-operation as well as global and
regional developments of mutual interests were discussed. These included trade
and investment; critical and emerging technologies including AI,
Semiconductors and DPI; clean and green energy; defence and security;
sustainable infrastructure; talent mobility; skilling and people to people ties. No
Agreements were signed during the visit. A Leaders’ statement and a Joint
Statement on the Second Ministerial Meeting of the India-EU Trade and
Technology Council were issued at the conclusion of the visit.
(b), (c) and (e) India and European Union have resumed Free Trade Agreement
negotiations in June 2022. Ten Rounds of Negotiation have been held till date.
21.03.2025 387
The last Round of Negotiation was held from 10th-14th March, 2025 at Brussels.
The proposed FTA is aimed at strengthening trade and investment linkages
between India and the EU and support supply chain resilience through enhanced
economic cooperation.

CHARGES FOR DEPORTATION OF INDIANS
21.03.2025 390
3516 SHRI MANISH TEWARI
Will the Minister of EXTERNAL AFFAIRS be pleased to state:-
(a) whether the Union Government has paid any charges per person as
deportation costs for Indian nationals recently deported by the United States,
if so, the details thereof;
(b) whether due process under U.S. immigration law, during expedited removal
proceedings, was followed for these deported Indian nationals, if so, the
details thereof;
(c) whether the Government has raised any concerns with U.S. authorities
regarding Indian deportees being handcuffed, shackled and treated in a
manner that undermines their basic human dignity and rights, if so, the
details thereof; and
(d) whether Prime Minister Narendra Modi has raised this issue during his recent
discussions with President Donald Trump in Washington D.C., if so, the
details of the discussions and any assurances received thereon?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF ENVIRONMENT, FOREST
AND CLIMATE CHANGE; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF
EXTERNAL AFFAIRS (SHRI KIRTI VARDHAN SINGH):
(a) No.
(b) to (d) Since January 2025, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security’s
Customs and Border Protection (CBP) vertical has been carrying out deportation
21.03.2025 391
operations as part of the larger “National Security initiative” led by the U.S. White
House – National Security Council (NSC). The individuals that have been
deported in this operation were placed under “expedited removal” process,
wherein a determination of inadmissibility can be made by the immigration officer
without having to go through an immigration judge in the U.S.
The Ministry of External Affairs remains engaged with the U.S. side regarding
the humane treatment of deportees during deportation operations. The Ministry
has strongly registered its concerns with the U.S. authorities on the treatment
meted out to deportees on the flight that landed on February 5th, particularly
with respect to use of shackles, especially on women.
It may be noted that the U.S. Standard Operating Procedure to organize and
execute deportations effective from November 2012 calls for the use of restraints
on deportees. The U.S. authorities have conveyed that restraints are applied to
ensure the safety and security of the deportation mission. While women and
minors are generally not shackled, the flight officer in charge of a deportation
flight has the final say on the matter. The U.S. side has confirmed that no women
or children were restrained on the deportation flights that landed in India on
February 15th and 16th respectively. This has been confirmed and recorded by
our agencies after interviewing the deportees on their arrival in India.
The need to promote safe, orderly and legal migration while cracking down on
illegal immigration networks was discussed during the recent visit of Prime
Minister to the U.S. on 12-13 February 2025. India’s consistent stance against
illegal immigration while seeking humane treatment of deportees was reiterated.
21.03.2025 392
Both sides acknowledged the need to cooperate closely in aggressively
addressing illegal immigration and human trafficking by taking strong action
against bad actors, criminal facilitators, and illegal immigration networks.
SUSTAINABLE MANAGEMENT OF MEDICINAL PLANTS SCHEME
3517. SHRI KESINENI SIVANATH:
Will the Minister of AYUSH be pleased to state:
(a) the total amount of funds sanctioned and released under the Conservation,
Development and Sustainable Management of Medicinal Plants Scheme till
date, State/UT-wise;
(b) the total number of projects funded by the said scheme till date, State/UT-
wise especially in the State of Andhra Pradesh;
(c) the details regarding the projects completed, ongoing projects and the
timeline required for their completion along with the funds released till date;
(d) the details of the total number of farmers trained under the projects supported
for information, Education and Communication (IEC) activities of the said
scheme, classified, State/UT-wise;
(e) whether the Government has any data regarding the funds sanctioned and
released under the component of Quality Testing and Certification of raw
material of the said scheme; and
(f) if so, the details thereof, State/UT-wise and funds released till date in this
regard?
21.03.2025 393
THE MINISTER OF STATE OF THE MINISTRY OF AYUSH; AND MINISTER
OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HEALTH AND FAMILY WELFARE (SHRI
PRATAPRAO GANPATRAO JADHAV):
(a) National Medicinal Plants Board (NMPB), Ministry of Ayush, Government of
India is implementing Central Sector Scheme on “Conservation, Development
and Sustainable Management of Medicinal Plants” throughout the country
including Andhra Pradesh wherein following activities are supported:
(i) Information, Education and Communication (IEC) activities like
Training / Workshops / Seminars/ Conferences etc. to promote the
medicinal plants.
(ii) Establishment of nurseries.
(iii) In-situ conservation / Ex-situ conservation.
(iv) Livelihood linkages with Joint Forest Management Committees
(JFMCs) / Panchayats / Van Panchayats / Biodiversity Management
Committees (BMCs) / Self Help Groups (SHGs).
(v) Research and Development.
(vi) Promotion, marketing and trade of medicinal plants produce.
(vii) Forward and backward linkage in supply chain of medicinal plants
(Integrated Component) under which the following activities are
supported:
 Infrastructure for Quality Planting Material to raise the planting
material of medicinal plants for cultivation.
21.03.2025 394
 Information Education Communication (IEC) activities to aware the
farmers.
 Infrastructure for Post-Harvest Management and Marketing to
increase the marketability of the medicinal plants, adding value to the
produce, increasing profitability and reducing losses.
 Quality Testing and Certification of raw material.
This Scheme has been approved for continuation for a period of five years
from 2021-22 to 2025-26. Grant-in-Aid sanctioned / released during 2021-22
to 2024-25 under Central Sector Scheme for Conservation, Development and
Sustainable Management of Medicinal Plants is placed at Statement-I.
(b) and (c) The National Medicinal Plants Board (NMPB), Ministry of Ayush is
presently implementing its “Central Sector Scheme on Conservation,
Development and Sustainable Management of Medicinal Plants” throughout the
country including the state of Andhra Pradesh. The details regarding the number
of projects funded, the projects completed, ongoing projects, funds released till
date and the timeline required for their completion is placed at Statement-II.
Under this scheme, few Regional Centers are also supported under Project
mode. The details of ongoing/foreclosed RCFCs and fund released to these
RCFCs from 2017-18 to till date are placed at Statement-III.
(d) National Medicinal Plants Board (NMPB) Ministry of Ayush has supported 24
projects under the training activities to train the farmers. Approximately, 15,430
21.03.2025 395
farmers were supported for training under these proposals from the financial year
2021-22 to 2024-25. The details are placed at Statement-IV.
(e) and (f) Yes, Sir. National Medicinal Plants Board (NMPB), Ministry of Ayush
has supported 04 projects under ‘forward and backward linkage in supply chain
of medicinal plants’ – Integrated Component of Central Sector Scheme (CSS) for
conservation, development and sustainable management of medicinal plants
from the financial year 2023-24 to till date to the Andhra Pradesh, Jammu and
Kashmir, Mizoram and Uttarakhand for an amount of Rs. 442.50 lakh. Out of
which an amount of Rs. 23.00 lakh was approved for the Quality Testing and
Certification of raw material to the states. The details are given at Statement – V.
