SHRI MUKESHKUMAR CHANDRAKAANT DALAL (SURAT): I support demand
for grants for 2025-26 for Jul Shakti ministry.
This ministry is in it's infant stage in a way that it was structured only in 2019 &
present dynamic & dedicated minister Shree C.R. Patil Ji is only second minister in
the history. The has done remarkable job which the country has noted in very short
span of time.
The task of water management resources is duel & shared between the center &
the state. It is very truly said that next world war could be fought on water. But
congress never understood importance of water & like all other initiatives, it is Modi
ji who formed new ministry of Jal Shakti. The ministry ensures adequate availability
for diverse needs of water such as drinking, domestic, sanitation, irrigation &
industrial.
Water scarcity is to pause greatest challenge in future on account of increased
demand coupled with shrinking supplies due to over utilization & pollution. Water
is a cyclic resource with abundant supplies on the globe. Approximately 71% of the
earth's surface is covered with water but freshwater constitutes about only 3% of
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1093
total water supply on the earth, a very small portion is effectively available for
human use & it varies with space & time. The world has witnessed many tensions
& disputes on sharing & control of this scarce resource, we too have witnessed in
our country across among communities, regions & states.
The ministry is comprising of mainly The Department of Water Resources, River
Department & Ganga Rejuvenation & the Department of Drinking Water &
Sanitation for which record breaking Rs 99503 cr. combined has been allocated in
this budget. Under the guidance, motivation & supervision by hon. Modi saheb "Jal
hai to Jivan hai", the department initiated two centrally sponsored schemes:
The Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) started way back in 2019 has been allocated
massive Rs 67000 crore in this budget. JJM ensured good water quality supply to
all 19 crores households & public institutions of which 15 crores households have
functional household tap water connections (FHTCS). As on February 2024, over
14.24 crore (73.93%) rural households have tap water at their doorsteps. After a
village achieves 100% functional tap water connections, the gram panchayat
formally certifies the village as "Har Ghar Jal",
Second scheme is Swachh Bharat Mission Grameen (SBM-G) has been allocated
Rs. 7192 crores in this budget. All villages were declared ODF in 2019 under phase
1 of the scheme. Under phase II, out of 5.86 lakh villages covered under ODF Plus
villages, 5.63 lakh villages (96%) are declared ODF Plus villages. Similarly, The
Department of water Resources, River Development & Ganga Rejuvenation
(DOWR), looking after water resource management, flood & irrigation
management, ground water management & rivers rejuvenation, has been allocated
21.03.2025 1094
Rs. 25277 crores in this budget. The department monitors river inter linking project
& PM Krishi Sinchai Yojana (PMSKY) which was launched in 2015-16 to increase
cultivable area under irrigation, improve water availability to farms & increase water
use efficiency, PMSKY has been allocated Rs. 8260 crores for 2025-26 which
implements two schemes-
The Accelerated Irrigation Benefit Programme (AIBP) which takes up medium
irrigation projects.
Har Khet Ko Pani (HKKP) takes up minor irrigation projects.
India accounts for about 2.45 percent of the world's surface area, 4 percent of the
world's water resources & 17 percent of the world's population. Total water
available from precipitation in the country in a year is barely 4000 cubic km from
surface & replenishable ground water is 1869 cubic km. Out of this only 60 percent
is of beneficial use. So, it comes to about total only 1122 cubic km.
There are four major sources of water i.e. rivers, lakes, ponds & tanks. We have
about 10360 rivers & their tributaries longer than 1-6 km each. India has last
coastline due to which number of lagoons & ponds are formed. Similarly, there are
four types of natural freshwater sources: surface water, subterranean river flow,
ground water & frozen water. Oceans are the largest source of water on earth
holding about 96.5 all the planet's water.
India has been water stressed since 2011. Per capita projected water availability
in India in cubic meters was 1820 in 2001, 1651 in 2011, 1486 in 2021, 1367 in
2031, 1282 in 2041 & 1228 in 2051. 61% of utilizable water is from surface water
sources & 39% from ground water where the government has done commendable
21.03.2025 1095
job by making thousands of borewell throughout the country. 90% of water is used
for agriculture, 7% domestic use & 2% for industrial use. I fully appreciate the hard
work put in & the new direction that hon. minister Shree CR Patil ji has given to his
ministry under the able leadership of Modi ji particularly in exploiting, strengthening
& generating new storage capacity of underground water in the country during
2024-25. These efforts will go long way in future to generate new capacity of water
supply for future generation in the country. I fully end endorse & support demand
for grant for 2025-26 for Jal Shakti ministry.
Pani re Pani Tera Rang Kaisa, Jis me Mila do Lage us Jaisa

माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) : आप (cid:179)या बोलना चाहते ह(cid:167)?
SHRI N. K. PREMACHANDRAN (KOLLAM): Thank you very much, Sir.
I fully agree with the hon. Minister. Yes, it is absolutely the dream of the hon.
Prime Minister – Har Ghar Jal, that is, Jal Jeevan Mission. It is the flagship
programme of the NDA Government, led by Shri Narendra Modi. We fully
appreciate it. We are all supporting it. Let the dream of the Prime Minister be
fulfilled.
There is a specific question. It is not only on my part but almost all the
Members from the Opposition have criticized it that so many misappropriations are
going on as far as the Jal Jeevan Mission is concerned. The hon. Minister has said
that the implementing authority is the State. But in such a case, can the
Government of India evade their responsibility? They are saying that the
implementing authority is the State Governments, so it is absolutely the
responsibility of the State Governments; we are not responsible. You are
responsible to the Parliament because for the year 2024-25, we have given you
more than Rs. 70,000 crore. You have only spent Rs. 22,000 crore. Then what is
the purpose?
My point is this. What is the purpose for which the Demand is being granted?
21.03.2025 1117
I would like to ask whether the Government of India under the leadership of the
hon. Prime Minister – since it is the dream of the hon. Prime Minister, he has
repeated several times – will conduct a social audit throughout the country, thereby
exposing the reality of the scheme. That has to be done. That is the point which I
would like to make.

प(cid:229)रवार क(cid:236) मेहनत, उनके पशुओ ं क(cid:236) मेहनत, खेती क(cid:236) जो वै(cid:208)यू है, उसका माक(cid:165) ट म(cid:164) (cid:202)याज होना
चािहए । सारी क(cid:236) सारी चीज(cid:332) को अगर िमला ल(cid:164), तो जो (cid:214)वामीनाथन जी ने सी-2 का फाम(cid:170)ूला िदया,
सी-2 + 50 परस(cid:164)ट, यानी लागत का डेढ़ गनु ा समथ(cid:170)न मू(cid:208)य ।

से वष(cid:170) 2025 आ गया है, लेिकन अभी तक सरकार का यह संक(cid:208)प पूरा नह(cid:233) ह(cid:242)आ ह ै । 12वां बजट भी

SHRI SUDHAKAR SINGH (BUXAR): I beg to Move:
Need to implement Swaminathan Formula for MSP whereby the
government must ensure that Minimum Support Price (MSP) is legally
guaranteed and calculated as per the C2+50% formula to provide fair
compensation to farmers and protect them from market fluctuations.
(1)
Need to expand and strengthen Agricultural Cold Storage and
Processing Units in Bihar to reduce post-harvest losses and increase
farmers' incomes. (2)
Need to have comprehensive crop insurance and disaster relief
reforms in Bihar where the farmers frequently suffer crop losses due
to floods, droughts and erratic weather patterns. (3)
Need to impose a complete ban on gene editing in crops to protect
farmers' rights, biodiversity and traditional seed sovereignty. (4)
Need to address and implement all demands of Sanyukta Kisan
Morcha including legal guarantee for MSP, withdrawal of cases
against protesting farmers and compensation for the families of
deceased farmers. (5)

SHRI D. M. KATHIR ANAND (VELLORE): In 2025-26, Rs 1,37,757 crore has
been allocated to the Ministry (2.7% of the union budget). The allocation towards
the Ministry is estimated to decrease by 2.5% over the revised estimates of 2024-
25. According to the revised estimates of 2024-25, the expenditure of the Ministry
has increased by 6.8% over the budget estimate. The inflation is much higher and
the increase in the cost is still going high. This means the budget allocation for
2025-26 literally reduced. But the Finance Minister juggles with just numbers. this
is a government which professes so much about the welfare of Farmer has totally
ignored about their peace and prosperity. It is unfortunate that the union budget is
silent about the need to implement the recommendations of the Dr. M.S.
Swaminathan Committee for the welfare of farming community and the need to
provide remunerative prices to farmers for their agriculture produce in order to save
them from abject poverty and miserable conditions, leading to suicides.
Out of the total allocation to the Ministry, 92% is estimated to be spent by the
Department of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare while the remaining is estimated
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1152
to be spent by the Department of Agricultural Research and Education.
The Department of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare spends 77% of its budget on
three major schemes: PM-Kisan Samman Nidhi (direct cash transfer of Rs 6,000
per year per farmer family). Modified Interest Subvention Scheme, and Crop
Insurance Scheme.
The Economic Survey (2023-24) also noted that several issues affect the growth in
the agriculture sector. These include:
Fragmented land holdings.
Low productivity of agriculture,
Insufficient access to quality inputs,
Inadequate access to marketing infrastructure,
High Dependency on rains and poor irrigation infrastructure,
Lack of farm mechanisation, and
Poor access to farm investment mechanism
It is indeed a great achievement of the farmers of the country whose untiring
hard labour has showed the world that farmers feed the people of all walks of
life. It's true that many countries have experienced scarcity of food grains and
faced starvation during the Corona crisis. But we in India have ensured that
nobody remained hungry during the worst pandemic in 100 years. The credit
goes to our Farmers and sons of the soil.
Uzhudhuntu Vaazhvaare Vaazhvaarmar Rellaam
Thozhudhuntu Pinsel Pavar
Which means, they alone live who live by agriculture; all others lead a cringing,
21.03.2025 1153
dependent life.

It is very unfortunate that the incidence of death by suicide by farmers and farm
workers is on the rise. More than one lakh farmers have committed suicide in the

In Tamil Nadu our Beloved leader Dr. Kalaignar. M. Karunanidhi has announced
free electricity for the farmer's in the year 1989. It is still continues in Tamil Nadu
under the dynamic leadership of Hon'ble Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu Thalapathy

implemented.
India is a global agricultural powerhouse, leading in milk, pulses, and spices
production, and also ranks second in rice, wheat, cotton, sugarcane, and other key
crops, with a large portion of its land cultivated, though much of it is rain-fed.
India has a total geographical area of 328.7 million hectares, with around 54.8%
21.03.2025 1154
used for agricultural land. About 141 million hectares is the reported net sown area,
and 219.158 million hectares is the gross cropped area. The net irrigated area
during 2021-22 was 77.9 million hectares, with tube wells being the primary source
of irrigation. Around 63% of the cultivated land is rainfed (roughly 125 million
hectares), while 37% is irrigated (70 million hectares).
India is the world's largest producer of milk, pulses, and spices, and second-largest
producer of rice, wheat, cotton, sugarcane, farmed fish, sheep & goat meat, fruit,
vegetables, and tea.
According to 2022-23 Agriculture data, India's foodgrain production had touched
330.5 million metric tons and horticulture output hit a record 351.92 million tons.
India's agricultural exports have seen significant growth, increasing from USD
22.70 billion in 2013-14 to USD 48.15 billion in 2023-24.
Leading agricultural exports include marine products, basmati and non-basmati
rice, spices, buffalo meat, sugar, and miscellaneous processed items. The USA,
China, UAE, and Saudi Arabia are major importers of India's agricultural products.
Marine products exports were valued at over 610 billion Indian rupees in FY24. In
April-July 2024, the overall value of agricultural exports stood at US$ 15.76 billion.
During FY24, India exported 19,678 metric tonnes of floriculture products, earning
Rs.717.83 crore (USD 86.63 million). India is also a leading exporter of fresh
grapes, shipping 343,982.34 MT worth Rs.3,460.70 crore (USD 417.07 million)
globally in 2023-24. But the farmers and farm workers agro based people are
penalized by the union Government. They suffer silently in penury and agony.
Agriculture employs more than 50% of the Indian workforce. The economic
21.03.2025 1155
contribution of agriculture to India's GDP is steadily declining with the country's
broad-based economic growth. Rice and wheat are major food grains, accounting
for a large portion of the total food grain production. Sugarcane, cotton, groundnuts,
and fruits and vegetables are also significant crops. India is a leading producer of
milk, with a large cattle population, and is also a significant exporter of beef and
veal.
Taking the average household size of marginal farmers to be 5.7, the annual per
capita income from crop cultivation is extremely low at Rs. 4308, and the monthly
per capita income at Rs. 359. This is way below the poverty line set by the
Ranganathan Committee report (2014) at Rs. 972 per month per capita, i.e., Rs.
11,664 per capita annually. As expected, the per capita income increases with an
increase in landholding size. The per capita income earned from crop production
is higher at Rs 71670, for the farmers with large landholding sizes which is 14.5
times more than the marginal farmers. The per capita income share from crop
production as a proportion to the per capita income from all sources also increases
with the increase in land holding size which suggests that in the case of marginal
and small farmers income from crop produces not being a viable option these
farmers largely depend on wage labour for their sustenance.
In India, the average income of agricultural households from farming has been
growing, albeit at a slow pace. This may be explained by a much higher increase
in the input cost that barely corresponds with an increase in the price of output.
Natural calamities, erratic rainfall and temperature further add to farmers' woes.
Farmers have no other option but to resort to other sources viz. livestock, wage
21.03.2025 1156
labour and non-farm business for their sustenance and risk mitigation. The situation
is quite vulnerable for the marginal (less <1 hectare) and small farmers (1-2 hectare
of land) as their income is way behind that of the medium and large farmers. Nearly
86% of the land holdings in India are about two hectares, of which a sizeable
number constitutes less than one hectare.
In India, the average income of agricultural households from farming has been
growing, albeit at a slow pace. This may be explained by a much higher increase
in the input cost that barely corresponds with an increase in the price of output.
Natural calamities, erratic rainfall and temperature further add to farmers' woes.

number constitutes less than one hectare.
Equally important is to bring higher efficiency in the existing marketing channels,
including government procurement centres, the opening up of FPOs, cooperatives
and other value chains so that farmers are able to get remunerative prices for their
produce.
As mentioned above, poor farmers cannot remain dependent only on farming as a
source of livelihood to meet their consumption and other expenditures.
Livestock appears to be the preferred activity, perhaps at the cost of farming.
Extending credit, livestock insurance and extension services can help farmers and,
21.03.2025 1157
also their women to take advantage of the growing opportunities in the dairy sector.
Higher public investment is required in research on productivity enhancing
technology with due consideration of mitigating greenhouse gas emissions,
opening of veterinary clinics/hospitals, vaccination set-up centres and automation
systems.Continued efforts are needed to train the rural youth to increase
employment intensity in rural manufacturing and tertiary sectors, especially in agro
and food processing. Strengthening small-scale rural industries with adequate
financial incentive structures can also be helpful to absorb labour.
The marginal farmers may not be efficient in the use of inputs and hence require
considerable handholding of the government. Though some of the sampled
marginal farmers are aware of government facilities for the purchase of inputs, sale
of produce to procurement centres/agencies and scheme of direct cash transfers,
the level of utilization of these facilities/programmes by them is very low. It is
important to increase awareness about the flagship programmes.
Government may initiate a differential income support scheme for marginal farmers
that provides them higher support. Such a targeting is possible using the database
of PM Kisan and JAM trinity.
A faster updation of land records by each state can be useful in better targeting of
direct support. Government should issue a farmer card to women farmers and
labourers to enable them to avail the benefits under various schemes.
The pace of public investment in canal irrigation, research, extension, and rural
infrastructure should be continued to incentivize farmers to make investments. Due
to increasing migration of male members in the family, women tend to look after
21.03.2025 1158
farming. Investment support for machinery/farm implements and opening of app
based custom hiring centres can improve mechanization and enable them to grow
diverse crops, augment yield and generate surplus.
In a vast country like our India, while some places are severely affected by scarcity
of rainfall and drought, some places are affected by excess rainfall and flooding.
While parts of Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Gujarat and Rajasthan are affected
by severe drought due to shortage of rainfall during the crop seasons, the states of
Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Odisha and west Bengal are affected severely by
cyclones and floods. In both the instances the farmers are most affected as their
crops are damaged completely. Hence the Farmers are in the clutches of either a
severe flood or drought. This is a very bad situation for farmers of this country. It
not only affects their crops but also make them suffer most to repay their loans
resulting in the suicides of farmers in the country.
Agriculture and agro based industries are the back bone of our country. Farmers
and Agro-based allied workers constitute about 60% of our total population.
Braving most difficult problems our farmers continue to toil hard to fulfill our farm
needs. Their service and sacrifices has to be appreciated appropriately for them to
engage continuously practice agriculture. Farmers need to be encouraged with
financial support as well as ensuring them with adequate supply of seeds.
fertilizers, insecticides & pesticides farm equipments etc.
Farmers should be encouraged with good reasonable Minimum Support Price
(MSP) for their produce. There are hundreds of crops raised by our farmers, but
MSP is not fixed for all the farm produce. The government needs to pay more focus
21.03.2025 1159
and attention in this area.
The Government should also instill confidence in their minds by effecting good
procurement policies. Without these two vital actions farmers will be reluctant to
take up agriculture and will affect our agrarian economy and agriculture will decline
fast. As a result we will be dependent on imported goods from foreign countries,
which again increase the price.
More than 55% of Indians make a living from farming, with agriculture being the
largest source of livelihoods in the country. In 2024, agriculture accounted for 23%
of India's GDP and employed 62% of the country's total workforce.
A large proportion of farmers in India are small and marginal farmers, with around
86% having less than one hectare of land. A significant portion of India's population
lives in rural areas, with 65% of the country's population living in rural areas. About
two-thirds of India's population depends on agriculture.

of robbing Peter to pay Paul must be stopped. Otherwise there will be unrest in the
country in as far as the revenue sharing.

SHRI RAJABHAU PARAG PRAKASH WAJE (NASHIK): Agriculture is the
backbone of our nation, and the sweat and toil of our farmers sustain not just our
economy but the very lives of our people. Yet, they continue to face numerous
challenges that demand urgent intervention from this Government.
Fair Prices and Market Reforms: One of the major challenges our
farmers face is the issue of fair pricing. While they put in immense effort in
cultivating crops, the returns they receive are often insufficient. The implementation
of Minimum Support Price (MSP) for all major crops needs to be strengthened, and
procurement mechanisms must be streamlined to ensure farmers get fair
compensation for their produce. The delay in payments under procurement
schemes must also be addressed to protect their financial security.
Irrigation and Water Conservation: Water scarcity remains a persistent issue in
Nashik and many other parts of India. There is a dire need to expand irrigation
facilities and promote sustainable water management techniques, such as micro-
irrigation, watershed management, and efficient canal systems.
Crop Insurance and Financial Assistance: the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana
(PMFBY) has been a significant step in securing farmers against losses. However,
there have been grievances regarding delays in insurance claim settlements and
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1161
discrepancies in the compensation for our farmers. Special schemes should be
designed to provide timely financial support with lower interest rates, reducing their
dependency on informal lenders who exploit them.
Strengthening Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs): Farmer Producer
Organizations (FPOs) have been instrumental in improving the bargaining power
of small farmers. However, they need greater financial support, skill development
programs, and market linkages.
Control Over Prices of Pesticides, Seeds, and Fertilizers: Another major concern
is the increasing prices of essential agricultural inputs such as pesticides, seeds,
and fertilizers, which are often dictated by private companies. These rising costs
put an additional financial burden on our farmers, reducing their profitability.
Additionally, strict monitoring should be in place to prevent hoarding and artificial
price hikes by companies.
Problem of Farmers in my Constituency: Additionally, I would like to bring to the
attention of this House the urgent need to remove the 20% export duty on onions
to address falling domestic prices, surplus production, and rising global
competition. Onion prices have dropped to 15-20 per kg due to peak arrivals from
Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Rajasthan, and Bihar, with further
declines expected.
Competing nations like Pakistan and China are offering onions at $125 per ton
cheaper, capturing markets in Far East Asia and the Gulf nations, which have
traditionally been dominated by Indian exporters. Even Bangladesh has reduced
imports from India, and the continuation of the export duty since December 2024
21.03.2025 1162
has further weakened India's position, impacting farmers and exporters.

SHRI SACHITHANANTHAM R. (DINDIGUL): On behalf of CPI (M) I express my
views in Demands for Grants in agriculture and farmer welfare 2025-2026.
 Farmers are the backbone of India infect they are our Annadatas. Agriculture
in the largest livelihood provider in India with an estimated 93.09 million
households as agricultural and 90 and 150 million Indians are farmers.
 But in 2025-2026 Rs. 1,37,757 crores have been allocated to the ministry
This is simply 2.7% of the union budget.
 Out of total allocation to the ministry 92% is estimated to spent by to
(1,27,290 cores) dept of agriculture and farmers welfare and to rest 8% to
be agriculture research and education this equation is improper.
 In 1,27,290crores 77% i.e. 98,013 cores spends for Three schemes.
(i) PM Kisan (ii) Crop insurance (iii) Crop loan interest subvention.
 The small and marginal farmers are facing challenges to get these benefits.
 To generate income and create employment opportunities for small and
marginal farmer's union govt launched a scheme called formation and
promotion of new farmers producers organisations. This scheme was totally
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1166
failure scheme nothing has been done by this.
 Out of the total income of an agricultural house hold, 54% was income from
wages, only 22% from production yield and 17% from farming animals in
Tamilnadu. Indian average is 40: 37:15. Agricultural income of 10 states are
below the average.
 Access to quality of seeds is measured by the seed replacement rate (SRR)
It is a measure of total cropped area which is covered with certified seeds.
Niti Aayog had noted that to SRR is India remains low due to a demand
supply mismatch and low production. Out of the Total availability of seeds
26% was supplied by the public sector as compared to the 74% distribution
but ion through private distributers. Public sector seed production to be
raised to 50% so that the price of seed will be in the control.
 In India about half of to agricultural area under crops is completely
dependent on rainfall.
 Climate change which affects the rainfall some time is may be high. Some
time it may very low. We cannot store the rain water while rainfall is high. We
need more reservoirs. Constructing dams and reservoirs are not done in
recent periods. But it is very essential for the irrigation. We have to allocate
funds for construction dams and reservoirs as well as for micro irrigation
system.
 Agricultural loan waving scheme is now needed. Because as per NCRB
Report 1,17000 farmers committed suicide in the past 10 years. So please
21.03.2025 1167
give provision of funds for waiving of loans to the small and marginal farmers.
 Please implement Dr. MS Swaminathan Committee recommendations on
MSP in C2+50 formula. That only give benefits in the farmer's income.

योजनाए ँचलाई गई है और िनि(cid:306)त(cid:322)प से हमार े िकसानो को इसका लाभ भी िमल रहा है इन योजनाओ
का उ(cid:293)े(cid:212)य िकसान(cid:332) क(cid:236) समिृ (cid:294) सिु नि(cid:306)त करना है ।
(cid:255)धानमं(cid:253)ी िकसान स(cid:204)मान िनिध (PM-KISAN) हमारी सरकार क(cid:236) एक (cid:255)मुख योजना है, इस योजना
को 1 फरवरी 2019 को लॉ(cid:198)च िकया गया था । इस योजना का उ(cid:293)े(cid:212)य दशे के छोटे और सीमांत िकसान(cid:332)
को आिथ(cid:170)क सहायता (cid:255)दान करना है ।

इस योजना के तहत िकसान(cid:332) को (cid:255)ितवष(cid:170) ₹6,000 क(cid:236) िव(cid:176)ीय सहायता दी जाती है जो ₹2,000 क(cid:236)
तीन िक(cid:214)त(cid:332) म(cid:164) सीधे उनके ब(cid:167)क खाते म(cid:164) भेजी जाती है । इस योजना का लाभ सभी भूिमधारक िकसान
प(cid:229)रवार(cid:332) को िदया जाता है, चाहे उनके पास िकतनी भी भूिम हो ।
कृिष और संब(cid:294) (cid:177)े(cid:253)(cid:332) के िलए भारत सरकार (cid:300)ारा संचािलत एक संशोिधत (cid:202)याज अनदु ान योजना है,

2023-24 से 2025-26 तक िव(cid:214)ता(cid:229)रत िकया है और इसके िलए ₹34,856 करोड़ का बजट आवंिटत
िकया गया है ।

दर 4% रह जाती है ।

SHRI ANUP SANJAY DHOTRE (AKOLA): I would like to express my views on
the supplementary demand for grants for the Ministry of Agriculture.
First, I would like to our Honorable Prime Minister, Shri Narendra Modiji, for his
vision, which has changed the country in the last ten years. All ministries are
developing the country like never before. And now, even Akola, Maharashtra, is
witnessing development with our double-engine government.
I would like to thank our Honorable Minister of Agriculture for allocating additional
funds for various schemes and programs aimed at ensuring agricultural
development and farmers' welfare.
The focus of our minister to provide support to farmers through schemes like the
Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) and the Pradhan Mantri Fasal
Bima Yojana (PMFBY) is commendable. Under PM-KISAN, ₹ 75,000 crores have
been allocated to provide financial assistance of ₹6,000 per year to eligible farmers,
benefiting over 14 crore farmers across the country.
Our ministry's emphasis on improving agricultural infrastructure and promoting
farmers' welfare through initiatives like the Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana (RKVY)
is laudable. Under RKVY, ₹10,000 crores have been allocated to promote
agricultural research and development, and to improve agricultural productivity and
quality.
The budget allocation for Agriculture in 2025-26 is ₹1,32,513 crores, which is higher
than the previous year’s allocation. This allocation is divided between the following
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1194
departments:
- Department of Agriculture, Cooperation and Farmers Welfare: ₹94,044
crores
- Department of Agricultural Research and Education: ₹8,500 crores
- Department of Animal Husbandry and Dairying: ₹4,300 crores
I would like to appreciate the government’s efforts in:
- Increasing the Minimum Support Price (MSP) to 3-7% for crops, benefiting
farmers and ensuring fair prices for their produce.
- Implementing the Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana(PMKSY), which
aims to improve irrigation facilities and promote water conservation in the
agricultural sector.
- Promoting organic farming and reducing the use of chemical fertilizers
through initiatives like the Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana (PKVY).
- Strengthening the agricultural research and development ecosystem
through initiatives like the National Agricultural Research System (NARS).
I would like to make a request to our Honorable Minister for my constituency of
Akola, Maharashtra. Sir, Akola is a major agricultural hub, and we are hoping to
become the number one agricultural district in Maharashtra with your help.
The following projects require immediate attention:
1. Akola Irrigation Project: This project has the potential to irrigate 50,000 hectares
of land, benefiting farmers in Akola and surrounding districts.
2. Watershed Development Project: This project aims to improve water
conservation and reduce soil erosion in Akola, benefiting farmers and the
21.03.2025 1195
environment. The estimated cost of the project is ₹200 crores.
3. Agricultural Research and Development Center: Establishing a research center
in Akola will provide farmers with access to cutting-edge technology and expertise,
improving agricultural productivity and quality.
4. Farmers' Welfare Scheme: Implementing a comprehensive welfare scheme for
farmers in Akola will provide them with financial support, insurance, and other
benefits.
5. Agricultural Infrastructure Development Fund: Allocating funds for agricultural
infrastructure development in Akola will improve storage facilities, markets, and
other infrastructure, benefiting farmers and the agricultural economy.
To conclude my speech, I must again thank our Honorable Minister for the great
work being done in the Agriculture Ministry and also request our Honorable Minister
to please look into the demands of the people of my Akola constituency.

SHRI OMPRAKASH BHUPALSINH ALIAS PAVAN RAJENIMBALKAR
(OSMANABAD):.
For Kharif Season 2024-25, Soybeans were purchased from the farmers
under the Government’s by guarantee Nafed and NCCF, for which the farmers
registered online. Regarding extending the deadline for buying soybeans from the
farmers, I met the Hon'ble Minister of Agriculture on 04/02/2025 and requested to
extend the deadline till March 31. 44013 farmers of my Lok Sabha constituency
had registered to sell soybeans with guaranteed price, out of which 15749 farmers
 English translation of the speech originally laid on theTable in Marathi.
21.03.2025 1196
could sell soybeans out of the remaining registered 28, 264 farmers. The purchase
was not made due to the expiry of the extended period. In the early days, due to
technical problems in the government's website, purchases were stopped for many
days. Due to the fact that the purchase center did not have the sacks available, so
the soybeans of the farmers could not be purchased in large quantities. The
difference between the value of soybeans in the current market and the base price
of the government should be credited to the farmers’ account in the form of subsidy
to all the registered farmers. In reference to the cancellation of the circular dated
30th April 2024 regarding Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana, I met the hon.
Minister of Agriculture and requested him to cancel the unjust circular of 30th April,
but no action has been taken in this regard yet. Due to this circular, 32 revenue
boards out of 52 revenue boards in the Dharashiv district, were excluded due to
receipt of advance notices. For last year's insurance of Rs 297 crores, 25 per cent
more advance notices were served to 57 out of 57 revenue boards this year, and
government circular of April 30 came into effect. If the circular is cancelled, the
farmers would have got an insurance of Rs. 25 thousand 678 per hectare, as this
circular is causing great injustice to the farmers. So, the said circular should be
cancelled immediately. The insurance rights of farmers should be restored and the
reimbursement should be credited to farmers’ accounts.
All the farmers whose land has been transferred after the death of their father
through inheritance after the year 2019 or who have become farmers after
purchase should be included in the Pradhan Mantri Krishi Samman Yojana and the
remuneration given under this scheme should be increased and credited directly to
21.03.2025 1197
the account of the farmers. Thank you.

म(cid:167) पालघर िजले का (cid:255)ितिनिध(cid:194)व करता ह(cid:243),ँ जो एक आिदवासी बह(cid:242)ल (cid:177)े(cid:253) है और यहा ँ के िकसान(cid:332) को
अभी भी कई चुनौितय(cid:332) का सामना करना पड़ रहा ह ै । अब तक जल शि(cid:265) अिभयान के तहत पालघर
के कई गावँ (cid:332) म(cid:164) माइ(cid:248)ो इरीगेशन िस(cid:214)टम लगाए गए ह(cid:167), िजससे िकसान(cid:332) को जल सर(cid:177)ण म(cid:164) मदद िमली
है । कृिष उपज िवपणन सिमित (APMC) को िडिजटल िकया गया है, िजससे िकसान(cid:332) को उनक(cid:236) फसल
का बेहतर दाम िमल रहा है । साथ ही, आिदवासी िकसान(cid:332) को बांस और औषधीय पौध(cid:332) क(cid:236) खेती के


SHRI RAO RAJENDRA SINGH (JAIPUR RURAL): I would firstly like to express
our sincere commendation to the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare for the
outstanding achievements it has made under the able stewardship of Hon. PM Shri
Narendra Modi Ji. I seek to address to you a few matters of immense public
importance that pertain with the long-term policy goals of the Ministry.
Employing over 60% of the Indian population, Agriculture still remains a key facet
of the Indian Economy and Society. While the contribution of Agriculture to our
economy may have declined post-liberalisation due the phenomenal rise of our
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1204
industry and services sector, maintaining food security and ensuring farmers'
welfare is extremely important if we are to ensure equitable and all round growth.
As per-hectare yields dwindle and more people gravitate towards other
"aspirational" white collar jobs due to their lucrative nature, it is important that we
as a country do not ignore what constitutes the base of our economy. Food is the
most basic need of mankind. We may establish all the great bridges and railway
lines, make the loftiest of malls in our lively urban centres, develop the most modern
of missile systems in our indigenous defence labs, send the most modern satellites
on unprecedented explorations, we are doing that and we should do that, however
we must surely not forget our most primate of requirements, which is adequate
food. Under this government, our country has taken major strides in ensuring food
security for the most marginalized sections of the society, even during an
unprecedented tragedy such as COVID-19, which hugely impacted the world, in
some cases beyond repair.
Since the last 6 years, Agriculture has been growing at a 5% which is considered
to be outstanding, by its standards. High-value sectors like Horticulture, Livestock
and Fisheries have become key drivers of the overall economic growth of the
nation. In the allied sector, the Fisheries sector has shown a phenomenal CAGR
of 8.7%, followed by an 8% CAGR in the livestock sector. India currently ranks 2nd
largest globally in fish production. Our total fish production has almost doubled as
compared to FY14 and our seafood exports have risen by almost 1.5 times in the
last 5 years. Kharif Foodgrain production is expected to reach 1647.05 LMT, an
increase of 89.37 LMT from the previous year. This house shall be delighted to
21.03.2025 1205
note that according to the Economic Survey 2024-25, the coverage of area under
irrigation has increased between FY16 and FY21 FROM 49.3% to 55.3% of the
Gross Cropped Area (GCA), while irrigation intensity has risen from 144.2% to
154.5% which is a laudable achievement. Our overall foodgrain production has
risen by about 10% in the last 3 years. It must be noted that all of this has been
achieved in a world where, besides geopolitical uncertainties, there has also been
a consistent rise in adverse ecological mishaps. According to the Economic Survey
2024-25, our country has been seeing an increase in the frequency of dry spells
during the summer monsoon which have been 27% more common the 30 year
period from 1981-2011 as compared to 1951-1980. In Central India, the frequency
of extreme daily rainfall events (exceeding 150 mm) has increased by about 75%
from 1950 to 2015.
As we discuss the workings of this Ministry, I would especially like to highlight the
initiatives by the Ministry that have greatly benefited farmers across the country.
Some of the most notable achievements include:
Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) Was Launched in 2019, this
direct income support scheme has provided financial assistance of ₹6,000 annually
to small and marginal farmers. This initiative has provided crucial support to millions
of farmers, especially during times of distress. As of 31st October 2024, 23.61 lakh
farmers have been enrolled under the PM Kisan Maandhan. As of August 2024,
Rs 3.24 lakh crore have been transferred to about 12.85 crore beneficiaries.
PM Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) crop insurance scheme, aimed at providing
financial protection to farmers against crop losses due to natural calamities, has
21.03.2025 1206
been a game-changer. The scheme has provided timely support to farmers facing
the aftermath of floods, droughts, and other natural disasters. With 56.96 crore
applications insured since its inception in 2016-17, the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima
Yojana (PMFBY) has demonstrated substantial impact, paying out Rs 1,54,469
crore in claims. This remarkable reach and financial assistance highlight the
scheme's vital role in supporting farmers during challenging times. By offering a
comprehensive and accessible insurance product, PMFBY empowers farmers,
improves agricultural practices, and contributes to the overall growth and stability
of the Indian agricultural sector.
National Agriculture Market (e-NAM) digital platform has provided farmers with
greater access to markets, reducing middlemen and ensuring better prices for their
produce. Rajasthan, particularly Jaipur, has seen a positive impact from e-NAM,
with enhanced access to national markets for its farmers. According to the
Economic Survey, As of 31st October 2024, over 1.78 crore farmers and 2.62 lakh
traders have registered on the e-NAM portal. As of March 2023, a total of 1,361
mandis were also integrated with the e-NAM platform. 91 According to the
Economic Survey (2023-24), farmers surveyed reported receiving higher prices for
their produce after implementation of the scheme. About 54% also preferred
transacting through e-NAM portals over the traditional market due to transparent
procedures and other benefits. The Standing Committee on Agriculture (2019)
recommended that the government expand the coverage of the e-NAM scheme in
states where APMC markets are non-existent. The Committee also recommended
that the government undertake training programmes to enhance digital literacy
21.03.2025 1207
among farmers and improve their participation in the scheme.
The government has implemented extensive soil health management schemes,
and the Soil Health Card Scheme, which helps farmers understand the condition
of their soil, leading to more efficient use of fertilizers. This has helped improve crop
yields and reduce the overuse of fertilizers, thus protecting the environment.
Fertiliser subsidy is estimated to constitute 3.3% of the total budget of the Union
government in 2025-26. Given India's import dependence in the domain, the
government has ensured timely interventions during times of crisis in the fertiliser
market in order to shield the farmer of any such ill-effects, a good example of the
same came in April 2024, when the Cabinet approved a one-time special package
on Di- Ammonium Phosphate (DAP) fertiliser (a grade of P&K fertiliser) beyond the
Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) scheme which was extended in January 2025 in
order to contain the surging prices of DAP.
The Union Budget 2025-26 reiterated the importance ascribed by the Modi
Government to Agriculture. This budget describes Agriculture as the 1st Engine of
development. The Prime Minister Dhan-Dhaanya Krishi Yojana has been launched
in partnership with the states, covering 100 districts with low productivity, moderate
crop intensity and below-average credit parameters, to benefit 1.7 crore farmers.
The Government is also launching a 6-year "Mission for Atma Nirbharta in Pulses"
with focus on Tur, Urad and Masoor. A National Mission on High Yielding Seeds is
also being launched aiming at strengthening the research ecosystem, targeted
development and propagation of seeds with high yield, and commercial availability
of more than 100 seed varieties. A 5-year mission has been announced to facilitate
21.03.2025 1208
significant improvements in productivity and sustainability of cotton farming, and to
promote extra-long staple cotton varieties. This mission shall also help address the
issue of rising cotton imports in the country and pave the way for enhanced exports
of cotton. By enhancing the loan limit under the Modified Interest Subvention
Scheme from ₹ 3 lakh to ₹ 5 lakh, for loans taken through the KCC, the Government
has ensured greater access to credit for the farmer.
While the Ministry's efforts have had a far-reaching impact, it is important to
recognize the specific challenges faced by farmers in Rajasthan, my home state,
which is unique due to its geographical and climatic conditions:
Rajasthan is one of the driest states in India, and water scarcity is a major concern
for our farmers. The state is highly susceptible to droughts, which impact
agriculture, livelihoods, and drinking water availability. The per-capita water
availability in Rajasthan is even lesser and considered to be about 780 cubic
metres. The state's dependence on rainfall is highly unreliable, and thus, solutions
for water conservation and management are critical. Many areas in the state are
dependent on rain-fed agriculture, and this unpredictability often leads to crop
failure. This, compounded by the fact that water quality in the state is deeply
affected due to presence of substances like uranium, makes the existing water
resources sparsely employable. In states like Punjab, over extraction of
groundwater resources is directly proportional to the electricity subsidies provided
to farmers which removes any disincentives to conserve electricity.
This leads to depletion of state finances and water resources at the same time.
This strategy, adopted by a few states, deserves a hard reconsideration if we are
21.03.2025 1209
to effectively deal with the issue of water scarcity and ensure sustainable
development for our people. In order to effectively deal with the issue, since the
FY16 fiscal year, the government has been implementing the Per Drop More Crop
(PDMC) initiative, a component of the Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana
(PMKSY), to promote water efficiency. From FY16 to FY25 (end of Dec. 2024), ₹
21968.75 crore was released to states for implementation of PDMC Scheme and
an area of 95.58 lakh ha has been covered under which is about 104.67 per cent
higher as compared to the Pre- PDMC period.
While schemes like PM-KISAN and PMFBY are beneficial, water availability
continues to be a fundamental issue affecting productivity.
In 2022-23, 42% of the total cropped area was cultivated with water intensive crops
such as paddy, wheat, and sugarcane. In Rajasthan, the agricultural economy is
still heavily dependent on wheat and mustard crops, leaving farmers vulnerable to
market fluctuations. The lack of diversification into high-value crops or modern
agricultural practices limits the income potential of many farmers.
Under the Pradhan Mantri Kisan Urja Suraksha Evan Utthaan Mahabhiyan Yojana
(PM-KUSUM), the central government offers subsidies of up to 30% or 50% of the
total cost for installation of solar agricultural pumps. As of March 2021, five states
are offering free electricity to farmers with other states offering electricity to farmers
at subsidised rates. These states include Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Punjab,
Tamil Nadu, and Telangana. In 2018, NABARD had noted that in certain states
such as Andhra Pradesh, Punjab, Haryana and Tamil Nadu, access to highly
subsidised or free canal water has led to over-irrigation of crops. This has led to
21.03.2025 1210
wasteful use of water and energy needs in certain regions along with deprivation
of the same in certain other regions.
As the Economic Survey of 2021-22 also rightly pointed out, there is an urgent need
for crop diversification in view of the severe water stress in areas where paddy,
wheat and sugarcane are grown. This is not just because of the water-intensive
nature of such crops but also because of a consistent change in social attitudes.
Between 2011-12 and 2023-24, there has been a decrease in the share of monthly
per capita consumption expenditure (MPCE) spent on food items in both rural and
urban areas. The share of cereals in total MPCE for rural areas decreased from
10.8% in 2011-12 to 5% in 2023-24 while that for urban areas decreased from 6.7%
to 3.8% in the same period. On the other hand, the share of food items like milk
and milk products and fruits have increased in the total MPCE. A greater
procurement of wheat and paddy by the Government implies a greater share of the
same being distributed under Pradhan Mantri Gareeb Kalyan Anna Yojana
(PMGKAY). This makes a large share of the grain distributed under PDS,
vulnerable to black marketing or being wasted. Given that for a large part of the
country, a bare minimum has been ensured with the PMGKAY, it is also our
responsibility to ensure that nutritious food is available to all people and there is
considerable incentive to the farmer to give up the cultivation of water intensive
crops, especially in states like Rajasthan and Punjab.
With the gradual overuse of pesticides, limited use of modern technologies and
modern farming equipment, many farmers across the country, as in Rajasthan, are
facing the problem of low yields. This gap affects their income levels and increases
21.03.2025 1211
their vulnerability to external shocks like drought or pest outbreaks. According to
the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO), India ranks among the top countries
in terms of acreage under cultivation of cereals, rice, and pulses. However, our
output per hectare in India is much lower than the global average, especially for
crops like wheat, maize and paddy which constitute most of our cultivation. Low
agricultural yields translate into low and insecure incomes for farmers and large
tracts of agricultural land being locked into low-value agriculture.
In order to improve output yield concerted attempts must be made by reducing
staple crop area under cultivation, diversifying crops under cultivation and
improving resource use efficiency.
In Rajasthan, many farmers have small, fragmented land holdings, making it
difficult to adopt modern agricultural practices or to achieve economies of scale.
This affects productivity and makes it difficult for farmers to access financing or
take part in large-scale farming ventures. In 2006, the National Commission on
Farmers' (Chaired by Shri. M.S. Swaminathan) noted that fragmented and
scattered operational holdings are a major challenge to agricultural productivity. In
2015-16, 86% of the total operational holdings in the country were less than two
hectares. Small scale farmers face a profound lack of access to credit, logistics,
markets and technology. The Committee on Doubling Farmers Income (DFI) noted
that land fragmentation is also associated with the division of land and sharing the
parcels among inheritors. One of the solutions recommended by the DFI
Committee has been to aggregate farmers and promote land pooling to target
fragmentation of land holdings. Structures such as Farmer Producer Organisations
21.03.2025 1212
(FPOs) and Self-Help Groups (SHGs) offer farmers the advantages of larger
economies of scale throughout the agriculture value chain. Collectivization leads to
higher collective output, increased land productivity, and higher bargaining power
for each farmer-member.
The government launched the Formation and Promotion of 10,000 FPOS scheme
in 2020 with a total outlay of Rs 6,865 crore. The scheme aims to enhance
agricultural productivity through efficient, cost-effective, and sustainable resource
usage. As of August 2024, 8,875 FPOs and 19.7 lakh shareholder farmers have
been registered across the country. In 2025-26, the government has allocated Rs
584 crore to the FPO scheme, which is similar to the revised estimate of 2024-25.
Under the scheme, FPOs are provided financial assistance of up to Rs 18 lakh per
FPO for a period of three years.
For horticultural commodities, if there is a 10% increase in production or 10% fall
in prices in comparison to last year's normal prices then the Market Intervention
Scheme (MIS) is implemented. In the scheme, there is provision for procurement
of 20% of total production on cost of cultivation finalized by Government of India.
As per scheme, the loss up to the extent of 25% of procurement cost is to be shared
proportionately by Central and State Government. It is to mention that if the proper
price is not realized by the farmer, then the actual loss incurred is much greater
than the 25% limit. The probable reasons of this may be:
The horticultural commodities viz. garlic and onion are perishable in nature and
these commodities could not be stored for longer duration.
The Government of India does not announce the MSP for horticultural commodities
21.03.2025 1213
while the price of agricultural commodities, which fall under MSP, have been raised
1.5 times in previous years. However, no amendments are made in MSP for the
horticultural commodities.
Therefore, following amendments are proposed in Market Intervention Scheme:
In place of procurement cost, the calculation should be done on the net loss
incurred by purchase & sale of commodities.
The loss up to the extent of 50% to be shared proportionately between Central and
State Government.
Pulses can be a better source of protein as compared to cereals and can help
improve nutrition intake for NFSA beneficiaries. Under the first phase of the
PMGKAY between April to June 2020, the central government distributed one kg
of pulses per household per month free of cost for NFSA beneficiaries. Between
2013-14 and 2023-24, the domestic production of pulses has increased at an
annual rate of 2.3%, similar to the increase in the total production of foodgrains.
However, the production of pulses has been characterised by significant variability.
For instance, in 2016-17, production of pulses increased by 42% over 2015-16, but
in 2018-19, it decreased by 13% over 2017-18. Moreover, India continues to be
import dependent for meeting its domestic needs for consumption of pulses. India's
import dependency for pulses in 2020-21 was 9% which is estimated to decrease
to 3.6% by 2030-31. In 2023-24, India's production of pulses decreased by 7% to
242 lakh tonnes compared to 2022-23 while imports almost doubled to 47 lakh
tonnes. Share of pulses has also declined in the Monthly Per Capita Expenditure
in 2011-12 (2.9%) to 2023-24 (2.0%), In order to address the aforementioned
21.03.2025 1214
issues, the Government has announced the Mission for Atma Nirbharta in Pulses
where NAFED will regularly and readily procure 3 pulses. This is a step in the right
direction so as pay heed to a significant issue.
while Rajasthan faces its unique set of challenges, the Ministry of Agriculture and
Farmers Welfare has made significant progress in transforming the agricultural
landscape. However, we must continue to focus on water conservation,
technological innovation, crop diversification, and ensuring equitable benefits for
all farmers.
The Union Budget 2025-26 ticks most of the boxes. It doesn’t shrug, core issues
in the sector, under the rug but actively addresses them. There is scope for
improvement, as there always is. But the fact that we’re moving in the right
direction, assures me that the sector shall hugely contribute to our collective
endeavor of Viksit Bharat by 2047.
Thank you.

pertaining to Ministry of Agricultre and Farmer's Welfare and its working. In 2025-
26, Rs 1,37,757 crore has been allocated to the Ministry (2.7% of the union budget).
The allocation towards the Ministry is estimated to decrease by 2.5% over the
revised estimates of 2024-25. According to the revised estimates of 2024-25, the
expenditure of the Ministry has increased by 6.8% over the budget estimate.
The Union Budget 2025-26, is comprehensively biased as the budget grossly
ignores the needs of Tamil Nadu and betrayed the expectations of the people,
therefore, the union budget is completely anti-poor, anti-women, anti-youth and
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1226
anti-farmer. The Union Budget is prejudiced as it completely ignored the demands
of Tamil Nadu, despite the fact the economic survey, ranking of higher Education
Institutions and the NITI Aayog Reports praised the State's activities. Needless to
say, reduction in central funding increase the financial burden on the State
Government.
I strongly demand from the Union Government for effective implementation of PM
Dhan Dhaanya Krishi Yojana in Tamil Nadu to cover all districts in the State
comprising of maximum number of farmers to enhance productivity, crop intensity,
and ensuring high credit access. Proper utilization of earmarked fund to the tune of
Rs. 100.00 crore in 2025-26 under National Mission on High Yielding Seeds must
focus on ensuring commercial availability of maximum number of seed varieties,
development and propagation of climate and pest resilient high yielding seeds, and
strengthening research on high yielding seed varieties. I further demand that
Farmers of Tamil Nadu must be benefited with loan limit under the Modified Interest
Subvention Scheme to be increased from three lakh rupees to five lakh rupees.
Keeping in view the fact there will be focus on Horticulture entailing a
comprehensive programme for the promotion of production, efficient supply,
processing, and remunerative prices for fruits and vegetables, the scheme benefit
the people of Tamil Nadu in particular. Adequate fund out of the total allocation of
Rs. 500.00 crore under the programme for 2025-26 be earmarked for the State of
Tamil Nadu so that there is maximum number of formation and participation of
Farmer Producer Organisations and Cooperatives across the State.
Self-sufficiency in pulses: A new Mission for Aatmanirbharta in
21.03.2025 1227
Pulses will be launched for a period of six years. The Mission will aim at achieving
self-sufficiency in pulses production through procurement of Tur, Urad, and
Masoor. An allocation of Rs 1,000 crore has been made for this scheme for 2025-
26.
A new urea plant may be established in Tiruvannamalai constitutency with
adequate infrastructure for production of sufficient quantum of urea to fulfil the
needs of the nearby States. There is an urgent need for promotion of agro based
industries in the State to promote export to global markets and exploring
employment generation in the agro based industry sector. I also urge the
Government to set up R&D Centre at Tiruvannamalai Parliamentary Constitutency.
I humbly appeal to the Finance Minister to earmark sufficient fund to build
marketing and cooperative infrastructure for commercial production of agricultural
items in Tamil Nadu in general and my constituency Tiruvannamalai in particular
for enhancing farmer’s income and for enhancing the contribution of State
Agriculture Sector in the GDP of the country.
Thank you.

म(cid:164) यह घटकर (cid:321) 20,500 करोड़ रह गया । 2022-23 म(cid:164) यह और कम होकर मा(cid:253) (cid:321) 16,400 करोड़ रह


SHRI S. SUPONGMEREN JAMIR (NAGALAND):
The field workers of the department should identify the genuine farmers for
allotment of farm tools.
The farmers are facing problems to avail the farm loans. Some banking institutions
should be identified specifically by the Ministry as designated Loan Banking
Institutions.
Gradually, the farmers are losing interest to cultivate because of lack of proper
facilities and this should be recognized by both the Centre and the States.
There should be an exchange programme for farmers for educational or to acquaint
them with the different means of productions. The Ministry should adopt policies
state wise to conduct exchange tour programmes for the respected farmers.
Rejuvenation for farmers is the need of hour. There is a need to bring a positive
policy where country farmers will have dignity as farmers of India.
*

SHRI V. K. SREEKANDAN (PALAKKAD): Respected Speaker Sir,I thank you
very much for allowing me to make a submission on the Demands for Grants under
the control of the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare.The people of
Kerala's main meal is rice and to meet this needs increased cultivation is required
in proportion to the growing population of the State.But what we are witnessing is
a declining trend in rice cultivation and production. This is the phenomenon across
the State. While the production of rice in wetland fell by 10.5% in 2023-24
compared to 2022-23, productivity and the area under cultivation fell by 4.9% and
5.9% respectively. When it comes to rice cultivation in the State of Kerala, the title
of rice bowl of Kerala is with the district of Palakkad, as rice cultivation is being
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1239
carried out in many thousands of hectares of land in the district. However, Palakkad
recorded the biggest production decline in the wetland category, say about 23%.
The paddy farmers are forced to quit farming due to many factors. One of the
reasons is cost escalation in all input materials used for farming as well as labour
charges. Palakkad is the district which is on top among the districts in Kerala that
has shown declining trend in farming. Another reason for showing less interest in
farming is because of delay in procuring their hard-earned produce and sometimes
paddy remained open in the field itself for a number of days, getting damaged due
to rain, etc. The wild animals damaging their standing crops is also a matter of
concern, leading to financial losses. The price that they are getting for their produce
is also not in proportion to the cost involved. Another and vital issue the farmers
are facing is pertaining to climatic conditions - unexpected rains and no rains.
Palakkad is not only a rice bowl of Kerala, but also producing many agricultural
produces. I have been demanding to provide a special package for the farmers of
Palakkad in this august house from time to time, say from the day one l became a
Member of Parliament from Palakkad parliamentary constituency way back in the
year 2019, and I demand the same again here in the interest of farmers belonging
to Palakkad district. The farmers of Palakkad not only produce paddy, but other
agricultural produces because the soil of Palakkad is such that it suits for all
agricultural activities. That is also one of the reasons why I demand for a special
agricultural package for the farmers in Palakkad district, so that farmers can
undertake cultivation in expanded areas.
The Government with much fanfare announced way back in the year 2016 to
21.03.2025 1240
double farmers income by 2022. Now we are in the midst of year 2025, we have
not seen any positive steps to increase the farmer’s income to double and whereas
the ground reality is that the farmers were not able to even recover the amount they
spent on farming. In many cases reported across the country the farmers were
forced to throw their hard-earned produce on the roads as a mark of protest for not
able to realize the cost of production and make repayment of bank loan took by
them for cultivation.
The legally guaranteed minimum support price for crops is the long standing
demand of framers across the country. The historic farmers strike near Delhi border
was also for the said cause. It is no doubt that the legally guaranteed minimum
support price for crops will address the burning issue of farmers suicides in the
country. Sir, during the last ten years the country witnessed a whopping number of
farmers suicides, it has been reported by the National Crime Record Bureau at
1,12,000. The rapid increase in suicide rates amongst farmers is importantly also
contributable to poor government policy towards the agricultural sector. The
amount given under the PM KISAN scheme needs to be increased from Rs. 6,000
to Rs.12,000 per annum as the current amount given by the government is not
sufficient for the purposes the scheme introduced and such seasonal incentives
may be extended to tenant farmers and farm labourers as well. The Government
should also introduce a comprehensive crop insurance plan for small holding
farmers.
The low budget allocations for key agriculture schemes are also a cause of
concern. The farmers in the country are struggling with the cost of urea and its
21.03.2025 1241
timely non-availability. The reduction in the allocation for the fertilizers is likely to
impact fertilizer subsidy. It is the need of the hour to produce more yield in lesser
areas. To achieve this our farmers should get quality seeds and fertilizers and the
most critical factor for farming is good seeds. Good quality seeds can boost
production by 20%. Therefore, the Government should study the recommendations
of the recently concluded National Seed Congress.
With this I conclude and oppose many aspects of the budget allocations.
Thank you.

SHRI KODIKUNNIL SURESH (MAVELIKKARA): I would like to highlight the
urgent need for policy and governance interventions to safeguard India's
agriculture sector, which remains the backbone of our economy. Despite its
fundamental role in ensuring food security and rural employment, agriculture is in
a deep crisis due to policy neglect, rising costs, climate vulnerabilities, and unfair
market conditions. Farmers continue to struggle with inadequate financial support,
exploitative trade practices, and the looming threat of climate change. This
discussion on the Demands for Grants is not just about financial allocations it is
about the survival and dignity of our farmers.
One of the biggest betrayals of this government has been its failure to enact a legal
guarantee for Minimum Support Price (MSP). Farmers fought historic protests
demanding fair prices, yet the government has not honored its commitment.
Procurement mechanisms remain weak, forcing many farmers to sell their produce
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1242
at prices lower than the declared MSP. The increasing corporate control over
agriculture is another alarming trend, where large agribusinesses dictate terms to
small and marginal farmers, pushing them into deeper distress. Institutional loans
have become increasingly inaccessible, leading to a surge in farmer indebtedness.
The government must prioritize interest-free loans through cooperative banks to
support small farmers and prevent their exploitation by private moneylenders. A
National Paddy Board must be established, with its headquarters in Kuttanad, to
ensure scientific planning, fair pricing, and sustainability for paddy cultivation
nationwide.
Meanwhile, reckless import policies have worsened the situation by allowing
cheap, foreign produce to flood the domestic market. Rubber, coconut oil, wheat,
and pulses are being imported at lower tariffs, causing price crashes and
endangering the livelihoods of Indian farmers. The Centre must revise import tariffs
and implement stricter trade policies to protect domestic agriculture.
Climate change has further exacerbated agrarian distress. Increased frequency
and intensity of extreme weather events like droughts, floods, and storms. These
changes can affect soil fertility, crop yields and livestock production, leading to
reduced productivity and income for farmers. Farmers may need to invest more in
pest and disease management practices, which can increase their costs and
reduce their profits. India urgently needs a National Climate Resilient Agriculture
Policy that integrates better irrigation, climate-resistant seed varieties, and
comprehensive crop insurance.
Unpredictable rainfall affects several agricultural operations and unexpected
21.03.2025 1243
rainfall during harvest will lead to total crop loss. Heavy rains that result in flooding
can be detrimental to crops and soil. In India, around 33.9 million hectares of
cropped area have been damaged due to hydro-meteorological calamities
including heavy rainfall and floods between 2015-16 and 2021-22.
Kerala's farmers face unique challenges that demand urgent central intervention.
Paddy cultivation is declining due to rising labour and input costs, while
procurement delays discourage many from continuing production. Farmers in
Kuttanad, the "Rice Bowl of Kerala," struggle due to inadequate storage facilities
and delayed payments. The rubber sector, a major source of income for Kerala's
farmers, is also in crisis. Prices have plummeted due to cheap imports, lack of
procurement support, and the absence of a price stabilization mechanism. The
government must increase import duties on rubber and implement a Rubber Price
Stabilization Fund to ensure fair pricing for farmers. Similarly, coconut farmers
suffer due to price fluctuations and unregulated palm oil imports. The Centre must
provide an MSP for coconuts and direct financial assistance for pest control and
replanting.
A Special Agricultural Zone must be declared for Kuttanad, recognizing its unique
landscape and integrating it into the Global Important Agricultural Heritage
Systems (GIAHS). A dedicated governance agency must be established to ensure
holistic planning and policy implementation. MSP must be aligned with the actual
cost of production in Kerala, and Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) and
cooperatives must be strengthened to enhance collective farming, procurement,
and market linkages. Government to support FPOs by giving special concessions
21.03.2025 1244
in procedural issues, GST, etc. This reduces the returns to farmers whose margins
are relatively low to begin with.
Infrastructure for water management and climate adaptation must be prioritized by
strengthening bunds, flood management systems, and drainage networks. Floating
solar and wind energy projects should be promoted as part of an integrated
renewable energy strategy. Organic farming should be encouraged with
certification support, and climate- resilient crop varieties must be developed.
Community-led water conservation and rainwater harvesting must be expanded,
while biodiversity conservation efforts should be strengthened through sustainable
practices.
Skill development and digital transformation must be integrated into agricultural
reforms. Innovative skill training and incubation programs should be developed to
support agri-preneurship. Al and IOT-based smart farming solutions must be
implemented to enhance productivity and efficiency. Women and youth must be
encouraged to participate in agribusiness through dedicated support for startups,
cooperatives, and leadership roles. Holistic rural development initiatives must also
be prioritized, including village-level multi-purpose centers that integrate education,
banking, markets, and healthcare. Strengthening livestock and dairy farming with
local breeds, implementing drinking water and rainwater harvesting systems, and
ensuring soil health monitoring will further contribute to sustainable agricultural
growth. Government to bring-in subsidy scheme similar to DEDS for the benefit of
allied farmers, as well as to increase the GDP growth.
Crop diversification must be promoted through the introduction of short-duration,
21.03.2025 1245
high- yielding, flood-resistant rice varieties in Kuttanad. Traditional Pokkali farming
should be revitalized to harness its saline-resistant properties. Agroforestry must
be encouraged to improve resilience and productivity, while inland fisheries, dairy,
and poultry sectors should be strengthened. Additionally, value addition centers
with climate-smart infrastructure must be set up at the local level to improve
processing and storage. Strengthening rural cold storage and supply chain
infrastructure is essential to minimize post-harvest losses. The promotion of
coconut-based value-added industries and the implementation of integrated pest
and disease management techniques will help stabilize farm incomes.
Focusing on Mavelikkara Lok Sabha Constituency, several agriculture-related
issues need immediate attention. Kuttanad farmers continue to face procurement
delays and financial distress due to erratic government policies. A dedicated
procurement center must be established in Mavelikkara to ensure timely payments.
The absence of cold storage and food processing units exacerbates post-harvest
losses, particularly for paddy and plantation crops. The Centre must allocate funds
for a modern agro-processing facility in Mavelikkara to reduce losses and create
employment.
Additionally, the Government must address the neglect of agricultural
infrastructure, particularly irrigation and soil conservation measures, which are
crucial for Kerala's farming community.
Given these challenges, I demand immediate action from the Union Government.
First, a legal MSP guarantee must be enacted to ensure fair prices for farmers.
Second, the budget allocation for agriculture must be increased to strengthen rural
21.03.2025 1246
infrastructure under RIDF through NABARD and farm support programs. Third,
crop insurance and disaster relief funds must be enhanced to protect farmers from
climate-related losses. Fourth, a special financial package for Kerala's agricultural
sector must be implemented, particularly for rubber, coconut, and paddy farmers.
Fifth, agricultural credit through cooperative banks must be boosted to ensure easy
access to loans for small farmers. Sixth, import tariffs must be revised to prevent
cheap foreign produce from flooding Indian markets. Seventh, cold storage and
food processing units must be set up in Kerala to support value-added agriculture.
The fate of Indian agriculture is at stake. Farmers are the backbone of our nation,
yet they are being systematically weakened by flawed policies, poor financial
support, and corporate interests. It is time to move beyond rhetoric and take
decisive action. The government must allocate adequate funds and prioritize
farmer welfare over corporate profits. The farmers of India, Kerala, and Mavelikkara
are watching - and they demand justice. It is our responsibility to ensure that their
voices are heard and their demands met.

