
SHRI TANGELLA UDAY SRINIVAS (KAKINADA): Today I express my views not
just as a representative of my people but as a voice for millions of farmers who toil
under the sun, braving uncertainty to ensure that India never sleeps on an empty
stomach. From the fertile plains of Punjab to the lush coastal fields of Andhra
Pradesh, our farmers are not just cultivators of crops, they are the very heartbeat
of this nation.
Under the visionary leadership of Prime Minister Narendra Modi, this government
has demonstrated an unwavering commitment to agriculture. The budget for the
Ministry of Agriculture has been increased to ₹1,27,290 crore-an increase of nearly
₹5,000 crore. This Is not just a financial allocation-it is a promise, a commitment to
modernization, sustainability, and the welfare of those who work the land.
Several landmark initiatives stand as a testament to this commitment. The PM-
KISAN scheme, which has been a lifeline for small and marginal farmers, has been
allocated ₹63,500 crore, ensuring financial stability for over 11 crore farmers across
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1252
the country. The Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana has received an enhanced
allocation of ₹8,500 crore, reinforcing efforts to improve soil health, optimize
fertilizer use, and strengthen agricultural infrastructure. Recognizing the
importance of sustainable farming, the National Mission on Natural Farming has
been allocated ₹616 crore to encourage eco-friendly, chemical-free agricultural
practices. In a significant step towards modernization, the Namo Drone Didi
scheme has been expanded to ₹676 crore, empowering women self-help groups
with drone technology to make farming more efficient and precise.
These initiatives reflect a strong intent, but for policies and budgets to make a real
impact, they must reach the farmers who need them most. And for that, I turn my
attention to my home state, Andhra Pradesh.
Here, tenant farmers form the backbone of agriculture, accounting for over 50% of
the workforce. In coastal regions like Kakinada, nearly 65-80% of paddy farmers
cultivate leased land. Yet, despite their contribution, these farmers have been
systematically excluded from crucial welfare schemes such as PM-KISAN, denied
access to bank credit, and left without input subsidies. This exclusion has forced
them into the grasp of informal moneylenders, trapping them in cycles of debt that
often end in tragedy.
Under the previous YSRCP regime, tenant farmers in Andhra Pradesh suffered
immensely. The state ranked second in farmer suicides, with tenant farmers being
the most vulnerable. Neglect and indifference toward their plight, coupled with
divisive policies, shattered the livelihoods of thousands of families. Instead of
providing relief, the government chose to look the other way as these farmers were
21.03.2025 1253
pushed to the brink.
In this dark period, our Deputy Chief Minister, Pawan Kalyan, emerged as a beacon
of hope. Using his personal funds, he provided direct financial compensation to
over 500 families of tenant farmers who had tragically taken their own lives.
Through his Kooli Rythu Bharosa Vatra, he actively engaged with struggling
farmers, listened to their grievances, and fought for their rights at the highest levels.
He inspired all of us, as representatives, to extend our support, reinforcing the idea
that governance is not about power but about responsibility
With the NDA alliance now leading Andhra Pradesh, we are committed to reversing
these Injustices. Recognizing the urgent need to protect tenant farmers, the state
government has introduced the Andhra Pradesh Land Cultivators Rights Act-2024.
This landmark legislation ensures that tenant farmers receive Crop Cultivator
Rights Cards within just 15 days, allowing them access to bank loans, input
subsidies, crop insurance, and cash transfer schemes,
However, while Andhra Pradesh is taking bold steps in the right direction, the
Central Government must step in to ensure that schemes like PM-KISAN extend
to tenant farmers nationwide. This is not just about policy; this is about justice.
Additionally, to support tenant farmers at a national level, I urge the Central
Government to create a Tenant Farmers Development Fund with an initial
allocation of ₹10,000 crore. This fund will ensure that registered tenant farmers
under state-recognized frameworks like Andhra Pradesh's CCRC system receive
PM-KISAN benefits, providing them with much-needed financial stability. It will
enable access to subsidized credit through Kisan Credit Cards, preventing tenant
21.03.2025 1254
farmers from being forced into the exploitative hands of private moneylenders. It
will also ensure their inclusion in crop insurance schemes, reducing their
vulnerability to climate shocks and crop failures. Furthermore, the fund will promote
mechanization and modern agricultural techniques, helping smallholder tenant
farmers improve productivity and increase their Incomes.
This initiative has the potential to transform the lives of over 2 crore tenant farmers
across India, ensuring they receive the same benefits and protections as
landowning farmers.
Our tenant farmers are the silent backbone of Indian agriculture. Their exclusion
from financial support is a grave injustice, one that we must correct without further
delay. By establishing a Tenant Farmers Development Fund, we can give them the
security, dignity, and financial stability they rightfully deserve.
Let us take this bold step together. Let us create a future where every farmer-
landowning or tenant-stands strong, secure, and prosperous. Let us build an
Atmanirbhar Bharat where no farmer is left behind.
Thank you!

SHRI PRABHAKAR REDDY VEMIREDDY (NELLORE): Thank you for this
opportunity to place my views on the Demands pertaining to the Ministry of
Agriculture and Farmers Welfare.
We are being an agrarian nation, farmer being the backbone of agriculture and, as
Dr. Abdul Kalam said, India having core competence in agriculture, we have to put
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1255
every single effort to push our agriculture production, productivity and welfare of
our farming community.
This discussion on Demands under the control of the Ministry of Agriculture and
Farmers Welfare gives us another opportunity to present our views before the hon.
Minister for overall development and growth of our agriculture, allied sectors and
farmers.
There is no doubt that the Ministry has got 2.7% of the Union Budget, but it has got
2.5% less than what it got at RE last year. For 2025-26, Ministry has got Rs. 1.37
lakh crores when compared to Rs. 1.41 lakh crores at RE of 2024-25. But, if you
look at it closely, five schemes of the Ministry - PM-KISAN, Crop Insurance,
Modified Interest Subvention Scheme, RKVY and PM-AASHA - take away Rs.
1,13,783 crores and the Ministry left with just Rs. 24,000 crores. So, first question
is how hon. Minister, who is former CM, is going to manage with this meager
amount. Secondly, how hon. Minister is going adjust the new schemes announced
in the Budget, such as PM Dhan Dhaanya Krishi Yojana, National Mission on High
Yielding Seeds, Enhanced credit limit under Kisan Credit Card, Focus on
Horticulture, new Urea Plant in Assam and Self-Sufficiency in Pulses, and allocate
resources.
The second point I wish to make is relating to the Ministry's one of the Flagship
Schemes - PM-KISAN. I thank the hon. PM on behalf of the farming community for
releasing 19th Installment of PM-KISAN exactly one month ago and so far GOI
spent more than Rs. 3.6 lakh crores. Sir, it is welcome that FM has allocated Rs.
63,500 crores to PM-KISAN for 2025-26. But, Sir, PM-KISAN and other schemes
21.03.2025 1256
helping only agriculture landowners and not tenant farmers who constitute 17.3%
as per NSO's latest figures and AP has highest (42.4%) tenant farmers, followed
by Odisha (39%) and West Bengal (29.5%).
Secondly, GOI is giving PM-KISAN to only 9.8 crore farmers against the original
estimation of 14.5 crore farmers in 2019. It means, 5 crore left out are tenant
farmers and more than 2 crore tenant farmers are from AP alone. So, I appeal to
the hon. Minister to consider extending PM-KISAN to these 5 crore left out tenant
farmers which will go a long way in doing justice to the entire farming community.
The next point I wish to touch upon is relating to the growth in agriculture. It is
unfortunate that in spite of we are being an agrarian country, agriculture providing
employment to more than 50% of people and providing nearly 20% value addition,
the growth is not even 50% of rest of the economy. We had grown a little over 5%
in 2019-20, but never touched this since then. What are the reasons? What are the
reasons that we have not been able to increase productivity of our major crops
even to the level of world average is an important aspect that we need to introspect
and take corrective measures. Our productivity of paddy is only 60% of world's
highest; productivity of wheat is just 40% and maize is only 1/3rd, So, I feel, there
is a need to bring structural, technological, targeted utilization of resources,
diversification and other measures to push our productivity.
Farmer's income is one area where we are not able to achieve what we intend to.
We have Committees and Commissions to push farmers income and the latest one
being Doubling Farmers' Income in 2016. But, still, we are not able to double this
income. I personally feel that Dr. Swaminathan Commission's recommendations
21.03.2025 1257
have to be implemented in its spirit only then we can see that farmers' get better
income.
Before touching a few other issues, I wish to say about my State - Andhra Pradesh
- briefly and request the hon. Minister to help AP, as it is leader in many agri
products, so that it becomes a growth engine for Agriculture Ministry:
Eluru district in Andhra Pradesh is leader in producing Cocoa in the country. But,
there is frequent fluctuation in the Cocoa prices in the international market and
hence farmers in AP are facing problems. So, there is a need to help them
whenever there is fall in prices and also extend them every help, right from financial
assistance to use of technology, quality, etc.
Andhra Pradesh, with more than 1 lakh hectares, has the highest land under
organic farming in the country. So, organic farmers have to be extended every help.
There is also need to take steps by the Government for need-based capacity
building programme for State extension agencies, agriculture universities, farmers
for further pushing organic farming, awareness, etc., in AP. Secondly, Ministry
should consider addl. help to States like AP which are performing well in organic
farming.
India is world leader in banana production and Andhra Pradesh with nearly 65-70
lakh tonnes is leading producer in the country. But, the unfortunate part is that there
is no specific and specialized programme for banana cultivation in the country,
except common programmes like Mission for Integrated Development of
Horticulture, etc. So, I request for consideration of the hon. Minister for creating a
separate and focussed programme for bananas.
21.03.2025 1258
Again, AP is producing 90% of palm oil production in the country. There is no doubt
that there is National Mission on Edible Oil - Oil Palm helping to some extent. All I
suggest to the hon. Agriculture Minister is to make district as a unit and implement
it effectively, so that we can produce more, reduce our dependence on countries
like Malaysia and save foreign exchange.
There is a demand for setting up of Central Agricultural University in Andhra
Pradesh. Sir, this issue has also come up for discussion before the High Level
Committee constituted by Home Ministry with officials from GoAP. I would be
delighted, people of AP would be delighted and, more particularly, farmers of AP
would be delighted if GOI sets up one Central Agricultural University in AP since
the ultimate beneficiaries are none other than farmers.
The next point is about heavy dependence on rain by farmers in the country. More
than 50% of net irrigated area is dependent on rainfall. When Vajpayeeji was Prime
Minister, he constituted a Task Force on Micro-Irrigation headed by our hon.
APCM, Shri Chandrababu Naidu garu, and the Task Force submitted its Report in
February, 2004. No doubt, we are implementing More Crop Per Drop and other
schemes. But, so far, we have not been able to cover even one lakh hectares and
beneficiaries are just 48 lakh farmers out of 14.5 crore farmers - it is an estimation
made in 2019 under PM-KISAN. So, there is need for transformational change in
the approach towards micro-irrigation.
While I compliment the Government for bringing down subsidy on fertilizers and
Urea since 2022-23, but still Government is spending Rs. 1.68 lakh crores next
year on these subsidies. Total fertilizer subsidy is estimated to be 3.3% of the
21.03.2025 1259
country's total Budget. It is huge and I wish that the hon. Minister to focus more on
this and see that we bring down imports, push production of Urea and other
fertilizers and encourage organic farming in the country.
One issue I wish to bring to the hon. Minister which I read is that Pakistan is illegally
cultivating Indian Agricultural Research Institute's improved basmati rice varieties
in Multan, Bahawalnagar and Hafizabad. It was also reported that there is also
seed piracy by Pakistan. The Indian basmati are protected varieties, but still they
are illegally producing them. I am sure, this will impact our Basmati rice exports.
So, I request the hon. Minister to take necessary steps.
Finally hon. Minister is also aware of this - I would say that 18% GST on implements
and equipment used for agriculture is burdening farmers. And, there have been
demands to take this issue before the GST Council and convince it to bring this
rate down to 0%. I would say that this is the responsibility of the hon. Agriculture
Minister and, I am sure, he would do this for the farming community. So, these are
some of the issues which I wish to touch and appeal to the hon. Agriculture Minister
to ponder over them carefully and meticulously and take appropriate measure to
see that next time when we discuss Demands of Agriculture Ministry in this House
or Working of the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare in the Upper House
there would be substantial improvement in whatever I and other Members have
mentioned in the House for welfare and wellbeing of our farming community.
With these observations, I support the Demands of the Ministry.
Thank you.
21.03.2025 1260

DR. MOHAMMAD JAWED (KISHANGANJ): I would like to draw the attention of
the Government towards the persistent and alarming issues faced by the farmers
of my constituency, Kishanganj & Purnea, Bihar Agriculture is the backbone of our
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1268
economy, and Kishanganj & Purnea predominantly agrarian, with thousands of
families depending on farming for their livelihood. However, despite the hard work
and dedication of our farmers, the agricultural sector in Kishanganj & Purnea
continues to struggle due to multiple challenges that demand urgent intervention.
Farmers in Kishanganj & Purnea & in rest of Bihar are facing an acute shortage of
fertilizers, particularly urea, DAP (Di-Ammonium Phosphate), and potash. This
persistent crisis severely affects crop yields and compels farmers to purchase
fertilizers at inflated prices in the black market. The shortage often coincides with
peak sowing seasons, exacerbating financial stress on farmers. The inefficient
supply chain, poor stocking at cooperative societies, and hoarding practices need
to be addressed urgently. Additionally, many farmers in remote areas are unable
to access fertilizers due to poor distribution networks. The Government must
ensure a dedicated fertilizer supply chain with timely distribution to meet demand
during crucial farming cycles.
Kishanganj is severely affected by frequent floods, especially during the monsoon
season. The Mahananda, Kankai, and Mechi rivers often overflow, causing
devastating floods that destroy standing crops, livestock, and stored grains.
Alongside this, soil erosion remains a major challenge, where fertile topsoil is
continuously washed away, reducing land productivity. This ongoing loss of
cultivable land plunges farmers into deeper economic distress.
I strongly urge the Government to implement the following measures to address
this:
Construction of permanent embankments in flood-prone areas.
21.03.2025 1269
Dredging of rivers to improve water flow and reduce the risk of overflows.
Strengthening soil conservation projects such as check dams, terracing, and
plantation of erosion-resistant vegetation.
Despite being an agrarian region, my constituency, Kishanganj's dependency on
rainfall for irrigation is alarmingly high. The absence of sufficient canal networks,
check dams, and tube well systems drastically hampers crop productivity. This
reliance on erratic rainfall leaves farmers vulnerable to drought conditions and dry
spells.
I demand that the government allocate funds for:
The construction of irrigation canals to connect major rivers with farmlands.
Installation of solar-powered tube wells to ensure water access during dry periods.
Revival of traditional water harvesting systems to promote water conservation.
Farmers in Kishanganj & Purnea, are yet to fully benefit from advancements in
modern agricultural technology. The absence of mechanized equipment, combined
with a lack of awareness about scientific farming methods, has stunted productivity.
I recommend the following measures:
Establishing agricultural training centers to educate farmers on modern techniques,
crop diversification, and climate-resilient crops.
Providing subsidies for purchasing advanced machinery like seed drillers,
rotavators, and combine harvesters.
Organizing awareness campaigns to inform farmers about organic farming
methods and improved pest control practices.
Farmers in Kishanganj & Purnea, often struggle to secure fair prices for their
21.03.2025 1270
produce. The absence of organized markets, cold storage facilities, and proper
transportation infrastructure leads to significant post-harvest losses. This compels
farmers to sell their produce at distress prices to middlemen.
To address this, I urge the government to:
Establish dedicated agricultural markets in strategic locations to provide farmers
direct access to buyers.
Develop cold storage units to preserve perishable produce and reduce wastage.
Introduce transport subsidies to help farmers reach distant markets.
Kishanganj & Purnea lacks adequate cold storage facilities, which severely impacts
the preservation of perishable crops like maize, vegetables, and fruits. The
absence of cold storage units results in considerable post-harvest losses, reducing
farmers' profits and discouraging them from diversifying their crops.
I strongly urge the Government to establish modern cold storage units in
Kishanganj to ensure farmers can store their produce safely and sell them at better
prices during off-seasons.
The Seemanchal region, including Kishanganj & Purnea, faces additional
challenges due to its geographical and socio-economic conditions. The region's
proximity to international borders often results in logistical delays and reduced
market access. Moreover, a significant portion of the agricultural workforce
migrates to other states, resulting in severe labor shortages during peak seasons.
The Government must introduce region-specific policies that include:
Incentives for local laborers to engage in agriculture during crucial seasons
Special economic zones for agro-based industries to boost employment and
21.03.2025 1271
market linkage.
Enhanced border trade mechanisms to improve the export potential of agricultural
produce.
Kishanganj is a major tea, jute & maize-producing region, yet farmers face
exploitation due to the absence of a guaranteed Minimum Support Price (MSP).
Without MSP protection, farmers are often forced to sell their produce at
unreasonably low prices to middlemen. This exploitation has contributed to rising
indebtedness and distress migration.
I urge the Government to announce an MSP for maize, Tea & jute (enhanced)
along with other key crops such as jute and pulses. This will provide farmers with
financial security, encourage better cultivation practices, and ensure fair pricing for
their hard work.
Farmers in Kishanganj & Purnea frequently suffer crop losses due to floods,
droughts, and pest infestations. However, the existing crop insurance schemes
have proven ineffective due to cumbersome claim procedures, delayed
disbursements, and inadequate outreach to marginalized farmers.
I demand that the Government:
Streamline crop insurance claims to ensure quick disbursements.
Extend insurance coverage to all registered farmers and simplify the enrollment
process.
Introduce a special relief package for Kishanganj farmers to compensate for
previous crop losses.
Demands for Immediate Action:
21.03.2025 1272
Ensure uninterrupted supply of fertilizers by improving the distribution network and
cracking down on black-market practices.
Expedite the construction of embankments, dredging of rivers, and implementation
of soil erosion control projects.
Allocate funds for developing irrigation infrastructure, including check dams,
borewells, and canal systems.
Establish agricultural training centers to educate farmers on modern techniques
and soil conservation.
Develop cold storage units and improve transportation infrastructure to reduce
post-harvest losses.
Launch region-specific schemes for Seemanchal farmers to improve credit access,
promote organic farming, and facilitate better market linkage.
Introduce an MSP for maize, jute, tea and pulses to secure fair prices for farmers.
Revise the crop insurance framework to ensure faster claim settlements and
improved accessibility.
Electrical connection for rural farmers for irrigation.
Introduce other cash crops farming for Seemanchal region.
agriculture in Kishangan, holds immense potential, but this potential is being
wasted due to administrative neglect and infrastructural gaps. I urge the
Government to treat this as a priority and take concrete steps to resolve these
challenges. By empowering our farmers with the right resources, knowledge, and
facilities, we can not only uplift Kishanganj & Purnea's agriculture but also
contribute significantly to the nation's food security.Thank you.

DR. KADIYAM KAVYA (WARANGAL): Today I express my views with a sense
of duty and concern for the well-being of millions of farmers across our great nation,
especially those in the heartlands of South India and Telangana. Agriculture, as we
know, is not just an economic activity; it is the very foundation of our society. It
sustains the lives of more than 60% of our population, yet, year after year, we see
its struggles ignored or undermined. This Government, through the 2025-26
national budget, claims to offer solutions, but as an Opposition MP, it is my solemn
duty to critically assess whether these provisions are truly in the interest of our
farmers or just another set of promises designed to placate the masses.
To borrow a well-known English proverb: "Actions speak louder than words." While
the Government is quick to announce grandiose plans and figures, the real
question is whether these allocations will translate into real, meaningful change for
the farmers of India. As we examine the agricultural provisions of this year's budget,
I invite this house to consider whether we are merely treading water, or if we are
truly setting a course for reform that will uplift our agricultural sector and the farmers
who form its backbone.
Let us begin with the simple fact: agriculture in India is in crisis. Our farmers are
caught in a web of challenges - from erratic rainfall and declining soil fertility to the
spiraling costs of inputs like seeds and fertilizers. And yet, the 2025-26 budget
barely scratches the surface of these deep-rooted issues.
The reality on the ground is grim. According to a report by the National Sample
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1274
Survey Office (NSSO), nearly 50% of farmers are in debt, with many forced to take
loans from informal sources at exorbitant interest rates. The situation in South
India, particularly Telangana, is no different. The farmers of Telangana, who rely
heavily on irrigation, have been left to deal with the failure of the government's
promises on water management. While the government talks about large irrigation
schemes, the ground reality is that many farmers still have unreliable access to
water for their crops.
As the old adage goes, "You can't make a silk purse out of a sow's ear." The
government cannot hope to resolve these deep structural problems simply by
allocating more funds to the same failed schemes. What is needed is not just
financial support, but systemic reforms that address the root causes of agricultural
distress.
A key component of the budgetary provisions for agriculture is the allocation for
subsidies, particularly for fertilizers. The government has earmarked ₹1.5 lakh crore
for fertilizer subsidies, claiming this will ease the burden on farmers. But here, too,
we see the government's shallow approach. While subsidies may provide
temporary relief, they fail to address the underlying problems that farmers face.
The reality is that our agricultural system has become overly dependent on
fertilizers and chemical inputs. This dependency is not sustainable. As another
proverb wisely says, "Give a man a fish, and you feed him for a day. Teach him to
fish, and you feed him for a lifetime." Instead of simply increasing fertilizer
subsidies, the government should be investing in long-term, sustainable farming
practices that reduce the need for such chemicals. The focus should be on organic
21.03.2025 1275
farming, soil health management, and promoting water-efficient technologies that
will help farmers in the long run.
Subsidies can no longer be the band-aid solution to an agriculture sector that
requires genuine reforms. What we need is a comprehensive plan for agricultural
transformation - one that empowers farmers with the tools they need to increase
productivity and reduce their dependency on expensive inputs.
In South India, water scarcity remains one of the most pressing concerns for
farmers. States like Telangana, Tamil Nadu, and Andhra Pradesh have faced
severe droughts in recent years, and the failure of irrigation systems only
exacerbates the situation. The 2025-26 budget promises ₹50,000 crore for
irrigation, but let us not be fooled by these numbers. We have seen this before -
grand promises made, but no real impact on the ground.
Telangana's Kaleshwaram Lift Irrigation Scheme, which was supposed to
revolutionize water supply for the state's farmers, has been beset by delays and
cost overruns. This project proved a futile effort as engineering lapses took the toll.
Despite the state government's best efforts, the central government's support has
been far from sufficient, and the reality is that many farmers still lack access to
reliable water.
The English proverb "A stitch in time saves nine" comes to mind. This proverb
emphasizes the importance of addressing problems before they spiral out of
control. Telangana's water crisis has been a long-standing issue, yet the
government's response has been slow and insufficient. What farmers in Telangana
and other drought-prone areas need is reliable irrigation systems that function in
21.03.2025 1276
both the short term and long term. Simply throwing more money at the issue without
addressing the systemic failures will not yield positive results.
This Government has often touted its welfare schemes, such as the Pradhan Mantri
Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) and the Farmer Welfare Fund, as steps toward
ensuring financial support for farmers. However, the reality is far less impressive.
While ₹6,000 annually may seem like a substantial amount, it is merely a token
sum that does little to address the systemic issues in agriculture.
Consider the condition of Telangana's farmers, many of whom are in severe debt.
The government's schemes are often poorly implemented, and delays in
disbursements are commonplace. As the proverb goes, "Don't count your chickens
before they hatch." The government often makes claims about the success of its
welfare schemes, but the actual outcomes are far less positive. Many farmers
continue to wait for their due payments, and the bureaucratic inefficiencies that
plague these schemes mean that the real benefits do not reach the intended
recipients.
Furthermore, the government's claims that PM-KISAN and other schemes are
transformative only reveal their ignorance of the deep structural inequalities that
plague our agricultural system. These one-time payments are not enough to lift
farmers out of poverty or address the systemic issues in agriculture.
Let us now focus on Telangana, where the promises of the central government ring
hollow. Telangana's farmers face unique challenges, not least of which is water
scarcity. The state government has worked tirelessly to improve water resources
through initiatives like Mission Kakatiya and the Kaleshwaram Project, but the
21.03.2025 1277
Central Government's support has been sporadic at best.
In this context, the 2025-26 budget's allocation for irrigation is not just insufficient -
it is an affront to the hard work of Telangana's farmers. The central government's
failure to address inter-state water disputes, provide timely financial support, or
allocate adequate funds for irrigation projects directly impacts farmers' livelihoods.
As the proverb "The proof of the pudding is in the eating" reminds us, it is only
through action and tangible results that we can measure the effectiveness of the
government's policies. Telangana's farmers, despite their best efforts, continue to
struggle due to the lack of substantial support from the central government. They
deserve better.
In conclusion, I express my views not only as an Opposition MP but as a voice for
the farmers of India - those who till the soil, who feed us, and who struggle every
day against a system that has neglected them for far too long. The 2025-26 budget
does little to address the urgent needs of our agricultural sector. It is time we
recognized that true agricultural reform cannot come from mere token allocations
or subsidies that only scratch the surface.
We need a government that takes bold, visionary steps to ensure the welfare of our
farmers. This includes a comprehensive strategy for irrigation, long-term reforms in
subsidies, a shift towards sustainable farming practices, and the urgent need for
reliable credit systems for small and marginal farmers.
As we consider the budget and its impact on the agricultural sector, I leave you with
the words of the famous proverb: "A penny saved is a penny earned." We cannot
afford to waste any more time or resources on inadequate measures. The time for
21.03.2025 1278
meaningful reform is now. Let us work toward a future where the prosperity of our
farmers is not an afterthought, but the very foundation of our nation's well-being.
Thank you.

SHRIMATI SANGEETA KUMARI SINGH DEO (BOLANGIR): I on behalf of my
party support the demand for grants of ₹1,37,756.55 crore for the Ministry of
Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare for the year 2025-26. This budget reflects the
unwavering commitment of the Government of India under the visionary leadership
of Hon'ble Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi towards making agriculture a robust
engine of India's growth. The increase in budgetary allocation from ₹11,915.22
crore in 2008-09 to over ₹1.37 lakh crore in 2025-26 is not just financial arithmetic
- it is a testament to this Government's resolve to transform rural India.
Over the past two decades, India's foodgrain production has risen remarkably from
204.6 million tonnes in 2004-05 to an estimated 332.3 million tonnes in 2023-24.
Alongside, Minimum Support Prices (MSP) for paddy and wheat have grown
substantially, with the MSP for paddy increasing from ₹850 per quintal in 2008-09
to ₹2,300 per quintal in 2023-24, and wheat rising from ₹1,080 to ₹2,425 per quintal
in the same period. The total MSP payment made to farmers for paddy and wheat
has surged threefold in the last decade, ensuring better returns and farmer security.
Farmer-centric schemes such as PM-KISAN, under which ₹3.46 lakh crore has
been disbursed directly into farmers' accounts, and PMFBY, which has enabled
₹1.65 lakh crore in crop insurance claims, have provided a vital safety net to millions
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1279
of farmers. In addition, initiatives like e-NAM have digitally linked over 1,400
mandis, enhancing market access and price transparency. The Agricultural
Infrastructure Fund (AIF) has further sanctioned ₹52,738 crore to support over
87,500 projects, strengthening post-harvest and storage systems across the
country.
India's agriculture is not only self-reliant today but is contributing significantly to
global food security. Food exports now constitute 11.7% of India's total exports,
with seafood exports registering a 29.7% rise between FY20 and FY24. Allied
sectors have emerged as crucial drivers of agriculture, adding diversity and value
to the sector.
The Economic Survey confirms that India's agriculture sector has demonstrated
resilience, registering an average growth rate of 5% between FY17 and FY23. The
Gross Value Added (GVA) from agriculture, forestry, and fishing has shown steady
momentum, rising from a modest 1.2% in 2014-15 to 2.1% in 2023-24.
Furthermore, the Gross Cropped Area has increased from 120.2 million hectares
in 2004-05 to 132.1 million hectares in 2023-24, reflecting increased cultivation and
better resource utilization.
Under the Prime Minister Dhan-Dhaanya Krishi Yojana, 100 agri-districts with low
productivity and below-average credit flow are being developed to benefit 1.7 crore
farmers through targeted skilling, infrastructure, and agri-technology.
The newly announced National Mission on High Yielding Seeds will enhance the
availability of improved varieties, while the 6-year Mission for Aatmanirbharta in
Pulses will boost domestic production of tur, urad, and masoor. These efforts are
21.03.2025 1280
expected to reduce import dependency and ensure price stability.
In terms of financial inclusion, the enhancement of the KCC loan limit from ₹3 lakh
to ₹5 lakh is a welcome move and will particularly benefit small and marginal
farmers who form the backbone of Indian agriculture. Additionally, the
comprehensive programme for vegetables and fruits is a crucial step to reduce
post-harvest losses and ensure remunerative prices to horticulture farmers.
I would like to place on record the commendable efforts of the State of Odisha,
which has emerged as a rising star in Indian agriculture. Odisha's irrigation
infrastructure is being transformed through 94 new irrigation projects with an
investment of ₹8,420 crore.
These projects will boost agricultural productivity and ensure better water
availability across the state. Odisha has also seen an impressive rise in women
cultivators - nearly 87% growth in the past five years - marking a silent revolution
in gender inclusion in agriculture.
Moreover, the state is actively contributing to India's food basket and has benefited
from Union schemes such as PM-KISAN and PMFBY, effectively reaching the
grassroots. Odisha's model in sustainable agriculture and fisheries, with a focus on
technology and inclusivity, serves as an inspiration for other states.
With increased investment in agricultural R&D, enhanced use of digital
technologies, and farmer-centric reforms, this Government has made agriculture a
truly aspirational sector. The integration of agriculture with the Public Distribution
System, through full e-KYC compliance, and the strengthening of food processing
under the PLI scheme, further adds value across the value chain.
21.03.2025 1281
In conclusion, I once again support the demand for grants for the Ministry of
Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare. With ₹1.37 lakh crore in allocation, we are not
just funding a ministry-we are investing in the dignity, prosperity, and future of
India's farmers. I urge the House to pass this demand unanimously as a step
toward building a prosperous, resilient, and globally competitive agriculture
ecosystem. Jai Hind. Jai Kisan.

ADV. DEAN KURIAKOSE (IDUKKI): I would like to mention, that as we all know,
Agriculture remains the backbone of our India's economy, providing livelihoods to
about 70% of the Country's workforce. It is a sad state of affairs, that the agriculture
sector that engages 70% of our population only constitutes 17% of our GDP.
While the Centre has introduced several initiatives to support farmers, there are
areas where further improvements and focused interventions are necessary
without which all the slogans that our Prime Minister has provided will remain on
paper only.
I would like to point out a few major concerns.
The farming budget for 2025-26 has dropped to ₹1,37,757 crore, down 2.5% from
last year's revised estimate. Three giant schemes namely PM-Kisan, Modified
Interest Subvention Scheme, and Crop Insurance take up 77% of all the money.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1286
However, another major worry is the fact that, the Crop Insurance plan has been
cut by 23%, leaving farmers more at risk when crops fail.
Agricultural growth remains very erratic and is projected at 3.8% for 2024-25, which
is still below the overall economic growth rate of the country.
If the agriculture sector does not grow then Sir, we cannot expect the Economy to
grow also.
The Farmers in our country still face many problems: small plots of land that are
hard to farm efficiently, water systems that waste too much, and not enough
modern equipment to help with the work.
Despite the Central Government's push for the electronic National Agriculture
Market (e-NAM), only 1,361 mandis (markets) have been integrated into the system
nationwide. This limited coverage means thousands of farmers in India are still
dependent on local traders, often receiving prices below market value.
The procurement situation shows significant gaps: while 43% of paddy and 25% of
wheat were purchased at Minimum Support Price (MSP) in 2023-24, procurement
of pulses, oilseeds and other crops remains extremely low. This creates particular
challenges for farmers who grow diverse crops. Especially in my State of Kerala
where there are cash crops the farmers are receiving very low prices for cardamom,
rubber, pepper, cloves, tea, coffee etc.
In this regard, I request the Government, to extend MSP to all the cash crops
mentioned above, Additionally, we also need a legally binding MSP framework to
ensure farmers receive fair prices for their produce, providing much-needed
stability to the agricultural sector in the state. The LDF government always claims
21.03.2025 1287
that it is the champion of the farmers. They will fight against the farm laws and
unfair policies of the Central Government. However, in Kottayam district, paddy
was not lifted by the Government for more than 2 weeks. Usually this process is
done in just 2or 3 days. In Thiruvarpu paddy field of 9000 Padasekharam, The
harvested paddy is spread all over the field. And it has been lying there for 17 days.
Every day, the farmers have to manually turn the heaped-up paddy. Meantime,
the mill owners kept up the pressure on the farmers, demanding a 'procurement
discount'. They refused to lift the paddy without the discount. In the meantime twice
it rained, The moisture content of the paddy stockpile increased, allowing the mill
owners to demand a greater discount. Finally after 17 days, that too after two days
of consecutive rains, the farmers were forced to relent and they were forced to let
the millers take their paddy with a discount. The State Government , shamelessly
abandoned the cause of the farmers and openly sided with the mill owners. I
request the Central Government to seriously consider the plight of the farmers in
Kerala.
Post-harvest losses drain ₹1.5 lakh crore yearly from farmers' incomes, with fruits
(₹29,545 crore) and vegetables (₹27,459 crore) suffering the most. Despite the
"World's Largest Grain Storage Plan," India faces a critical shortage requiring 6.1
crore tonnes of cold storage but having only 3.9 crore tonnes available. The
Government should take urgent measures to increase the storage capacity
available.
While the PM-KISAN scheme retains an allocation of ₹63,500 crore in the union
budget, there is no increase despite rising inflation and production costs. The
21.03.2025 1288
Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) scheme currently provides
₹6,000 annually to farmers which has remained unchanged over the years. Given
the rising cultivation costs, this amount is insufficient for small and marginal
farmers. We suggest that the Ministry increase financial support to a significant
amount under PM-KISAN. With these suggestions I conclude. Thank You Sir.

SHRI T. M. SELVAGANAPATHI (SALEM): Sir, I am proud to be a fifth-generation
farmer. I, on my behalf and on behalf of my Party, rise to oppose Demand No 1
and Demand No. 2 pertaining to the Ministry of Agriculture.
Due to paucity of time, I will try to put my objections in a nutshell. Many have
lost their lives in the year 2020, braving the hot sun and the cold at the border of
Delhi. I expected something from this Government to be done. The Government
also has made promises. I oppose this Demand because the Grant given to this
Ministry is not meant to fix the legally guaranteed Minimum Support Price for crops,
which has been the dream of 70 crore agrarian population of this country. As
recommended by the much-respected National Commission for Farmers, the
formula of C2 plus 50 per cent for all crops, is missing. The second foremost
objection is this. This does not speak a word about the waiver of loan for almost 18
crore farmers who are debt-trapped in this poor agrarian economy. Also, what
21.03.2025 1306
about the much-awaited comprehensive insurance scheme? The scheme which
we have now does not even compensate the cost of the seeds, leave alone the
other inputs to the agriculture operation.
More than 200 people have died. This Government has made several
promises to end the strike. None of them has been fulfilled, not even attempted to
be fulfilled. Even the Standing Committee on Agriculture, which is supposed to be
the mini-Parliament, where the ruling Treasury Benches MPs and the Opposition
MPs are there, have unanimously given a report that this MSP had to be given to
end the lakhs and lakhs of farmers' suicide in this country, the menace which has
been continuing for almost ten years. Sir, 1,11,000 farmers have ended their life
because of misery and the debt trap that they have been undergoing. The
Committee gave a report with regard to waiver of loan. More than that, the famous
Dr. M. S. Swaminathan's Committee, National Commission for Farmers, which had
given a holistic approach to give a boost to this economy, agriculture economy, has
been put in cold storage. Farmers are not given proper cold storages. Rather, this
Government is putting all the Committee reports, including the Standing Committee
report into cold storages because the rural voters have not voted for this regime. Is
that their fault? Sir, whereas if you look at the corporate loans that have been
waived, how this regime is pro-corporate is evidenced by an answer to a Question
by none other than the hon. Finance Minister.
Sir, in 2022-23, Rs. 2,16,324 crore have been written off. In 2023-24, Rs.
1,70,270 crore and in 2014-15, Rs. 58,786 crore have been written off. In 2018-
19, almost 2,36,265 crore have been written off for a very small group of corporates
21.03.2025 1307
in this country, which is deplorable. If you look at the amount of the public money
that has been written off, it was almost Rs. 16,35,000 crore for the corporate sector,
whereas 70 crore of this country's population belongs to the farming community.
What we eat like rice, roti, is the result of blood and sweat of our farmers. Their
long-pending demand has not been taken up. Look at the contrast. After the
assumption of our Dravidian model of Government in Tamil Nadu, our dynamic
leader, hon. Chief Minister Shri M. K. Stalin, has waived farmers' loans to the tune
of nearly Rs. 12,110 crore, benefiting 16.5 lakh farmers.
15.00 hrs
He almost disbursed crop loans to the tune of Rs.54,800 crore, half of your
Budget, to almost 67.81 lakh farmers of the State. The interest subvention is zero
per cent. Compared to the Centre, it is three per cent where the farmers have to
sell out. At the same time, in order to meet the short-term agriculture loan also,
crop loan to the tune of Rs.17,000 crore will be provided this year alone in the State
of Tamil Nadu. The capital loan for livestock is almost Rs.7,000 crore to 12.24 lakh
farmers who have been advanced with the monumental livestock loan. With all
these measures, now Tamil Nadu stands first in the poverty alleviation programme
among all the States compared to the other States in the country because we have
a separate budget for agriculture, which no other State has got. Therefore, this
year alone, we have given about two lakh free pump sets, which no other State has
done. I am raising all these issues because the Central Government may take a
cue and follow the Dravidian Model. That would benefit the 60-crore farmer
population.
21.03.2025 1308
The Finance Minister in the Budget speech had categorically said that a key
driver for growth and Viskit Bharat, the first engine of growth, is the agriculture
sector. What you have allotted is a hypocrisy. Out of an enormous budget of
Rs.56.75 lakh crore, you have allotted a pittance of Rs.1.77 lakh crore, which is
almost 2.07 per cent compared to the revised estimate of 2014-15. Your Viksit
Bharat scheme would derail. The first engine would derail if you look at the agrarian
economy in this fashion. Therefore, what you utter that this is the first engine of
growth for attaining Viksit Bharat in 2047 is meaningless. Even the Fertilizer
Department had reduced the allocation from Rs.1.83 lakh to 1.56 lakh crore.
Almost Rs.26,500 crore is missing, which will have a very bad impact on the
fertilizer subsidy. In agriculture, what is needed is capital investment, which is
absolutely neglected by this Government. If you take the statistics from 2020-21,
it is completely coming down from 4.83 per cent to three per cent this year. If there
is no capital expenditure, how do you think the Finance Minister or the Agriculture
Minister depicts that agriculture is the first engine of growth for obtaining Viksit
Bharat? For a country of 70 crore farmers, if this is the allocation, it is deplorable.
This House is well aware of the fact that India was dependent on PL 480 for
importing wheat from the United States of America. Thanks to the Green
Revolution brought by our forefathers, especially Dr. M.S. Swaminathan and Dr. C.
Subramaniam who hail from our State. This Government is trying to reverse the
trend that was set earlier where we absolutely stopped importing wheat from the
United States of America. Now, a very alarming situation is there that the
Government would allow duty-free import of American agriculture and dairy
21.03.2025 1309
products. Even the Commerce Minister has gone on record saying in the Press
that they are going to allow, and they have given a nod to duty-free import of
American foodgrains and dairy products. The President of the United States,
Trump has gone on record that India has agreed to the conditions imposed by the
United States of America. Mexico, China and even the European countries are
resisting this. We have become spineless.
Sir, this will absolutely give a death knell to our farmers. There will be a huge
dumping when we allow duty free imports. There is going to be a massive dumping.
Therefore, the Government and the hon. Prime Minster should revisit it because
this will completely wipe out the first engine of the growth to achieve the Viksit
Bharat.
Sir, agriculture is a State subject. Therefore, this Government should allow
the State to frame its policies, and a centralised policy and planning should not be
thrust upon because farming differs from size to size, State to State, and climate to
climate. This is the diverse position of the agrarian farming. We do not understand
it, and, therefore, the problem exists. It should be given up at one go.
Sir, now, I come to insurance coverage.

Sir, even the insurance coverage allocation to agriculture has been reduced

year. In Tamil Nadu, there are 14 lakh farmers holding an area of 34 lakh acres.
They have registered in the crop insurance scheme. The State’s contribution of Rs.
220 crore has been paid completely. But the Centre has given a pittance amount
of Rs. 70 crore and almost Rs. 140 crore have been pending for months together.
This is one demand.
Another demand is this. To strengthen the agriculture infrastructure, we have
been demanding this. The cap on the interest subvention is only Rs. 2 crore. What
we want is that the cap should be increased to Rs. 20 crore so that larger
investments would be there and the private parties would be investing more on
agriculture to strengthen our infrastructure.
Sir, another PM’s flagship scheme is PMKSY. It is called the Command Area
Development and Water Management for irrigated areas. About 27 projects are
pending to cover an area of 83,125 hectares with an outlay of Rs. 587 crore. That
has been pending since 2018-19. This is the way the Government of India is
dealing with the problems of agriculture.

SHRI T. M. SELVAGANAPATHI : Sir, eNAM is very important. The hon. Minister

server issue. At gate entry itself, the commodities do not appear, and, therefore,

We have been writing to them. Earlier, the eNAM payment was 80 per cent

SHRI VIJAYAKUMAR ALIAS VIJAY VASANTH (KANYAKUMARI): I would like
to express my views on the Demands and Grants for Agriculture and Farmers’
Welfare for the year 2025-26, As we all know, agriculture is the backbone of our
great nation. It sustains the livelihood of millions of our citizens, especially in rural
areas, and is a key driver of economic growth. It is, therefore, our sacred duty to
ensure that our farmers have the necessary tools, resources, and support to thrive.
While we acknowledge the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare’s
continued efforts in supporting our agricultural sector, we must also recognize the
challenges that persist, particularly in our rural heartlands. Farmers across the
country continue to face numerous challenges such as the vagaries of climate
change, soil degradation, increasing water scarcity, and fluctuating market prices.
These issues have only worsened in recent years, and there is an urgent need for
focused interventions at both the national and state levels.
The previous year saw the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare being
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1312
allocated Rs.1,38,564 crore, which accounted for 5% of the central government's
total budget. While this allocation is commendable, we must acknowledge that it
falls short in addressing the ever-growing needs of the agricultural community,
especially with the increasing unpredictability in weather patterns, rising input
costs, and infrastructural deficiencies that hinder farmers’ productivity.
Kanniyakumari, located at the southernmost tip of India, is blessed with fertile land
and a temperate climate ideal for various agricultural activities. The district is
primarily dependent on agriculture, with crops like clove, rubber, coconut, banana,
and tapioca being extensively grown. However, despite these advantages, the
farmers of Kanniyakumari continue to face numerous challenges that threaten their
livelihoods and the sustainable growth of the region’s agricultural sector. Some of
these persistent challenges include:
Farmers in Kanniyakumari, especially those cultivating crops like clove, face
immense difficulties due to inadequate post-harvest processing facilities. The
absence of cold storage units and processing centres has led to Significant
wastage and low prices for the produce.
Despite the region's potential, the irrigation infrastructure in Kanniyakumari remains
underdeveloped. The Neyyar Idathukarai channel, which plays a vital role in
floodwater management and irrigation, has not received the necessary investment
to be modernized.
Like many parts of the country, Kanniyakumari too is witnessing erratic rainfall
patterns and unseasonal weather events, which severely affect the crop yields.
Farmers lack adequate training and resources to adapt to climate-resilient
21.03.2025 1313
practices, thus exacerbating their hardships.
The absence of organized markets and proper linkages between farmers and
consumers has led to the exploitation of farmers, with middlemen dictating the
prices of produce.
Hon'ble Speaker Sir, the farmers of Kanniyakumari have been waiting for urgent
intervention and support from both the state and central governments for many
years. There have been several long-pending demands that need to be addressed
with utmost priority:
Recently, Kanniyakumari was honoured with the Geo Tag for clove production, a
recognition that highlights the uniqueness of this region’s clove cultivation,
However, despite this significant achievement, the clove farmers continue to face
numerous challenges that hinder their growth and potential.
The clove industry is still underdeveloped in the region, with farmers lacking access
to advanced agricultural practices, modern equipment, and proper processing
facilities. The absence of a dedicated support system and market linkages has
meant that our clove farmers are often unable to capitalize on the full potential of
their produce.
Therefore, I request the Hon'ble Minister to allocate Rs.500 crore to develop a
Special Scheme for Clove and Spice Production in Kanniyakumari. This scheme
should include:
 Training for farmers on advanced agricultural techniques, organic farming,
pest control, and post-harvest handling.
 Setting up processing units for cloves and other spices to add value to the
21.03.2025 1314
produce, enhance employment opportunities, and generate local income.
 Market linkages to help farmers access both national and international
markets at fair prices.
Such a scheme will not only uplift the farmers of Kanniyakumari but also position
the region as a leader in spice production, particularly cloves, which are in demand
worldwide.
The Neyyar Idathukarai Channel, which is responsible for carrying floodwater to
agricultural lands, plays a crucial role in floodwater management, irrigation, and
preventing soil erosion in Kanniyakumari. However, this channel is in dire need of
modernization and restoration. The current state of the channel leads to
waterlogging and inefficient water distribution, which ultimately hampers
agriculture, especially during the dry season.
The farmers of Kanniyakumari have long demanded the restoration and
development of this channel to improve irrigation facilities and flood control
mechanisms. Given its importance for the agricultural productivity of the entire
district, | urge the Hon'ble Minister to allocate Rs.500 crore for the development
and modernization of the Neyyar Idathukarai channel, which will ensure efficient
water management, increased irrigation, and better flood control for the agricultural
lands in the region.
Farmers in Kanniyakumari face immense difficulties in accessing markets due to
the lack of proper rural road infrastructure. The roads are either poorly maintained
or do not connect directly to key market centres, leading to increased transportation
costs and post-harvest losses. Furthermore, the absence of cold storage units and
21.03.2025 1315
warehousing facilities in the region means that perishable goods, such as fruits and
vegetables, often go to waste before they can be sold at markets.
I request the Hon'ble Minister to allocate Rs.71,000 crore for the development of
rural infrastructure, including:
 Improvement of rural roads and connectivity to ensure that farmers can
easily transport their goods to markets.
 Setting up cold storage units and processing centres to minimize post-
harvest losses and enhance the value of agricultural produce.
 Establishment of modern warehouses for better storage and marketing
facilities for perishable and non-perishable agricultural produce.
These investments will enhance market access, reduce wastage, and improve the
incomes of farmers across Kanniyakumari.
Farmers in Kanniyakumari are particularly vulnerable to the impacts of climate
change, with erratic weather patterns leading to both droughts and floods, affecting
the yield of crops. The shift in weather patterns has made it difficult for farmers to
rely on traditional farming methods.
There is an urgent need for the adoption of climate-resilient agricultural practices
to safeguard the livelihoods of our farmers. This includes introducing drought-
resistant crop varieties, improving water conservation techniques, and providing
training on rainwater harvesting, soil conservation, and pest management.
I request the Hon'ble Minister to allocate $300 crore to fund schemes that promote
climate-resilient practices in Kanniyakumari and the state of Tamil Nadu. These
21.03.2025 1316
schemes should:
 Encourage the adoption of sustainable and climate-resilient farming
techniques such as agroforestry, organic farming, and conservation tillage.
 Provide subsidies for water-efficient irrigation systems, such as drip
irrigation, to reduce water wastage and improve crop productivity.
 Support the development of drought-resistant crop varieties and improve the
overall adaptability of agriculture to climate change.
In Kanniyakumari, as in other parts of Tamil Nadu, farmers continue to face
financial distress due to crop failures, lack of access to affordable credit, and rising
input costs. The long-standing issue of debt burden among farmers needs to be
addressed immediately.
I request the Hon'ble Minister to introduce a loan waiver scheme to support
distressed farmers and allocate Rs.3,000 crore for this purpose. Additionally,
farmers should be provided easy access to credit with low interest rates to help
them invest in modern farming techniques and mitigate the risks of crop failures.
Let me now turn my attention to the specific issues faced by the farmers of my
constituency, Kanniyakumari, and the farmers of Tamil Nadu as a whole. As a
representative of this region, I am deeply committed to ensuring that our agricultural
sector is provided with the resources it needs for sustained growth and prosperity.
Tamil Nadu seeks 23,000 crore for direct financial assistance and loan waivers for
farmers who are struggling with debt, particularly those in distress after consecutive
crop failures due to unpredictable weather patterns. Many farmers are caught in a
21.03.2025 1317
cycle of debt, and this scheme will provide much-needed relief, allowing them to
start afresh without the burden of unpaid loans.
These measures are necessary to ensure that the farmers of Tamil Nadu are
equipped to face the changing climatic conditions and global market shifts.
In addition to these state-specific demands, [ urge the Hon'ble Minister to consider
the following national initiatives, which will significantly benefit farmers across the
country:
An allocation of Rs.8,079 crore for strengthening agricultural research and
education is essential. We need to invest in developing climate-resilient crops,
pest-resistant varieties, and sustainable farming practices. Our research institutes,
like the Tamil Nadu Agricultural University (TNAU), must be adequately funded to
carry out cutting-edge research to ensure the long-term sustainability of our
agricultural practices.
It is crucial to provide an interest subsidy of Rs.713,000 crore to make credit more
affordable and accessible for our farmers, especially those in remote areas. The
availability of low-interest loans will help farmers invest in modem technology,
better seeds, and more efficient irrigation systems, leading to improved productivity
and farm income.
A continued commitment of 210,000 crore is needed to provide crop insurance,
especially in disaster-prone areas like Kanniyakumari, to shield farmers from the
financial impact of natural calamities. The Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana
(PMFBY) should be expanded and streamlined to ensure that farmers receive
timely compensation, which will help them recover from crop losses and continue
21.03.2025 1318
farming.
Let me now tum to the agriculture budget of the state of Tamil Nadu for the year
2025-26, which is an essential component of the overall development agenda.
Tamil Nadu, as one of the leading agricultural states in India, has taken significant
steps to support farmers and the agricultural sector. Below are the key schemes
and allocations included in the Tamil Nadu State Agriculture Budget:
 Farmer Welfare Fund: Aimed at providing financial aid to farmers in distress,
especially in case of crop failure or unexpected circumstances. The
allocation for this fund is 71,500 crore.
 Crop Insurance Scheme (Tamil Nadu): This includes the National Agriculture
Insurance Scheme (NAIS) and the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana
(PMFBY). This scheme provides protection to farmers against crop losses
due to natural calamities. The allocation is Rs.800 crore.
 Input Subsidy Scheme: Tinancial assistance for purchasing fertilizers, seeds,
and agricultural equipment, especially for small and marginal farmers. The
allocation for this scheme is 21,200 crore.
 Micro-Irrigation Scheme: Subsidies for adopting micro-irrigation systems like
drip and sprinkler irrigation, especially in water-scarce regions. The
allocation for this scheme is 2,500 crore.
 Farm Pond Construction Scheme: Financial aid for farmers to construct farm
ponds for water conservation and irrigation purposes. The allocation for this
scheme is Rs.500 crore.
21.03.2025 1319
 Restoration of Tanks and Channels: For maintaining and restoring the
traditional irrigation systems, particularly for small farmers in rural areas. The
allocation is Rs.1,000 crore.
 Organic Farming Promotion Scheme: Assistance for transitioning
conventional farms to organic farming, along with certifications for organic
products. The allocation is Rs.400 crore.
 Climate-Resilient Agricultural Practices Scheme: Encouraging farmers to
adopt climate-resilient crops and practices, including rainwater harvesting
and soil conservation. The allocation for this scheme is 300 crore.
 Agri-Infrastructure Development Scheme: Enhancing agricultural
infrastructure like cold storage units, processing units, and transportation to
prevent post-harvest losses. The allocation for this scheme is 2,000 crore.
 TNAU Strengthening: Funding for research on crop varieties resistant to
diseases, pests, and climatic changes. The allocation for this scheme is
Rs.400 crore.
 Development of New Crop Varieties: Research in developing new varieties
of crops that can withstand drought, pests, and diseases. The allocation is
Rs.300 crore.
 Subsidy for Agricultural Equipment: Assistance for purchasing modern
agricultural tools, machinery, and equipment, particularly for small farmers.
The allocation is 7600 crore.
 Subsidy for Fertilizers and Pesticides: Providing subsidies for fertilizers,
21.03.2025 1320
pesticides, and other inputs required to improve productivity. The allocation
is =1,000 crore.
 Rural Roads and Connectivity Scheme: Enhancing transportation to
markets, reducing the post-harvest loss, and ensuring timely delivery of
inputs to farms. The allocation for this scheme is 71,500 crore.
 Cold Storage and Warehousing Scheme: Setting up modern cold storage
units and warehouses for the storage of perishable agricultural products. The
allocation is 7700 crore.
The future of our country depends on the prosperity of our farmers. The agricultural
sector needs focused attention, innovative solutions, and timely interventions to
secure the livelihood of farmers and to boost productivity. I humbly request the
Hon'ble Minister to consider the demands and grants presented for the year 2025-
26, with particular attention to the specific needs of Kanniyakumari and Tamil Nadu.
The allocations and schemes I have outlined are aimed at creating an inclusive,
sustainable, and prosperous future for farmers. It is our collective responsibility to
ensure that agriculture remains a thriving sector that contributes significantly to the
nation's growth and the well-being of our farmers.

###** पीएम िकसान स(cid:204)मान िनिध योजनाः
** इस योजना क(cid:236) श(cid:321)ु आत 24 फरवरी 2019 को ह(cid:242)ई थी, िजसका उ(cid:293)े(cid:212)य छोटे और सीमांत िकसान(cid:332)
को सालाना आरएस 6,000 (तीन िक(cid:214)त(cid:332) म(cid:164) आरएस 2,000) क(cid:236) आय सहायता देना था । यह रािश
सीधे उनके ब(cid:167)क खात(cid:332) म(cid:164) डीबीटी के ज(cid:229)रए पह(cid:242)ंचती है ।
आपने 2019 स े 2025 तक करीब आरएस 4.5-5 लाख करोड़ का आवंटन िकया । लेिकन 2019-25
तक आरएस 40,000-50,000 करोड़ का बजट सरड(cid:164) र ह(cid:242)आ, जो म(cid:180)ु य (cid:322)प से श(cid:321)ु आती वष(cid:334) म(cid:164) पा(cid:253)ता
स(cid:194)यापन और आधार िलंिकं ग क(cid:236) देरी के कारण था ।

आपने ।

दभु ा(cid:170)(cid:181)य देिखए और सरकार का िनक(cid:204)मापन दिे खए ।

यह भारत के कुल कृिष (cid:211)यापार का एक छोटा िह(cid:214)सा है । और 1,361 मंिडय(cid:332) म(cid:164) से के वल 50-60% ही
पूरी तरह िडिजटल (cid:322)प से सि(cid:248)य ह(cid:167) । बाक(cid:236) म(cid:164) आंिशक या नाममा(cid:253) उपयोग हो रहा है । इसिलए इस का
भिव(cid:213)य इस बात पर िनभ(cid:170)र करता है िक सरकार और रा(cid:186)य इन चुनौितय(cid:332) को िकतनी ज(cid:208)दी और (cid:255)भावी
ढंग से दूर कर पाते ह(cid:167) ।
(उझानी का संदभ(cid:170) दे सकते ह(cid:167))
सवाल इतना है िक जब सरकार आवंिटत बजट ही नह(cid:233) खच(cid:170) कर पाएगी तो िकसान(cid:332) को कृिष (cid:177)े(cid:253) के
भारी-भरकम बजट आवंटन से कोई राहत कै से िमल पाएगी?
एडं िवथ *(cid:229)रकम(cid:164)डेशंस*:
*Enhance Transparency*: Establish independent committees to probe scams,
ensuring swift action against guilty parties.
*Improve Implementation*: Strengthen ground-level monitoring of schemes like
PM-KISAN and PMFBY, addressing data and infrastructure gaps.
*Legal Guarantee for MSP*: Make Minimum Support Price (MSP) a legal right to
ensure fair prices for farmers.
*Technology Support*: Extend drone, Al, and smart farming to small and marginal
farmers, with training and subsidies to bridge the digital divide.
2025-26 budget, with its reductions, must be revisited to prioritize farmer welfare.
We cannot afford to ignore the plight of our farmers, who are the backbone of our

nation. It's time for introspection and action, lest future generations hold us
accountable.

ह ै । (cid:179)या कारण है िक (cid:202)लैक माक(cid:165) ट म(cid:164) खाद तो उपल(cid:202)ध रहता है, पर(cid:198)तु सहका(cid:229)रता (cid:300)ारा िकसान(cid:332) को
नह(cid:233) िमल पाता है । मेर े रा(cid:186)य िबहार से नेपाल सटा है, वहा ँ खाद क(cid:236) काफ(cid:236) त(cid:214)करी होती है । िजसके

ह ै । यह बजट विृ (cid:294) के वल िव(cid:176)ीय (cid:255)ावधान नह(cid:233) बि(cid:208)क िकसान(cid:332) के जीवन म(cid:164) सकारा(cid:194)मक प(cid:229)रवत(cid:170)न लाने

DR. SHIVAJI BANDAPPA KALGE (LATUR): Thank you for giving me the
opportunity to express my views on Demands for Grants (DFG) for the Ministry of
Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare.
It pains me to see the situation of our farmers who form the backbone of our
country. Despite tall promises and grand announcements, this Government has
once again failed to address the real concerns of our Annadattas, who toil day and
night to feed the nation.
Despite the NDA government's claims of prioritizing farmers and agriculture under
Prime Minister Narendra Modi, the Union Budget once again exposed its fake
promises. There was not even a token mention of implementing the Swaminathan
Committee report on agricultural pricing, nor any concrete step toward making the
Minimum Support Price (MSP) legally binding.
For years the poor farmers have been demanding these crucial reforms, but the
Government has consistently ignored their plight, offering nothing but empty
rhetoric. This clearly shows how much "committed" the Government is towards
farmers. This is also a prime example of how the Modi Government is more
interested in headline management than in genuinely addressing the agrarian
crisis.
This year's budget has completely abandoned Indian agriculture. The economic
survey paints a very rosy picture, but if you look closely, you will understand that it
is nothing but just another set of lies which the Government is feeding the innocent
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1347
people. The growth rate of crop yields has plummeted, falling below even the
previous decade. The farmers, who feed the nation, their real incomes are sinking
while the prices of fertilizer, pesticides, and seed prices have skyrocketed. This
Government has reduced Indian farmers to mere election slogans while delivering
nothing but empty promises and disastrous policies. If this is not an outright
betrayal of India's farmers, then what this is.
This Government extended the financial protection to employees earning up to Rs.
12.75 lakh annually, in contrast, the farmers who barely earn Rs. 1 lakh have been
completely neglected. This discrimination underscores the Government's
misplaced priorities, favoring salaried sections. Moreover, the Union Budget was
nothing more than just making old announcements which has little to no real benefit
for farmers. Many of the so-called 'new' schemes were previously declared, but
failed to make any tangible impact.
Despite grand rhetoric about supporting farmers, the Modi Government has once
again proven that its policies cater to corporates and the privileged, while India's
food providers continue to suffer under mounting debt, price uncertainty, and
economic hardship.
There have been budget cuts for the Department of Agriculture and Farmers
Welfare, despite Government's tall claims of supporting farmers. The Centre has
slashed Rs. 3,905.05 crore from the Department's allocation compared to the
revised estimates of 2024-25. For this financial year, the budget stands at Rs.
1,27,290.16 crore, which is significantly lower than last year's revised estimate of
Rs. 1,31,195.21 crore.
21.03.2025 1348
What is particularly alarming is the Centre's massive cut to the Pradhan Mantri
Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY), a Scheme the Government is very proud of. This
year, funding for PMFBY has been slashed by Rs. 3,621.73 crore, despite the
Government's claims that more states are participating. In the last revised budget,
the Scheme was allocated Rs. 15,864 crore, but in this Budget, it has been reduced
to a mere Rs. 12,242.27 crore. This is a clear hypocritical behaviour of the
Government. While on one hand it promises farmers security, however on the other
hand, it systematically slashes down the budget for vital support schemes. These
budget cuts reflect a deliberate attempt to undermine farmers' welfare, raising
serious concerns about the Centre's true commitment to the agricultural sector.
Today we have been in an era where you order food and it gets delivered in eight
minutes, where technology has transformed every aspect of our lives, from artificial
intelligence revolutionizing industries to automation enhancing efficiency in every
sector. The world is rapidly adopting smart solutions like self-driving cars, Al-
powered healthcare, blockchain-based financial transactions, and precision
farming that optimizes agricultural yield. The Government has been talking about it
indefinitely but I want to ask what steps have the Government taken in this respect?
The budget also failed to mention about equipping farmers to cultivate high-value
cash crops.
This Budget has once again exposed the Modi Government's apathy and betrayal
of our farmers. The continued neglect of their demands clearly shows that this
Government is more interested in optics than real action. This Budget is not just a
missed opportunity but an attack on the dignity and survival of Indian farmers. The
21.03.2025 1349
Government's empty rhetoric stands exposed, and the nation must ask: For whom
does this Government truly govern, India's farmers or its privileged elites?
