SHRI RAVINDRA VASANTRAO CHAVAN (NANDED): Respected Sir, I want to
put forward the concerns of the oil seed farmers in my region. For the last three-
and-a-half years due to the suspension of future contracts in oil seeds, the farmers
have no visibility of prices. They are forced to sell their produce in distress.
However, at one point of time by using commodity derivatives through their FPO
they used to hedge their price risk using Soyabean Futures on NCDEX. After the
suspension of Soyabean futures contract in November 2021, the farmers are
rendered helpless. In the absence of benchmark pricing, farmers are not aware of
the future prices of their produce at the harvesting and only depend on the mandi
traders limiting the farmers bargaining power. Multiple independent research
reports (IIM Udaipur, IIT Bombay, BIMTECH, IIM Ahmedabad etc.) and our
economic survey 2024-25 has recommended that the suspended commodity
derivatives (Soyabean, Ref Soyoil, CPO, Chana, Moong, Mustard seed, Wheat)
should be revoked to give the commodity market participants market driven tools
to hedge their price risk helping them with efficient price discovery. These research
reports have clearly mentioned that in the absence of fair benchmark price
discovery, the price dispersion between various mandis has increased and also
commodity derivatives don’t cause inflation – they are rather tools to manage price
volatility in the spot markets.
As far as NCDEX is concerned it is the largest domestic commodity exchange
regulated by SEBI. NCDEX is owned by strong financial institutions like NABARD
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1353
(National Bank of Agriculture and Rural Development), NSE (National Stock
Exchange), LIC (Life Insurance Corporation of India) etc. As a demutualized
platform, its core function is to provide efficient tools for price risk management and
effective price discovery. They have been working with FPOs/farmers to empower
and educate them about these market driven tools since 2016. Currently there are
655 FPOs and millions of farmers associated with them.
Farmers from my constituency have not received remunerative price of MSP
(Minimum Support Price) in soybean. This year the production of Soyabean is
approximately 125000 lakh MT and the Government through its nodal procurement
agencies like NAFED, FCI etc. has been able to procure only one-fifth of the total
production. This clearly means that for the rest four-fifths of the quantity, the
farmers have not received MSP but a market price which is 18 per cent below MSP
(MSP Soyabean- Rs.4892/quintal and market price Rs. 4100/quintal). They are
also not aware of future prices of soybean due to the suspension. Now, what should
my farmers do in coming month of July when the kharif sowing season starts?
Generally, they sow the crop of soybean by considering the future price of NCDEX
in the harvesting period (September/October). Farmers might shift to other crops
as there is now an absence of future prices and losses incurred during current
season. The “mission oil seeds” by the Government to increase the production of
oil seeds in the country will not be achieved without the future prices being known
to the farmers and market participants.
Why is the Government unable to take a decision on revocation empowering our
farmers, when these tools have been empowering our farmers?
21.03.2025 1354

DR. PRABHA MALLIKARJUN (DAVANAGERE): I just as a representative of the
people of Davanagere, would like to raise of millions of farmers across this great
nation – farmers who toil day and night to feed the country, yet struggle to feed
their own families.
The Government often speaks of “Sabka Saath, Sabka Vikas”. But I ask today –
whose Vikas are we really talking about? Is it the development of the farmers
whose incomes have remained stagnant for years? Is it the progress of the families
who have lost their loved ones to mounting debts? Or, is it the growth of a sector
that registered a mere 1.3 per cent growth in 2023-24, far below the national
average of three per cent over the past two decades?
About ‘Broken Promises and Rising Hardships’, in the previous term, the Prime
Minister promised to double the farmers’ incomes by 2022. But where is that
promise today? Farmers’ incomes did not double, but food inflation did. Rising
input costs, stagnant prices, and climate uncertainties have pushed our farmers to
the brink. The reality is that if we adjust for inflation, real income has barely
increased, leaving farmers in a perpetual cycle of distress.
With incomes barely growing and the cost of food, fuel, and fertilizers skyrocketing,
our farmers are now just one drought away from a debt trap.
About the ‘Crisis of Farmer Suicides’, the numbers paint a grim picture – farmer
suicides continue to haunt rural India. Why? It is because of crippling debts caused
by: crop losses due to erratic weather: Over 50 per cent of agricultural land is still
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1355
rain-fed; lack of fair prices because many crops are not covered under MSP. Even
if they are, two major crops, paddy and wheat, are cornering them; failure of crop
insurance schemes, which do not reach enough farmers. AIso, the current
Government has made a 23 per cent cut in the insurance scheme. What kind of
farmers’ development is this?; only 10 per cent of farmers have crop insurance,
and claims are often delayed; and institutional credit has increased but 63 per cent
of small farmers remain highly indebted.
The Government claims that the number of suicides is reducing. But is it that
because the crisis is improving or because hopelessness has forced farmers to
abandon their lands entirely?
Now, I come to low investment in Agricultural Investment and Research. Adding to
this crisis is the alarming low investment in agricultural research of mere eighth per
cent of total Agricultural Budget. Research and innovation in farming have taken a
backseat, and, without scientific advancements, we cannot expect to improve
productivity. If India truly wants to support its farmers, it must increase investment
in agricultural research to boost productivity.
Now, I come to the Crisis in Davanagere. Coming to my own constituency –
Davangere, known as the “Rice Bowl of Karnataka”, is facing severe distress. Low
procurement is leaving farmers with unsold produce. Why has the Central
Government capped the procurement after encouraging Paddy with an MSP?
Irregular rainfall and frequent droughts have worsened the situation. Low insurance
claims and delays in delivering.
Now, I come to Call to Action. The hands that feed this nation deserve dignity,
21.03.2025 1356
security, and prosperity. We need a comprehensive MSP policy covering more
crops, especially millets which can be grown with little water yet nutrition rich. We
also need better access to crop insurance and financial relief in times of distress,
and increase in allocation of funds. We also need a higher investment in agricultural
research up to 20 per cent of Agri budget to ensure long-term productivity.
This is not just about farmers; this is about food security, rural livelihoods, and
the backbone of our nation. We cannot afford to fail them any longer.
Jai Jawan, Jai Kisan!


SHRI BALWANT BASWANT WANKHADE (AMRAVATI): Thank you and the
party leadership for giving me this opportunity to express my views on demands for
grants under the control of the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare. As a
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
21.03.2025 1361
member representing Amravati Lok Sabha constituency, while participating in this
important discussion today, I felt the need to reflect deeply on the agricultural
policies of our country. India is an agrarian country, where more than 60% of the
population is dependent on agriculture. However, the situation of our farmers does
not seem to have improved. The Indian agriculture sector is facing many challenges
due to rising suicides, crisis of low productivity, erratic market prices, vagaries of
nature and inadequate government support.
I will not only consider supporting the government's demands for grants, but
also review the agricultural policies of the government as a responsible member. If
we do not take concrete steps for sustainable agricultural development in the
future, there will be far-reaching consequences for farmers and the economy as a
whole.
India is an agrarian country where about 46 per cent of the workforce
contributes to agriculture and it contributes about 18 per cent to its Gross Domestic
Product (GDP). The Union Finance Minister rightly called it the "first growth
engine". But the provision made in this year's budget is not consistent with this
important recognition.
• The government has announced the Pradhan Mantri Krishi Dhan Yojana,
which aims to benefit 1.7 crore low crop farmers in 100 districts. However, the
budget of the Department of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare has been cut by
₹3,905.05 crore. The much-lauded Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY),
which provides insurance to protect farmers against crop losses, has also seen
significant budget cuts. This is a worrying trend, especially for a country that
21.03.2025 1362
depends on the strength of its farmers.
• Maharashtra leads the way with the highest enrollment in the Pradhan
Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana, as farmers here grapple with the effects of climate
change like frequent droughts every year.
• There has also been a cut in the budget of the fertilizer department, which
may affect the fertilizer subsidy. These cuts will directly impact smallholder farmers,
who are already struggling with high costs and volatile market conditions.
• Cotton was historically called "white gold" in Maharashtra, but today its
luster is fading. Cotton farmers in Maharashtra have been demanding technological
improvements and increased budgetary allocations for some time now as a solution
to the problem of declining productivity. But Finance Minister Shrimati Nirmala
Sitharaman has allocated only Rs. 500 crore for the National Cotton Technology
Mission in the budget for the financial year 2025-26. This paltry allocation is
woefully inadequate to address the declining productivity problem plaguing cotton
farmers for the past decade.
The issue of farmers' suicide and indebtedness is highly serious. I want to
talk about the condition of farmers in Vidarbha and Marathwada parts of
Maharashtra. In the last few years, the rate of farmer suicides has increased
tremendously here.
Over 49 thousand farmers have committed suicide in last 24 years A
shocking fact has been pointed out that in 24 years in the state, as many as 49
thousand farmers have become victims of Asmani and Sultani crises. Most of these
21,120 farmer suicides occurred in West Vidarbha, under which 12,512 farmers
21.03.2025 1363
died in Marathwada. Although the agriculture department has 35 schemes to
improve the living standards of the farmers, there is no certainty that the needy
farmers will benefit from the lottery system of 'DBT'. Last year, 1,051 farmers died
in five districts of Amravati division, 104 in Wardha district of Nagpur division and
948 in Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar division. Of these, 981 cases are eligible for
government assistance, 414 are ineligible, and 711 cases are pending. These
figures are indicative of the failure of our agricultural policy.
The Government should go beyond mere rhetoric and implement immediate
loan waivers, reasonable Minimum Support Price (MSP) guarantees and effective
crop insurance payments so that farmers do not feel helpless and neglected in this
tough battle for their livelihood.
Major causes of farmer suicides:
Crisis of Smallholders: 80-85% of our country's farmers are marginal land
holders, owning only 2 acres or less. In such a situation, they do not have access
to modern technology and facilities required for large-scale farming.
Increased risk due to erratic and inadequate rainfall: Due to climate change,
erratic rainfall, drought, hail Storming and heavy rains are causing massive
agricultural losses in many states including Maharashtra.
Uncontrollable Debt Burden: According to a National Sample Survey Office
(NSSO) report, 52% of India's farmers are indebted. Borrowing from moneylenders
puts farmers in more trouble than borrowing from banks as interest rates are very
high.
No Guarantee of Crops: Farmers do not get fair value for their labour. The
21.03.2025 1364
minimum support price (MSP) announced by the government is always below the
market price, resulting in losses to the farmers. For this I make the following
demands to the government:
A separate fund of special financial assistance should be created for farmers
in Maharashtra, which will be used for emergency measures to prevent suicide.
An interest-free loan scheme should be introduced so that small farmers can
be freed from the clutches of moneylenders.
A crop insurance scheme should be implemented as an alternative to loan
waiver, and restrictions on insurance companies should be reduced. It should be
designed so that the farmers will get more benefit.
Today, farmers are the backbone of the country's nutrition security. The
Government has to take more concrete measures for its protection. Agricultural
development is not just a matter of slogans, but an area of real action.
Therefore, I request that by revising these subsidy demands, priority should
be given to schemes that are truly transformative for the country's farmers.
"Jai Jawan, Jai Kisan, and Jai Science!"

SHRIMATI ANITA SUBHADARSHINI (ASKA): I would like to express my views
to speak in this august house today on the demand for Grants on Agriculture &
Farmers’ Welfare for the year 2025-26. An amount of Rs.1,37,757 crores has been
allocated to the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers’ Welfare during the financial year
2025-26. Hon. Finance Minister Smt. Nirmala Sitharaman has described
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1365
agriculture in her budget speech as ‘First engine of growth’. In an estimate the
agriculture sector provides employment to 46 per cent of labour force in the country
while contributing 16 per cent to the total value added. The Government is
determined to increase the production of pulses, oilseeds, Dairy & fisheries in order
to meet domestic demand and to some extent reduce dependency on imports. The
Government has set the target of production of pulses to 30 million ton by the year
2030. The ambitious goal of increasing agriculture export to 80 billion dollar by the
year 2030 from the current export of 50 billion dollar would definitely improve the
rural economy.
The Government is working on Long Term plan of investment for inter linking of
rivers to provide irrigation facility round the year, climate adaptive technologies, soil
health management for a robust & sustainable agriculture to boost rural economy.
The Pradhan Mantri Dhan Dhaanya Krishi Yojana is likely to benefit 1.7 crore
farmers in 100 districts with a low crop production. The PM Kisan Samman Nidhi
the direct cash transfer of Rs.6000/ per farmer to their bank account for investment
on farming. The Loan limit under Modified Interest Subvention Scheme (MISS) has
increased from Rs.3 lakh to Rs.5 lakh for loan through Kisan Credit Cards (KCC)
that facilitate short term loans to 7.7 crore farmers, fishermen & Dairy farmers of
the country.
The Government has also announced a 6 year Long Mission for Aatmanirbharta in
pulses with a special focus on Tur, Urad, and Masoor varieties of pulses.
Hinjili of my constituency is famous for vegetable production but there is no
storage facility at present, as a result of which vegetable perish easily and farmers
21.03.2025 1366
are selling at low prices at local market. More precisely the district of Ganjam is
famous for cashew production. As there is no proper upgraded processing unit the
cashew farmers faces problem to sell for good price at local market.
The Government may take pragmatic steps to open cold storage chains, encourage
orchard farming and cash crop cultivation, easy availability of loans & revision of
minimum support price of Agri products for uplifting farmer’s economic condition.


SHRI BHASKAR MURLIDHAR BHAGARE (DINDORI): I want to make following
demands for the farmers who produce onions, grapes, sugarcane, soybean,
vegetables and other crops in my Dinodori Lok Sabha constituency.
Onion Farmers-
- Minimum Support Price (MSP)- It costs an onion farmer approximately Rs. 1,500
to produce 1 quintal of onion. But, since the price of onion is continuously
decreasing, a minimum support price should be announced.
- Rates planning and export policy- By removing restrictions on onion exports,
 English translation of the speech originally laid on the Table in Marathi.
21.03.2025 1371
export fees should be totally removed and the farmers should get remunerative
price.
- Grant For Storage facility - Farmers should get subsidy for storing goods for
themselves.
- The 'GST' on onion sales should be removed- The GST on goods required for
production of onion or sale of onion produce should be cancelled.
Grapes Farmers-
- Reducing production costs-
Fertilizers and pesticides are required to produce grapes. Even when the prices
are very high of fertilizers and pesticides, GST is being charged. So the GST
should be reduced.
- Encouragement to plant a new varieties of grapes -
Bring new varieties of grapes from abroad and make it available to the farmers at
a low price.
- Export Facility - Remove the obstacles in grape exports and encourage for
selling the produce in foreign market.
- Processing Sector promotion - For increasing the production of wine juice,
dryfruits from grapes special grants should be given.
- Agricultural insurance plan- Grape growers should be protected from heavy
rainfall, severe cold, hail storm and vagaries of weather.
Direct markets- ENAM or other digital platforms should be used for grapes sale.
Sugarcane Farmers-
-Reduce production costs - Grants should be given to fertilizers and irrigation
21.03.2025 1372
equipments required for sugarcane production. GST should be waved off.
-Subsidy For Ethanol production- Ethanol Production should be given more
subsidy and the sugar factories should be encouraged to produce it.
-Sugar sales policy-
Customers should be given sugar at low rates through ration cards and sell the
remaining sugar stock in open market. So, the factories will be saved and the FRP
can be given. Kindly look into this matter.
India is an agricultural country and the global recession in the world has hit many
countries the farmers in the agriculture sector saved our country. The agricultural
sector is providing employment to the people of the country.
Due to agriculture, our country is on the path of prosperity and progress. But the
farmer is facing many problems. More than ten farmers are committing suicide
daily, this is very unfortunate. Agriculture and farmers should prosper. The
policies of the government should be in the favour of farmers.
- Production cost is increasing due to inflation. But farmer is not sure about the
price he will get for his crop. Government should look into this matter.
- There should be a provision of rate fixation while deciding the export policy for
onion, grapes, soybeans, cereals, cotton etc.
- By using the revenue of agriculture department and modern technology, the
policy should be determined to take into account the number of crops and how
much it needs.
- Dams and canals should be constructed to cover cultivation areas under the
water.
21.03.2025 1373
- Farmers should be provided with seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, and technological
upgradation at affordable prices.
- To increase irrigation coverage, drip irrigation scheme should be promoted.
- The number of various agricultural laboratories and agricultural colleges should
be increased. Latest AI technology should be used for it.
- Organic farming should be encouraged for poison -free crops.
- Agriculture supporting and agricultural processing industries should be
promoted.
- The entire outstanding loan of the farmers should be waived off. And fresh loan
should be provided at cheaper interest rates.

SHRI BALASHOWRY VALLABHANENI (MACHILIPATNAM): I feel it an honour
for this opportunity to express my views on the Demands of the Ministry of
Agriculture and Farmers Welfare. Before I start, I thank my leader, Shri Pawan
Kalyan, who has given me this opportunity to represent Machlipatnam.
We are happy and, I am sure, hon. Minister is also happy for getting allocation of
Rs. 1.37 lakh crore for 2025-26 - an increase of four per cent over the BE of 2024-
25. This Budget helps the Government to double farmers’ income and take up other
welfare measures for farmers. As a representative from AP, I am particularly happy
to see that this Budget addresses the concerns of our Annadatas through various
schemes and programmes.
As we know almost 60 per cent of our population is dependent on agriculture and
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1374
its related activities. Despite the initiatives taken by our Government, there are so
many issues that yet need to be address. I would like to mention here and
congratulate ICAR - Indian Council for Agriculture Research that has developed
109 high-yielding, climate-resilient, and bio-fortified varieties of crops. What makes
this achievement special, Sir, is that these varieties are not only high-yielding but
also pest-resistant reducing – a major factor in crop loss. Moreover, these are
naturally bio-fortified meaning they already contain essential micronutrients and
macronutrients, eliminating the need for separate fortification.
With these scientific advancements, we are not just ensuring food security but also
nutrition security, while promoting sustainable agriculture and enhancing farmers’
income. Here, I would like to request the Government that these initiatives may
please be promoted among the farmers. A team of Agriculture Ministry should visit
every district of the country. Farmers are not getting good quality seed and
fertilizers. That is needed to be assured. Farmers’ full grown crops get destroyed
either because of untimely rains or drought during summers. Although it is not fully
in one’s hand but technical guidance to farmers can help the farmers to grow crops
accordingly. The Government should guide them and support them. Accordingly,
insurance support to farmers in case of crop loss shall be extended at the earliest
without any delay.
A very important factor is financial support; Although there are multiple Government
schemes but bankers are not supporting them positively. This needs to be assured.
There are many loopholes in the system and that need to be addressed.
One important issue which I have raised on multiple occasions in Parliament is
21.03.2025 1375
supporting our tenant farmers. There is no clear definition for tenant Farmers in
the country because of which they are suffering a lot. The allocation under the
Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi Scheme has been increased to Rs. 63,500
crore. I thank the hon. PM for releasing 19th Installment of PM-KISAN recently and
so far the Government of India spent more than Rs. 3.6 lakh crores. But the point
is PM-KISAN Samman Nidhi and other schemes help only agriculture landowners
and not tenant farmers who constitute 17.3 per cent as per NSO’s latest figures
and AP has highest (42.4 per cent) followed by Odisha (39 per cent) and West
Bengal (29.5 per cent).
Secondly, GOI is giving PM-KISAN Samman Nidhi to only 9.5 crore farmers against
the original estimated Rs.14.5 crore in 2019. It means, five crore left out farmers
are tenant farmers and more than two crore tenant farmers are from AP alone. So,
I appeal to the hon. Agriculture Minister and also hon. FM and PM to consider
extending PM-KISAN Samman Nidhi to these five crore left out tenant farmers.
Thirdly, no doubt, Government of India is giving credit and insurance under KCC,
PMFBY, RWBCIS. But, there are still challenges. Some of them are relating to
documentation under KCC and enrollment is contingent on State-specific land
record documentation which excludes a large number of tenant farmers. So, I
appeal to the hon. Agriculture Minister to have some holistic, comprehensive and
concrete approach for the benefit of tenant farmers
The second point I wish to make is relating to Agriculture Infrastructure Fund which
has got Rs. 900 crore which is 50 per cent more for 2025-26. This has really helped
in post-harvest management infrastructure across the country. Sir, AIF aims to
21.03.2025 1376
disburse Rs. one lakh crore between 2020-21 and 2025-26. But, so far only Rs.
58,000 crore have been sanctioned in the first about five years and there is only
one year left. So, I suggest for consideration of the hon. Minister to pay more focus
on this scheme since it directly benefit the farmers. With 50 per cent increase, I am
sure, it will really help in creating more and better infrastructure in the country in
the coming years. The Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana (RKVY) is another scheme
which has got Rs. 8,500 crore, an increase of 12.5 per cent from BE 2024-25 and
42 per cent from RE 2024-25. Similarly, Krishionnati Yojana has received Rs.8,000
crore, an increase of 7.5 per cent from BE 2024-25. These investments will create
the backbone for a modern agricultural ecosystem, benefiting farmers all over the
country.
NAMO Drone Didi scheme has received Rs.676.85 crore. The increase of 170 per
cent from RE 2024-25 is truly encouraging. The objective to empower 15,000
Women SHGs with drone technology. Government of India has so far given about
100 Drones to AP which is very less. Sir, Government of AP has recently unveiled
Drone Policy 4.0 which will help a lot in creating not only ecosystem but also making
Drones available for Drone Didis. So, I suggest the hon. Minister to take advantage
of this and provide more and more Drones to SHGs in AP.
AP is the leader in natural farming and the Ministry getting Rs. 616 crore for
National Mission on Natural Farming. It reflects as to how this Government is
serious on natural farming. So, this Government is giving everything possible to
push natural or organic farming. But, all I appeal to the hon. Minister is to further
encourage States like AP, HP, Gujarat, Kerala, Sikkim, etc., which are leaders in
21.03.2025 1377
natural farming to become growth engines of agriculture.
My JanaSena Party welcomes new initiatives to deal with sectoral challenges. Rs.
1,000 crore has been allocated for new Mission for Pulses and it will definitely boost
domestic production and reduce imports. Taking advantage of this, I wish to submit
that Government of AP started giving tur dal through PDS to poor. It is also
planning to provide Millets, etc., through PDS. So, I appeal to the hon. Minister to
help the State in this regard which helps to achieve twin objectives of providing
nutritious food to poor and also bringing down import of pulses thereby save foreign
exchange.
It is good that you are giving Makhana Board in Bihar. We have no problem. But,
there have been demands to set up Turmeric Board in Andhra Pradesh. But, there
is no mention about this in the Budget or there is no move by the Ministry on this
for the last many years. So, I appeal to the hon. Minister to consider this positively.
I am pleased to highlight the tremendous potential to build upon our Government's
visionary BhoomihHeen Kisan initiative. The hon. Prime Minister’s foresight has
already led to remarkable progress, with Rs. 41,487.61 lakh successfully disbursed
to 116,112 Joint Liability Groups in Andhra Pradesh during 2021-221. It is a
testament to our commitment to inclusive agricultural growth. To further amplify this
success, I propose that we should enhance our data collection on tenant farmers,
which would enable our Government to extend the benefits of our flagship schemes
even more effectively.
The strengthening of Joint Liability Groups and strategic adjustments to priority
sector lending sub-targets would perfectly complement the Government’s farmer-
21.03.2025 1378
first approach, particularly benefiting states like Andhra Pradesh where tenant
farming is prevalent. These refinements would advance our shared vision of
ensuring that every farmer, regardless of land ownership status, participates in our
nation’s agricultural prosperity under the visionary leadership of our Prime Minister.
Lastly, although linkage of rivers is not directly concerning the Department of
Agriculture and Farmers Welfare, it is a key factor. Your Department should
coordinate with the Ministry of of Jal Shakti and facilitate our Farmers for irrigation
activities.
With these submissions and hoping that the hon. Minister will look into the issues I
have raised seriously, I support the Demands of the Ministry. Thank you.

SHRIMATI SUPRIYA SULE (BARAMATI): Just days before the discussion on
the Demands for Grants of the Ministry of Agriculture, Maharashtra was shaken by
the tragic suicide of Kailash Arjun Nagre, a young, award-winning farmer from
Buldhana district. Mr. Nagare was not an ordinary farmer-he was celebrated for his
progressive practices, recognized for his model farming techniques, and held up
as a success story. Yet, behind the accolades lay mounting debts, price
uncertainties, and systemic neglect that ultimately claimed his life. His story is not
an aberration; it mirrors the daily struggles of millions of farmers across India, who
find themselves trapped in a cycle of rising input costs, unstable incomes, and
broken promises.
In the Budget speech, the hon. Finance Minister declared agriculture to be the "first
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1379
engine of growth" for the Indian economy and expressed hope that India would
become the food basket of the world. Yet, as we examine the fine print of this
Budget, it becomes painfully clear that the Government continues to ignore the
basic, long-standing demands of the farming community. Farmers have been
pleading, year after year, for a legal guarantee to MSP, a comprehensive farm loan
waiver, a universal pension scheme, and protection from hikes in electricity tariffs.
Instead, what we see are incremental allocations and headline schemes, without
addressing these structural issues at the heart of agrarian distress. The disconnect
between the Government's proclamations and the ground realities in villages like
Kailash Nagre's could not be more stark.
India's agricultural sector is in the grip of a persistent and multifaceted crisis,
exacerbated by policy stagnation and inadequate structural reforms. While
agriculture continues to employ 46 per cent of India's labour force, its contribution
to the national Gross Value Added (GVA) languishes at just 16 per cent. Despite
multiple Government schemes, the sector's growth has remained erratic, averaging
only 3 per cent annually, while the overall economy has grown at 7 per cent per
year. This widening gap reflects systemic neglect in addressing core issues like
low productivity, over-dependence on monsoons, and fragmented landholdings.
The crops sub-sector, which forms the backbone of Indian agriculture, has grown
at a mere 2.1 per cent annually between 2011-12 and 2022-23, even as allied
sectors saw healthier growth. Yet, Government policy continues to focus
disproportionately on flagship schemes like PM-KISAN transfers, offering a meagre
2500 per month, rather than investing in comprehensive rural infrastructure or
21.03.2025 1380
structural reforms.
Compounding the problem, agricultural productivity in India remains significantly
lower than global standards. For example, in 2022-23, the average yield for paddy
in India was 4,229 kg/hectare, well below the world's highest of 7,080 kg/hectare,
and for wheat, India's yield stood at 3,537 kg/hectare, a stark contrast to the global
high of 8,590 kg/hectare. This crisis is compounded by Government-driven policy
distortions and chronic underinvestment in critical areas. For instance, 86 per cent
of India's farmers cultivate on holdings smaller than two hectares, yet the
Government's institutional credit and mechanization policies largely fail to reach
these smallholders. The average monthly income of an agricultural household
touched Rs 10,218 by 2019, but a staggering 40 per cent of this low income came
from wage labour, not agriculture itself. This income, in combination with very high
rates of indebtedness, makes it difficult for farmers to survive.
According to a study by the OECD on the world's 54 major agriculture economies,
only Indian farmers have continued to cultivate losses, year after year, ever since
the study began in the year 2000. Crop losses in other countries were covered with
subsidy support. While farmers in only two other countries, Vietnam and Argentina,
also incurred losses, these were covered by Government subsidies, meaning only
India's farmers face a crisis of this nature.
The budget allocation for the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare for FY26
stands at Rs 1.37 lakh crore, a slight decrease from the revised estimates of Rs
1.41 lakh crore in FY25. This reduction translates to just 2.7 per cent of the total
budget expenditure. Such a decrease indicates that the Government has failed to
21.03.2025 1381
recognise the urgency tackling the deeper issues plaguing the agricultural sector.
The Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana has faced some of the largest cuts. The
Centre has claimed that more States have joined this scheme, yet in the last
revised estimates, the scheme received Rs. 15,864 crore while in this budget, the
allocation is Rs. 12,242 crore—a reduction of Rs. 3621 crores.
The fertilizer subsidy has also been reduced from Rs 1.83 lakh crore in FY25 to
Rs. 1.56 lakh crore for FY26. According to NABARD, input costs have risen by over
Rs 4,000 per acre in Maharashtra over the last two years alone. In combination
with the fact that the Government continues to levy GST on fertilizer, despite
farmers' fervent requests that it receive an exemption, and that India imports
around 80 per cent of its natural gas for urea production, making the cost of
fertilizers vulnerable to fluctuations in global gas prices, this reduction in the
subsidy could further jeopardize farmers' razor thin profit margins.
The Parliamentary Standing Advisory Committee on Agriculture has for a hike in
PM-KISAN from the existing Rs. 6,000 to Rs. 12,000 per year, reinforcing farmers'
long-standing demands that PM-Kisan payouts be indexed to inflation, but we have
little reason to hope for these recommendations to be implemented, as the
budgetary support for the scheme remains stagnant at Rs. 63,500 crores.
The Government has allocated Rs. 7100 crore to establish Makhana Board in Bihar
to improve production, processing, value addition, and marketing of makhana.
Given that a National Research Centre for Makhana has existed under the ICAR
Research Complex for Eastern Region (ICAR-RCER) in Darbhanga, Bihar since
2002, one must wonder whether such a performative gesture that pulls valuable
21.03.2025 1382
resources away from critical schemes is fiscally prudent in a time of crisis such as
this.
Another looming threat that has not been addressed adequately in the Budget is
the potential impact of increasing US tariffs on Indian agricultural exports. President
Trump recently signed a Presidential Memorandum proposing the imposition of
reciprocal tariffs to counter foreign trade barriers on US goods, and has made it
clear that India will not be exempt from these reciprocal tariffs. There is currently a
large gap between India and America's tariffs on each other's products, particularly
in the agricultural sector.
India's simple average agricultural tariff stands at 39 per cent, and the trade-
weighted average is 65 per cent, while the US maintains a simple average
agricultural tariff of 5 per cent and a trade-weighted rate of 4 per cent.
According to a recent report by the Indian Council for Research on International
Economic Relations (ICRIER), the imposition of reciprocal tariffs on Indian
agricultural produce could have a range of impacts. Key exports such as frozen
shrimp, basmati rice, and natural honey could become significantly less competitive
in the American market. Targeted tariff hikes on specific products, such as dairy
and processed foods, could severely impact India's value-added agricultural
exports. Additionally, the US could impose stricter sanitary and phytosanitary
(SPS) regulations or licensing requirements, increasing compliance costs and
delaying shipments for Indian exporters.
These developments would leave our farmers, many of whom are already under
great financial strain and heavily dependent on exports, vulnerable to slipping into
21.03.2025 1383
a crisis. Yet, there is no mention in the Budget about a contingency framework to
cushion our exporters against such shocks—no special incentives for
diversification, no increase in corpus of the Price Stabilization Fund to offset global
price crashes, and no push for new Free Trade Agreements with alternative
markets.
One of the most pressing demands echoing from every mandi, village, and farm
across Maharashtra and much of India is the call for a legal guarantee to Minimum
Support Price (MSP). The Government must recognize that announcements of
MSP rates without statutory backing have little meaning when procurement
mechanisms are weak, market prices fluctuate far below MSP, and farmers remain
at the mercy of middlemen and volatile markets. This is especially true for
soyabean, onion, and cotton, which are critical to Maharashtra's agrarian economy.
In the 2023-24 crop year, soyabean was cultivated on over 40 lakh hectares in
Maharashtra, making the State the largest soyabean producer in India. Yet, market
prices for soyabean have routinely crashed below MSP, sometimes by as much as
8500-700 per quintal, particularly during peak harvest season, due to lack of
effective procurement and glut conditions. In the absence of a legal MSP
guarantee, farmers are forced to engage in distress sales, with no credible
alternative buyer ensuring a fair price.
Similarly, onion farmers in Nashik and Ahmednagar, year after year, face brutal
price crashes. In 2023, onion prices fell to as low as $4-6 per kg at farmgate, even
as retail consumers paid 725-30 per kg. Without an MSP safety net or structured
market intervention, thousands of onion farmers have been pushed into debt traps.
21.03.2025 1384
The Market Intervention Scheme (MIS), which could have cushioned such shocks,
has been grossly underutilized and inconsistently applied, as evident from
negligible allocations in this year's Budget.
Cotton is the lifeline of millions, yet official procurement at MSP covers barely 25-
30 per cent of total production. Last year, Maharashtra's cotton prices plunged by
12 per cent, triggered by global price dips and lack of procurement infrastructure.
The much-publicized Cotton Technology Mission has received a token allocation
of *500 crore this year, but it is grossly insufficient to address the crisis of scale
facing cotton cultivators in the absence of legal status of MSP, leaving cotton
farmers vulnerable to exploitation by traders and international market fluctuations.
The Government's announcements to fix MSP at 1.5 times the cost of production
have not translated into guaranteed incomes, simply because there is no statutory
compulsion for the market or Government agencies to procure all eligible produce.
A legal guarantee of MSP would assure farmers of a minimum monthly income,
particularly for crops which are prone to price volatility. For millions of small and
marginal farmers across India, this would be a matter of survival, not merely a
change in policy.
A critical issue that has received little attention in this year's Budget is the
Government's erratic approach to agricultural exports. Time and again, sudden
export bans and restrictions are imposed without a transparent, data-driven policy
framework. While ensuring adequate domestic reserves is undoubtedly essential
for food security, it is equally important to allow exports after reserve thresholds are
secured, especially to safeguard farmers' incomes and India's credibility as a global
21.03.2025 1385
supplier. We need only look at the recent experience of farmers in Maharashtra to
understand the grave consequences of this ad-hoc approach.
In 2022-23, Maharashtra's onion farmers suffered tremendously due to abrupt
export bans. Despite a bumper harvest, the Government imposed a ban on onion
exports to control retail inflation. This led to a massive glut in the local markets,
crashing wholesale prices to as low as &4-6 per kg, while farmers' input costs had
soared due to rising fertilizer, labour, and transportation expenses. Farmers were
forced into distress sales, unable to recover even their basic costs. The
Government's intervention to procure a fraction of the produce was too little, too
late.
The sugar sector has faced similar uncertainty. In 2023, restrictions on sugar
exports citing stockpiling concerns were imposed despite record production levels.
Many sugar mills dependent on export revenues and struggled to clear pending
payments to sugarcane armers. Mills had signed contracts in anticipation of export
opportunities, only to have them rendered void overnight, damaging both farmer
earnings and India's standing in global trade.
I strongly urge the Government to move away from this reactionary export policy.
If we truly want to make India the "food basket of the world," our farmers must have
access to global markets, not just as a privilege, but as a right-after safeguarding
domestic needs.
Countries that have succeeded in transforming their agricultural sectors have done
so by investing heavily in research, mechanization, extension, and innovation
ecosystems. India cannot hope to double farmers' incomes or ensure food security
21.03.2025 1386
without placing agricultural R&D at the heart of its policy strategy, particularly as
we confront challenges of stagnant yields, erratic weather patterns, pest outbreaks,
and the growing threat of climate change.
Yet, year after year, the Government's interventions in this regard are confined to
token announcements with inadequate funding. For 2025-26, the Department of
Agricultural Research and Education has been allocated only Rs 10,466 crore, a
mere 3.1 per cent increase over last year. Shockingly, this accounts for only 7.6
per cent of the total budget of the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare,
despite research and extension being the backbone of long-term agricultural
growth. Moreover, allocations for frontline institutions like Krishi Vigyan Kendras,
tasked with last-mile delivery of scientific advice to farmers, have been reduced-
from Rs 234 crore last year to Rs 204 crore this year.
Without investment in developing region-specific, climate-resilient, pest-resistant
crop varieties and efficient agronomic practices, we are condemning our farmers
to low incomes and vulnerability. Maharashtra, facing recurrent droughts, pest
attacks like pink bollworm on cotton, and soil degradation, needs urgent research-
driven solutions. Yet, there is no special push to strengthen agro-climatic research
in such high-risk zones.
While Maharashtra's farmers grapple with rising debts, unpredictable incomes, and
systemic neglect, shocking reports of corruption within the State's Agriculture
Department have added insult to injury. Just days before this Budget was
presented, anti-corruption activists brought to light a staggering 788 crore scam
allegedly perpetrated under the leadership of Maharashtra's Agriculture Minister.
21.03.2025 1387
It has come to light that the department procured several lakh bottles of nano urea
and nano DAP fertilizers at inflated prices without conducting any proper tendering
process. The procurement price per bottle stood at Rs 92 and Rs 269 respectively,
against open market rates of Rs 220 and Rs 590-a blatant overpricing that
defrauded public funds.
What makes this scam especially heinous is not merely the financial malpractice
but its direct impact on farmers. In a State where farmers are driven to suicide
under crushing debt burdens, siphoning off crores meant for essential agricultural
inputs is not just corruption - it is a betrayal. Every rupee misappropriated is a rupee
denied to a farmer struggling to afford fertilizers, seeds, or basic irrigation.
Unfortunately, this is not an isolated incident. Just as disheartening is the recent
exposure of a massive 85000 crore scam under the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima
Yojana (PMFBY)-a scheme specifically designed to offer security to farmers
against crop loss.
This scam came to light on 19 December 2024, when a sitting BJP MLA from
Maharashtra raised serious allegations of widespread fraud in the implementation
of PMFBY. According to the MLA, in villages like Ramapur Thanda, Udgir, crop
insurance claims were made for over 4,000 hectares, despite the village not
possessing even a fraction of that cultivable land. Such blatant inflation of claims
exposes how corrupt networks have manipulated data to siphon off funds intended
for genuine beneficiaries.
Following this revelation, on 21 December 2024, the Chief Minister publicly
admitted to what he termed a "new Beed Pattern" scam and assured that an
21.03.2025 1388
investigation would be conducted.
Shockingly, the Agriculture Minister, in a statement made on 21 January 2025,
confirmed the validity of the BJP MLA's claims and admitted that irregularities and
fraud had indeed occurred-but conveniently blamed them not on Government
officials, but on private Common Service Centres (CSCs).
However, the scale of the scam, as highlighted by the same MLA on 22 January
2025, is far beyond what the Government appears willing to acknowledge. He
asserted that the fraud may total a staggering 25000 crores, revealing glaring
loopholes in monitoring and oversight. He cited the example of a single farmer
allegedly obtaining crop insurance in eight different districts — a logistical
impossibility, laying bare the depth of the administrative failure.
This scam is not merely a case of financial impropriety-it is an outright betrayal of
Maharashtra's farmers, who pin their hopes on crop insurance during years of
drought, pest attacks, or unseasonal rains. Instead of providing relief, the scheme
has been hijacked by profiteers exploiting systemic weaknesses, leaving genuine
farmers to face delays, rejections, and harassment when they file legitimate claims.
A sitting MLA has alleged that Maharashtra's recently-resigned Minister of Food,
Civil Supplies & Consumer Protection, during his earlier term as Minister of
Agriculture, orchestrated a scam in which 5141 farmers were lured to deposit Rs 8
lakhs each with the promise of a Rs 36 lakh subsidy for a sugarcane harvester, In
reality, however, harvester machine subsidies were to be provided only to 141
farmers for 141 machines. Thousands of farmers were thus duped of several crores
of rupees.
21.03.2025 1389
These incidents exemplify how governance failures, lack of transparency, and
absence of robust accountability mechanisms continue to deepen the agrarian
crisis. They raise grave concerns about whether the funds allocated in this Budget-
whether for fertilizers, crop insurance, or research-will genuinely reach farmers or
be lost to similar malpractices.
The Government must institute independent inquiries into these irregularities, bring
those responsible to justice, and implement stringent reforms to ensure
procurement and distribution processes in the agriculture sector are transparent,
accountable, and corruption-free and implement robust reforms to close loopholes
in PMFBYs administration. Otherwise, no policy or budget allocation, however well-
intentioned, will truly serve the interests of India's farmers.
While the nation grapples with these crises, there is a silent revolution unfolding in
Baramati-one that offers a beacon of hope for the future of Indian agriculture.
Baramati has become a model for how cutting-edge technology, when harnessed
responsibly, can empower farmers, boost productivity, and make agriculture
climate-resilient.
Over the last few years, Baramati has pioneered the integration of Artificial
Intelligence in farming practices, in collaboration with Microsoft's FarmVibes.Al
platform. This initiative-one of the first of its kind in India-has been rolled out in
partnership with the Baramati Agro cluster, Oxford University researchers, and
local Farmer Producer Organisations (FPOs).
Through Al-driven tools, farmers in Baramati are now able to access real-time
insights on optimal sowing times, weather patterns, pest outbreaks, and irrigation
21.03.2025 1390
schedules. Satellite imagery, sensor data, and predictive models are being
deployed to guide small and marginal farmers in making informed decisions that
directly improve their yields and reduce input costs.
Take the case of sugarcane farmers in Baramati-traditionally vulnerable to erratic
weather and pest infestations. Using Al models, they now receive customized
advisories on when to apply fertilizers, how to manage water efficiently, and how
to prevent pest attacks like the early shoot borer. Preliminary data shows that such
interventions have resulted in a yield increase of up to 30 per cent and input cost
reductions of nearly 15 per cent.
What is more heartening is that this technological transformation is not limited to
large landowners. Over 3,000 small and marginal farmers-many cultivating on less
than two hectares-have benefitted from training sessions, mobile-based advisories,
and access to Al-generated insights, ensuring that the digital divide does not
exclude those who need support the most.
Baramati's experience proves that if we invest in farmer-centric innovation, guided
by transparency and community participation, we can unlock the true potential of
Indian agriculture. I urge this Government to study the Baramati model, scale such
interventions nationwide, and allocate dedicated funds for integrating Al and
emerging technologies into the broader agricultural strategy-especially for climate-
vulnerable and resource-poor regions.
21.03.2025 1391

SHRIMATI PRATIBHA SURESH DHANORKAR (CHANDRAPUR): I would like
to raise mines of the agriculture sector which is the backbone of the Indian
economy. India has a large number of agriculture and agro-based industries. But,
the situation of farmers is very miserable. If the country's economy survives, the
economy will be strengthened. But, during the last 10 years, the condition of
farmers has become pathetic, because no MSP has been given to agriculture
produces.
I would like to demand that farmers should be given a guaranteed price. Due
to discontinuation of cotton purchase by CCI, cotton is lying in the houses of many
farmers. My request is that till the last farmers’ cotton is sold, the cotton buying
centers should be kept open. Along with that, I request you to increase the number
of cotton buying centers. In my Lok Sabha constituency Chandrapur and Yavatmal
districts, the rate of farmer suicides is very high. So, I am demand that the Union
government should take necessary measures to prevent farmer suicides. Similarly,
loans should be made available for agriculture-based industries for the benefit of
farmers.
Government of Maharashtra had promised loan waiver. But, still the farmers

companies to pay the crop insurance amount immediately.
As many villages in my Lok Sabha constituency are covered with forest, wild

ADV. GOWAAL KAGADA PADAVI (NANDURBAR): Agriculture employs nearly
half of the India population. Yet, the Modi Government has slashed the Agriculture
Ministry’s budget share from 4.69 per cent in 2020-21 to just 2.72 per cent in 2025-
26. Since 2018-19, agricultural growth has stagnated at an average of 18 per cent
exposing the failure of the Government policies. The average farm household
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1393
earns just Rs. 13,661 per month, while 55 per cent are trapped in debt. In the last
10 years till 2022, more than one lakh farmers have committed suicide. Yet the
Government has not disclosed the full data. Farm labourers, often from SC and ST
community and other disadvantaged groups face low wages, lack of social security,
proper working conditions but are not adequately represented in policies.
There was no mention of Swaminathan Report in the Finance Minister’s
speech as well as there was no mention of legal guarantee of MSP to the farmers.
It still remains a fact that under the PMFBY the private insurance companies benefit
from the same and not the farmers. Most of the Government schemes focus on the
land owning farmers, not on the tenant farmers, the landless labourers, the women
who toil on the lands. This should be reconsidered at the earliest.

SHRI ESWARASAMY K. (POLLACHI): I would like to present my views on the
Demand for Grants for Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare for the financial year 2025-
26. Agriculture, the backbone of this country, continues to be neglected by the
Union Government, which offers lofty rhetoric but fails to back it with tangible
support. The budget allocation for the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare
stands at Rs. 1,37,757 crore, a 2.5 per cent decrease from the revised estimates
of 2024-25. While the Government claims to be prioritizing farmers, this cut
exposes the stark reality of its commitment. Farmers are the most vulnerable
section of our economy, facing unpredictable weather conditions, rising input costs,
and fluctuating market prices. Instead of supporting them with increased
investments, this Government has chosen to reduce its spending, making it clear
that agriculture is not its priority.
Tamil Nadu has taken a proactive approach to protect farmers despite facing
discriminatory fiscal policies by the Centre. The State has consistently invested in
irrigation, crop diversification, and farmer welfare programmes. While Tamil Nadu
contributed 9.21 per cent to India’s GDP, its share in tax devolution has been a
mere 4.08 per cent, a blatant injustice to a State that has long been a leader in
agricultural and industrial development. Our hon. Chief Minister, Thalapathy M.K.
Stalin, has spearheaded key initiatives such as the Tamil Nadu Mission for
Sustainable Agriculture, smart irrigation projects, and a State-level procurement
mechanism to support small and marginal farmers. However, the Union
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1396
Government has failed to extend its share of support in these schemes, forcing
Tamil Nadu to bear the entire burden. This consistent neglect by the Centre has
left Tamil Nadu to fend for itself, placing additional pressure on State resources.
The PM-KISAN allocation remains stagnant at Rs. 63,500 crore, despite
inflationary pressures and increased cost of inputs. Further, the allocation for the
Crop Insurance Scheme has been slashed by 23 per cent, showing the Union
Government’s blatant disregard for farmer security. Tamil Nadu has suffered
particularly in MSP procurement. The procurement of pulses, millets, and oilseeds
under PM-AASHA is insufficient, leading to low farm incomes and distress sales.
The Centre’s Mission for ‘Self-Reliance’ in pulses does not include any firm
commitments on higher MSPs, direct procurement, or State- level interventions,
making it another hollow announcement. The failure to ensure proper procurement
mechanisms has left farmers struggling to secure fair prices for their produce. The
State’s efforts to support small and marginal farmers through its procurement
schemes should have been complemented by the Centre, but instead, Tamil Nadu
is forced to shoulder this burden alone.
The Union Government continues to make grand announcements with little
substance. The newly introduced PM Dhan-Dhaanya Krishi Yojana claims to
support 100 low-productivity districts with Rs. 100 crore allocated for high-yield
seed development. However, given India’s vast agricultural sector, this is a mere
pittance. Additionally, the failure to adequately increase the Kisan Credit Card
(KCC) credit limit in real terms has left small farmers struggling for working capital.
Agricultural growth in India remains volatile. In 2023-24, the sector contributed only
21.03.2025 1397
16 per cent to GDP while employing 46 per cent of the labour force. The overall
economic growth has far outpaced agricultural growth, widening rural distress and
income inequality. The Government claims that agriculture is one of its four engines
of growth, yet its budgetary allocation does not reflect this commitment. If
agriculture is truly a priority, why are farmers being left behind with inadequate
support and declining incomes?
Tamil Nadu, being a water-stressed State, has repeatedly requested increased
allocations for interlinking rivers, micro-irrigation, and groundwater recharge
projects. The Centre’s neglect in addressing the Cauvery water dispute and failure
to support critical irrigation projects have left our farmers at the mercy of
unpredictable monsoons. The Rs. 500 crore allocation for horticulture is too little to
effectively implement sustainable agriculture measures. The failure to invest in
irrigation infrastructure has exacerbated water shortages and increased
dependency on erratic rainfall, further endangering food security. Tamil Nadu has
demanded an enhanced MSP for paddy, millets, and pulses, increased
procurement under decentralized procurement schemes, and greater funds under
the Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana (RKVY). However, the Centre has chosen to
ignore these concerns. The State’s ambitious Agricultural Infrastructure
Development Programme has received no Central support despite repeated
requests. Farmers in Tamil Nadu are struggling to sustain themselves due to high
input costs, poor market linkages, and a lack of investment in post-harvest
infrastructure. Without adequate financial support from the Centre, the State
Government is doing everything in its power to protect farmers, but this should not
21.03.2025 1398
be a one-sided effort.
While the Government boasts of high GDP growth, farmers’ income remains
stagnant. The average monthly farm household income has increased by just 59
per cent in the last decade, whereas industrial and service-sector wages have risen
far more rapidly. The Centre’s over-reliance on corporate agribusiness and contract
farming policies is pushing small farmers into economic peril. The allocation to
agricultural research and education has seen only a marginal increase of 3.1 per
cent, which is inadequate given the challenges posed by climate change, pest
outbreaks, and falling soil fertility. The much-hyped National Mission on High-Yield
Seeds has been allocated a mere Rs. 100 crore, showing that the Government
lacks any serious vision for agricultural transformation. Research and innovation
are crucial for improving productivity and sustainability in agriculture, yet the Union
Government has once again failed to prioritize this essential sector. Tamil Nadu
has long been a leader in agricultural research and innovation, yet it receives little
recognition or support from the Centre for its efforts.
Tamil Nadu has led the way in farmer welfare despite being continuously denied
its rightful share of tax devolution and Central funds. The Union Government’s
neglect, inequitable policies, and budgetary cuts have pushed farmers further into
distress. There must be an increase in MSP for pulses, millets, and oilseeds to
ensure fair remuneration. Full restoration of funds under PM-Fasal Bima Yojana is
needed to cover climate risks. Fair tax devolution to Tamil Nadu is required to
address the unjust revenue-sharing formula. Increased investment in irrigation,
climate resilience, and sustainable farming is essential. Without these corrective
21.03.2025 1399
measures, India’s farmers will continue to face economic hardships.
The Government must act now before the agricultural sector plunges into deeper
crisis. Tamil Nadu’s model of farmer-centric policies, sustainability, and
technological advancement should be recognized and supported by the Union
Government. Instead of neglecting the State, the Centre should learn from Tamil
Nadu’s success and work collaboratively to improve agricultural productivity,
ensure food security, and enhance farmer incomes across the country.
Thank you.

SHRI KAMAKHYA PRASAD TASA (KAZIRANGA): I hereby support the
Demands on Grants for the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmer Welfare of 2025-26.
Under the dynamic leadership of Shri Narendra Modiji as Prime Minister of our
country, the Ministry of Agriculture fulfils the demands of the farmers which are
called "Annadata" for our country. As all are aware that under the dynamic
leaderhip of Shri Narendra Modiji the following schemes are introduced and
executed, namely, PM KISHAN, PM-KMY, PM-FBY, MISS, AIF, formation and
promotion of new 10,000 FPOS, National Bee Keeping and Honey Mission NBHM,
MIS PSS, Namo-Drone DIDI under Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana lots of works are
done for the Agriculture sector. Introduction and execution of Soil Health Card,
Rainfed Area Development, Per Drop More Crop, Micro Irrigation Fund,
Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana, Sub-Mission on Agriculture Mechanization,
Crop Residue Management, Agro-forestry, National Food Security Mission under
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1400
Krishonnati Yojana, Sub-Mission on Seed and Planting Material, National Mission
on Edible Oils, Mission for Integrated Development of Horticulture, National
Bamboo Mission, Integrated Scheme for Agriculture Marketing, Mission Organic
Value Chain Development for North Eastern Region, Sub-Mission on Agriculture
Extension, Digital Agriculture.
These Schemes will change the scenario of Agriculture Sector in a large scale.
The vision of Assam agriculture is that of a vibrant sector which is an engine of
growth for the state economy, provides food and nutritional security, supports the
farmers for socio-economic uplift to have a comfortable life, minimizing
environmental degeneration and helps the state in the path towards food self-
sufficiency and poverty eradication.
Sustainable agricultural development in Assam is crucial for food security,
economic growth, and environmental protection, requiring strategies like promoting
organic farming, improving infrastructure, and supporting local farmers.
A thriving agricultural sector can drive economic growth by creating jobs,
generating income, and boosting rural livelihoods. Sustainable agriculture helps
preserve natural resources like soil, water, and biodiversity, which are essential for
a healthy ecosystem. Assam faces challenges like traditional farming practices,
limited access to technology, and infrastructure gaps, which hinder agricultural
progress.
Agriculture is the principal occupation of majority (60%) of the rural population in
the state in terms of employment and livelihood. Agriculture sector continues to
providing employment of more than 50 per cent of the total workforce and support
21.03.2025 1401
more than 75 per cent population of the state directly or indirectly.
Key Strategies for Sustainable Agricultural Development in Assam:
Promote Sustainable Farming Practices:
 Organic Farming: Encourage farmers to adopt organic practices to reduce
reliance on synthetic chemicals and improve soil health.
 Water Conservation: Implement water-efficient irrigation techniques and
promote rainwater harvesting to address water scarcity.
 Crop Diversification: Encourage farmers to diversify their crops to reduce
vulnerability to climate change and market fluctuations.
 Soil Health Management: Promote practices that improve soil fertility and
reduce erosion, such as crop rotation and cover cropping.
Improve Infrastructure:
 Storage and Processing Facilities: Invest in cold storage and food
processing facilities to reduce post-harvest losses and enhance value
addition.
 Transportation Networks: Improve Road and rail infrastructure to
facilitate the efficient movement of agricultural produce to markets.
 Energy Solutions: Provide access to reliable and affordable energy
sources for irrigation and other agricultural activities.
Support Local Farmers:
 Extension Services: Provide farmers with access to training and extension
services on sustainable farming practices.
21.03.2025 1402
 Credit and Finance: Ensure access to affordable credit and financial services
for farmers to invest in sustainable technologies and practices.
 Market Access: Facilitate access to markets for farmers' produce, both local
and international.
Policy and Regulatory Framework:
 Develop Clear Policies: Establish clear policies and regulations that
support sustainable agricultural development.
 Incentivize Sustainable Practices: Provide incentives for farmers to adopt
sustainable practices, such as subsidies for organic inputs or tax breaks
for sustainable farming.
 Promote Research and Development: Invest in research and
development to identify and promote sustainable technologies and
practices that are suitable for Assam's context.
Horticulture:
Assam produces a wide range of horticultural crops including fruits, vegetables,
flowers, spices, nuts, tubers, medicinal and aromatic plants. The climatic condition
and diverse landscape of the state have helped to develop diverse ecological
habitats such as forests, grasslands, wetlands etc. which harbour and sustain wide
ranging floral and faunal species in the state. The state has tremendous scope to
promote horticulture sector that can help ensure food and nutritional security, as
well as enhance employment opportunities via cultivation and post-harvest
management and value addition of horticulture produces.
21.03.2025 1403
Horticulture crops occupy about 15 percent of gross cropped area of the State with
an annual production of more than 67 lakhs metric tonnes of various horticultural
produces. The state has now emphasized on the commercialization of the sector
as a priority basis. The emphasis of the state is now on raising productivity of
various commercially potential crops through use of better technology,
management, and provision of planting materials to the farmers. Moreover,
emphasis is also placed on pre and post-harvest treatment, proper harvest
techniques, setting up of collection centres with grading facilities, transport facilities
and marketing facilities.
Assam and other north-eastern states are always following organic farming. Like
more specialized schemes required for the welfare of the farmers of north-eastern
region especially for Assam farmers. As our Prime Minister and our Government
change the scenario of North Eastern States.
I hereby request Hon'ble Agriculture Minister to promote the below mentioned
crops to give a special attention and instruct his Ministry to take special initiative.
Joha Rice Joha rice, aromatic rice cultivated in the Northeastern region of India is
effective in lowering the blood glucose and preventing diabetes onset and hence is
an effective nutraceutical of choice in diabetes management.
Joha is a short-grain winter paddy known for its significant aroma and noteworthy
taste. The traditional claims are that the consumers of Joha rice have low incidence
of diabetes and cardiovascular diseases, but these needed scientific validation.
Bora Saul Rice
Bora Saul is a variety of glutinous rice found in Assam, India. It has an important
21.03.2025 1404
role in festivals of Assam. During traditional occasions like Bihu, this variety of rice
is eaten with served with doi (curd), gur (jaggery) and cream.
Assam Lemon
Assam lemon is one of the most important crops of Assam and other parts of N.E.
region. Fruit is widely used for culinary, beverages, industrial and medicinal uses.
Assam Potato
Assam small Potato is one of the important cash crops grown in the Brahmaputra
Valley Zone of Assam. It is only available in Assam and North-eastern states.
Bhut Jolokia
Bhut Jolokia, also called the Ghost Pepper, is one of the hottest chillis in the world.
People who love super spicy stuff love it to bits. It is only available in the north-
eastern states and it is also a medicinal plant.
Moran Ginger
Moran Ginger is one of the variety Ginger which is medicinal one and it is only
available in Moran, Assam.
Carombulla
This indigenous fruit of Assam is very popular for its medicinal value and an ideal
fruit for various value addition and with medicinal properties.
Special schemes should be implemented for NER other than PM-Kisan Samman
Nidhi.
Agricultural technologies change every day and it is more pertinent in view of the
climate change effects. Therefore, a strategy for developing human resources is
necessary to achieve desired goals.
21.03.2025 1405
Young people should encourage to enter in agricultural business. Due to
Industrialization and urbanization the cultivated areas are reducing every year.
Attractive specialized schemes should encourage and attract young people for
enter into agricultural business. Single windows schemes should implemented for
north-eastern states.

करने के िलए तैयार है ।

SHRI G. M. HARISH BALAYOGI (AMALAPURAM): I thank you for giving me the
opportunity to express my views on the Demand for Grants for the Ministry of
Agriculture & Farmers Welfare for 2025-26.
On behalf of my leader and the hon. Chief Minister of Andhra Pradesh, Shri Nara
Chandrababu Naidu Garu, I extend my appreciation to our hon. Prime Minister,
Shri Narendra Modi ji, hon. Finance Minister, Shrimati Nirmala Sitharaman ji, and
hon. Agriculture & Farmers Welfare Minister for their unwavering commitment to
the development of the agricultural sector. Their initiatives have been instrumental
in enhancing farm productivity, improving rural livelihoods, and ensuring the
welfare of our farmers.
Agriculture remains the backbone of our country, and within this, horticulture plays
a crucial role in economic development. Among various horticultural crops, coconut
holds immense potential, particularly in coastal states. Andhra Pradesh, blessed
with the second-longest coastline in the country, provides an ideal climate for
coconut cultivation. The high demand for coconut and its derived products in both
domestic and international markets makes it a highly lucrative crop.
However, despite having the highest coconut productivity in India at about 16,000
nuts per hectare, Andhra Pradesh lags behind in total production due to its limited
cultivation area of approximately 1.07 lakh hectares. This clearly indicates a
significant opportunity to scale up coconut production with increased focus and
financial allocation to the Coconut Development Board.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1413
A region that stands out in coconut cultivation is the Dr. B.R. Ambedkar Konaseema
district of my constituency, which accounts for 50 per cent of Andhra Pradesh's
total coconut production. The region's unique agro-climatic conditions make it an
ideal hub for coconut cultivation and value addition industries. However, despite
this potential, coconut farmers in the district face multiple challenges, including
monoculture-related soil depletion, pest infestations, and climate-induced stress.
Many farmers are moving away from coconut cultivation due to increased input
costs and lower returns, which is exacerbated by low wholesale prices and
inadequate quality control measures. Therefore, it is imperative that the
government not only provides financial support but also implements structured
interventions to address these challenges.
To strengthen the coconut sector, I urge for the better implementation of key
schemes under the Coconut Development Board. The production and distribution
of quality planting material must be expedited through DSP Farms, Regional
Coconut Nurseries, and Nucleus Coconut Seed Gardens. Expanding the area
under coconut cultivation, particularly in high-potential regions like Konaseema,
should be prioritized. Integrated farming techniques should be promoted, including
the adoption of demonstration plots and organic manure units to improve soil
fertility and sustainability. The replanting and rejuvenation of aging coconut
gardens must be financially supported to ensure the long-term viability of the
sector.
Value addition and marketing remain critical for improving farmers' incomes. The
Technology Mission on Coconut must be leveraged to facilitate research and
21.03.2025 1414
innovation in coconut processing. Strengthening market intelligence services,
export promotion, and establishing quality testing laboratories will enable better
market access and improved pricing for farmers.
There is also an urgent need to enhance publicity, extension services, and skill
development initiatives so that farmers are aware of best practices and emerging
technologies in coconut farming. The current publication of the Indian Coconut
Journal in Telugu only biannually is insufficient. Given the high literacy rate in
Konaseema, more frequent dissemination of technical information in the local
language will be instrumental in educating farmers on sustainable practices.
Insurance coverage is another area that needs urgent attention. The Coconut Palm
Insurance Scheme and Kera Suraksha Insurance Scheme should be expanded to
cover more farmers, ensuring financial security against climate risks and pest
infestations. Additionally, infrastructural development, better administration, and
efficient project management under the Coconut Development Board should be
strengthened for effective execution of these initiatives.
Furthermore, diversification of coconut-based products must be encouraged to
make farming more profitable. High-value products such as activated charcoal,
coconut sugar, virgin coconut oil, and coir-based goods have a growing demand in
the global market. Special incentives and subsidies should be introduced to
establish processing units in coconut-rich districts like Konaseema. The use of
modern technology, such as drone-based pesticide application and pest
surveillance using Unmanned Aerial Vehicles, can help mitigate crop losses and
improve productivity. Intercropping with crops such as black pepper, cocoa,
21.03.2025 1415
turmeric, and vegetables should be promoted to enhance soil health and generate
additional income for farmers.
To fully realize the potential of the coconut sector, localized production centres for
biological control agents must be established to combat pest infestations
effectively. Regular district-level awareness programs should be conducted in
collaboration with the Horticulture Board and the Coconut Development Board to
educate farmers on best practices and emerging threats. Training programs can
be organized through agricultural universities to equip extension officers with
region-specific knowledge, who can then disseminate this information at the
grassroots level.
Coconut farming is an integral part of our agricultural landscape, and with the right
policies and targeted financial allocations, Andhra Pradesh, particularly
Konaseema, can emerge as a leader in coconut production and processing. I urge
the hon. Minister and the Government to increase the budgetary allocation to the
Coconut Development Board and ensure effective implementation of the existing
schemes. By doing so, we can safeguard the livelihoods of thousands of farmers,
boost exports, and enhance India's global standing in the coconut industry.


रोजगार देने वाला (cid:177)े(cid:253) भी है ।

इस (cid:177)े(cid:253) को ताकत िमली है, वरना िवगत यूपीए क(cid:236) सरकार के दौरान िकसान बड़ी स(cid:180)ं या म(cid:164) आ(cid:194)मह(cid:194)या
करते थ े । इस िकसान (cid:255)ेम के िलए म(cid:164) (cid:255)धानमं(cid:253)ी जी व कृिष म(cid:253)ं ी जी का अिभनंदन करना ह(cid:243)ं ।
बजट म(cid:164) िजस बात को और रखे ांिकत िकया गया है, उसम(cid:164) युवा, िकसान, मिहला और गरीब को चार
(cid:255)मुख वग(cid:170) बताया गया है । इस (cid:255)कार का आधुिनक वग(cid:234)करण िकसानो को बह(cid:242)त बड़ी ताकत दते ा है ।
एवं सरकार का (cid:197)यान आकिष(cid:170)त करता है । नीितगत समथ(cid:170)क भी िमलता है । िमल रहा है । हाथ बीमा
इसम(cid:164) एक है ।

का समाधान मोदी सरकार (cid:300)ारा (cid:255)(cid:214)तुत कराता है । मझु े लगता है इसम(cid:164) मा(cid:198)यवर कृिष म(cid:253)ं ी जी का एक

अ(cid:194)यंत मह(cid:194)वपूण(cid:170) है । पहल े वाली सरकार े लालिमच(cid:170) व समेि◌न(cid:170) हरी िमच(cid:170) म(cid:164) अंतर भी नह(cid:233) जानते थे ।

 माननीय सभापित महोदय, यह बजट िकसान(cid:332) क(cid:236) आय को दोगुना करने और भारत को कृिष
उ(cid:194)पादन म(cid:164) आ(cid:194)मिनभ(cid:170)र बनाने के (cid:255)ित हमारी सरकार क(cid:236) (cid:255)ितब(cid:294)ता का सा(cid:177)ात (cid:255)माण है ।
िव(cid:176)ीय सहायता, बीमा, बाजार पह(cid:242)ंच, तकनीक(cid:236) (cid:255)गित और िटकाऊ खेती के साथ एक सम(cid:250)
रणनीित के मा(cid:197)यम से हम भारतीय कृिष को अिधक मजबूत, (cid:255)ित(cid:214)पध(cid:234) और सम(cid:294)ृ बना रहे ह(cid:167)।
 आइए हम सब िमलकर इस बजट का समथ(cid:170)न कर,(cid:164) जो के वल एक िव(cid:176)ीय द(cid:214)तावेज नह(cid:233) है,
बि(cid:208)क हमारे अ(cid:198)नदाताओ ंके (cid:255)ित हमारी वचनब(cid:294)ता है - एक ऐसा वादा िक वे कभी अपनी
(cid:255)गित और समृि(cid:294) क(cid:236) राह म(cid:164) अके ले नह(cid:233) ह(cid:332)गे । जय जवान, जय िकसान !
