SHRI BHARTRUHARI MAHTAB (CUTTACK): India's agriculture sector is the
backbone of the nation, directly and indirectly supporting nearly half of the
population. Despite contributing approximately 18 per cent of the GDP, its vast
potential remains largely untapped. With 328 million hectares of land, which is 4
percent of the world's total area. India possesses 11.2 per cent of the world's arable
land the second largest after the United States. The country receives an average
annual rainfall of 1,170 mm, comparable to the global average, yet faces severe
water scarcity, holding only 4 per cent of the world's freshwater resources while
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1425
sustaining 17.8 per cent of the global population.
India ranks among the top agricultural producers, achieving record food grain
production of 330.5 million metric tonnes in 2022-2023 and horticultural output
exceeding 355 million metric tonnes. Agri-exports also surged to 53 billion USD in
FY2022-2023 including rice, spices, fresh fruits and processed foods. However,
productivity in key crops remains below global benchmarks, highlighting the
sector's unexploited potential. For instance, India's average rice yields stands at
4.0 tonnes per hectare, whereas China achieves 7.1 tonnes and the global average
is 4.8 tonnes. Similarly, wheat productivity in India is 3.5 tonnes per hectare, far
behind China's 5.7 tonnes. The disparity is even more pronounced in soybean
yields, with India producing only 1.2 tonnes per hectare compared to the U.S.
average of 3.0 tonnes. Despite possessing more arable land i.e. 156 million
hectares and higher annual rainfall of 1,170 mm than China's 120 million hectares
and 645mm rainfall, India's agricultural GDP is merely 500 billion USD, just one-
third of China's 1.5 trillion USD. This stark contrast underscores the urgent need
for structural reforms, advanced agronomic practices and widespread
technological adoption to enhance India's agricultural output and global
competitiveness.
Under Prime Minister Modi's leadership, initiatives like Direct Benefit Transfer
(DBT) and support through FPOS have strengthened agriculture. However, small
and marginal farmers, who are 85 per cent of the farming population, often remain
excluded due to lack of awareness, accessibility and resources. There are several
challenges hindering agricultural growth. United access to cutting-edge agricultural
21.03.2025 1426
technology is a major contribution to low productivity and farmer distress. With 15
crore farmers spread across 6.5 lakh villages the current extension services are
grossly inadequate. While new technologies such as drones, bio-stimulants and
drip irrigation promise improved efficiency, awareness and affordability remain key
barriers especially for small and marginal farmers.
Budget 2025-2026 has rightly, prioritized structural, medium to long-term issues in
the farm sector. These reforms can ensure that the farm sector remains a key
engine to growth, raising average farm income progressively while entrenching
food and nutrition security. The Prime Minister's Dhan-Dhanya Krishi Yojana aims
at addressing structural impediments encountered in 100 districts, which are to be
selected in terms of low farm productivity, post-harvest losses, inadequate access
to irrigation and credit, lack of crop diversification.
While India faces persistent deficits in pulses, in vegetables it is the supply chain
inefficiency, large post-harvest losses, and the rising impact of extreme weather
events that have led to both high inflation and large price volatility. Recognizing the
nature and dimension of this challenge, the Government has announced a six-year
Mission for Atmanirbharta in Pulses with a special focus on Tur, Urad and Masoor.
A critical component of this mission will be that two Central agencies - NAFED and
NCCF will provide procurement assurance for these three pulses during next four
years.
It is gratifying to note that credit intensity of agriculture i.e. annual disbursement of
farm credit, both short term and long term, as a percent of annual Farm GDP, has
increased from less than 30 per cent in 2010-2021 to more than 53 per cent now.
21.03.2025 1427
Over time, with the rising cost of cultivation, however, there was a need for raising
the scale of finance made available to the farming community, particularly, small
and marginal farmers. In December 2024, the RBI announced an increase in the
limit for collateral free agricultural loan from Rs. 1.6 lakh per borrower to Rs. 2 lakh.
The Budget has, enhanced the loan limit under the MISS (Modified Interest
Subvention Scheme) to Rs. 5 lakh from Rs. 3 lakh for loans taken through KCC.
These measures could be expected to help achieve near 100 per cent institutional
coverage of Farm Credit.
With an aim to create digital identities for 11 crore farmers by 2027, the Agricultural
Ministry is creating Farmers' Registry in FY2025-2026 and doubling its allocation
to Rs. 10,000 crore. Six crore farmers will be covered in FY 2024-2025, three crore
in FY 2025-2026 and two crore farmers in FY 2026-2027. The Government has set
a target of creating six crore farmer IDs by March end this year.
In 2025-2026, Rs. 1,37,757 crore has been allocated which is 2.7 per cent of the
Union Budget. Out of the total allocation to the Ministry, 92 per cent is estimated to
be spent by Department of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare while the remaining
is estimated to be spent by the Department of Agricultural Research and Education.
77 per cent of the budget of Department of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare goes
to three major schemes i.e. (1) Kisan Samman Nidhi, which is direct cash transfer
of Rs. 6,000 per year per farmer family; (2) Modified Interest Subvention Scheme
and (3) Crop Insurance Scheme.
In 2006, the National Commission on Farmer's chaired by Dr. M. S. Swaminathan
noted that fragmented and scattered operational holdings are a major challenge to
21.03.2025 1428
agricultural productivity. The Committee on Doubling Farmers Income (DFI) noted
that land fragmentation is also associated with the division of land sharing the
parcels among inheritors. One of the solutions recommended by the DFI
Committee has been to aggregate farmers and promote land pooling to target
fragmentation of land holdings. Structure such as Farmer Producer Organisations
(FPO)s) and Self-Help Groups (SHGs) offer farmers the agriculture value chain.
Collectivisation leads to higher collective output, increased land productivity and
higher bargaining power for each farmer-member.
Availability of certified seeds is another important factor in ensuring optimum
production for crops. There is need to access quality seeds.
As in India, about half of the agricultural area under crops is completely dependent
on rainfall, there is a need for proper irrigation facility. 69 per cent of the total paddy
and 96 per cent of total wheat in the country is cultivated using irrigation techniques.
However, the irrigation efficiency for the majority of major sources of irrigation
remains very poor. Most of the land under irrigation is irrigated using flood irrigation
reducing water efficiency.
Access to affordable credit is important for enhancing agricultural productivity by
making available the right inputs to farmers. Access to institutional credit to farmers
has improved in the last ten years growing at an annual average rate of 14 per
cent. In 2023-2024, institutional loans worth Rs. 24.8 lakh crore were extended to
the agriculture sector. However, indebtness continues to be high amount marginal
and small farmers. The prevalence of agricultural credit from non-institutional
sources remains high among marginal farmers. A Working Group was set up by
21.03.2025 1429
the RBI to review the state of agricultural credit in the country. It noted that the
interest subvention scheme was introduced with the aim of boosting crop-related
investment credit in the sector. But, the share of short-term credit paid under the
scheme at sub-vented interest rates has increased.
The Ministry incurs the second largest expenditure on Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima
Yojana (PMFBY). Crop insurance offers farmers security against crop losses cause
due to natural disasters, extreme weather events and non-preventable risks.
PMFBY was launched in 2016 with the objective to provide affordable crop
insurance to farmer. As on January 2025, total area of 4,803 lakh hectare has been
insured under the scheme with the sum insured being Rs. 19.5 lakh crore. About
68 crore farmers have been insured under the scheme so far. In 2025-2026, the
Government has allocated Rs. 12,242 crore for the Crop Insurance Scheme. This
is 23 per cent lower than the revised estimate for 2024-2025 and the lowest
allocation since 2018-2019. In addition, the amount of insurance claims paid to
farmers has also been declining. This is not a good sign. Now there is a need for
rethinking on crop insurance scheme that is prevalent today in the country and
make it attractive and famers' friendly.
Remunerative pricing for agricultural produce is a crying need of the hour. The
Government fixes MSP for 22 mandated agricultural crops as the basis of the
recommendations of Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP). The
MSP set by the Government for the financial year 2024-25 for paddy is Rs. 23 per
kg and Rs. 24.25 per kg for wheat. Dr. Swaminathan Commission had
recommended setting the MSP for crops at least 50 percent higher than the
21.03.2025 1430
weighted average of the production cost. Adapting this recommendation, the
Government in the Union Budget of 2018-2019 had announced that MSP for crops
will be fixed at 1.5 times the production cost.
In her speech, the Finance Minister has announced a new Mission for procurement
of pulses with a special focus on Tur, Urad and Masoor. The scheme has been
allocated Rs. 1,000 crore for the year 2025-2026. A very important decision was
taken by the Government recently. The Government launched the Prandhan Mantri
Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) Scheme in 2019. The scheme aims to provide
income support of Rs. 6,000 per year to all cultivable land- holding families. The
transfer is made in three equal installments directly in the Aadhaar seeded bank
accounts of eligible farmers. I may draw the attention of the Government that other
than the landholding farmer families, there are large number of landless and tenant
farmers. They also should be included in the scheme and provided with income
support.
I would also like to draw the attention of the Government that high GST rates - 18
per cent on drones and 12 per cent on drip irrigation systems- increase financial
strain on farmers. While fertilizer subsidies provide some relief, agricultural friendly
tax reforms and long-term policy frameworks are essential to ensure the
widespread adoption of modern technologies. Weak agricultural R&D and lack of
indigenous innovation remains severely underfunded. Despite allocating Rs. 1 lakh
crore R&D budget, there is little visible impact as most innovations are still driven
by multi-national corporation (MNCs). Additionally, urea smuggling to Bangladesh
and industrial diversion have worsened domestic shortages. Despite the presence
21.03.2025 1431
of fertilizer subsidy scheme, weak enforcement and corruption have fuelled the
parallel black market for agro-inputs.
There is need to carry forward the legacy of Lal Bahadur Shastri, whose leadership
was pivotal in India's agricultural Revolution through his ‘Green Revolution'. During
a time of crisis, Shastriji's vision of self-reliance - from fasting to conserve Food to
promoting wheat cultivation laid the foundation for India's food security. His
leadership during the 1965 Indo-Pak war, when he coined the iconic slogan "Jai
Jawan, Jai Kisan" reflects his deep commitment to both national security and
farmers' welfare. Today, as a result of the Green Revolution, India not only feeds
its population but is global exporter of agricultural products worth over Rs. 50 billion
annually. Former PM Atal Bihari Vajpayee "Jai Vigyan" in 2002 underscored the
role of science in national progress. In 2019, Prime Minister Modi reinforced this
vision with "Jai Anusandhan" emphasizing research and innovation. However, true
agricultural transformation remains incomplete with stronger R&D investments and
effective grassroots implementation. While nations like Israel allocated up to 5 per
cent of GDP to R&D, India's 0.6 per cent investment severely limits agricultural
advancements. AI-driven precision farming, smart irrigation and sustainable inputs
hold the key to unlocking India's agricultural potential. By prioritizing innovation,
skill-based education and efficient agri-input utilization, India can triple its
agricultural GDP-secure global leadership and fulfil its vision of becoming the
world's top economy by 2047.
21.03.2025 1432

के िकसान नई िदशा म(cid:164) (cid:255)गित कर सक(cid:164) गे और कृिष (cid:177)(cid:253)े म(cid:164) एक सकारा(cid:194)मक बदलाव आएगा ।

SHRI JAGDAMBIKA PAL (DOMARIYAGANJ): Hon. Speaker Sir, I rise to
wholeheartedly support the Demand for Grants 2025-2026 for the Ministry of
Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare. Agriculture is the backbone of our nation,
providing livelihood to nearly 46 per cent of our workforce while contributing 16 per
cent to the GDP. The Government’s unwavering commitment to farmers is evident
in this year’s allocation of Rs. 1,37,757 crore, which constitutes 2.7 per cent of the
Union Budget. This allocation not only strengthens the farmer-centric schemes but
also ensures India’s progress towards self-reliance in food production, higher
incomes for farmers, and sustainable agricultural practices.
A Decade of Transformative Agricultural Progress. Over the past ten years, India’s
agriculture sector has witnessed unprecedented reforms, record investments, and
landmark policy decisions that have empowered farmers, improved productivity,
and ensured food security. Let us recall the situation before 2014, when farmers
were left struggling due to poor MSP implementation, lack of financial assistance,
rising input costs, and no structured support system.
Doubling Farmers’ Income – A Commitment Fulfilled
 In 2016, the Committee on Doubling Farmers’ Income (Dr. Ashok Dalwai)
was constituted, leading to policy interventions such as crop diversification,
higher investments in agri-infrastructure, and promoting value addition.
 The average monthly income per agricultural household increased by 59 per
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1438
cent, from Rs. 6,426 in 2013-2014 to Rs.10,218 in 2018-2019.
Implementation of the Swaminathan Report – A Historic Step.
 For decades, farmers demanded the implementation of M.S. Swaminathan
Commission’s recommendations, especially the MSP at 1.5 times the cost
of production. While previous governments ignored these recommendations,
our government fulfilled this promise in 2018, ensuring farmers receive fair
and remunerative prices.
 The Minimum Support Price (MSP) mechanism was strengthened, with Rs.
1.07 lakh crore spent on procurement of pulses, oilseeds, and copra under
PM-AASHA since 2018-2019.
 Record wheat and rice procurement – In 2023-24, 43 per cent of paddy and
25 per cent of wheat production was procured at MSP.
Direct Benefit Transfers – Farmers Empowered.
 PM-Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) was launched in 2019, providing Rs.
6,000 per year to farmers directly in their bank accounts.
 As of August 2024, Rs. 3.24 lakh crore has been transferred to 12.85 crore
farmers, bringing financial security and stability.
 And now the Government has released its 19th instalment.
Revolutionizing Farm Credit and Insurance.
 Modified Interest Subvention Scheme (MISS) increased the Kisan Credit
Card loan limit from Rs. 3 lakh to Rs. 5 lakh, with Rs. 22,600 crore allocated
for 2025-2026.
21.03.2025 1439
 PM Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) – Rs. 19.5 lakh crore worth of crops have
been insured under this scheme, covering 68 crore farmer applications.
Advancing Agri-Tech and Research.
 The National Mission on High Yielding Seeds was launched, with an
allocation of Rs. 100 crore, ensuring commercial availability of climate-
resilient and pest-resistant seed varieties.
 Investment in Agricultural Research has steadily increased by 3.1 per cent
in 2025-2026, reaching Rs. 10,466 crore.
The key highlights of Budget 2025-2026 for Agriculture are as follows. This year’s
Budget continues the momentum with new initiatives:
 PM Dhan Dhaanya Krishi Yojana – Targeting 1.7 crore farmers in 100 low-
productivity districts to boost production.
 Aatmanirbhar Bharat in Pulses – Rs. 1,000 crore allocation to reduce import
dependency on Tur, Urad, and Masoor.
 Horticulture Boost – Rs. 500 crore allocated to ensure better supply chains,
processing, and price realization for fruits and vegetables.
 Fertilizer Self-Sufficiency – A new Urea plant in Assam with a capacity of
12.7 lakh tonnes to reduce import dependence.
Connecting with Farmers – Real Impact on the Ground. Farmers today do not just
cultivate crops; they are entrepreneurs, innovators, and contributors to India’s
economic strength. The Government’s focus on:
- Ensuring higher incomes through fair MSP and PM-KISAN.
21.03.2025 1440
- Protecting crops and livelihoods with PM Fasal Bima Yojana.
- Strengthening irrigation under PM Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (94 lakh hectares
covered).
- Improving market access through e-NAM (1.7 crore farmers registered).
These steps are transforming rural India and securing a prosperous future for every
farmer.
A Vote for Farmers’ Welfare and National Progress. This Demand for Grants is not
just about financial allocation; it is a reflection of our Government’s vision,
commitment, and action for the farmers of India. In 10 years, agriculture has gone
from crisis to confidence.
This Budget empowers every farmer, fuels India’s self-reliance in food production,
and drives the vision of Viksit Bharat.
I urge all Members of the to support this Demand for Grants and stand with our
Annadatas, who feed the nation and fuel the economy.
(ends)

21.03.2025 1448

SHRI RAJU BISTA (DARJEELING):The Budget 2025-26 paves the way for a
"Viksit Bharat - Developed India" The main focus of this Budget has been tax
reforms, agriculture, tourism, and strengthening infrastructure, development rural
and mountain regions.
The Budget not only continues with the highest Capital Expenditure (CapEx)
ever but also takes radical steps towards energizing domestic demands. Our
Government is transforming the agricultural sector fundamentally. This year, our
Government has allocated a record Rs.1.37 lakh crore for the Ministry of
Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare.
This year, our Government, under the leadership of PM Modi ji and
Agriculture Minister Shri Shivraj Singh Chouhan ji, is prioritizing several key
initiatives. The PM Dhan Dhanya Krishi Yojana aims to target 100 districts with low
productivity and transform them into high productivity areas. Additionally, the
National Mission on High Yielding Seeds will ensure the commercial availability of
more than 100 seed varieties. To support farmers, the Enhanced Credit Limit under
the Kisan Credit Card will increase the loan limit to Rs.5 lakh, providing working
capital to 7.7 crore farmers. There will also be a focus on horticulture through a
comprehensive program for the promotion, efficient supply, and processing of fruits
and vegetables.
Furthermore, a new Mission on Aatmanirbharta in Pulses will ensure self-
sufficiency in this essential crop. To bolster these efforts, a new urea plant will be
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1449
established in Assam with an annual capacity of 12.7 lakh tonnes.
Enhanced Formal Credits – Under the leadership of PM Modi ji, the financial
security of people in our nation, especially in rural regions, has increased manifold.
In the financial year 2013-14, formal institutional credit for farmers was at 64 per
cent, while non-institutional credit stood at 36 per cent. Over the past 10 years, this
has shifted significantly, with formal institutional credit rising to 75 per cent and non-
institutional credit decreasing to 25 per cent. Today, more and more farmers are
connecting with the formal financial sector, thanks to the JAM (Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-
Mobile) Trinity initiated by PM Modi ji. As a result, fewer farmers are being exploited
by money lenders, which is a testament to the inclusive policies of our Government.
The Government, under the leadership of the hon. Prime Minister, Shri
Narendra Modi, is providing a platform for exporting products from interior regions
like Darjeeling to the world market. Our Darjeeling hills, Terai, and Dooars region
possess immense potential to become an export hub for horticultural products,
including tea, orange, pineapple, strawberry, cardamom, ginger, bananas,
potatoes, and a remarkable range of flowers.
However, the lack of support from the West Bengal Government towards
agriculture in our region has left our export potential untapped. I am taking this
opportunity to highlight the urgent needs of farmers in my Darjeeling Lok Sabha
constituency.
The development of irrigation facilities in West Bengal is crucial, as the
region's agriculture heavily relies on natural rains and available water sources. The
North Bengal districts of Darjeeling, Kalimpong, Alipurduars, and Jalpaiguri face an
21.03.2025 1450
acute water deficit for agricultural purposes, making their entire agricultural system
dependent on rainwater. This lack of irrigation facilities leads to significant crop
losses for farmers, particularly between September and April, when drought-like
conditions prevail.
Therefore, I urge the Central Government to allocate funding for these four
districts to conserve existing water-shade areas, develop agricultural irrigation
infrastructure, and implement modern irrigation practices such as drip irrigation as
well as the renewal of traditional springs and water bodies.
The establishment of a Centre of Excellence for Horticulture and Floriculture
in Kalimpong is crucial, as the Darjeeling hills, Terai, and Dooars regions are hubs
of horticulture and floriculture in West Bengal. Yet nothing has been done to provide
fruit growers with improved technology, knowledge and skills regarding modern
fruit farming. A Centre of Excellence for Horticulture and Floriculture would enable
these growers to cultivate better varieties of fruits, such as Mandari Orange, Plum,
Pears, Pineapple, Litchi, and Passion Fruit, as well as flowers like Gladioli, Orchids,
Cacti, Amaryllis, Anthuriums, and Roses, ultimately enhancing their productivity. I
had previously requested the establishment of this Centre, and I was informed that
the proposal has been forwarded to the State Government for inclusion in the
Annual Action Plan (AAP). However, I am still awaiting a response from the Ministry
regarding this matter.
The inclusion of tea workers under the Kisan Samman Nidhi would be a significant
step towards recognizing their contributions and providing them with financial
support. Even though the tea garden workers do not get paid proper or do not get
21.03.2025 1451
any facilities, they are counted as "industrial workers". Hence, they are deprived of
many Central and State Govt Schemes, like Kisan Samman Nidhi Yojana. The tea
workers do not have right to land, so they cannot avail benefits of PM Awas Yojana.
I urge the Government that the tea workers should be included in the Kisan
Samman Nidhi Yojana. They too are farmers
Revival of potato seed farms is another long pending demand of the people
of my constituency. Prior to the 1990s, Darjeeling used to be a hub of potatoes not
only in India, but internationally. There were multiple potato seed farms that grew,
conserved and experimented with potato seeds from all across the world. Over
1200 varieties of potatoes, including both original and hybrid, were kept in seed
form in these seed farms and exported internationally.
Tragically after 1990s, the Government of West Bengal completely abdicated
their responsibilities and these potato seed farms were left fallow. These seed
farms can be revived, not only for potatoes but also for other fruits and vegetables.
Then, the Government should decide a Minimum Support Price for ginger
and cardamom crops. In the mountain regions, farming is very difficult and due to
geographic conditions two of the major crops from Darjeeling and Kalimpong are
cardamom and ginger. Yet these two crops do not get Minimum Support Price.
Providing MSP support to these two crops will help the farmers from the region to
regain their confidence and also encourage youths to continue growing these crops
in their traditional farms.
Support to Mandarin Orange and Iskus Farmers is the need of the hour. In
Darjeeling and Kalimpong region, we produce the best Mandarin Orange and
21.03.2025 1452
Iskus, also known as Chayote or squash. In 2023, the Central Government had
helped in exporting our oranges to Bahrain. However, in the past few years, the
production of Mandarin Oranges has declined by near 80 per cent. Despite such a
massive fall, there is not enough focus on finding sustainable solutions to support
the orange farmers.
Similarly, Iskus/ Chayote is the best food for diabetic patients. However, not
much has been done to promote this versatile vegetable that can cater to every
taste bud. I am requesting the Ministry to kindly take active steps to provide support
to Mandarin Orange farmers from our region. I also request the Ministry to examine
the export potential of Iskus and take necessary action towards their promotion and
international sale.
Support to pineapple farmers – Phansidewa, Naxalbari, Khoribari and
nearby places in Darjeeling Terai produce some of the best pineapples in India. In
North Bengal, pineapple farming is done in 20000 hectors and supports the
livelihood of close to 10 lakh individuals directly or indirectly. In Phansidewa block
alone, close to 60,000 individuals are dependent on pineapple farming for their
subsistence. Despite this, the West Bengal Government has failed to provide any
support to the pineapple farmers.
I, therefore, request the Ministry to kindly extend support to the pineapple
farmers for finding national and international markets through the North Eastern
Regional Agricultural Marketing Corporation (NERAMC).
The effective implementation of eNAM, along with enhanced digital
marketing initiatives, is crucial for achieving better outcomes. Majority of the
21.03.2025 1453
farmers in West Bengal are yet to derive the benefits of eNam and Digital Marketing
Initiatives started by the Government of India. Lakhs of computers, hardware and
requisite manpower are severely lacking in the Kisan Mandis of West Bengal.
The development of international marketing avenues is essential for
expanding business reach and enhancing global presence. Darjeeling is the only
district in India that shares border with four countries, namely, Nepal, Bhutan, China
and Bangladesh. It is also the gateway to North East India and South East Asian
countries.
Being an important agricultural zone, that produces the world’s famous
Darjeeling tea and oranges, Kalimpong flowers, Phansidewa Pineapples - agri-
export from here has a huge potential to grow. However, due to the lack of proper
marketing initiatives, and support to farmers to develop value chain, the farmers
here continue to be deprived of the benefits of markets that are readily available
internationally. Focus on developing proper marketing avenues for farm produce
from this region will help the farmers augment their income and improve their living
standards.
The establishment of the Krishi Vigyan Kendra in Darjeeling aims to enhance
agricultural practices and support local farmers. The district of Darjeeling despite
being an agricultural district is deprived of a Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK). While
assurances regarding the establishment of a KVK have been given in the past, it is
yet to manifest. I, therefore, request for KVK Darjeeling to be established without
any further delay.
Similar subsidies as are given to the North East Region should be given to
21.03.2025 1454
my region. The North Bengal districts of Darjeeling, Kalimpong, Alipurduar and
Jalpaiguri are geographically contingent to the North East region. The farmers,
livestock rearers, and fishermen from these districts face similar problems as those
from the North East States. There is lack of infrastructure, transportation,
connectivity, modern means of farming etc. Therefore, there is a strong case of
these districts to be brought under the same tax and subsidies/ grants regime as
the rest of North East states. This will go a long way in ensuring conservation of
farm-based livelihood system. It will also ensure great investment in agriculture
sector in the north Bengal region.

SHRI YADUVEER WADIYAR (MYSORE): I support the Demand for Grants for
the Agriculture Ministry. Under the visionary leadership of Prime Minister Narendra
Modi, numerous initiatives have been launched with the goal of modernising
agriculture, improving productivity, ensuring fair prices for farmers, and promoting
sustainability.
Our agricultural sector is a pillar of our economy and our society. The demand for
grants for agriculture for the year 2025-26 is not just about numbers; it is about
securing a future where our farmers are empowered, resilient, and prosperous. It
is about ensuring that the land that has sustained us for generations continues to
thrive for future generations.
The need for investment in agriculture has never been more pressing. If the vision
of our Prime Minister, that of Viksit Bharat, i.e. the complete economic development
of India, is to be fully realised, there is a need to ensure that our farmers are
supported, our ecosystems are protected, and our agricultural industry remains
competitive in a rapidly changing global economy. The demand for grants for
agriculture for 2025-26 is a crucial step toward achieving these goals.
My colleagues have highlighted the many benefits that the Prime Minister’s policies
and schemes have ensured for Indians, given the limited time, I would like to speak
to policies that particularly benefit my state of Karnataka.
As far as climate resilience and water management is concerned, our country,
particularly my State of Karnataka, and my constituency has faced severe climate
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1459
challenges over the years. Erratic rainfall, droughts, and floods have significantly
impacted the agricultural production in our state. To address these challenges, it is
essential that we invest in climate-resilient farming practices, including better water
management systems, rainwater harvesting, and the adoption of drought-resistant
crops.
The demand for grants includes funding for the implementation of efficient irrigation
systems like drip and sprinkler irrigation, which will ensure that our farmers are not
solely dependent on the unpredictable monsoon. My State of Karnataka also has
vast potential for rainwater harvesting. and it is time we enhance our efforts in this
area, ensuring sustainable water use for agriculture.
As far as infrastructure development and rural connectivity is concerned, the
development of rural infrastructure is another critical area of focus. Farmers need
access to better roads, storage facilities, and cold chains to prevent post-harvest
losses and improve market linkages. The demand for grants will support the
construction of rural roads and the development of integrated market systems
where farmers can sell their produce directly to consumers. eliminating
intermediaries and ensuring fair prices.
Additionally, the establishment of more warehouses, cold storage units, and value-
added processing units will help farmers store their crops during surplus seasons
and sell them at better prices during periods of demand.
As far as technology and innovation in agriculture is concerned, in today's rapidly
evolving world, technology is key to ensure that agriculture is adapting to changing
circumstances. We must prioritise the adoption of smart farming practices, such as
21.03.2025 1460
precision agriculture, use of drones for crop monitoring, and the incorporation of
Al-driven solutions to monitor soil health and predict crop yields.
To empower our farmers, we need to increase funding for agricultural research and
extension services, which will help farmers access knowledge on the best
practices, pest control, and disease management. Providing grants for setting up
agricultural technology centres will allow us to extend these innovations to our
farmers, improving productivity and sustainability
As far as financial support for farmers is concerned, our farmers face significant
financial challenges due to high input costs, fluctuating market prices, and climate-
induced risks. We must ensure that our farmers have access to affordable credit
and insurance to safeguard them from such vulnerabilities. The demand for grants
will include funds for extending subsidies on agricultural loans, promoting crop
insurance schemes, and providing grants for organic farming initiatives, which offer
high returns and reduce dependency on chemicals.
Some of the landmark schemes given by the Modi-led Governments need to be
highlighted as they have been the engines that have powered the revolution in the
agrarian sector.
As far as Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) is concerned, one of
the most significant steps taken by the Government to support our farmers is the
launch of the Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi (PM-KISAN) scheme. This
initiative provides direct financial assistance of Rs. 6,000 per year to small and
marginal farmers. It aims to ease the financial burden on farmers, especially in
times of distress, ensuring they have access to working capital for their farming
21.03.2025 1461
activities. By providing this support directly to the bank accounts of farmers, PM-
KISAN has played a pivotal role in ensuring that farmers receive timely financial
aid.
As far as PM Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) is concerned, agriculture is highly
dependent on weather conditions, and crop failure due to natural calamities has
been a recurring challenge. In response, the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana
(PMFBY) was introduced to protect farmers against such risks. This crop insurance
scheme provides affordable insurance coverage, offering financial protection to
farmers in the event of crop loss. With a record number of farmers enrolled, the
scheme has proven to be a safety net for millions of farmers, helping them recover
from the shocks of unpredictable weather patterns.
As far as Soil Health Management and National Mission on Sustainable Agriculture
is concerned, the Modi Government has placed a strong emphasis on the
sustainability of agriculture. Under the National Mission on Sustainable Agriculture
(NMSA), initiatives have been undertaken to promote organic farming, water-
efficient practices, and integrated nutrient management. Through Soil Health
Management, the Government has encouraged farmers to use organic and
balanced fertilisers, ensuring that soil health is maintained and agricultural
productivity remains sustainable.
India, and in particular, my State of Karnataka has many diverse agro-climatic
zones, from dry land farming areas to irrigated regions, all of which require tailored
approaches to ensure sustainable agricultural practices. The demand for grants will
prioritise soil health management through organic farming, agroforestry, and
21.03.2025 1462
sustainable land-use practices.
Investing in soil health will reduce the dependency on chemical fertilisers, ensuring
that our soil remains fertile for generations to come. Moreover, promoting organic
farming through grants will not only protect the environment but also help farmers
access premium markets for organic produce.
Lastly, I would like to, with your permissions, add a few specific demands to the
agriculture ministry with regards to my constituency.
Sir, Mysore district is famous for growing tobacco, but the tobacco farmers
are not recognised under Fasal Bhima Yojana. I request the ministry to provide
them this privilege, so that they are given the benefits that other farmers are given.
Secondly, given the fact that tobacco is a sin good, I would like to ask that the
Ministry have a specific plan to start research or pilot projects towards providing
alternate crops or alternate livelihood for those farmers who are farming crops such
as tobacco, which are used for the production of sin goods.
Lastly, I would like to thank the Finance Minister, Shrimati Nirmala Sitharaman, on
this occasion, for removing the GST on black pepper for the agriculturist. It has
benefited many farmers in the region of Kodagu and in Karnataka. This shows the
tremendous concern our Government and the Prime Minister have for our farmers.
Agriculture is the backbone of our country’s economy, with a significant population
of our country dependent on it for their livelihood. The Demand for Grants for 25-
26 ensures that there is a bright future for our agricultural sector.
I urge all Members of this House to support the Demand for Grants, as this is an
investment in our agricultural future and the well-being of millions of farmers across
21.03.2025 1463
the State.
Thank you.

SHRIMATI POONAMBEN MAADAM (JAMNAGAR): Thank you for giving me
this opportunity to express my views on the Demands for Grants presented by our
hon. Agriculture and Farmers Welfare Minister, Shri Shivraj Singh Chouhan ji.
Before delving into the details, I must first commend this government for its
unwavering commitment to our nation's agriculture sector. Under the visionary
leadership of our Hon'ble Prime Minister, Shri Narendra Modi ji, we have witnessed
remarkable achievements in agricultural productivity and sustainability. I take
immense pride in being part of an administration that has spearheaded
transformative reforms to drive growth in this vital sector of our nation.
Agriculture is the backbone of our economy. According to the Economic Survey,
the agriculture sector contributes approximately 16 per cent of the country's GDP
for FY24 at current prices and supports about 46.1 per cent of the population.
Guided by the principles of "Sabka Saath, Sabka Vikas, Sabka Vishwas, Sabka
Prayas," this Government has undertaken many initiatives to empower our
'Annadatas, enhance food security, and modernize the agricultural sector of our
nation. Over the years, our foodgrain production has seen remarkable growth,
rising from 252 million tonnes in 2014-15 to an estimated 332.3 million tonnes in
2023-24. To support our farmers financial needs, this government launched the
Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi Scheme to provide direct income support to
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1464
farmers. So far, over Rs.3.46 lakh crore has been disbursed to more than 11 crore
farmers, empowering them with financial stability without any middlemen involved.
In my constituency, 1.77 lakh farmers have benefited, with a total amount of
Rs.859.43 crore disbursed to support their agricultural needs.
To further support our farmers, the National Mission on Oilseeds is being
implemented to increase domestic production and reduce import dependence.
Additionally, in a push towards sustainable farming, this government actively
promotes Natural Farming and ensures farmers have access to affordable
fertilizers. The e-NAM initiative has integrated 1,410 mandis across the nation,
registering 1.79 crore farmers and 2.63 lakh traders. As of December 2024, trade
worth an impressive Rs. 4.01 lakh crore has been facilitated which has boosted
market access and the incomes of our farmers. The promotional efforts for millets
have increased production while institutional credit expansion and agricultural
research and development investments continue to transform the sector.
As emphasized by our hon. Finance Minister Shrimati Nirmala Sitharaman ji,
agriculture is first engine of India's development journey. This commitment can be
seen in the sixfold increase in the Union agriculture budget, which has grown from
Rs. 21,933.50 crore in 2013-14 to Rs. 1,37,757crore in 2025-26. This just goes to
show this Government's determination to empower our farmers and enhance the
agricultural sector.
A commendable step in this direction is the introduction of the Prime Minister Dhan-
Dhaanya Krishi Yojana, which will focus on uplifting 1.7 crore farmers across 100
districts with low productivity, moderate crop intensity and below-average credit
21.03.2025 1465
access. Furthering the vision for self-reliance in the agricultural sector, the launch
of the National Mission on High-Yielding Seeds will ensure access to high-yielding,
pest-resistant, and climate-resilient seed varieties. Moreover, the establishment of
a second National Gene Bank with 10 lakh germplasm lines will provide critical
conservation support for future food security.
The Mission for Cotton Productivity is another commendable initiative that will
enhance sustainability, promote extra-long staple cotton varieties, increase cotton
farmers' incomes, and ensure a steady supply of quality cotton for the nation's
traditional textile sector. Moreover, the increase of loan limits from Rs. 3 lakhs to
Rs. 5 lakhs under the Modified Interest Subvention Scheme, the establishment of
a Makhana Board in Bihar, the setting up of a 12.7 lakh metric ton Urea Plant in
Assam, and the introduction of a framework for sustainable harnessing of fisheries
from Indian Exclusive Economic Zone and High Seas are all strategic initiatives
that will strengthen India's agricultural foundation and ensure long-term growth for
our farmers.
Over the past decade, the remarkable transformation of our agriculture sector is
undeniable and stands as clear evidence of our nation's sustained progress. From
soil health cards and the financial inclusion of farmers to the use of drones for
pesticide and fertilizer spraying, every initiative introduced has been aimed at
boosting agricultural resilience. Building on this strong foundation, this Government
has laid out comprehensive and future-ready initiatives to boost agricultural
productivity and uplift the lives of our Annadatas. With a clear focus on
modernization, improved credit access, and the empowerment of women in
21.03.2025 1466
agriculture, along with digital integration and climate-resilient practices, we are on
the way to a self-reliant agricultural economy, bringing us closer to the vision of a
Viksit Bharat.
With that, I support the Demands for Grants for Agriculture and Farmers Welfare.
Thank you!

CAPTAIN BRIJESH CHOWTA (DAKSHINA KANNADA): Thank you for giving
me the opportunity to support the Demand for Grants for the Ministry of Agriculture
and Farmers Welfare.
Agriculture is the lifeline of our nation, sustaining nearly 58 per cent of India’s
population and contributing significantly to our economy. It is the hard work of our
farmers that feeds the country, drives rural employment, and ensures food security
for 1.4 billion Indians. This government, under the visionary leadership of Prime
Minister, Sri Narendra Modi Ji, has worked tirelessly to strengthen India’s
agricultural sector, ensuring farmers receive the dignity, respect, and prosperity
they deserve.
In this budget, our government has allocated Rs.1,37,757 crore to the Ministry of
Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare, which is 2.7 per cent of the Union Budget. Before
2014, Indian agriculture faced multiple challenges. There was no integrated vision
for the sector. Farmers struggled with inconsistent policies on subsidies,
procurement, and pricing. Only 42 per cent of the total cultivated land was under
irrigation, leaving the rest dependent on unpredictable monsoons. Farmers relied
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1467
on informal moneylenders due to restricted access to institutional credit, pushing
them into cycles of debt. Middlemen-controlled mandis led to unfair pricing and
exploitation of small farmers. Crop loss due to weather shocks or pest infestations
often pushed farmers into financial ruin. Lack of access to modern techniques and
scientific advancements limited productivity and income growth.
The Modi Government has implemented several flagship schemes to revolutionise
Indian agriculture, empowering farmers with financial stability, modern technology,
better infrastructure, and increased productivity. Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman
Nidhi scheme was launched in 2019 to provide direct income support to small and
marginal farmers. Over 11.8 crore farmers across India receive an annual benefit
of 26,000 in three instalments. Inn Karnataka, 55 lakh farmers have benefited,
receiving direct transfers into their bank accounts, ensuring financial security and
timely investment in inputs. Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana was launched in
2016. This crop insurance scheme protects farmers from yield losses due to natural
disasters. Over 42 crore farmers have been insured since inception, and claims
worth Rs.1.4 lakh crore have been paid. In Karnataka, in 2022-23 alone, 16.3 lakh
farmers in the State availed crop insurance, with claims exceeding Rs.3,500 crore.
Soil Health Card Scheme was introduced in 2015 to promote balanced fertiliser use
and soil conservation. Over 23 crore Soil Health Cards have been distributed,
improving soil productivity. In Karnataka, more than 1.5 crore farmers have
received soil health cards, leading to a 20-30 per cent reduction in chemical
fertilizer usage and higher yields. Kisan Credit Card loans provide easy credit to
farmers at four per cent interest with government support. Over eight crore farmers
21.03.2025 1468
have received Rs.24 lakh crore in loans. In Karnataka, 42 lakh farmers have availed
credit under this scheme, significantly reducing reliance on informal lenders. E-
National Agriculture Market was launched in 2016. E-NAM integrates wholesale
mandis across the country into a single online market. Over 1,361 mandis in 23
States have been linked, enabling 1.75 crore farmers to sell their produce
transparently. In Karnataka, 65 major mandis, including those in Mangalore,
Hassan, and Chikkamagaluru, are connected to e-NAM, ensuring fair pricing for
farmers.
The Government has introduced several new initiatives to enhance agricultural
productivity and farmers’ income. PM Dhan Dhaanya Krishi Yojana aims to benefit
1.7 crore farmers in districts with low productivity, improving irrigation, credit
access, and cropping intensity. National Mission on High Yielding Seeds has been
allocated Rs.100 crore. This mission will focus on developing climate-resilient and
pest-resistant seeds. By increased KCC Loan Limit , farmers can now avail up to
Rs.25 lakh under the Kisan Credit Card, up from the previous limit of Rs.3 lakh.
The coastal region of Dakshina Kannada is a vital agricultural hub, where
thousands of farmers depend on their land for sustenance and livelihood. However,
they face distinct challenges that threaten their productivity and income, requiring
targeted intervention and urgent support. Areca nut is a major cash crop in
Dakshina Kannada, supporting over 2 lakh farmers. However, the spread of Yellow
Leaf Disease (YLD) and other issues threatens productivity and farmer incomes. A
dedicated research centre in Karnataka will help develop disease-resistant
varieties and improved cultivation techniques and give way for Indian led research
21.03.2025 1469
based on local data and conditions. Many areca nut farmers rely solely on one crop,
increasing vulnerability to market fluctuations and disease outbreaks. Recent visits
from the Central Coffee Research Institute have confirmed the potential of coffee
as a viable crop in the region, especially in areas with favourable climatic
conditions. However, institutional support is lacking compared to neighboring
regions like Chikkamagaluru and Kodagu, which have received strong backing
from the government and the Coffee Board. While farmers have taken the initiative
through various collaborations, they are still in need of technical guidance,
subsidies, and access to quality saplings. Research studies have also suggested
that coffee could be integrated with existing areca nut plantations, offering a
sustainable inter-crop option. The Government should introduce incentives,
technical training, and financial support for coffee plantation expansion in Dakshina
Kannada. There should be increased allocation of PM-KISAN funds and enhanced
subsidy schemes for small and marginal farmers in coastal Karnataka. Expand
insurance coverage under PMFBY to cover cash crops like areca nut and coconut.
The future of Indian agriculture is bright under the leadership of Sri Narendra
Modi ji. From income support to crop insurance, irrigation projects to digital
marketplaces, and scientific advancements to credit expansion, every step taken
in the last ten years has been farmer-centric. The 2025-26 Budget takes this
commitment further, ensuring that agriculture remains profitable, sustainable, and
resilient. However, we must also address regional needs. I urge the Government
to support the farmers of Dakshina Kannada with financial and technical
assistance. The Government should establish the Areca Nut Research Centre to
21.03.2025 1470
tackle Yellow Leaf Disease. It should promote coffee cultivation to help diversify
and stabilize farm incomes. By standing with our farmers today, we secure the food
security, economic stability, and prosperity of our nation tomorrow. Jai Jawan, Jai
Kisan! Thank you.


MS. BANSURI SWARAJ (NEW DELHI): Hon. Speaker Sir, I rise today to strongly
support the Demands for Grants for the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers
Welfare, and to commend the dynamic leadership of hon. Prime Minister Shri
Narendra Modi ji and the Government of India for their relentless focus on
empowering our farmers, ensuring food security, and transforming Indian
agriculture into a more sustainable, resilient, and technologically-advanced sector.
Agriculture is the backbone of our nation, employing nearly 46 per cent of our
workforce and sustaining millions of lives. This year, the Government has allocated
Rs. 1,37,757 crore to the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare. This
allocation marks a significant step toward strengthening the welfare of farmers,
enhancing agricultural productivity, and advancing our agricultural policies to meet
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1474
both national and global challenges. The focus on a farmer-centric Budget
demonstrates Modi Government’s commitment to ensuring that India’s agricultural
ecosystem is not only efficient but also resilient in the face of emerging challenges.
A key highlight of this Budget is the continued success of the PM-Kisan Samman
Nidhi (PM-KISAN), which provides Rs. 6,000 annually to over 12 crore farmers.
With Rs. 63,500 crore allocated to PM-KISAN this year, it ensures that farmers
continue to receive timely financial assistance. Since its inception, this initiative has
disbursed Rs. 3.24 lakh crore directly into the bank accounts of farmers, creating a
positive impact on rural livelihoods and economic sustainability.
The Government has also substantially increased investments in agricultural
growth programmes, with Rs. 8,500 crore allocated to the Rashtriya Krishi Vikas
Yojana (RKVY), a 42 per cent increase from last year. Additionally, the Krishionnati
Yojana has received Rs. 8,000 crore, furthering the cause of agricultural
modernisation, research-driven innovations, and enhancing the overall productivity
of our farms.
One of the cornerstones of our agricultural development is ensuring crop protection
and risk management, and the Government has shown exceptional foresight in
strengthening these areas. The PM Fasal Bima Yojana has been allocated Rs.
12,242 crore to protect crops from unpredictable weather patterns, ensuring that
farmers receive timely compensation for their losses. With over 68 crore farmer
applications insured, this scheme provides security to farmers across the country,
safeguarding an area of 4,803 lakh hectares of crops.
The Modi Government is equally committed to ensuring that India achieves self-
21.03.2025 1475
sufficiency in food production. The launch of the National Mission on High-Yielding
Seeds with an allocation of Rs. 100 crore aims to develop more climate-resilient
and pest-resistant seed varieties, improving the sustainability of farming practices.
Furthermore, with Rs. 1,000 crore allocated to the Mission for Atmanirbharta in
pulses, the Government is working towards reducing dependency on pulse imports
and enhancing domestic production of crucial crops such as Tur, Urad, and
Masoor. This will improve food security, stabilise prices, and increase farmers’
incomes.
The importance of water in agriculture cannot be overstated, and this Government
has been proactive in expanding irrigation and water conservation efforts. The
Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchai Yojana PMKSY) continues to receive robust funding,
with the expanded micro-irrigation coverage benefiting over 48 lakh farmers. The
Atal Bhujal Yojana has been allocated Rs. 8,500 crore this year to support ground
water conservation in drought-prone areas, while the Per Drop More Crop initiative
encourages efficient water use and reduces water wastage.
Our commitment to reducing post-harvest losses is evident in the Rs. 1,00,000
crore investment in the Agriculture Infrastructure Fund (AIF). This fund is being
used to develop critical infrastructure such as warehouses, cold storage, and
modern processing units. These investments will reduce wastage, ensure timely
delivery to markets, and provide farmers with better pricing opportunities.
The Government has also recognised the importance of sustainable farming
practices, and initiatives like the PM-PRANAM Scheme, are promoting balanced
fertiliser use to reduce over-reliance on chemicals. Furthermore, the Soil Health
21.03.2025 1476
Card Scheme, which has benefited 23.6 crore farmers, continues to provide
guidance on soil fertility and crop-specific nutrient requirements, fostering long-
term so il health.
The e-NAM platform has successfully linked 1,361 mandis, enabling farmers to
directly connect with buyers, reducing middlemen and ensuring better price
realisation. The Government has also committed Rs. 6,941 crore to the PM-AASHA
scheme, which provides price support to oilseeds, pulses, and copra, ensuring that
farmers get fair compensation for their produce.
However, we cannot ignore the increasing threat of climate change, which is having
a profound impact on Indian agriculture. Unpredictable weather patterns, rising
temperatures, and extreme weather events are making farming increasingly
challenging. In response, the Government must accelerate the adoption of climate-
resilient agricultural practices, such as drought-resistant crops, precision farming,
and efficient water management systems. It is critical that we support initiatives like
the National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture (NMSA) and the promotion of
organic and regenerative farming, which will help mitigate the adverse effects of
climate change while creating new income opportunities for farmers.
As the Member of Parliament representing the vibrant New Delhi Constituency, I
would also like to highlight the growing potential of urban farming and hydroponics
in transforming our cities into more sustainable and self-sufficient environments. In
a rapidly urbanising India, where space is limited, urban farming offers an
innovative solution to boost local food production, reduce transportation costs, and
minimise our environmental footprint.
21.03.2025 1477
New Delhi, with its population density and limited agricultural space, stands to
benefit immensely from the integration of hydroponics and vertical farming
systems. These methods use significantly less water compared to traditional
farming and can produce high yields of vegetables and fruits in small spaces, such
as rooftops and balconies. This not only contributes to urban food security but also
promotes the efficient use of urban resources.
The Government has already begun recognising the importance of sustainable
agriculture in cities. We must now accelerate the adoption of smart farming
techniques such as hydroponics, aquaponics, and rooftop gardening. In New Delhi,
we can create model projects in collaboration with local authorities, NGOs, and
startups to establish hydroponics systems in urban communities. These initiatives
could also serve as learning centres for residents and farmers, encouraging the
next generation to embrace modern farming methods.
By promoting urban farming and hydroponics, we can also create green spaces,
enhance local biodiversity, and reduce the carbon footprint associated with the
long-distance transportation of food. This is an important step toward building a
more sustainable and resilient New Delhi, a city where every citizen has access to
fresh and nutritious produce.
I urge the Government to consider further investments in promoting such urban
agriculture initiatives, especially in metropolitan regions like New Delhi, where the
population is rapidly growing and the need for innovative solutions to food security
is becoming more pressing.
In conclusion, this Budget reflects Modi Government’s steadfast commitment to
21.03.2025 1478
securing the future of agriculture and the welfare of farmers. The allocations and
schemes outlined are forward-thinking, comprehensive, and in line with the Prime
Minister’s vision of empowering farmers and modernising agriculture. I urge all
Members to support the Demands for Grants for the Ministry of Agriculture and
Farmers Welfare, as these investments will strengthen our rural economy, uplift the
lives of millions of farmers, and ensure food security for our nation.
Jai Hind! Jai Bharat!

SHRI SUKANTA KUMAR PANIGRAHI (KANDHAMAL): It is with immense pride
and responsibility that I rise today to discuss the transformative vision embodied in
the Union Agriculture Budget 2025-26. This budget is not just a financial document;
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1483
it is a roadmap for the future of India’s agricultural sector, a sector that forms the
backbone of our economy and the lifeline of our rural population.
The Union Agriculture Budget 2025-26 is unique in its approach, as it places
agriculture at the core of India’s economic development strategy. By positionng
agriculture as the “first engine of development”, this budget acknowledges the
sector’s critical role in ensuring food security, promoting rural prosperity, and
driving inclusive growth. It is a bold and forward-looking framework that addresses
long-standing challenges while embracing innovation and sustainability.
Today, I will highlight the key features of this landmark budget, its transformative
initiatives, and its potential to reshape India’s agricultural landscape.
The allocation of Rs.1,37,757 crore to the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers’
Welfare reflects a strategic reorientation of our economic priorities. While this
allocation represents a slight reduction compared to previous years, it is important
to note that allied sectors such as fisheries, animal husbandry, and dairying have
received a significant boost with a 37 per cent increase in funding to Rs.7,544 crore.
This strategic shift underscores the Government’s recognition that agriculture is not
an isolated sector but part of a broader ecosystem that includes allied activities. By
diversifying income streams for farmers and reducing dependency on traditional
crops, this budget lays the foundation for a more resilient and sustainable rural
economy.
One of the standout initiatives in this budget is the Prime Minister Dhan-Dhaanya
Krishi Yojana. This district-level program targets 100 districts with low agricultural
productivity. It focuses on enhancing productivity through crop diversification,
21.03.2025 1484
improving irrigation facilities, and strengthening post-harvest storage infrastructure.
By addressing local challenges with tailored solutions, this initiative represents a
paradigm shift in agricultural policy. It moves away from a one-size-fits-all approach
to a more nuanced strategy that leverages existing schemes while focusing on
specific geographies. This initiative has the potential to uplift underperforming
regions and bring them into the mainstream of agricultural development.
In this era of rapid technological advancement, digital transformation is no longer
optional it is imperative. The Union Budget 2025-26 introduces several digital
initiatives aimed at modernizing Indian agriculture:
 A digital crop survey will be conducted in 400 districts to gather real-time
data on crop patterns and productivity.
 The integration of JanSamarth-based Kisan Credit Cards will streamline
credit delivery to farmers and improve financial inclusion in rural areas.
These initiatives promise to revolutionize agricultural planning by enabling data-
driven decision-making. They will also empower farmers with better access to credit
facilities and reduce delays in financial assistance.
India has long been dependent on imports for key pulses such as tur, urad, and
masoor. To address this vulnerability, the budget introduces the Mission for
Aatmanirbharta in Pulses of Rs.1,000 crore allocation with a procurement
guarantee mechanism. This initiative aims to stabilize prices while ensuring self-
sufficiency in pulse production. It aligns seamlessly with the broader vision of an
Atmanirbhar Bharat, reducing import dependency and enhancing food security.
Another critical initiative is the National Mission on High Yielding Seeds having
21.03.2025 1485
Rs.100 crore allocation, which focuses on developing climate-resilient seed
varieties. By addressing yield gaps and promoting advanced seed technologies,
this mission positions Indian agriculture to better withstand climate challenges
while boosting productivity.
The Mission for Cotton Productivity seeks to enhance India’s position as a global
leader in premium cotton production. By adopting advanced technologies and
improving quality standards, this initiative creates synergies between agriculture
and industrial competitiveness, particularly in textiles a sector vital for exports.
One of the most innovative regional initiatives in this budget is the establishment of
a Makhana Board in Bihar. Makhana (fox nut) is an important crop with immense
nutritional value and export potential. The board will focus on improving production
techniques, processing infrastructure, and global marketing strategies for makhana
This initiative exemplifies how regional strengths can be leveraged for national
growth. By promoting makhana as a superfood on global platforms, we not only
boost farmers’ incomes but also enhance India’s reputation as an exporter of high-
quality agricultural products.
The emphasis on sustainable fisheries development -- particularly in island
territories – demonstrates a commitment to utilizing marine resources responsibly.
Fisheries are not only vital for nutritional security but also hold significant export
potential.
A 37 per cent increase in allocations for animal husbandry and dairying reflects the
Government’s focus on diversification within agriculture. Livestock farming offers
an alternative income stream for farmers while reducing dependency on traditional
21.03.2025 1486
crops.
The Comprehensive Programme for Vegetables and Fruits aims to promote
cluster-based production near consumption centres. By addressing post-harvest
losses and transportation challenges, this initiative ensures better prices for
farmers while meeting urban demand efficiently.
Enhanced Financial Support for Farmers
Recognizing rising input costs and the need for modern farming practices, this
budget increases the loan limit under the Modified Interest Subvention Scheme
from Rs.3 lakh to Rs.5 lakh per farmer. This move empowers farmers to invest in
productivity-enhancing technologies such as mechanization and precision farming
tools.
The budget emphasizes natural farming practices that reduce dependency on
chemical inputs while ensuring environmental sustainability. These efforts align
with global trends toward eco-friendly agriculture.
Investments in warehousing, logistics, and processing aim to minimize post-
harvest losses while improving market access for farmers – a critical step toward
building resilience against climate-induced disruptions.
While this budget lays out an ambitious roadmap for agricultural transformation, its
success hinges on effective implementation at every level – national, State, and
local. Some key challenges include:
Ensuring timely disbursement of funds to avoid delays in project execution.
Raising awareness among farmers about new schemes through grassroots
campaigns.
21.03.2025 1487
Addressing policy bottlenecks that hinder market reforms.
Building capacity among farmers to adopt advanced technologies introduced
under various missions.
Collaboration between Central and State Governments will be crucial to
overcoming these challenges and ensuring that benefits reach smallholders the
backbone of Indian agriculture.
The Union Agriculture Budget 2025-26 represents a bold vision that combines
traditional strengths with modern innovations to transform Indian agriculture into
a globally competitive sector. By focusing on digital integration, climate
resilience, regional specialization, allied sectors like animal husbandry and
fisheries, financial inclusion through enhanced credit access, as sustainability
measures like natural farming practices – this budget creates a comprehensive
framework for growth.
It is not just about numbers or allocations; it is about empowering our farmers with
tools they need to thrive in an ever-changing world – whether through technology
adoption of beer market linkages.
This budget calls upon all stakeholders – farmers themselves; policymakers;
researchers; private enterprises; cooperatives; State Governments – to work
collaboratively toward achieving higher productivity levels while ensuring rural
prosperity remains at its heart.
Let us seize this opportunity together so we can transform aspirations into reality –
for our farmers today – and generations yet unborn tomorrow!
21.03.2025 1488

SUSHRI S. JOTHIMANI (KARUR): Thank you for allowing me to express my
views on the Demands for Grants for Agriculture. At the outset I would like to remind
the Union Government the heartbreaking images of thousands of farmers marching
to Delhi—leaving behind their fields, their families, their livelihoods—simply to
demand their rights. And how did this government respond? With tear gas, water
cannons, and barricades, as if our farmers were enemies, not the backbone of our
nation. The Prime Minister often says that farmers are one of the four “castes”
whose upliftment will drive India’s progress. But what he didn't tell us was that his
idea of "upliftment" includes repression, neglect, and broken promises. Back in
2013, when farmers came to Delhi in protest, the UPA government sent a
delegation of six cabinet ministers to meet them, listen to their demands, and set
up committees to address their concerns. But today, under the BJP regime, farmers
must die by suicide, fast unto death, or face state-sponsored brutality just to be
heard. And still, their cries go unanswered. You make only tall claims; You boast
about agricultural growth but the reality tells a different story.
Under UPA I, agriculture grew at 4.2% per year.
Under UPA II, it was 3.7% per year.
Under Modi 1.0, it dropped to 2.9%.
Under Modi 2.0, it is just 3.5%.
This is because we had focussed on Agriculture and supported the farmers but you
are running a ‘suit boot sarkar’.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1489
The number speaks for itself. In the last 10 years, this government has written off
Rs. 16.35 lakh crore in bad loans—Rs. 9 lakh crores for large industries alone. It’s
the figure given by the Finance Minister to a question in this house.
Moreover, in 2019 they slashed corporate taxes by 1.6 Lakh crore per year,
handing around Rs. 10 Lakh crores to big businesses over six years. When it
comes to their crony friends, this government is generous beyond measure. But
what about our farmers?
50% of agricultural households are in debt. Since this government came to power,
over 1 lakh farmers have died by suicide. And yet, the Modi government refuses to
waive farm loans. | ask this government—who is feeding this nation? Is it Ambani
and Adani, or is it our farmers and labourers, who toil in the fields under the
scorching sun and pouring rain?
Another group that the Prime Minister conveniently remembers when it suits him is
women. But unsurprisingly, he has abandoned women farmers too. 80% of
economically active women work in agriculture.
They make up 48% of self-employed farmers. Yet they own less than 15% of land.
Why? Because this government refuses to recognize them as “farmers.” Instead,
they are labelled as "cultivators" or “agricultural labourers,” cutting them off from
benefits like crop insurance, subsidies, and institutional credit. The unemployment
of women is highest in the country including farm sector.
If the PM is truly concerned about Nari Shakti, why has he failed every single
woman working in our fields?
In 2016, this government promised to double farmers’ income by 2022. But today,
21.03.2025 1490
that promise has vanished—missing from the PM’s so-called "guarantees."
However, do you know what has doubled. The number of Indian billionaires. Their
total wealth has tripled. This is Modi’s “New India’—where the rich get richer and
the farmers get poorer. In November 2021, Hon'ble Prime Minister personally
promised to agitating farmers for a legal guarantee of MSP. But now it’s a forgotten
promise. Because Farmers don’t buy electoral bonds for BJP. I just want to ask a
simple question: Every Industrial product has MSP on it. Why not an agricultural
product? Despite all the noise about PM Fasal Bima Yojana, let’s face reality. The
real beneficiaries of this scheme are private insurance companies, not farmers.
Farmers don’t get compensation on time. It is a mystery that how the insurance law
has drafted to benefit the insurance companies, not the farmers. No body knows
how these insurance companies who gets paid and who doesn’t and on what basis.
Even its difficult for Hon Minister to understand! We four MPs together tried our
best in DISHA committee meeting.
After centuries of British rule and devastating famines, the then Hon’ PM Indira
Gandhi Ji with her vision and courage along with the then Agriculture Minister C.
Subramaniam, and Scientist Dr. M.S. Swaminathan ushered in the Green
Revolution and ensure the food security. Indira Gandhi's vision and her team's hard
work drove the Green Revolution, securing surplus grains. But | Caution you—
neglecting farmers and agriculture risks our food security.
We need a Second Green Revolution—one that continue to ensure food security,
raise farmers and farm labourers’ incomes, and makes agriculture sustainable.
This means:
21.03.2025 1491
Expanding MSP to cover all major crops.
Strengthening irrigation and rural infrastructure.
Increasing financial assistance for FPOs—one in every Panchayat.
Recognizing women farmers and granting them equal rights. Making crop
insurance transparent and farmer-centric. Encouraging diversification towards
high-value crops. Before | conclude, I would like to remind the Hon'ble Agriculture
Minister about two pending requests from my constituency to take immediate
action. Establishment of cold storage facilities in Vedasandur, Karur, Kadavur,
Manapparai, and Viralimalai. Action on the e-NAM proposal - Chinnadharapuram
regulated market storage godown at Kuppam Village, K.Paramathi block. Our
farmers are not asking for charity. They are asking for their fair share—fair prices,
market and institutional support, modern technology, and financial security. This
government must stop hiding behind slogans, theatrics, and corporate favouritism.
It is time to walk the talk. It is time to deliver on the promises made to the farmers
of this country. If this government does not act now, history will remember this
Government—not for their tall claims, but for their betrayal of India’s farmers. Thank
you.
SHRI DAGGUMALLA PRASADA RAO (CHITTOOR): I rise today to discuss the
Demands for Grants under the control of the Ministry of Agriculture for 2025-26.
Under the leadership of Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi and Finance Minister
Shrimati Nirmala Sitharaman, the Union Budget reaffirms its commitment to
farmers’ welfare. With a 3.89 per cent increase from the previous year, it introduces
new schemes to strengthen India’s agricultural sector.
21.03.2025 1492
Today, I would like to talk about the three critical pillars of agriculture:
‘Farmer’, ‘Finance’, and ‘Future’. These fundamental components shape the
policies that will define the destiny of our agrarian economy.
I come to ‘farmer’. The PM-KISAN scheme has been a gamechanger, with
over Rs. 3.46 lakh crore disbursed to 11 crore farmers across the country in 18
instalments. In Andhra Pradesh alone, Rs. 836 crore has been transferred to more
than 41 lakh farmers. However, there are two areas where we can further enhance
this initiative. Over 50 per cent of the Andhra Pradesh’s agricultural workforce
comprises of tenant farmers. Recognizing them under PM-KISAN will ensure
equitable benefits. The State Government, under Chief Minister Nara Chandrababu
Naidu, has already promised it by recognising the tenant farmers through the
Tenant Farmer Protection Act. And I urge the Centre to come up with a similar
initiative.
The PM-KISAN was launched in 2019. The scheme provides Rs. 6,000 per
year. Given the successful implementation over six years, I urge the Government
to increase this amount to account for inflation and ensure greater financial security
for our farmers.
Now, I come to the second pillar, that is, ‘Finance’. A major achievement of
this Government has been an exponential rise in institutional credit flow to
agriculture from Rs. 8.5 lakh crore in 2014-15 to Rs. 25.48 lakh crore in 2023-24.
This year’s Union Budget has further reinforced this commitment by increasing the
Kisan Credit Card loan limit from Rs. 3 lakh to Rs. 5 lakh.
However, a critical challenge remains for informal lending. Nearly 30-40 per
21.03.2025 1493
cent of farmers still rely on private moneylenders due to stringent CIBIL score
requirements for agricultural loans in some banks. This must be addressed. The
farmers should not be denied access to formal credit based on credit scores. A
foolproof implementation of relaxed credit norms will ensure more farmers benefit
from institutional loans and break from high-interest informal borrowing.
The third pillar is future. As the global food demand surges, a quiet yet
profound shift is underway. Two out of every five Indian farm workers have moved
away from agriculture in the past three decades. How do we secure our agricultural
future?
The answer lies in AgriTech. Encouraging AgriTech startups and
implementing modern technology-driven solutions will enhance productivity,
efficiency, and profitability for our farmers.
A visionary approach to revitalizing agriculture could follow the model of 'One
Family, One Entrepreneur', introduced by our CM Nara Chandrababu Naidu in
Andhra Pradesh. At the national level, this could be ‘One Farming Family, One
Agripreneur’, ensuring entrepreneurial growth in rural households while securing
India’s agricultural future.
Now, I would like to highlight three critical initiatives in the Union Budget.
First is the Cotton Technology Mission. Second is the National Mission on
High-Yielding Seeds.
These two will encourage agriculture in this country.
Further, I have one point that is related to Chittoor of Andhra Pradesh. It is
about mangoes.
21.03.2025 1494
Andhra Pradesh is a significant mango-producing State in India with districts
like Krishna, Chittoor and Godavari regions known for mango cultivation,
particularly the Banganapalle variety, and there is a production of around 45-50
lakh tons annually.
Totapuri is grown in Chittoor district. Everywhere it is grown for domestic
purpose. But in Chittoor district, it is produced for the pulp purpose. The mango
farmers are facing problems now.
So, I would urge the Government to support mango farmers who are
experiencing a crisis due to lack of remunerative prices for their produce as a result
of the adverse climatic conditions for mango production. There is an urgent need
to extend more support to the mango farmers and mango-based industries in
Chittoor district.
Being a Member of Parliament in Lok Sabha, I have already given the
representation to the hon. Agriculture Minister to set up a National Mango Board to
solve all the issues relating to mango and it will give a Constitutional platform to
address the much required initiatives for the sake of farmers.
I have one more issue. For export, there is a hurdle in the European
countries. One of the hurdles is Ukraine war because of that we are not able to
export the pulp. Another hurdle is European countries where they put more import
duty on Indian products compared to other countries.
So, I urge the Ministry of Commerce to look into this matter.
