
SHRI SANJAY HARIBHAU JADHAV (PARBHANI): Hon. Chairman Sir, I rise to
participate in the discussion on Demands for Grants of the Ministry of Agriculture
for the year 2025-26. I would like to draw Govt’s attention towards various issues
related to farmers’ welfare. If we look at the farming expenses, total yield and the
prices which farmers get, it is quite visible that it is a non-profitable and worthless
business. Our Prime Minister Narendra Modi ji promised that farmers income
would be doubled. But, he could not do it. Now, Rs. 500 is given to farmers in the
name of Kisan Samman Yojana but, not the remunerative prices for his agricultural
produces. This is a very important issue. No bank is ready to provide loans to
farmers due to low CIBIL score. Farmers cannot get education loan for their
children. Many big industrialist, corporate houses and businessmen default the
loans worth crores of rupees and this Govt. write off their loans happily. But, when
it comes to farmers, this Govt. is not ready to take any responsibility.
I would like to draw Hon. Minister’s attention towards an important issue.
There were many cases of heavy downpour and cloudburst in the month of
September in Maharashtra and Govt. had promises to provide Advance insurance
bonus benefits of Rs. 335.90 crore, but that did not happen. Insurance companies
are minting money through crop insurance business but, the farmers are still
waiting for insurance benefits. This Govt. has ignored them completely. It is my
request to you that you must support them in this hour of pain and distress.
Maharashtra is an agriculture dominated State and 3.25 lakh hectare of land
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
21.03.2025 1499
is not cultivable as it is being utilized for industrial purposes. It is a need of the
hour, to think about it. Hon. Chauhan ji, your Govt. has started this PM crop
insurance Scheme, but there is a rampant corruption in this scheme and I can
provide you all the proofs and evidences. In my distinct 11221 people have taken
the benefits of this scheme through bogus insurance and Govt. has not taken action
in this direction. Those who are eligible do not get the benefits but the so-called
farmers are benefitting out of it. I have brought the complete list of these 11221
bogus beneficiaries and I would like to hand it over to you.
The Swaminathan Committee Report should be implemented immediately
when BJP was not in power, they used to talk about this Report. It is now more
than 11 years since Modi Govt. came to power but nothing has been done in this
regard. Big businessmen have looted the public money in the name of corporate
loans but this Govt. has no money to waive off poor farmers’ loans. So, I request
Prime Minster and Agriculture Minister to take steps in this connection. Hon. Ex-
Prime Minister of India Man Mohan Singh ji declared a waiver for farmers’ loan.
Since then, nothing of this sort has been done. Therefore, I would like to request
you to take an initiative in this direction. You will get blessings of poor and needy
farmers.

SHRI K. RADHAKRISHNAN (ALATHUR): Thank you, Sir. Sir, I oppose the
demands for grants of the Ministry of Agriculture and farmer’s welfare.
The reason why I oppose this is that there is nothing big in this budget that
protect the interests of farmers and farm workers. The government has the
responsibility to help the farmers in the country who constitute a major chunk of our
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Malayalam.
21.03.2025 1518
population. The farmers and farm workers play a crucial role in the wealth creation
of the country. Whether our governments are looking into their problems by keeping
this fact in mind is to be examined. When we examine this, we can find that their
life becomes difficult in every passing year. Many respected members who spoke
here pointed out this reality that the life of farmers is going through hardships.
Former Chief Minister of Punjab and MP who spoke just now has pointed out the
farmer’s agitation and the difficulties they face. The hon’ble minister should
examine whether we have given adequate provision in the budget by considering
this aspect.
Sir, in 2024-25 budget the allocation was Rs. 1,20,775 crore and in the
revised estimate this amount has gone up to Rs. 1,38,980 crore. But in this budget
the total allocation is Rs. 1,24,341 crore. That means, when we compare the last
year’s budget, the revised estimate, and the present budget allocation, we can see
that the amount has decreased by around Rs. 14,000 crore. In the present
scenario, whether we will be able to solve the problems of farmers with this amount
needs examination. Sir we are neglecting the farmers and the agriculture sector.
We must foresee the consequences and the disaster of such a negligence. When
the economic slowdown happened throughout the world, we were able to withstand
the same to an extent. How did we manage this? When the big powers like US had
suffered due to crisis how did we tide over the situation? When we examine that
we could see the importance of our agriculture sector. Over 52 per cent of our
population is depending upon the agriculture for their livelihood. We have
agriculture-based industry and agriculture-based economy. Just because of that
21.03.2025 1519
agriculture-based economy we could withstand the big economic crisis. So, the
main pillar that support our economy is the agriculture sector. Hence, we have to
give adequate allocation to protect such an important sector, but sadly every year
that allocation is going down, this is what we can see.
Sir, we cannot forget the agriculture while discussing about our economy.
We should intervene to protect every important agriculture produce. For that, we
should be able to provide timely help to the farmers. The main reason for the
farmers moving away from the agriculture is the rising input cost and the non-
availability of reasonable prices for their produce. So, we must have the mechanism
to ensure the reasonable price for their produce. That is why the hon’ble members
who had spoken here demanded the need for minimum support price. The state I
represent, Kerala have been asking for many years about minimum support price.
Many members have referred about Sri M.S. Swaminathan.
16.17 hrs (Shri Jagdambika Pal in the Chair)
HON. CHAIRPERSON : Kindly conclude now. You have taken more than five
minutes. Just put your demand.
SHRI K. RADHAKRISHNAN : Till now we could not fix the MSP as per his
recommendations. Farmers in Kerala are not getting enough price for their produce
in accordance with the input cost, and that is the reason for the agriculture crisis in
the state. So, to provide the minimum support price and to ensure that the famers
get the same, Centre government should intervene in all possible way. Since the
farmers who cultivate rubber, coconut, and paddy are in crisis centre government
must give necessary assistance to them. Most of the time Kerala does not get the
21.03.2025 1520
share from the centre. The state government have been demanding for quite some
time to increase the minimum support price of rubber to Rs 200. The centre should
accept this demand or else there will be a great crisis in agriculture sector. Sir, the
centre must take a stand to help the states like Kerala. Recently there was a big
natural calamity in the state and the damage was very heavy and there was crisis
in the agriculture sector. The state government approached the centre for
compensation on several occasions, but the centre is not helping. So, I demand
the centre should give assistance to the state and to take steps to help whenever
the natural calamity takes place.

(cid:174)ीमती द(cid:181)गुबाती पुरदं (cid:309)े री (राजाम(cid:198)ु दरी) : सर, म(cid:167) आपक(cid:236) आभारी ह(cid:243)ं िक आपने मुझ े िकसान और

21.03.2025 1526
बजटरी िडमांड्स फॉर (cid:250)ांट्स अंडर ए(cid:250)ीक(cid:208)चर एडं फाम(cid:170)स(cid:170) वेलफे यर के समथ(cid:170)न म(cid:164) खड़ी ह(cid:242)ई ह(cid:243)ं ।
The strength and vitality of our farmers is closely linked to the growth and
progress of our country. Today agriculture engages about 46 per cent of the
workforce and its contribution to the GDP of our country is around 18 per cent.
Despite this fact, the share of agriculture is progressively declining in our economy.
But the importance of this sector goes beyond this indicator.
About three-quarters of Indian families depend on rural incomes, and a large
section of the poor actually lives in rural areas. Over and above, the food security
of our country is largely dependent on the agriculture sector. Therefore, realizing
the potential, the relevance and the importance of the agricultural sector, innovation
in farming practices and constant focus on high-yielding crops should be the focal
point. It is also important to address the multifaceted challenges that this sector
actually faces.
When we look back, in the past one decade since 2014, various issues that
have been raised by my colleagues on the other side of the House have been
continuously addressed time and again. There is an increase in the allocation of
budget. This year the allocated budget is Rs.1,37,757 crore in comparison to
Rs.1,22,528 crore in the previous budget. It clearly emphasizes our commitment to
the fact that we would like to double the income of our farmers.
Credit availability is the first priority of the Government. It actually helps the
farmers to increase the productivity to meet the changing market demands and
also to brave the changing climatic conditions. Today, surveys have revealed that
only about 30.3 per cent of the agrarian families have access to institutional credit.
21.03.2025 1527
According to the latest report, which is normally done keeping the rural household
in consideration, in 2019 it had come to the fore that over half of the farming families
are caught in a quagmire of debt and each household has an outstanding debt of
Rs.74,121. This debt burden normally leads to a vicious cycle of poverty and in
extreme cases could even lead to farmer suicides. Here, the Kisan Credit Card
(KCC) has actually transformed the availability of institutional loans for our farmers.
According to a 2024 report, we have 7.75 crore Kisan Credit Cards in the
country and loan availed on these Kisan Credit Cards is around Rs.9.81 lakh crore.
In the past one decade, an amount of around Rs.1.24 lakh crore has been released
as interest subsidy on the Kisan Credit Cards.
The proportion of small and marginal farmers who have been availing
institutional credit today has risen from 57 percent in 2014-15 to 76 percent in 2022-
23. Therefore, we need to understand and realise that the increase in the available
amount under the Kisan Credit Cards, which has been raised from Rs. 3 lakh to
Rs. 5 lakh, is a transformational change and is going to support large sections of
our farming society.
Today, India is home to 18 per cent of the world’s land but houses only about
four per cent of the global water resources. To top it all, climate change, due to
global warming, is a reality and is here to stay. The dependency of the farming
community or the over-reliance on monsoons has hampered our agricultural
growth. According to the reports of 2022-23, only 52 per cent of our cultivable land
has access to irrigation facilities. It is estimated that due to global warming, about
15 per cent to 18 per cent of income would be reduced for families that possess
21.03.2025 1528
land, while nearly 25 per cent of income would be reduced for those farming
families that do not have land. Over-pumping of water has led to underwater
depletion, and water logging has increased soil salinity. Heavy rains have caused
soil erosion. Therefore, research and extension play a very important part, and we
need to welcome the National Mission on Higher Yielding Seeds and the Gene
Bank, which is going to house about 10 lakh germ plasms. It is a very welcome
measure.
Under the National Mission for High Yielding Seeds, research will be
conducted to develop seeds that are resilient to both pests and climatic conditions,
and at the same time, would be able to withstand floods, droughts, or saline
conditions in our country. Therefore, all of us across the board must welcome this
measure. Regarding the Gene Bank, this would ensure that… (Interruptions)
HON. CHAIRPERSON : Kindly conclude.
SHRIMATI DAGGUBATI PURANDESWARI : Sir, give me two minutes.
HON. CHAIRPERSON: One minute.
SHRIMATI DAGGUBATI PURANDESWARI: Then I will have to lay this down on
the Table.
HON. CHAIRPERSON: There are so many speakers. The Minister has to reply at
5 p.m.
SHRIMATI DAGGUBATI PURANDESWARI: I will lay my speech on the Table, but
I would like to raise a couple of concerns here, especially regarding the Krishna
water distribution. The Bachawat Tribunal was constituted in 1969 to settle the
water disputes between the three States of Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and
21.03.2025 1529
Maharashtra. About 811 TMC was apportioned to Andhra Pradesh, and later, after
the bifurcation of the State, 512 TMC was allocated to Andhra Pradesh and 299
TMC was allocated to Telangana. This allocation was based on the command area
as well as the existing utilization mechanisms. Today, to revisit the terms of
reference and to suggest going back retrospectively to share the water would be
doing injustice to Andhra Pradesh, so I request the Government to look into it.
Lastly, the word ‘tariff’ has never made it to the news as much as it has today
after the President of the United States, Mr. Donald Trump, announced reciprocal
tariffs. The products imported into the U.S. from countries with which it has a trade
deficit would negatively impact every sector in our country. … (Interruptions)
In January 2025, US Department of Agriculture in its paper had noted that
India is confronting production challenges in agriculture due to climate change and
in this situation, US could find a good market if exploited. Hence, we need to tread
the soil with great caution.
HON. CHAIRPERSON: Thank you. Your point has been well taken.

SHRI DURAI VAIKO (TIRUCHIRAPPALLI): Hon. Chairman Sir, Vanakkam. With

Government has not waived off farm loans which shows how our farmers are
betrayed. The farmers of our country are agitating against this Union Government.
Minimum Support Price, MSP for their agricultural produce is one of their pertinent
demands and they want this MSP to be fixed and legalized. During the last 10 years
the input cost along with the prices of seeds and fertilizers have gone upwards two
to three times. But the prices of agricultural products have not gone up. Our farmers
do not get remunerative prices for their produce. Fixing the right price for the
agricultural produce is still a mirage. Prices of agricultural produce, fixed in such a
way, should therefore be profitable and remunerative. This should be aimed at
improving the livelihood of farmers. As recommended by Prof. Dr. M.S.
Swaminathan, the prices guaranteed as such should be fixed on the basis of
C2+50% formula. Minimum Support Price (MSP) fixed in such a way should be
implemented 100 percent and a law should be legislated in this regard without any
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
21.03.2025 1532
further delay. I condemn this Union Government for making strict guidelines as
regards implementation of Crop Insurance Schemes thereby denying the benefits
to farmers, at large. Every farmer pays premium for the crops and land separately
and get into the cover of insurance schemes. But when the crops are damaged,
the insurance claims are reviewed and settled only if the Taluk or Birga where the
farmers live in fully affected. This is completely dishonest and injustice on the part

presently are not aimed to benefit the farmers. On the contrary, the corporate

purse. Therefore, I make a fervent appeal to consider and review claims individually

(cid:174)ी िदलीप शइक(cid:236)या (दारंग-उदालगुड़ी) : सर, आज का िदन इितहास म(cid:164) बह(cid:242)त बड़ा मह(cid:194)व रखता ह ै ।
आज का िदन लोकतं(cid:253) का िवजय िदवस ह ै । 24 जून, 1975 को इंिदरा गांधी जी न े इस देश म(cid:164)
आपातकाल लगाई थी और 19 महीन े आपातकाल चला । देश के गणतं(cid:253) का गला घ(cid:332)ट िदया गया ।
हमारे बीच म(cid:164) जो लोकतं(cid:253) सेनानी नह(cid:233) रहे, म(cid:167) आज लोकतं(cid:253) िवजय िदवस के अवसर पर लोकत(cid:253)ं
सेनािनय(cid:332) का स(cid:204)मान करते ह(cid:242)ए (cid:174)(cid:294)ा सुमन अिप(cid:170)त करता ह(cid:243)ं और जो सेनानी िजंदा ह(cid:167), उनके अ(cid:184)छे
(cid:214)वा(cid:214)(cid:195)य के िलए (cid:255)ाथ(cid:170)ना करता ह(cid:243)ं । सभापित जी, अब म(cid:167) िवषय पर आता ह(cid:243)ं । भारत र(cid:194)न से स(cid:204)मािनत
भूपेन हजा(cid:229)रका जी ने कृषक(cid:332) के िलए बह(cid:242)त अ(cid:184)छा गीत गाया था । म (cid:167) इस गीत के (cid:300)ारा कृषक(cid:332) के िलए
(cid:174)(cid:294)ा (cid:255)कट करता ह(cid:243)ं और गीत गाने क(cid:236) भी कोिशश करता ह(cid:243)ं:

“O mor dharitri aai, saronote diba thai
Khetiyokor nistar naai maati bine asohaai
Doya kora doyashila aai,
Rod borosun kaati kori,
Gham pelau soh kori,
Tomar bukur son sopaau
Aane niye karahi,
Maati aaru khetiyokor
Samondh aase jug jugor,
Krisok maatir milonor, diye ontoraai”
This means in English:
“Oh, my mother earth,
Let me rest at your feet
….. English translation of this part of speech originally delivered in Assamese.
21.03.2025 1535
The farmers do not have a path to salvation
Without you, we all are helpless
Please have mercy on us,
Braving the sun and rain
I harvest gold,
While others snatch away the harvest
Soil and farmers,
Share a bond for ages,
In the union of farmer and soil,
This is the only barrier.” *
सभापित जी, माननीय नर(cid:198)े (cid:254) मोदी जी के नेतृ(cid:194)व म(cid:164) िपछले 11 साल(cid:332) म (cid:164) कृिष, कृषक(cid:332) के िहत,
कृिष (cid:177)े(cid:253) के िवकास, कृषक(cid:332) को (cid:198)याय िदलाने और कृषक(cid:332) को उनके उ(cid:194)पाद क(cid:236) डेढ़ गनु ा एमएसपी देने
का (cid:255)यास िकया गया है । इसके बह(cid:242)त-से प(cid:229)रणाम हमारे सामने ह(cid:167) । म(cid:167) सबसे पहले अ(cid:198)नदाता कृषक(cid:332) को
स(cid:208)ै यूट करता ह(cid:243)ं और ध(cid:198)यवाद दते ा ह(cid:243)ं िक िपछले 11 साल(cid:332) म(cid:164) कृिष (cid:177)े(cid:253) ने देश के आिथ(cid:170)क िवकास म(cid:164)
बह(cid:242)त बड़ा योगदान सिु नि(cid:306)त िकया ह ै । देश क(cid:236) जीडीपी म(cid:164) 18 परस(cid:164)ट से अिधक का योगदान रहा है ।

ह ै इसी कारण िकसान(cid:332) के क(cid:208)याण के िलए के (cid:198)(cid:254) क(cid:236) हमारी मोदी सरकार सतत् (cid:255)य(cid:194)नशील है । सरकार
न े (cid:255)धानम(cid:253)ं ी िकसान स(cid:204)मान िनिध, उ(cid:198)नत िसंचाई के साधन, Microdrip Errigation मृदा परी(cid:177)ण

पूित(cid:170) करते ह(cid:242)ए िकसान(cid:332) क(cid:236) अिधक पैदावार बढ़ान े क(cid:236) िचंता करते ह(cid:242)ए सरकार हमेशा सि(cid:248)ए ह ै । िवप(cid:177)
के सबस े बड़े माननीय नेताजी अपने भाषण(cid:332) म(cid:164) कहते ह(cid:167) िक ऐसी मशीन लगाऊँ गा िक इस साइड स े आलू
डालो और उस साइड स े सोना िनकलेगा । एवं ह(cid:229)रयाणा के िकसान(cid:332) को जलेबी क(cid:236) फै (cid:179)(cid:367)ी लगाने क(cid:236)
सलाह देते ह(cid:167) और हरी िमच(cid:170) क(cid:236) जगह लाल िमच(cid:170) क(cid:236) खेती करन े क(cid:236) सलाह देते ह(cid:167) ।
म(cid:167) एक किवता के मा(cid:197)य म स े मोदी सरकार के बार े म(cid:164) कहना चाहता ह(cid:243)ं - गांव क(cid:236) गिलय(cid:332) म(cid:164) तब
उ(cid:204)मीद(cid:332) के दीप जलते ह(cid:167), जब ब िकसान के साथ, सरकार िमलकर चलती ह ै । आज माननीय मोदी जी
क(cid:236) सरकार िकसान(cid:332) के साथ कदम स े कदम िमलाकर चल रही ह ै । िजसस े िकसान(cid:332) के जीवन म(cid:164) अब
नया सवेरा आया ह ै । िकसान(cid:332) क(cid:236) योजनाए ंिसफ(cid:170) कागज(cid:332) म(cid:164) नह(cid:233) यथाथ(cid:170) के धरातल पर उतर रही ह(cid:167) और
उसका प(cid:229)रणाम ह ै िक देश के िकसान सम(cid:294)ृ हो रहे ह(cid:167), खुशहाल हो रहे ह(cid:167) और कृिष के (cid:255)ित लोग(cid:332) का
झुकाव लगातार बढ़ रहा ह ै । कृिष भारत के अथ(cid:170)(cid:211)यव(cid:214)था क(cid:236) रीढ़ ह ै । िकसान देश के भा(cid:181)य िवधाता है
परंत ु आजादी के बाद स े लगातार िकसान(cid:332) के साथ छल िकया गया, धोखा िदया गया, उ(cid:198)ह(cid:164) लूटा गया ।
आजादी के 50 वष(cid:334) तक कां(cid:250)ेस एवं कां(cid:250)ेस सरकार(cid:332) का एक ही काम रहा ह,ै िकसान(cid:332) का शोषण, दमन
एवं िकसान(cid:332) क(cid:236) योजनाए ं िसफ(cid:170) कागज(cid:332) पर रही ह(cid:167) ।

देश म(cid:164) जब जब गैर कां(cid:250)ेसी सरकार रही, तब तब िकसान(cid:332) के िलए सोचा गया और िकया गया,

SHRI E. T. MOHAMMED BASHEER (MALAPPURAM): The ongoing farmers'
protests have been fueled by a series of unfulfilled promises from the Government.
Key demands include a legal guarantee for the Minimum Support Price (MSP) for
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1542
crops, a debt waiver for farmers, and pensions for both farmers and farm laborers.
Additionally, farmers are calling for no hikes in electricity tariffs, the withdrawal of
police cases filed against them during protests, and justice for the victims of the
2021 Lakhimpur Kheri violence. They seek the reinstatement of the Land
Acquisition Act of 2013 and compensation for the families of farmers who lost their
lives during the protests of 2020-21. As the agitation continues, another meeting is
scheduled for May 4th. However, there is a prevailing sentiment that meetings alone
may not lead to meaningful change, as the saying goes, 'If you don't want to do a
thing, then form a committee.' It is crucial that these discussions do not end without
addressing the farmers' legitimate concerns.
The MSP Policy Implementation is currently at a pivotal stage, raising the
question of why a comprehensive white paper or status report on its progress has
not been produced. A critical analysis of past experiences in implementation
reveals several key points. Notably, the budget announcements for 2024 included
six major initiatives, yet the outcomes of these announcements remain unclear.
There is a pressing need to focus on technological integration, sustainable
practices, and financial support to enhance agricultural productivity and improve
farmers' welfare. Additionally, the necessity for an Action Taken Report (ATR) is
evident to ensure accountability and track the effectiveness of these initiatives.
Now, I would like to touch upon certain issues pertaining to my State of
Kerala. The issues concerning Kerala's coconut industry highlight the need for
urgent attention and action. Historically, the procurement of raw coconut was a
common practice, but this has ceased, necessitating its restoration to support local
21.03.2025 1543
farmers. Additionally, the current coconut prices present an opportunity that must
be made sustainable to ensure long-term viability. It is crucial to approach
productivity with seriousness, as this will directly impact the economic stability of
the sector. Furthermore, the PM-KISAN scheme, which offers a financial benefit of
Rs.6,000, should be significantly enhanced to provide better support for coconut
farmers and stimulate growth in the industry.
My State of Kerala has been going through certain bitter experiences. In
Kerala, the issue of human-wildlife conflict has become increasingly severe, with
460 fatalities reported over the past five years, as highlighted in data presented to
the Rajya Sabha. This alarming statistic places Kerala among the worst-affected
States in India regarding wildlife-related deaths. The conflicts primarily involve
elephants, wild boars, leopards, and other wildlife, significantly impacting farmers
and rural communities. These groups face not only crop damage but also threats
to their lives, underscoring the urgent need for effective solutions to mitigate these
conflicts.
In Kerala, the agricultural landscape is undergoing significant changes,
particularly with the decline of traditional crops. While paddy remains a staple, other
important crops such as cashews, pepper, rubber, pulses, ginger, and turmeric are
experiencing reduced production levels. This shift is further compounded by some
farmers moving away from cultivating traditional crops like millets, which play a
crucial role in ensuring food security and promoting nutritional diversity. The
transition away from these vital crops raises concerns about the future of
agriculture in the region and its impact on food security and nutritional diversity.
21.03.2025 1544
Organic farming is increasingly recognized for its significance and relevance
in today's agricultural landscape. However, this sector encounters several
challenges that hinder its growth. Key issues include limited market access, which
restricts farmers from reaching potential customers, high certification costs that can
be prohibitive for many, and a general lack of awareness among both farmers and
consumers about the benefits and practices of organic farming. Addressing these
challenges is crucial for the advancement of organic agriculture.
The transition to agro machineries presents several implementation hurdles
that need to be addressed for successful adoption. One of the primary challenges
is the high cost associated with acquiring these machines, which can be a
significant barrier for many farmers. Additionally, the prevalence of small and
scattered land holdings complicates the situation, as these machines are often
more suitable for larger, consolidated areas. To overcome these challenges, it is
essential to provide comprehensive training for farmers on the effective use of agro
machineries, ensuring they can maximize their benefits. Furthermore, the lack of
repair and servicing facilities poses a risk to the sustainability of these machines,
as does the availability of spare parts. Addressing these issues will be crucial in
facilitating the successful integration of agro machineries into farming practices.
The issues related to chemicals and fertilizers are critical and multifaceted,
impacting various aspects of the environment and agriculture. Soil degradation is
a significant concern, as it diminishes the land's productivity and health.
Additionally, water pollution arises from the runoff of fertilizers, leading to
contamination of water bodies and affecting aquatic life.
21.03.2025 1545
Air pollution is another consequence, often resulting from the release of
volatile compounds during fertilizer application. Furthermore, the loss of
biodiversity is alarming, as chemical use can disrupt ecosystems and threaten
various species. Reduced soil fertility and crop quality are direct outcomes of
excessive chemical use, which can lead to diminished agricultural yields.
Moreover, the development of resistance in pests and diseases poses a challenge
to sustainable farming practices.
Lastly, both water and food contamination are pressing issues that arise from
the improper use of chemicals, ultimately affecting human health and safety.
Addressing these interconnected issues is essential for promoting a sustainable
agricultural future.
To build a coexistence policy that enhances employability in rural areas, it is
essential to address several key challenges. These include low awareness and
access to skill programs, the lack of modern curriculum in agricultural institutes,
inadequate infrastructure, and limited credit availability for agri-entrepreneurs.
As agriculture in India transitions from subsistence to enterprise mode, there
is significant potential for employment growth if we scale up skills, technology, and
support systems. By enhancing employability, we can mitigate rural migration,
increase incomes, and modernize Indian agriculture.
Shri M.S. Swaminathan aptly stated,
'If agriculture goes wrong, nothing else will have a chance to go right in
the country.'
This sentiment echoes the vision of Lal Bahadur Shastri's 'Jai Jawan, Jai Kisan,'
21.03.2025 1546
emphasizing the importance of both farmers and soldiers in nation-building.

(cid:177)े(cid:253) का (cid:255)ितिनिध(cid:194)व म(cid:167) सदन म(cid:164) कर रहा ह(cid:243) ँ । म(cid:167) इसिलए भी अपने आपको सौभा(cid:181)यशाली मानता ह(cid:243),ँ

काम करन े का अवसर िमल रहा ह ै । गत 2014 स े अभी तक ह(cid:242)ए कृिष (cid:177)े(cid:253) म (cid:164) हो रहे कायाक(cid:208)प स े यह

वष(cid:170) 2013-14 म(cid:164) UPA शासन काल म(cid:164) कृिष म(cid:253)ं ालय का वािष(cid:170)क बजट आवंटन जहां 27,663
करोड़ (cid:321)पए मा(cid:253) था, वह(cid:233) मोदी जी क(cid:236) सरकार न े इस े 5 गनु ा बढ़ाकर 1,37,756 करोड़ (cid:321)पए कर िदया
ह ै । इसके िलए मोदी जी आज देश का हर िकसान, हर गावँ आपका (cid:323)दय स े अिभनंदन कर रहा ह ै ।
मुझ े (cid:255)स(cid:198)नता ह ै िक सरकार (cid:300)ारा के (cid:198)(cid:254)ीय बजट म(cid:164) देश क(cid:236) कृिष को समृ(cid:294) बनान े के िलए कृिष
बजट बढ़ान े के साथ-साथ िकसान और कृिष िहत(cid:332) का िवशेष (cid:197)यान रखा गया ह,ै अनेक(cid:332) लोक-
क(cid:208)याणकारी योजनाओ ंऔर काय(cid:170)(cid:248)म(cid:332) को धरातल तक उतारन े का काय(cid:170) मोदी सरकार (cid:300)ारा िकया जा
रहा ह ै ।
पीएम धन धा(cid:198)य कृिष योजना के मा(cid:197)यम स े कम उ(cid:194)पादन वाल े 100 िजल(cid:332) को िचि(cid:198)हत करके
उनम(cid:164) कै स े उ(cid:194)पादन बड़े, िकसान कै स े खुशहाल हो, इस पर काम हो रहा ह ै । पीएम िकसान योजना के

डॉ. राजकु मार सांगवान (बागपत) : हमारे देश भारत क(cid:236) अथ(cid:170)(cid:211)यव(cid:214)था म(cid:164) कृिष आज भी (cid:255)मुख भूिमका

है यह कथन आज भी सौ फ़(cid:236)सदी सच और (cid:255)ासंिगक है ।

(cid:255)भावशाली योजनाए ं बना रही है और उन योजनाओ ंको पारदश(cid:234) तरीके से ि(cid:248)याि(cid:198)वत कर रही है ।

नई और साथ(cid:170)क योजनाए/ं नीितयां बनाई जा रही ह(cid:167) ।
इस बजट म(cid:164) कृिष (cid:177)े(cid:253) को जो आवंटन िकया गया है, उससे फसल(cid:332) क(cid:236) उ(cid:194)पादकता म(cid:164) विृ (cid:294) होगी,


…*Agricultural problem
Our country India is known as an agrarian country. But, in real sense the
agricultural problems are still not solved.
Sugarcane:
Solapur district is known for its sugar factories. But, the farmer is not
allowed to know how the recovery of sugarcane is done by the factory. As a result,
 Speech was laid on the Table.
….. English translation of this part of speech originally delivered in Marathi.
21.03.2025 1553
showing less recovery, the farmer is getting less rate of sugarcane. At present
some factories in Solapur district are charging Rs.3500 per ton as a declared rate.
While some factories Rs.2700/- Per ton which is a low rate. There is a huge gap
between Rs. 3500/ and Rs. 2700/- The rate per ton has such a variation in rate.
Why is this? A single rate is expected from all the factories in the district when the
recovery is similar. Weighing in sugarcane factory needs to be done online
originally. Because, weight fraud has become a common practice. So, the
government should make the weighing scale digital and fix the policy that the
sugarcane recovery should be informed to the farmers too.
Even when the factories are making profit by doing various ancillary
businesses such as power generation, ethanol, baggasse and molasses sales
from the sugarcane factories, the farmers are given lower rates by showing the
government that the factories are making losses.
Demand
Payment of sugarcane to the farmer should be made mandatory within 15
days once the sugarcane reaches the factory. This arrangement should be made
to pay the amount to the farmers within 15 days after selling the goods.
Milk:
India's population is over 135 crores, but milk production is very less in
comparison. But, due to adulterated milk availability in market, milk is not getting
remunerative price. So, the government should impose strict restrictions on
adulterated milk and stop the adulterated milk in the market. And fair price should
be given to the milk farmers. In the current market, cow's milk should be getting
21.03.2025 1554
at least Rs. 40-45 and for buffalo milk, it should be Rs 70 to 75. per litre.
A policy should be made to provide Guaranteed prices for agricultural
produces. Subsidy of milk is not provide on time. So, the rates should be
increased. When the farmers bring the produces then the prices of the goods in
the market decreases when the sugarcane gets ready to yield, sugar prices
decrease in November, December and January. Also, when the agricultural
produces such as jowar, wheat, rice etc get ready to harvest.
The prices drops in March and April. As a result, farmers do not get fair
price for their goods. Traders buy and store the same goods. When farmers run
out of produce, they sell their produce in the market at a higher price. So, this
government should give guaranteed price for agricultural produces.
Fertilizers, pesticides, equipments and tools, irrigation material, drips,
sprinklers, vehicles, (tractors and other similar vehicles) modern machinery,
electric motors are inherently expensive due to government's GST. The policy
has broken the back of the farmers. If farmers are to be truly emancipated, taxes
like GST should be completely waived and all agricultural facilities should be
provided to farmers at tax-free and subsidized rates without imposing any other
taxes.
At present, the farmer through P.M. Kisan Yojana gets Rs. 2000 every four
months from the central government. Also, Maharashtra government gives Rs.
2000 every four months under Namo Samman Yojana. This amount is very less,
even two sacks of fertilizers can be bought. So, the central government should
give additional Rs. 2000 per month and the state government should give Rs.
21.03.2025 1555
2000 per month.
The present government bans export of agricultural produce when the
market price is good. And imports the same goods from abroad. (Eg, Onion,
pulses, Sugar) Instead of doing this, the government should plan the import-
export policy in order to get a remunerative price for the agricultural goods,
considering the farmer and the farmer will get the right price for his goods. Also,
an export policy should be fixed decided at a discounted rate so that the farmer
can get direct prices for the goods without having to arrange an agent to send his
goods from his farm to the international market.
Government announces many subsidy schemes for educated unemployed
youth and farmers, but banks do not provide loans to farmers and unemployed
youths and violate rules made by government. As a result, the schemes of
government do not reach to the poor farmers and the unemployed youth. So, the
government should intervene and make it mandatory for the banks to give loans
to the farmers and the unemployed youth. Also, CIBIL condition should be
condoned while giving loans to farmers.
Banks charge interest at the rate of 8% for home loans and 12% for
agriculture ancillary businesses. Micro, Small, and Medium Schemes of the
Central Government as well as NLM are run by Central Government. For that,
bank regulations become problematic. Will the central government interfere in
this? Also, the limit of 3 lakh for crop loan has been increased to 5 lakh. But, it is
not being implemented. Farmers are not even getting the loan concession. What
decision will the government take for it?
21.03.2025 1556
So, all the above problems should be considered sympathetically and
action should be taken in this regard. Under Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana
In Solapur district onion, millet, pigeon pea, maize, groundnut Bananas,
pomegranates and grapes are considered as a crops and major fruits. After storm,
wind, unseasonal rain, heavy rain, they are forced to give advance notice of loss
within 72 hours, but after reporting the representatives of the insurance company
do not come for inspection even after a month. So, the farmers face difficulty in
cultivation of the next crop. Up to Rs 5000 is extorted and money is demanded
from the farmer as the compensation for Kharif season 2024 to be paid by the
end of February 2025. For Kharif season 2024 crops like millets. Tur, Maize,
Onion, Groundnut are included. Even though the insurance expected to be paid
by the end of February 2025, the farmers have not received the insurance by the
end of March. Kharif season 2025 is starting in just two months, the farmer does
not have money for cultivation and seeds. Therefore, it is necessary to
accumulate crop insurance as soon as possible. Also, due to unseasonal rains in
the month of May 2024, mango and pomegranate crops have been damaged, but
compensation has not been received for this loss.
70 thousand metric tons of pomegranates are exported from Maharashtra
every year. Out of this, 30 thousand metric tons of pomegranates used to be
exported from Solapur alone. Recognizing this potential, the Central Government
announced a Pomegranate Cluster worth 247.67 crores for Solapur in August
2023. Farmers were to be provided assistance under this scheme. But, the
process of this scheme has been delayed at the government level for a year.
21.03.2025 1557
The Union Ministry of Agriculture has fixed the overall horticulture cluster
for the country in 2021. 12 more were selected under the Group Development
Scheme. Solapur was selected for pomegranate production from Maharashtra. A
program of about 4 years was fixed by the National Horticulture Board to develop
the pomegranate cluster. From this project, the pomegranate orchards of 30,000
farmers on 20,000 hectares would get assistance. Now, with the withdrawal of the
company appointed for it, the challenges faced by the farmers will continue to
increase.
Due to natural calamities, the recognition of Solapur pomegranate in the
global market due to geographical indication is on the verge of being wiped out.
In the last 10 years, 12 thousand hectares of pomegranate orchards in the district
have been destroyed. The Pomegranate Cluster Scheme is in shambles due to
the neglect of the Central and State Governments. The company appointed by
the government by paying Rs 247.67 crore for pomegranate cluster, has pulled
out citing cost implications New tenders have been floated for the selection of new
company. But, the process is slow.
Wanted Export Center for Onion In Solapur district, onion is grown on 55
thousand hectares during kharif, late kharif and rabi seasons. Moreover, Solapur
is a city situated on the border of Andhra Pradesh, Telangana and Karnataka
states. The Solapur Agricultural Produce Marketing Committee here receives an
average of 50,000 quintals of onions from 500 trucks every day. It has a turnover
of around 2 thousand crores every year. Even Lasalgaon, known as the largest
onion market in Asia, has been overtaken by the Solapur Bazar Committee.
21.03.2025 1558
Onions are imported from 3 states to Solapur Agricultural Produce Marketing
Committee. But, there are no facilities for onion export in Solapur district. Onion
from here is sold in the states of Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh. If an
export center is set up in Solapur, the farmers here can benefit from it. Therefore,
it is necessary for the central government to make an effort for it.
The Grape growing farmers, due to demonetization and covid-19 Currently,
removing vineyards due to lack of remunerative market price around 5 lakhs per
acre is spent on setting up a vineyard. But a crop loan of one lakh per acre has
been given by the bank. If the government waives the crop loan of Rs 1 lakh, the
grape farmer will get a relief.
Under the National Agricultural Development Scheme, Per Drop More Crop
Drip scheme. subsidy of Rs. 16.5 crore Solapur district is pending with the central
government and approximately 400 crores of subsidy of farmers of Maharashtra
is still pending. The central government should make it available immediately.
Inclusion of sunflower and sorghum crops under kharif crop insurance
(sorghum crop insurance Grop and Fruit insurance, Solapur) - In Mangalvedha
Tehsil, sunflower is cultivated in maximum area during kharif season. Also,
sorghum crop is also cultivated on a large scale for animal fodder, but no
compensation is given to them if these crops are damaged due to the vagaries of
nature. Also, as no crop protection has been provided for it, a large number of
farmers are facing problems. Therefore, sunflower and jowar crops should be
included under Kharif crop insurance and also included in the government's Rs 1
crop insurance scheme.
21.03.2025 1559
Mohol Tehsil has been excluded from drought fund. But, Narkhed mandal
in Mohol tehsil of Solapur district is getting funds by declaring wet drought. But,
the village of Khuneshwar in Sawleshwar mandal, had heavy rains. The farmers
of this village have suffered huge losses. But, due to non-declaration of wet
drought in Savleshwar Mandal, Khuneshwar village is not getting funds.*

I Whole heatedly welcome the Union Budget with 1,37,575 Lakh Crores allotted to
Agriculture Sector 2.7% of 1,27,290 Cr (92%) being allotted for agriculture and
farmers welfare & 10,466 Cr for (8%) for research & education. I appreciate the

Farmer in order to compete & globally here are few inputs.First we need to reduce
the Cost of Production as it is a Serious problem & I request the FM to reduce the
cost of Insecticides, Pesticides, tractors and also reduce the GST to 5% for 10
Years. MNREGA that has been instrumental in generative employment and
financial assistance to the Rural Labourers. I request there be a restructured made
in MNGREGA where in Agricultural Labourers can work for 5 months where 50%
is given by farmers, 50% by MNREGA which will help farmers get agricultural
labourers & also prevent seasonal migration.
With the technology improving day by day & appreciate the initiative taken by
CG by introducing Drones however there are few limitations in controlling the pests
& disease & should be further improved. When remote sensing technology is used
in all the sectors why not use in agricultural sector in detection a natural calamity &

extent of crop loss & paid in 30 days, Embryo technology for Diary farmers where
there is 90% accuracy in Sex Selection of the cattle which would further develop
diary farmers. Artificial Intelligence results is 50% made capable which is no use to
the diary farmers . Another major issue is the low price realisation by the farmers
for e.g. Tomato being 33.5%, onion 36%, mango 40%. For e.g. 21.5 millions tons
of mango is produced annually pulp processing is about 10 lakh tons & fruit export
is 1 lakh tons.
When production value addition in other countries is almost 70% why cant we reach
it, production is huge but processing is negligible. I request to have a MOU for all
the crops other than cereal for production, processing, storage & exports & start
mango board which would benefit farmers of Andhra Pradesh, Telangana,
Karnataka & Uttar Pradesh.
There are about 2,69,833 graduates enrolled in Agricultural Sector like Agricultural
Engineer, Biotechnology, Polytechnic need to provide employment to all. People
are on the system in are sure there be improvement in Agrisector & future
generation.
I come from Nandyal which is in Rayalaseema zone which was once called
Rathnaleseem (land of gems) & had a glorious agricultural past. Farming is not
just an occupation but it is the identity, legacy and the only means of survival. For
the past few years it is experiencing severe crisis because of insufficient rainfall,
scarcity or no rainfall, recurred drought & insufficient irrigation system and brutally
middle men, pathilconda market which is largest wholesale hub 1Rs/Kg. Dhan
Dhanya Krishi Yojna where 100 Districts low procedure and modern crops intensity

to initiate the scheme in our society, specially in Rayalaseema and Nandayal.

seeds. Reviving this would be a game changer. Establishment of Millets Research

Completion of pending Irrigation Projects, interlinking of Godavari and Kaveri which
would solve drought across the country.

21.03.2025 1578

SHRI LAVU SRIKRISHNA DEVARAYALU (NARASARAOPET): It is with great
honour that I stand before you today to acknowledge the commendable efforts of
the Government of India in advancing the agricultural sector through the latest
budget allocation. The Union Budget 2025-26 has seen a substantial increase of
3.89 per cent in the allocation for agriculture, reaffirming the Government’s
unwavering commitment to farmer welfare and rural prosperity. The achievements
in food grain production, which have risen from 204.6 million tonnes in 2004-05 to
an estimated 332.3 million tonnes in 2023-24, are a testament to this commitment.
Furthermore, the remarkable growth in horticulture production, reaching an all-time
high of 355.25 million tonnes, and the surge in agricultural exports to Rs.4.2 lakh
crore in FY23 highlights the robust strides taken in the sector.
The PM-KISAN scheme continues to be a cornerstone of farmer empowerment,
with over Rs. 3.46 lakh crore disbursed to 11 crore farmers since its inception. In
Andhra Pradesh alone, Rs. 836.36 crore has reached more than 41.22 lakh
beneficiaries in the 18th instalment. Additionally, the steady increase in Minimum
Support Price (MSP) for paddy, from Rs. 850 per quintal in 2008-09 to Rs. 2,300
per quintal in 2023-24, has reinforced price stability and income security for our
farmers.
Andhra Pradesh has emerged as a frontrunner in sustainable and natural farming,
setting an example for the rest of the country. Our commitment to Andhra Pradesh
Community Managed Natural Farming (APCNF) has gained international
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1579
recognition. Delegations from countries such as the USA and Mexico visited my
district, Palnadu, to study our pioneering models. The vision of our Chief Minister
Nara Chandra Babu Naidu aligns seamlessly with the objectives of the Union
Government, ensuring that our State remains a key player in sustainable
agricultural practices.
The newly introduced budget schemes, such as the Cotton Technology Mission,
the Mission for Pulses, and the National Mission on Hybrid Seeds, present
immense opportunities for Andhra Pradesh. As the 8th largest cotton-producing
State, Andhra Pradesh stands to gain significantly from the Cotton Technology
Mission, enhancing traceability and ensuring higher returns for farmers. Similarly,
the Mission for Pulses, with a budget of Rs. 1,000 crore, will bolster our production
capacity, helping us compete with leading States like Karnataka and Gujarat.
Furthermore, the National Mission on Hybrid Seeds aligns with Andhra Pradesh’s
long-standing tradition of being a leader in seed production, enabling us to develop
high-yielding and region-specific hybrid seeds for rice, millets, and pulses.
With increased support from the Union Government, Andhra Pradesh is poised to
maximize the benefits of these schemes, further strengthening our agricultural
backbone and contributing to the national goal of food security and economic
growth.
While the Government’s initiatives have laid a strong foundation, there remain key
areas that require further attention. Firstly, the need for a comprehensive price
support mechanism for horticulture crops such as red chilies is paramount. Andhra
Pradesh’s Palnadu district, which covers nearly 90 per cent of its horticultural area
21.03.2025 1580
under red chili cultivation, has faced severe losses due to adverse weather
conditions. Given the substantial financial burden on the State Government, we
urge the Union Government to include red chilies under the Price Support Scheme
(PSS) through NAFED, in alignment with PM-AASHA, ensuring remunerative
prices for our farmers.
Secondly, Andhra Pradesh, despite being a leader in agricultural growth, hosts only
three ICAR research institutes out of the 102 across India. None of the 11
Agricultural Technology Application Research Institutes (ATARIs) are located in the
State. Given our commitment to sustainable farming and our contributions to the
agricultural sector, we strongly advocate for the establishment of additional ICAR
institutes in Andhra Pradesh. These institutions will serve as vital hubs for research,
innovation, and capacity building, further propelling our agricultural excellence.
Andhra Pradesh has demonstrated time and again its resilience, innovation, and
commitment to sustainable growth. With continued support from the Union
Government, we are confident that our farmers will thrive, our yields will multiply,
and our rural economy will flourish. Together, let us work towards an agricultural
revolution that not only uplifts Andhra Pradesh but also strengthens the entire
nation’s food security and economic stability. Thank you.

1,74,798 करोड़ (cid:322)पये व देश के िकसान(cid:332) पर 33,52,646 करोड़ (cid:322)पये का कजा(cid:170) बकाया है । आप जब
ध(cid:198)ना सेठ(cid:332) का राईट ऑफ़ के नाम पर लाख(cid:332) करोड़ का कजा(cid:170) माफ कर देते हो, तो मेरी मांग है िक


DR. K. SUDHAKAR (CHIKKBALLAPUR): Thank you for having allowed me to
express my views on demand for grants for agriculture 2025-26. I remember a
quote by legendary M A Swaminathan that says, “If agriculture goes wrong, nothing
else will have a chance to go right”. For any country, the degree of economic
progress is measured in terms of the progress that is made in agriculture. Our
country started focusing more on this sector only after 2014, till then it was largely
neglected. In 2025-26, Rs 1,37,757 crore has been allocated to the Ministry which
is 2.7 percent of the Union Budget 2025-26. This is an increase of Rs q18934
crores from the actuals of 2023-24. Out of the total allocation to the Ministry, 92%
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1598
is estimated to be spent by the Department of Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare
while the remaining is estimated to be spent by the Department of Agricultural
Research and Education. An increase of 3.1 percent has been given for agriculture
research and education. The Department of Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare
spends 77% of its budget on three major schemes: PM-Kisan Samman Nidhi (direct
cash transfer of Rs 6,000 per year per farmer family), Modified Interest Subvention
Scheme, and Crop Insurance Scheme. The actuals under PM KISAN for 2023-24
was Rs 61441 crores which is now increased to Rs 63500/crores under the Union
Budget 2025-26.
This year's budget launched PM DHAN DHAANYA KRISHI YOJANA. We are very
thankful for this. The scheme is being launched in partnership with states and
estimates to cover 1.7 crore farmers. The scheme will target 100 districts with low
productivity, moderate crop intensity, and below average credit access. I request
Chikkaballapur district to be brought under this scheme.
Another unique feature of this year's Budget is National Mission on High Yielding
Seeds: This mission focuses on: (i) ensuring commercial availability of more than
100 seed varieties, (ii) development and propagation of climate and pest resilient
high yielding seeds, and (ili) strengthening research on high yielding seed varieties.
Rs 100 crore has been allocated for 2025-26 under the mission.
My only request is to ensure that more research is done for production of drought
resistant seeds. This will support food security in our country.
The enhanced limit under Kisan Credit Card is welcome. Today Loan limit under
the Modified Interest Subvention Scheme will be increased from three lakh rupees
21.03.2025 1599
to five lakh rupees.
A comprehensive programme for the promotion of production, efficient supply,
processing, and remunerative prices for fruits and vegetables will be launched. The
programme has been allocated Rs 500 crore for 2025-26. It will target formation
and participation of Farmer Producer Organisations and Cooperatives across
states. While various government initiatives such as Venture Capital Assistance
(VCA), Equity Grant and Credit Guarantee Fund Scheme (EGCGS), NABARD
Credit Guarantee, and the Agriculture Infrastructure Fund have played a pivotal
role in supporting FPOs, these schemes primarily focus on governance, capacity
building, and infrastructure development. However, a direct business fund for FPOs
is still lacking, which limits their ability to scale operations and gain a substantial
market share. This is something innovative that the government can do. There is
also a greater need for promotion of floriculture in the country. My district
Chikkaballapur can be the centre of floriculture in the country. My humble request
to the Minister through you sir, is to help us make Chikkaballapur the centre of
global floriculture industry. A new Mission for Aatmanirbharta in Pulses will be
launched for a period of six years in this year's Budget. The Mission will aim at
achieving self-sufficiency in pulses production through procurement of Tur, Urad,
and Masoor. An allocation of Rs 1,000 crore has been made for this scheme for
2025-26. I request the Government's support for farmers of Karnataka under this
Mission.

has utilised 90% of its allocated budget on an average. This performance has

ensured that our farmers are adequately compensated for production. My request

SHRIMATI D. K. ARUNA (MAHBUBNAGAR): Thank you very much for giving
me an opportunity to express my views in the discussion on the demands for grants

steps from time to time and due to which our farmers are very much happy in the
country.
The second thing is establishment of National Mission on High Yielding Seeds
which is a welcome measure to increase the productivity of the items which the
farmers produce.
The third thing is establishment of second gene bank with 10 lakh germplasm lines
is a most welcome measure. It is to be noted here that almost three decades after
the first National Gene Bank was set up in India, the establishment of a second
such bank comprising a million germplasm lines will definitely achieve desired
results for future food and nutritional security and provide conservation support to
both public and private sectors for genetic resources which is the need of the hour.
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1601
The fourth thing is Kisan Credit Card loan limit to be raised from Rs. 3 lakh to Rs.
5 lakh. I am happy to say that the move is aimed at providing support to the 7.7
crore farmers, fishers, and dairy producers who are beneficiaries of this program
who are small and marginal farmers of our country. I am also happy to note that
under the new interest subsidy program, farmers can now borrow money of up to
75 lakh which may help them to raise their income levels also. This great move will
help the applicants who have to be tenant farmers, owner-cultivators,
sharecroppers, or members of joint liability or self-help organizations engaged in
farming or other producer activities like fishing or animal husbandry.
Loans amounting to Rs. 72 lakh or less will in future not require collateral. The RBI
has taken one of those steps in favour of making credit easy for farmers and this is
a milestone of our Government.
The fifth thing is five- year mission for cotton productivity after careful consideration
and to address the challenges of stagnant cotton productivity, the Government has
announced a five-year Cotton Mission to increase cotton productivity especially
extra-long staple varieties. Moreover, Science & Technology support will be
provided to farmers under this Mission and the cotton farming will be more
beneficial from this Mission.
Overall, one can say confidently that a slew of measures to spur Agricultural
Growth and Productivity, thereby benefitting our Annadata have been taken to give
desired results and to face the challenges in farming.

a Makhana Board in Bihar, which will improve production, processing, value
21.03.2025 1602
addition, and marketing of makhana as well as support the people engaged in these

also provide handholding and training support to makhana farmers and also work
to ensure they receive the benefits of all relevant Government schemes.
I am also confident that the Government will achieve the growth rate of more than
five per cent in agriculture and in turn, it will benefit the farmers of our country
particularly small and marginal farmers.

SHRI B. MANICKAM TAGORE (VIRUDHUNAGAR): On behalf of the backbone
of our country-our farmers demand amplify the urgent demand for agriculture to be
placed at the core of our national agenda. Agriculture is not merely an occupation;
it is the lifeline of India, sustaining over half our population, feeding 140 crore
citizens, and driving our economy. Yet, the plight of our farmers-nationally and
particularly in states like Tamil Nadu- reveals a story of neglect, despair, and
broken promises.
Our Leader of Opposition, Shri Rahul Gandhi, has been a steadfast voice for
farmers across India. He has consistently demanded a legal guarantee for
Minimum Support Price (MSP) based on the Swaminathan Commission's C2+50
per cent formula, ensuring farmers receive a fair return for their toil.
He has called for comprehensive farm loan waivers to break the cycle of debt that
has claimed countless lives. He has urged compensation and jobs for the kin of
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1603
farmers who died during the protests against the repealed farm laws over 700 brave
souls whose sacrifices this Government refuses to acknowledge. Just last month,
in February 2025, Shri Gandhi reiterated these demands, accusing the Modi
Government of laying "nails in the path of farmers" and betraying them for a
decade. The Congress stands resolute behind these demands, and I echo them
today in this august House.
But let me bring the focus to Tamil Nadu, where our farmers face unique and
pressing grievances. The Cauvery water dispute remains an open wound. Despite
legal rulings, the failure to secure Tamil Nadu's rightful share of water from
Karnataka has left our delta farmers especially in Thanjavur, Tiruvarur, and
Nagapattinam-struggling to irrigate their fields. The Mettur Dam, a lifeline for the
region, often runs dry due to delayed releases, pushing farmers into distress. In
2024 alone, samba paddy cultivation suffered as water shortages coincided with
untimely rains, a double blow worsened by climate change.
Moreover, Tamil Nadu farmers have long demanded the establishment of a
Cauvery Management Board with teeth-something promised but never delivered.
The Central Government's apathy has emboldened vested interests, leaving our
farmers to protest in vain. In Madurai and Trichy, they have raised their voices
against corrupt procurement centres, where they are forced to pay illegal handling
charges of Rs. 40 - Rs. 60 per bag. They demand CCTV surveillance and
accountability, yet the response is silence. Add to this the steep fall in turmeric
prices in Erode-down to Rs. 6,000 per quintal and the lack of subsidized fodder
seeds during droughts, and you see a state of abandonment.
21.03.2025 1604
The crisis is national too. Rising input costs-fertilizers, seeds, fuel-have made
farming unviable. Erratic weather devastates crops, yet crop insurance fails to
deliver. MSP, promised for 23 crops, remains a mirage for most. As colleague Raja
noted, it barely reaches 21 crops in practice. Farmers from Punjab to Tamil Nadu
have marched Khanauri, Shambhu, Jantar Mantar-only to face barricades, tear
gas, and now, eviction threats. This is not governance; it is oppression.
The Congress demands action. First, a legal MSP guarantee, as Shri Rahul Gandhi
has championed, rooted in the Swaminathan formula. Second, a nationwide farm
loan waiver to end the debt trap. Third, compensation and jobs for families of
martyred farmers, as justice delayed is justice denied. Fourth, a permanent
commission to oversee these reforms. Fifth, massive investment in climate-resilient
agriculture- irrigation, seed technology, extension services- -so farmers are not left
to battle nature alone. For Tamil Nadu specifically, we demand the immediate
constitution of a functional Cauvery Management Board, timely water releases, and
fair procurement systems free of corruption.
The BJP claims Jai Jawan, Jai Kisan, but where is the ‘jai’ for our kisans? Budgets
favour photo ops over fields; policies favour corporates over cultivators. Shri Rahul
Gandhi has warned that those who betray farmers cannot be trusted. The Congress
will not stand for this injustice. We will fight for every farmer's dignity, from the
Cauvery delta to the Gangetic plains.
I urge this House to rise above politics and unite for agriculture. Let us honour the
hands that feed us-nationally and in Tamil Nadu. Let their voices shape our laws
and budgets. The time for promises is over-the time for action is now.
21.03.2025 1605

सरकार के सहयोग के िलए म(cid:167) सम(cid:214)त दादरा नगर हवेली वािसय(cid:332) के तरफ से (cid:323)दय से आभार
(cid:211)य(cid:265) करती ह(cid:243) ँ ।

DR. C. N. MANJUNATH (BANGALORE RURAL) : Central Silk Board (CSB) is a
statutory body created by an Act of Parliament, namely, The Central Silk Board Act,
1948 for the development of sericulture across the country. CSB through its
Research & Development is catering to the needs of the sector working in co-
ordination with Sericulture Departments of different States.
The Board of CSB has a total strength of 39 members, headed by a Chairperson
appointed by Government of India, comprising Members of Parliament, officers of
central and state governments and industry representatives as its other members.
The Board is the highest body of CSB which deliberates and suggests policy
interventions for the development of sericulture industry apart from considering
other proposals required for the smooth functioning of CSB.
The position of Chairperson, CSB is lying vacant since August, 2019 and there is
an urgent need to fill this vacancy. The presence of a dynamic Chairperson augurs
well for the smooth and effective functioning of CSB.
Regarding strategy for utilization of Silk Waste, the fibrous waste which gets
 Speech was laid on the Table.
21.03.2025 1613
generated during the process of reeling and weaving is classified as silk waste
which is used as raw material for production of value-added products like spun silk
yarn, noil silk yarn, silk sheets etc. Currently, the domestic silk waste production is
around 6000 MT. India once had its own capacity to utilise silk waste locally, but,
as on date, only few spun silk mills are functioning. Silk waste is being exported in
large quantities to China and value-added products are coming back to India.
Reportedly, there are unfair trade practices in export of silk waste in the form of
under invoicing and export of cocoons suitable for reeling as silk waste.
With a measure to support value addition locally, there is a need to support
establishment of few spun silk mills along with imposing strict vigilance on export
of silk waste to counter unfair trade practices.
Regarding according permission to the private agencies for augmenting the
production of basic seed (P1), for accomplishing the envisaged target of production
of 2800 lakhs of bivoltine commercial silkworm seed by 2030, around 51 lakhs of
P1 basic seed is required. Currently, the production of P1 Disease Free Laying
(DFLs) is carried out by the P1 grainages of National Silkworm Seed Organization
(NSSO) and other State Government sericulture departments. However, the
current in-built capacity of NSSO and state government P1 grainages together is
hardly 20 lakhs of P1 basic seed.
Although the NSSO and a few State Governments are currently putting forth their
commendable efforts to meet the demand for P1 DFLs, their limitations and the
ever-increasing demand necessitate the participation of the private sector in P1
DFLs production. By leveraging the resources, expertise, and efficiency of the
21.03.2025 1614
private sector, they can enhance the overall availability and accessibility of P1
DFLs to the commercial silkworm seed producers of both the Government and
private agencies. Additionally, this approach would alleviate the burden on NSSO,
allowing them to focus on upstream multiplication (P4 to P2) levels, while adhering
meticulously to the procedures established by Japan International Cooperation
Agency (JC). In view of the above, permission may be accorded to any non-
governmental agency which possess experience and expertise to handle basic
seed and also enforcing stringent norms under the supervision of NSSO. Such an
agency must ensure the maintenance of all the quality standards and willing to sell
P1 DFLs on par with the government rates.
Regarding scheme for improving the soil health, Soil Organic Carbon (SOC) is a
critical component of soil health and plays a vital role in the production of quality
mulberry leaf. Its importance includes: Nutrient supply, Soil structure improvement,
Water retention, Microbial activity, Buffering capacity and Climate resilience.
Recent study indicates that SOC of mulberry gardens in Karnataka has depleted
to 0.4 per cent very much below the optimum level of 1.0 per cent. Higher level of
SOC in the mulberry gardens of Japan (1.8 per cent) and China (1.9 per cent) are
producing good quality mulberry leaves and in turn resulting in superior quality
cocoons and raw silk. In order to overcome this, it is necessary to introduce some
schemes to help farmers to improve their soil status
Regarding establishment of Silk Museum & Silk Tourism Corridor, with a production
of around 38500 MT of raw silk per annum, India is the second largest producer of
silk in the world. Indian Sericulture has a rich history of more than 200 years, being
21.03.2025 1615
known for its exquisite craftsmanship and unique designs made using handlooms.
Silk is an integral part of Indian culture and has a special place, especially amongst
womenfolk. Karnataka is a pioneering State in sericulture with around 40 per cent
of country's raw silk production coming from the State. Sericulture has a strong
presence in the southern part of the State. The GI registered brand Mysore Silk
given to sarees produced by Karnataka Silk Industries Corporation is quite popular.
There is a scope and need for creating an infrastructure to showcase the rich
history of Indian silk through establishment of a state-of-the-art Silk Museum. The
proposed facility can also have place for carrying out different activities of silk value
chain for visitors to see and an infrastructure for artisans to sell their products. The
ideal location for creation of such an infrastructure can be in Bengaluru Mysore
corridor, which is known for its big presence in sericulture. Also, few Silk Corridors
/ Silk Routes can be considered for creation in famous silk weaving clusters across
the country clubbing them with tourist destinations present in and around.
Regarding formation of National Task Force, the formation of a national-level Task
Force for monitoring sericulture is crucial to align the sector with India's Vision 2030
and 2047, focusing on sustainable growth and enhancing silk production
productivity and quality. The sericulture sector involves multiple stakeholders,
including farmers, Central agency, State agencies, scientists, private players, and
policymakers. A Task Force will ensure better coordination among these entities,
fostering synergies and avoiding duplications in efforts to promote innovation, new
technologies, and improved cultivation practices, to meet national goals. The Task
Force can also help India tap into global knowledge by fostering Partnerships and
21.03.2025 1616
collaborations, such as introducing Israeli agriculture technologies or promoting
research into sustainable sericulture practices. It can guide efforts to customize
international best practices to India's unique conditions. It would promote
innovation, especially in Seri-Tech and smart farming, support climate resilience,
and foster global collaborations, such as adopting Israeli agriculture technologies.
Additionally, the Task Force would enhance India's export competitiveness by
improving quality standards and pre-cocoon and post-cocoon practices, making
sericulture a key driver of economic growth by 2030 and 2047.
Regarding establishment of Cold Storages for preservation of bivoltine eggs, to
achieve a quantum leap in the production of internationally gradable bivoltine raw
silk and make India self-sufficient, the key requirement is the large-scale production
of high-quality, disease-free bivoltine silkworm seeds (eggs). Considering the
envisaged target of production of 20,000 MT of gradable bivoltine raw silk in the
country by 2030, the requirement of bivoltine commercial seed would be 2800 lakhs
on a very conservative consideration against the current estimated production of
800 lakhs. That means the production has to be increased by 250%. While both
State and Central Governments are engaged in seed production, they are unable
to fully meet the domestic demand owing to manpower crunch and other reasons.
Therefore, encouraging the private entrepreneurs for taking up large-scale bivoltine
silkworm seed production is the need of hour. However, existing private players
face challenges in scaling up due to the lack of adequate cold storage facilities,
which are critical for preserving silkworm seeds for four to six months, as
recommended by best practices. To address this, there is an urgent need to
21.03.2025 1617
establish four high-capacity cold storage units in strategically located areas in the
southern region of the country. These facilities will ensure the preservation and
availability of quality seeds year-round, facilitating consistent raw silk production
and helping India become import-free in bivoltine silk.
Regarding establishment of cocoon markets under PPP model and introducing
quality linked pricing system, although, the existing Government cocoon markets
are facilitating the farmers with instant sale of their produce and realization of
money on the very same day, auction system followed in those markets for fixing
the rate does not reflect the cocoon quality in true sense and leads to unpredictable
pricing. To encourage higher quality cocoon production, as followed in all the
advanced countries like China, it is essential to introduce scientific quality linked
pricing system (based on Renditta) in all the markets which will ensure, fair and
transparent pricing and also minimizes market fluctuations. During the year 2015
the Government of Karnataka had constituted a 'Technical Committee to study the
cocoon marketing system in Karnataka, the committee in its report, recommended
implementation of cocoon quality testing and grading process for both crossbred
and bivoltine cocoons and also provided testing guidelines in the form of a protocol.
That 'Technical Committee' recommended a protocol to assess the quality from
one kg of cocoons sample which is randomly taken from the cocoon lot arriving in
the market, considering Defective Cocoons, Cocoon Weight, Shel Weight, Cocoon
Shell Ratio and Estimated Renditta. Based on these quality parameters cocoon lots
are graded as Grade-A, B and C and the cocoon rates could be fixed. Such a
system could be introduced at every cocoon market involving a third party for
21.03.2025 1618
cocoon assessment, under PPP model.
Regarding designation of Special Sericulture Zones, rapid urbanization and
industrialization are threatening traditional mulberry cultivation areas, essential for
silk production. For instance, Bangalore Rural district of Karnataka had an
appreciable mulberry area of 22,682 hectares in 1997-98, which has declined to a
bare 5,052 hectares during 2023-24. To address this, it is crucial to declare Special
Sericulture Zones (SSZs), which would protect key agricultural land from being
repurposed for non-agricultural activities. This approach will help safeguard the
livelihoods of rural families dependent on sericulture, ensure a stable domestic
supply of raw silk, and reduce dependency on imports. Additionally, preserving
mulberry cultivation supports ecological balance and allows for the integration of
modern technologies like loT and Seri-Tech, fostering innovation and sustainable
practices in the silk industry. Establishing SSZs is a strategic step towards securing
silk production and promoting rural development.
Further, it is essential to declare Special Sericulture Zones (SSZs) and legally
protect them from being repurposed for non-agricultural activities. Enforcing
specific laws to ensure these zones are used exclusively for mulberry cultivation
will preserve vital agricultural land, safeguard rural livelihoods, and secure the
future of domestic silk production. This protection will also promote environmental
sustainability, encourage innovation through technologies like loT and Seri-Tech,
and support the continued growth of the silk industry. Establishing and legally
enforcing SZs is a critical step towards maintaining the balance between
development and sericulture, ensuring the industry's long-term viability.
21.03.2025 1619
Regarding manpower crunch and need for filling up of the vacancies, retirement of
scientists and other technical staff has created a large vacuum at Central Silk
Board, KSSRDI, Thalaghatpura and DoS Karnataka. Central Silk Board's staff
strength was reduced to 1897 during 2021-22 compared to 3275 during 2012-13.
Every year more than 250 staff are retiring leading to restructuring of the existing
units and resorting to multi-tasking. Similarly, against the sanctioned strength of
4299 posts, DoS Karnataka has only 1191posts filled thus the vacancy is 3108
posts (72 per cent). At KSSRDI, Thalaghatpura against the scientists' sanctioned
strength of 54 now it has only 14 scientists
Regarding leasing of unused infrastructure by DoS Karnataka, a substantial
infrastructure, including Basic Seed Farms (BSFs), grainages and Cold Storages
was established in the earlier years utilizing the funds provided by World Bank and
other agencies. However, due to manpower shortage and other challenges, much
of the infrastructure has become defunct and remains underutilized or totally
unutilized. To revitalize these assets, they can be leased out to NGOs or other
individuals through SAI, exclusively for undertaking sericulture related
developmental activities.
Regarding coordination between CSB and State Sericulture Departments (DoSs),
success of any program depends on the concerted efforts of all the agencies
involved and their active participation. Progress of sericulture in any state and in
turn in the country depends solely on the coordinated efforts of both Central Silk
Board and respective DoSs. In the past there used be monthly bivoltine
coordination committee meetings jointly held by DoS Karnataka and Central Silk
21.03.2025 1620
Board which has ceased long back and that's why the bivoltine implementation
programe is not running smoothly and the stakeholders are facing lot of problems.
For successful implementation of bivoltine program, monthly bivoltine coordination
committee meetings to be compulsorily attended by both State and CSB officers/
scientists, have to be revived with immediate effect
Regarding strengthening R and D, establishment of by-product laboratory,
strengthening R&D in sericulture is crucial for enhanced productivity and
sustainability, value addition through by-products and ensuring the industry's global
competitiveness, through the following core ideas establishing an exclusive by-
product institute under a Public-Private Partnership (PPP) model: A dedicated
institute focused on developing innovative uses for silk byproducts from mulberry
leaves, mulberry fruits, mulberry roots, pupae, moths, silkworm excreta, sericin,
fibrion and silk fibre waste can significantly boost value addition. Through a PPP
model, the government can provide infrastructure support, while private players
bring in cutting-edge technologies and commercialization of strategies.
Collaborative Research Initiatives: Encourage collaborations between research
institutions, universities, and industry leaders to work on high-impact research. This
includes improving cocoon and silk yield, pest resistance in host plants, and water
efficiency, while also exploring sustainable by-product processing methods that
create economic value from sericulture waste.
Advanced Technological Integration: Introduce modern technologies like
biotechnology, nanotechnology, and artificial intelligence in silk R&D. These can
enhance cocoon and silk quality, optimize resource use, and create innovative
21.03.2025 1621
byproducts like high-performance textile materials.
Innovation Hubs for Start-ups: Create innovation hubs within the by-product
institute that foster start-ups and entrepreneurs focusing on silk processing and
waste management. This could include incubators supported by industry
mentorship and funding for start-ups developing silk-based eco-friendly products,
medical products and cosmetics.
Policy Support and Incentives: Provide policy frameworks that encourage private
sector investment in silk R&D under the PPP model. This could involve tax
incentives for research initiatives, subsidies for R&D infrastructure development,
and grants for innovations that enhance silk by-product utilization, ensuring the
economic viability of the sector.
Regarding extending support to Soil to Silk Company, for the first time in India a
Soil to Silk Company has been established at Bangalore having members of SAI
as its advisors and shareholders. Intention is to strengthen the silk industry
mobilizing its experienced manpower. It has planned few prolific projects like;
Exposure of College Students to Sericulture Activities through seri-tourism, taking
up the production of Pl basic seed production to supplement the efforts of CSB and
DoS. It also has many other developmental activities on its agenda. In the initial
stages it requires financial, logical support and hand holding support from
both CSB and DoS.

(cid:174)ी िश वराज िसहं चौहान : माननीय सभापित महोदय, मुझे लगता है िक ये मेरे उ(cid:176)र को सुनने से घबरा

of order is in respect of Rule 331G (d) of the Rules of Procedure and Conduct of
Business in Lok Sabha. This House is going to approve, and is giving approval for

Sir, my point is about the Departmentally Related Standing Committees.
21.03.2025 1648
There is a specific provision in the Rule – ‘Procedure Relating to Demands for
Grants’. Sir, Clause (d) says that the Demands for Grants shall be considered by
the House in the light of the reports of the Committees.
It is quite unfortunate to note that most of the Departmentally Related
Standing Committees have not submitted their reports so far. Without having the
reports, we are making the discussions and we are considering the Demands. It is
not proper and fair. At least, I am seeking a ruling from the hon. Speaker. At least,
a Direction be given that the Standing Committees should submit their reports
before the Demands are taken up for consideration.
Sir, this is my point of order. Kindly have a ruling on this. Thank you.

APPROPRIATION (NO.3) BILL, 2025
माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) : आइटम स(cid:180)ं या-26, माननीय मं(cid:253)ी जी ।
THE MINISTER OF FINANCE; AND MINISTER OF CORPORATE AFFAIRS
(SHRIMATI NIRMALA SITHARAMAN): Sir, I beg to move for leave to introduce a
Bill to authorise payment and appropriation of certain sums from and out of the
Consolidated Fund of India for the services of the financial year 2025-26.
माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177) : (cid:255)(cid:307) यह है:-
“िक िव(cid:176)ीय वष(cid:170) 2025-26 क(cid:236) सेवाओ ं के िलए भारत क(cid:236) संिचत िनिध म(cid:164) से कितपय
रािशय(cid:332) के संदाय और िविनयोग को (cid:255)ािधकृत करने वाले िवधेयक को पुर:(cid:214)थािपत करने
क(cid:236) अनमु ित दी जाए ।”
(cid:255)(cid:214)ताव (cid:214)वीकृ त ह(cid:242)आ ।

SHRIMATI NIRMALA SITHARAMAN: Sir, I introduce the Bill.
माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177): आइटम नंबर 27 – माननीय िव(cid:176) म(cid:253)ं ी जी ।
THE MINISTER OF FINANCE; AND MINISTER OF CORPORATE AFFAIRS
(SHRIMATI NIRMALA SITHARAMAN): Sir, I beg to move:
“That the Bill to authorise payment and appropriation of certain sums
from and out of the Consolidated Fund of India for the services of the
financial year 2025-26, be taken into consideration.”
माननीय अ(cid:197)य(cid:177): (cid:255)(cid:307) यह है:
“िक िव(cid:176)ीय वष(cid:170) 2025-26 क(cid:236) सेवाओ ंके िलए भारत क(cid:236) संिचत िनिध म(cid:164) स े कितपय
 Published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part-II, Section- 2, dated 21.03.2025.
Introduced with the recommendation of the President.

रािशय(cid:332) के संदाय और िविनयोग को (cid:255)ािधकृत करने वाले िवधेयक पर िवचार िकया
जाए ।”
(cid:255)(cid:214)ताव (cid:214)वीकृ त ह(cid:242)आ ।

INTERNET
The Original Version of Lok Sabha proceedings is available on Parliament of India Website at the

www.sansad.in/ls
LIVE TELECAST OF PROCEEDINGS OF LOK SABHA
Lok Sabha proceedings are being telecast live on Sansad T.V. Channel. Live telecast begins at 11
A.M. everyday the Lok Sabha sits, till the adjournment of the House.
